topics.title: Water Demand Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Demand Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Demand Estimation** is the systematic engineering process of determining the **quantity of water** required to be supplied by a public water supply scheme to serve a given population over a specified **design period**, accounting for all categories of consumption (domestic, industrial, institutional, commercial, fire-fighting, public uses, and system losses).

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, water demand estimation is the **first and most critical step** in the design of any municipal water supply scheme. It governs the sizing of every downstream component — intake structures, rising mains, treatment units, service reservoirs, and distribution networks.

Mathematically, the total water demand is expressed as:

$$Q_{total} = P_n \times q_{avg} \times \text{Demand Factor}$$

where $P_n$ is the projected population at the end of the design period, and $q_{avg}$ is the per capita water demand in litres per capita per day (lpcd).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Planning a Wedding Feast"**
>
> Imagine you are hosting a wedding reception. Before you book a banquet hall, you must first ask:
> 1. **How many guests will come in 10 years?** (Population Forecasting)
> 2. **How much food will each person eat?** (Per Capita Demand)
> 3. **What about extra guests who show up uninvited?** (Peak Factor / Fire Demand)
> 4. **How much will be wasted or spilled on the floor?** (System Losses)
>
> Water demand estimation works **exactly the same way** — except the "guests" are citizens, the "feast" is the water supply, and the "spillage" represents real losses in the distribution network. If you under-estimate, the city faces water scarcity; if you over-estimate, taxpayers waste crores on oversized pipes and treatment plants that sit half-empty.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Standard Value (as per **CPHEEO Manual**) |
|---|---|
| **Per Capita Water Demand (q)** | **135 lpcd** (minimum for towns, with private connections) |
| **Per Capita Demand (metropolitan cities)** | **150 – 200 lpcd** |
| **Minimum Fire Demand** | As per **Kuichling's / Buston's / National Board of Fire Underwriters' formulas** |
| **Design Period** | **30 years** (general); **15 years** for pumping machinery |
| **Peak Factor** | **2.7** (in India, as per CPHEEO) |
| **UAR (Unit Annual Rate of Increase)** | **Decadal Growth Rate** from Census of India data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Favourite:** The value **135 lpcd** is the benchmark figure for domestic + non-domestic demand in Indian urban water supply schemes. Always cite CPHEEO when justifying any per capita figure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components / Types of Water Demand

A municipal water supply scheme must satisfy the following **six broad categories** of demand. Every KTU numerical problem expects you to identify and sum these components.

- **Domestic Demand (D):** Water used in residences for drinking, cooking, bathing, washing, flushing toilets, gardening. This is the **largest single component**, usually **50–60%** of total demand.
  - *Sub-uses:* Drinking (3 lpcd), Cooking (5 lpcd), Bathing (25–40 lpcd), Washing clothes (20 lpcd), Toilet flushing (25–40 lpcd), Gardening (10 lpcd).

- **Industrial / Commercial Demand (I):** Water consumed by factories, offices, hotels, malls, and commercial establishments. Usually **20–25%** of total demand in industrial towns, lower (5–10%) in residential towns.

- **Institutional Demand (Ins):** Schools, colleges, hospitals, government offices, railway stations, airports. Typically **5–10%** of total demand.

- **Public / Civic Demand (P):** Public standposts, fountains, street washing, public gardens, public latrines, road sprinkling, sewer flushing. Usually **5–10%**.

- **Fire Demand (F):** Water required to extinguish fires. Although intermittent and short-duration, it dictates the **peak hourly flow** in the design of distribution mains. Calculated using **Kuichling's formula**:
  $$F = 3182 \sqrt{P}$$ (in litres per minute, P in thousands)

- **Losses / Unaccounted-for Water (L):** Leakage from pipes, unauthorised connections, meter malfunctions, theft. In Indian conditions, this can be a staggering **15–40%** of total supply. This is a **mandatory component** in KTU answers.

---

### 2.2 Factors Affecting Per Capita Demand (PCD)

> [!NOTE]
> These factors are asked almost every year as a 3-mark or short essay question in KTU Module 1.

- **Climate & Weather Conditions:** Hotter, drier regions (e.g., Rajasthan, Kerala's dry season) require higher PCD.
- **Size of City:** Larger cities → higher PCD (better plumbing, more amenities, commercial activity).
- **Habits & Standard of Living:** Affluent populations consume more water (car washing, swimming pools, gardens).
- **Industrial Activity:** Industrial towns (e.g., Cochin's industrial belt) have higher PCD.
- **Cost of Water:** Tariff structure influences consumption. Subsidised water → higher demand (wasteful use).
- **Pressure in Distribution System:** Higher pressure leads to higher consumption due to continuous flow.
- **Quality of Water:** Poor quality water is often used inefficiently (people collect and store, then re-use).
- **Sewerage System Availability:** Cities with full sewerage have higher PCD (flush toilets, bathrooms).
- **System Efficiency & Metering:** Unmetered supplies have much higher losses.

---

### 2.3 Design Period — Definition & Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Period** is the number of years for which the water supply scheme is designed to adequately serve the population. It is selected based on:
> - Useful life of structural components (e.g., RCC reservoirs → 50 years)
> - Anticipated rate of growth
> - Ease of future expansion
> - Availability of funds and technology

| Component | Recommended Design Period |
|---|---|
| **Dams, treatment works, large mains** | **30 years** |
| **Pumping machinery, electric motors** | **15 years** |
| **Distribution pipes (small)** | **15–20 years** |
| **Service connections** | **5–10 years** |

---

### 2.4 Population Forecasting Methods (The KTU High-Yield Topic)

Population forecasting is the **most heavily tested topic** in Module 1. Six methods must be mastered:

#### (a) Arithmetic Increase Method
- **Assumption:** Population increases by a constant absolute number (the arithmetic mean of past decadal increments) every decade.
- **Formula:**
  $$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$$
  where $\bar{x}$ = average (arithmetic) decadal increase = $\dfrac{\Sigma x}{n}$.
- **Drawback:** Ignores the fact that growth is proportional, not additive. Suitable only for **old, saturated cities**.

#### (b) Geometric Increase Method
- **Assumption:** Population increases at a **constant percentage rate** (geometric mean of past growth rates) every decade.
- **Formula:**
  $$P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r_g}{100}\right)^n$$
  where $r_g$ = geometric mean growth rate = $\left[\dfrac{P_n}{P_0}\right]^{1/n} - 1$ (in decimals).
- **Drawback:** Gives very high future populations; not suitable for very large cities approaching saturation.

#### (c) Incremental Increase Method
- **Assumption:** The **increment itself** increases by a constant amount each decade (arithmetic increase of arithmetic increases).
- **Formula:**
  $$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$$
  where $\bar{y}$ = average incremental increase = $\dfrac{\Sigma y}{n}$.
- **Why used:** Compromise between arithmetic and geometric methods. **Most commonly used in KTU problems.**

#### (d) Decreasing Rate of Growth Method
- **Assumption:** Growth rate decreases by a constant factor each decade, approaching zero.
- **Formula:**
  $$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)\right]^{n-1}$$ (varies by sub-method)
- **Best suited for:** **Large, rapidly growing cities** (Delhi, Mumbai) that will eventually saturate.

#### (e) Logistic Curve Method (Verhulst-Pearl Model)
- **Assumption:** Population follows an **S-shaped curve** (slow start → rapid growth → saturation).
- **Formula:**
  $$P = \dfrac{P_{sat}}{1 + m \cdot e^{-n \cdot t}}$$
  where $P_{sat}$ = saturation population, $m$ and $n$ are constants determined from past data.
- **Best suited for:** **Mature, well-studied cities** with available saturation data.

#### (f) Graphical Comparison Method
- **Assumption:** The growth curve of the city is **analogous** to that of some other similar, well-populated city.
- **Procedure:** Plot past populations of both cities; shift the well-known city's curve to match the early years of the subject city; extrapolate.

#### (g) Zoning Method
- **Assumption:** City is divided into **zones** (e.g., commercial, residential, industrial), and each is forecast independently using density-based planning.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every 14-mark Part B question.**

| S.No. | Method | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Increase | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | Old, saturated towns |
| 2 | Geometric Increase | $P_n = P_0 (1 + r_g)^n$ | Fast-growing small towns |
| 3 | Incremental Increase | $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$ | Moderate-growth towns (KTU default) |
| 4 | Geometric Mean Growth Rate | $r_g = \left(\dfrac{P_{last}}{P_{first}}\right)^{1/k} - 1$ | Always compute this first |
| 5 | Per Capita Demand | $q = \dfrac{Total\ Daily\ Demand}{Population}$ | Basic relation |
| 6 | Total Quantity Required | $Q = P_n \times q$ (lpcd → MLD needs $\div 10^6$) | Always in lpcd or MLD |
| 7 | Kuichling's Fire Demand | $F = 3182 \sqrt{P}$ (L/min, P in thousands) | Fire demand problems |
| 8 | Buston's Formula | $F = 5663 \sqrt{P}$ (L/min) | Older Indian textbooks |
| 9 | National Board Formula | $F = 4637 \sqrt{P} \left[1 - 0.01\sqrt{P}\right]$ (L/min) | US-based; high accuracy |
| 10 | Peak Factor | $\text{PF} = \dfrac{Maximum Hourly Demand}{Average Hourly Demand}$ | 2.7 (India, CPHEEO) |
| 11 | Coincident Draft | $\text{Peak Demand} = \text{PF} \times \text{Average Demand}$ | Design of distribution mains |
| 12 | Design Discharge | $Q_{design} = \dfrac{P_n \times q_{max}}{86400}$ (L/s) | Pump and pipe sizing |

> **Note on Units:** $1\ \text{MLD} = 10^6\ \text{litres/day} = 1\ \text{Million Litres per Day} \approx 11.57\ \text{L/s}$

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planning:** Master plans for Smart Cities (e.g., Kochi Smart City) require 30-year water demand projections.
- **Pipe Sourcing & Procurement:** Pipe diameters are standardised (e.g., DI pipes come in 100mm, 150mm, 200mm…). Accurate demand estimation avoids over- or under-design.
- **Pump Selection & Energy Audits:** Pumps are the largest energy consumer in water utilities (often **60–80% of O&M cost**). Wrong demand estimation = wasted electricity.
- **Climate Resilience:** As Kerala faces monsoonal extremes, accurate demand estimation ensures storage adequacy during dry months.
- **Government Tendering (KWA, KUDC):** All Kerala Water Authority (KWA) projects and PWD schemes use CPHEEO-aligned demand estimation for DPR (Detailed Project Report) preparation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of Key Population Forecasting Formulas

#### 3.1.1 Geometric Mean Growth Rate ($r_g$)

> [!NOTE]
> **The geometric mean growth rate is the foundation** of both the geometric increase method AND the incremental increase method. Derive it once, use it forever.

Let the populations in successive censuses be $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ separated by equal time intervals (decades).

The decadal growth rates are:
$$r_1 = \frac{P_1 - P_0}{P_0},\quad r_2 = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1},\quad \ldots,\quad r_n = \frac{P_n - P_{n-1}}{P_{n-1}}$$

For the **geometric mean**, the overall growth factor over $n$ decades must equal the product of individual decadal growth factors:

$$1 + r_g = \left[(1+r_1)(1+r_2)\cdots(1+r_n)\right]^{1/n}$$

Substituting the definition of each $r_i$:

$$1 + r_g = \left[\frac{P_1}{P_0} \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_1} \cdot \frac{P_3}{P_2} \cdots \frac{P_n}{P_{n-1}}\right]^{1/n}$$

The intermediate terms cancel (telescoping product):

$$1 + r_g = \left[\frac{P_n}{P_0}\right]^{1/n}$$

$$\boxed{r_g = \left(\frac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1}$$

This is the **canonical formula** used universally. If the given data is for **4 decades (5 census years)**, then $n = 4$.

---

#### 3.1.2 Incremental Increase Method (Step-by-Step)

Let past decadal increments be $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ and the **increments of increments** (i.e., the *second differences*) be $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$, where $y_i = x_{i+1} - x_i$.

**Average decadal increase:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n}$$

**Average incremental increase:**
$$\bar{y} = \frac{y_1 + y_2 + \cdots + y_{n-1}}{n-1}$$

For the $k$-th decade after the last census, the expected increment is:
$$\text{Increment in decade } k = \bar{x} + k\bar{y}$$

Summing from $k = 1$ to $k = n$ (arithmetic series):

$$P_n - P_0 = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (\bar{x} + k\bar{y}) = n\bar{x} + \bar{y} \sum_{k=1}^{n} k$$

Using $\sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$:

$$\boxed{P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}}$$

> **KTU Tip:** When the question says "use the **most appropriate method**", the answer is **Incremental Increase** in 70% of cases.

---

#### 3.1.3 Kuichling's Fire Demand Formula — Derivation

Empirically, Kuichling observed that fire flow is proportional to the square root of population (small doubling in flow required for large population increase, due to overlapping fire zones, multiple fire stations, etc.).

$$F = K \sqrt{P}$$

With the **empirical constant** $K = 3182$ (in SI units: L/min, with P in thousands) for Indian conditions, we get:

$$\boxed{F = 3182\sqrt{P}\ \text{(L/min, P in thousands)}}$$

**Conversion to MLD (for total daily water balance):**

$$F_{MLD} = \frac{3182 \sqrt{P} \times t_{min}}{10^6 \times 1440}$$

where $t_{min}$ is the assumed fire duration (typically 2–4 hours per fire event).

---

### 3.2 Fully Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the type of question that appears as a 14-mark Part B problem in KTU ESE.** Work through it completely — the steps and units matter.

#### **Problem Statement:**
The population of a town recorded in successive censuses is given below:

| Census Year | 1971 | 1981 | 1991 | 2001 | 2011 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Population | 28,000 | 35,000 | 45,500 | 59,000 | 75,000 |

Estimate the population in **2031** using:
1. Arithmetic Increase Method
2. Geometric Increase Method
3. Incremental Increase Method

Also estimate the total water demand (in MLD) for 2031, assuming per capita demand = **150 lpcd**, and a fire demand equal to Kuichling's estimate for one major fire lasting **3 hours**.

---

#### **Solution — Step 1: Compute Decadal Increments and Incremental Increases**

$$x_1 = 35000 - 28000 = 7000$$
$$x_2 = 45500 - 35000 = 10500$$
$$x_3 = 59000 - 45500 = 13500$$
$$x_4 = 75000 - 59000 = 16000$$

Incremental Increases (second differences):
$$y_1 = 10500 - 7000 = 3500$$
$$y_2 = 13500 - 10500 = 3000$$
$$y_3 = 16000 - 13500 = 2500$$

Number of decades to forecast from 2011 → 2031: $n = 2$ decades.

---

#### **Solution — Step 2: Arithmetic Increase Method**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{7000 + 10500 + 13500 + 16000}{4} = \frac{47000}{4} = 11750$$

$$P_{2031} = P_{2011} + n \cdot \bar{x} = 75000 + 2 \times 11750$$

$$\boxed{P_{2031}^{arith} = 98500\ \text{people}}$$

---

#### **Solution — Step 3: Geometric Increase Method**

Geometric mean growth rate:

$$r_g = \left(\frac{P_{2011}}{P_{1971}}\right)^{1/4} - 1 = \left(\frac{75000}{28000}\right)^{0.25} - 1$$

$$\frac{75000}{28000} = 2.6786$$

$$(2.6786)^{0.25} = 1.2785$$

$$r_g = 1.2785 - 1 = 0.2785 = 27.85\%$$

$$P_{2031} = 75000 \times (1.2785)^2 = 75000 \times 1.6346$$

$$\boxed{P_{2031}^{geom} \approx 1,22{,}595\ \text{people}}$$

---

#### **Solution — Step 4: Incremental Increase Method**

$$\bar{x} = 11750 \quad \text{(as before)}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{3500 + 3000 + 2500}{3} = \frac{9000}{3} = 3000$$

$$P_{2031} = P_{2011} + n\bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$$

$$P_{2031} = 75000 + 2(11750) + \frac{2 \times 3}{2}(3000)$$

$$P_{2031} = 75000 + 23500 + 3 \times 3000$$

$$P_{2031} = 75000 + 23500 + 9000$$

$$\boxed{P_{2031}^{incr} = 1{,}07{,}500\ \text{people}}$$

---

#### **Solution — Step 5: Water Demand Calculation**

We adopt the **Incremental Increase** estimate as the most appropriate ($P_{2031} = 1,07,500$).

**Average daily domestic + non-domestic demand:**

$$Q_{avg} = P_n \times q = 1,07,500 \times 150 = 1,61,25,000\ \text{L/day} = 16.125\ \text{MLD}$$

**Fire Demand (Kuichling's, P in thousands = 107.5):**

$$F = 3182 \sqrt{107.5} = 3182 \times 10.368$$

$$F \approx 32{,}990\ \text{L/min} \approx 32.99\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

For 3 hours (180 min):

$$V_{fire} = 32.99 \times 180 = 5938.2\ \text{m}^3 = 5.94\ \text{ML}$$

(equivalently $5.94\ \text{MLD}$ if treated as one-day requirement)

**Total Daily Demand:**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{avg} + V_{fire} = 16.125 + 5.94 \approx 22.07\ \text{MLD}$$

> **Conversion sanity check:** 1 MLD = 11.574 L/s, so 22.07 MLD = 255.4 L/s peak design flow. This is the flow the rising main and treatment plant must handle.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Population Forecasting Methods

| Method | Best Suited For | Tendency (over/under-estimates) | Data Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic Increase** | Old, slow-growth towns | Under-estimates | Past decadal populations |
| **Geometric Increase** | New, fast-growing towns | Over-estimates for long periods | Past decadal populations |
| **Incremental Increase** | Moderate, stable growth | Most realistic (compromise) | Past populations + increments |
| **Decreasing Rate** | Large saturating cities | Realistic long-term | Past populations + growth rates |
| **Logistic Curve** | Mature cities with known saturation | Highly accurate | Saturation population |
| **Graphical Comparison** | When analogy city is available | Subjective but good | Both cities' histories |
| **Zoning Method** | Master-plan-level design | Best for large cities | Land-use and density plans |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Water Demand Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Study Area and Design Period] --> B[Collect Past Census Data: At least 3-4 decades]
    B --> C[Compute Decadal Increments and Incremental Increases]
    C --> D{Select Forecasting Method}
    D --> D1[Arithmetic Increase]
    D --> D2[Geometric Increase]
    D --> D3[Incremental Increase]
    D --> D4[Decreasing Rate]
    D --> D5[Logistic Curve]
    D --> D6[Graphical Comparison]
    D1 --> E[Projected Population Pn]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Per Capita Demand from CPHEEO Norms]
    F --> G[Component-Wise Demand: Domestic, Industrial, Institutional, Public]
    G --> H[Add Fire Demand: Kuichling or Buston Formula]
    H --> I[Add Losses and UFW: 15 to 40 percent]
    I --> J[Compute Peak Factor: PF equals 2.7 India]
    J --> K[Final Design Discharge Qd in MLD or L per s]
    K --> L[Sizing of Intake, Treatment Plant, Pumps, Mains, ESR]
```

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition of Total Water Demand

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Total Water Demand Q] --> D[Domestic Demand 50 to 60 percent]
    T --> I[Industrial and Commercial 20 to 25 percent]
    T --> INS[Institutional 5 to 10 percent]
    T --> P[Public and Civic 5 to 10 percent]
    T --> F[Fire Demand Calculated via Formula]
    T --> L[Losses and UFW 15 to 40 percent]
    D --> D1[Drinking 3 lpcd]
    D --> D2[Cooking 5 lpcd]
    D --> D3[Bathing 25 to 40 lpcd]
    D --> D4[Washing 20 lpcd]
    D --> D5[Toilet Flushing 25 to 40 lpcd]
    D --> D6[Gardening 10 lpcd]
    I --> I1[Factories and Plants]
    I --> I2[Hotels and Malls]
    I --> I3[Offices]
    F --> F1[Kuichling: F = 3182 sqrt P]
    F --> F2[Buston: F = 5663 sqrt P]
    F --> F3[NBFU: F = 4637 sqrt P times 1 minus 0.01 sqrt P]
    L --> L1[Pipe Leakage]
    L --> L2[Unauthorized Tappings]
    L --> L3[Meter Errors]
    L --> L4[Theft]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Population Forecasting Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Population Forecasting Method] --> Q1{Is growth rate constant?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the rate constant in absolute or percentage terms?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Decreasing Rate or Logistic Curve]
    Q2 -- Absolute constant --> M1[Arithmetic Increase Method]
    Q2 -- Percentage constant --> M2[Geometric Increase Method]
    Q3 --> Q4{Is saturation population known?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> M3[Logistic Curve Method]
    Q4 -- No --> M5[Graphical Comparison Method]
    M1 --> Comp[Compare all estimates and adopt most reasonable]
    M2 --> Comp
    M3 --> Comp
    M5 --> Comp
    Comp --> Final[Final adopted Pn]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Per Capita Demand" of water. List any four factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Per Capita Demand (PCD) is the **average quantity of water required per person per day** in a community, expressed in **litres per capita per day (lpcd)**. Mathematically:
> $$q = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{P}$$ where $Q_{total}$ is total daily water consumption (litres/day) and $P$ is total population served.

> **Factors affecting PCD (1.5 marks — any 4 of the following):**
> 1. **Climate:** Hotter regions → higher PCD.
> 2. **Size of city:** Larger cities → higher PCD.
> 3. **Standard of living:** Affluent populations consume more.
> 4. **Industrial activity:** Industrial towns → higher PCD.
> 5. **Cost of water / tariff:** Cheaper water → wasteful use.
> 6. **Quality of distribution:** Higher pressure → more leakage and use.
> 7. **Availability of sewerage:** Triggers flush toilet usage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between "Arithmetic Increase Method" and "Geometric Increase Method" of population forecasting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Arithmetic Increase Method (1.5 marks):**
> - Assumes population grows by a **constant absolute number** every decade.
> - Formula: $P_n = P_0 + n \bar{x}$.
> - Suitable for **old, saturated cities** with negligible percentage growth.
> - Tends to **under-estimate** the future population for long design periods.

> **Geometric Increase Method (1.5 marks):**
> - Assumes population grows at a **constant percentage rate** every decade (compound growth).
> - Formula: $P_n = P_0 (1 + r_g)^n$.
> - Suitable for **new, rapidly growing** industrial towns.
> - Tends to **over-estimate** for very long design periods and large cities.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme Outcomes]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** A town had a population of **25,000** in 2001 and **48,000** in 2021. Estimate the population in **2041** using:
> 1. Arithmetic Increase Method
> 2. Geometric Increase Method
> 3. Incremental Increase Method
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** For the most reasonable population estimate obtained in (a), calculate the **total water demand in MLD** assuming per capita demand = **135 lpcd**, fire demand using **Kuichling's formula** (duration = **2 hours**), and losses at **20%** of total demand.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a) — Population Forecast**

Number of decades from 2001 to 2021: $n = 2$.
Number of decades from 2021 to 2041: $k = 2$ (forecast horizon).

**Method 1 — Arithmetic Increase Method:**

Decadal increase: $x = \dfrac{48000 - 25000}{2} = \dfrac{23000}{2} = 11500$

$$P_{2041} = P_{2021} + k \cdot x = 48000 + 2 \times 11500 = 48000 + 23000$$

$$\boxed{P_{2041}^{arith} = 71{,}000\ \text{people}}$$

**Method 2 — Geometric Increase Method:**

$$r_g = \left(\frac{48000}{25000}\right)^{1/2} - 1 = (1.92)^{0.5} - 1$$

$$1.92^{0.5} = 1.3856$$

$$r_g = 0.3856 = 38.56\%$$

$$P_{2041} = 48000 \times (1.3856)^2 = 48000 \times 1.9200$$

$$\boxed{P_{2041}^{geom} = 92{,}160\ \text{people}}$$

**Method 3 — Incremental Increase Method:**

Since only 2 decades of data are available, we cannot compute incremental increases meaningfully. Therefore, the **average decadal increase** $\bar{x} = 11500$ and **incremental increase** $\bar{y} = 0$ (assumed, due to insufficient data). Thus:

$$P_{2041} = P_{2021} + k\bar{x} + \frac{k(k+1)}{2}\bar{y} = 48000 + 2(11500) + 0$$

$$\boxed{P_{2041}^{incr} = 71{,}000\ \text{people}}$$

> **Most reasonable estimate:** With only 2 decades of data, the **Incremental Increase Method** is unreliable (no $\bar{y}$ to compute). The **Geometric Increase Method (92,160)** is the most appropriate for a **fast-growing town**, as arithmetic and incremental methods under-estimate.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b) — Total Water Demand**

**Adopted population:** $P_{2041} = 92{,}160$ (geometric estimate)

**Average daily demand (domestic + non-domestic):**

$$Q_{avg} = 92160 \times 135 = 1,24,41,600\ \text{L/day} = 12.4416\ \text{MLD}$$

**Fire demand (Kuichling's, P in thousands = 92.16):**

$$F = 3182 \sqrt{92.16} = 3182 \times 9.600 = 30547.2\ \text{L/min}$$

Converting to L/day for 2 hours (120 min):

$$Q_{fire} = 30547.2 \times 120 = 36,65,664\ \text{L/day} = 3.666\ \text{MLD}$$

**Subtotal (before losses):**

$$Q_{sub} = 12.4416 + 3.666 = 16.108\ \text{MLD}$$

**Adding 20% losses:**

$$Q_{losses} = 0.20 \times 16.108 = 3.222\ \text{MLD}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 16.108 + 3.222 = 19.33\ \text{MLD}}$$

**Equivalent design discharge in L/s:**

$$Q_{design} = \frac{19.33 \times 10^6}{86400} = 223.7\ \text{L/s}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme Outcomes]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Incremental Increase Method** of population forecasting with a suitable formula. Why is it considered more realistic than the Arithmetic and Geometric methods for moderate-growth towns?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A city had the following census data:
>
> | Year | 1981 | 1991 | 2001 | 2011 | 2021 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Population | 50,000 | 62,000 | 76,000 | 92,000 | 1,10,000 |
>
> Estimate the population in **2041** using the **Geometric Increase Method**. If the per capita water demand is **150 lpcd** and losses are **15%**, find the total water demand in **MLD**. Also compute the design discharge in **L/s** using a peak factor of **2.7**.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Incremental Increase Method** is a **refinement of the Arithmetic Increase Method** that accounts for the fact that the decadal increase is **itself increasing** (or decreasing) in a roughly arithmetic pattern.

**Assumption (1 mark):** The decadal population increase grows by a **constant incremental amount** $\bar{y}$ each decade.

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$$

where:
- $P_0$ = last known population
- $n$ = number of decades to forecast
- $\bar{x}$ = average decadal increase
- $\bar{y}$ = average incremental increase (second difference)

**Why it is more realistic (4 marks):**
1. **Compromise between extremes:** Arithmetic method under-estimates (constant absolute growth), while Geometric method over-estimates (constant percentage growth). Incremental Increase bridges both.
2. **Captures the trend of trends:** It accounts for the fact that the *rate of growth* itself is changing — most Indian towns in their developing phase show this pattern.
3. **Empirically validated:** Studies by the Census of India and CPHEEO show Indian urban populations often follow this trajectory before saturation.
4. **Mathematically simple yet flexible:** It uses the same data as the other two methods, but adds a second-order correction term $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute geometric mean growth rate (1 mark):**

$$r_g = \left(\frac{P_{2021}}{P_{1981}}\right)^{1/4} - 1 = \left(\frac{1,10,000}{50,000}\right)^{0.25} - 1$$

$$\frac{1,10,000}{50,000} = 2.2$$

$$2.2^{0.25} = 1.2179$$

$$r_g = 0.2179 = 21.79\%$$

**Step 2: Project to 2041 ($n = 2$ decades from 2021) (1 mark):**

$$P_{2041} = 1,10,000 \times (1.2179)^2 = 1,10,000 \times 1.4833$$

$$\boxed{P_{2041} \approx 1,63,165\ \text{people}}$$

**Step 3: Average daily demand (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{avg} = 1,63,165 \times 150 = 2,44,74,750\ \text{L/day} = 24.475\ \text{MLD}$$

**Step 4: Add 15% losses (1 mark):**

$$Q_{total} = \frac{24.475}{0.85} = 28.79\ \text{MLD}$$

(Equivalently: $Q_{losses} = 0.15 \times 28.79 = 4.32\ \text{MLD}$; total = 24.475 + 4.32 = 28.79 MLD)

**Step 5: Design discharge with peak factor (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{peak} = 2.7 \times \frac{28.79 \times 10^6}{86400} = 2.7 \times 333.2$$

$$\boxed{Q_{design} = 899.6\ \text{L/s} \approx 900\ \text{L/s}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully Before Exam!)**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** The most common error is mixing **lpcd, MLD, and L/s** without proper conversion. Always show:
>    - $1\ \text{MLD} = 10^6\ \text{L/day}$
>    - $1\ \text{MLD} = 11.574\ \text{L/s}$
>    - $1\ \text{cusec} = 28.32\ \text{L/s}$
>
> 2. **Wrong $n$ in geometric mean (–1 mark):** When computing $r_g$ from $k$ decades of data, students often use $n$ (forecast horizon) instead of $k$ (number of past intervals). Remember:
>    - $r_g = (P_{last}/P_{first})^{1/k} - 1$, where $k$ = number of decadal intervals in past data.
>    - $P_n = P_0 (1+r_g)^{n}$, where $n$ = forecast horizon.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $\bar{y}$ sign in Incremental Increase (–1 to –2 marks):** If increments are **decreasing** (negative $\bar{y}$), the formula still works — but you must compute $\bar{y}$ correctly (sum divided by $n-1$, not $n$).
>
> 4. **Ignoring Fire Demand (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students compute only $P \times q$ and stop. KTU examiners **expect** fire demand to be added. If the question mentions fire, **always** use Kuichling's formula.
>
> 5. **Not Adding Losses (–1 to –2 marks):** Losses / UFW are part of total demand. If the question says "losses = 20%", it means the supply must be 20% **more** than the consumption demand, so divide by 0.80, not multiply by 0.20.
>
> 6. **Confusing Peak Factor with Maximum Daily Demand (–1 mark):** Peak factor in India (CPHEEO) is for **maximum hourly demand**, not maximum daily. Maximum daily factor ≈ 1.8; Maximum hourly factor = 2.7.
>
> 7. **Wrong Fire Demand Formula (–1 mark):** In Kuichling's formula, **P is in thousands** (i.e., P = 50 for 50,000 population). Forgetting this gives wildly wrong answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this list. Memorise the formula. Practice 3 numericals. Score full marks.**

- [ ] **Per Capita Demand = 135 lpcd** (CPHEEO minimum) — always cite this value.
- [ ] **Design Period = 30 years** for civil works, **15 years** for mechanical/electrical.
- [ ] **Six components** of total water demand: Domestic, Industrial, Institutional, Public, Fire, Losses.
- [ ] **Losses = 15–40%** in Indian systems (Kerala KWA aims for <15% by 2030).
- [ ] **Peak Factor = 2.7** (CPHEEO, India). Used for distribution main design.
- [ ] **Arithmetic Increase:** $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x}$ — old/saturated towns.
- [ ] **Geometric Increase:** $P_n = P_0 (1+r_g)^n$ — new/fast-growing towns.
- [ ] **Incremental Increase:** $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$ — moderate growth (KTU favourite).
- [ ] **Geometric mean growth rate:** $r_g = (P_{last}/P_{first})^{1/k} - 1$, with $k$ = number of past decadal intervals.
- [ ] **Kuichling's Fire Demand:** $F = 3182\sqrt{P}$ in L/min, with $P$ in **thousands**.
- [ ] **Conversion:** 1 MLD = $10^6$ L/day = 11.574 L/s.
- [ ] **Population forecast sanity check:** Always compare all three estimates; adopt the **moderate** one for design.
- [ ] **Saturated cities (Mumbai, Delhi):** Prefer Decreasing Rate of Growth or Logistic Curve.
- [ ] **Master plans for Kerala towns (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut):** Often use a combination of zoning + geometric method.
- [ ] **Final design flow** = Average demand × Peak factor / 86400, expressed in L/s.
- [ ] **Fire demand is added** in MLD, not as a separate flow, in total daily demand calculations.
- [ ] **Always show** all intermediate steps in numericals — KTU examiners award **step marks** generously.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Demand ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In **Environmental Engineering**, the term **"Water Demand"** is defined as the total quantity of water required by a community or a city for various purposes over a specific period, typically expressed in **litres per capita per day (lpcd)** or **million litres per day (MLD)**. The **Types of Demand** refer to the systematic classification of this total water requirement into distinct functional categories based on the end-use, consumer profile, and operational necessity. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCET503 - Environmental Engineering), the classification is broadly divided into: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"The total water demand of a town is the sum of various demands classified as domestic, industrial, institutional/commercial, public use, fire demand, and unavoidable losses/wastages."* The **Total Water Demand** can be mathematically expressed as: $$Q_{total} = Q_{domestic} + Q_{industrial} + Q_{institutional} + Q_{public} + Q_{fire} + Q_{losses}$$ Where each $Q_i$ represents the volume of water required per day (in litres or cubic metres) for the corresponding category. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a city as a **large human body**. Just as the human body needs blood for different organs (brain for thinking, muscles for movement, skin for cooling), a city needs water for different "organs" — homes, factories, hospitals, gardens, fire hydrants. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Family Budget" Model:** > Think of a family's monthly expenses. The total income (Total Demand) is split into different heads — grocery (domestic), fuel/transport (industrial), school fees (institutional), park outings (public use), and a small emergency fund (fire demand). You cannot spend all the money on groceries and ignore the school fees. Similarly, a water supply engineer must allocate water to *every* category. If you only design for domestic demand, the city will burn down during a fire because no water was reserved for the hydrants! ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants The following standard metrics are universally adopted in KTU-evaluated problems: - **Per Capita Demand (PCD):** 135 to 200 lpcd (varies as per IS 1172 guidelines for Indian cities). - **Unit Conversion Constants:** - 1 m³ = 1000 litres - 1 MLD = 10⁶ litres/day = 1000 m³/day - 1 hectare = 10⁴ m² - **Minimum Fire Flow (per IS 12314):** 50 to 100 kL/min for residential areas; 150+ kL/min for high-risk industrial zones. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always convert lpcd into MLD using the formula: $\text{MLD} = \dfrac{\text{lpcd} \times \text{Population}}{10^6}$. This conversion is tested in almost every Part B numerical. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Systematic Classification of Water Demand The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) groups water demand into **six major categories**. Each category is driven by distinct consumer behaviour, socio-economic factors, and engineering design constraints. #### **2.1.1 Domestic Demand** - **Definition:** Water used in private residences for drinking, cooking, bathing, washing clothes, flushing toilets, gardening, and pet care. - **Typical Range:** 40% to 50% of total demand. - **Key Sub-factors:** - Drinking and cooking: ~5 lpcd - Bathing and washing: ~50 lpcd - Toilet flushing: ~30 lpcd - Gardening and cleaning: ~25 lpcd #### **2.1.2 Industrial Demand** - **Definition:** Water consumed by manufacturing units, processing plants, cooling towers, boilers, and textile/chemical industries. - **Typical Range:** 20% to 25% of total demand. - **Key Drivers:** Type of industry (heavy vs light), process technology, recirculation efficiency, and effluent norms. #### **2.1.3 Institutional / Commercial Demand** - **Definition:** Water consumed by offices, schools, colleges, hospitals, hotels, restaurants, railway stations, and airports. - **Typical Range:** 10% to 15% of total demand. - **Key Drivers:** Footfall, building type, and operational hours. #### **2.1.4 Public Use Demand** - **Definition:** Water used for public infrastructure such as street washing, road sprinkling, public gardens, fountains, and public fountains/urinals. - **Typical Range:** 5% to 10% of total demand. - **Key Drivers:** Climate (hotter cities need more for dust suppression and gardening), city aesthetics, and public sanitation policy. #### **2.1.5 Fire Demand** - **Definition:** The volume of water required to extinguish fires in residential, commercial, and industrial zones. - **Typical Range:** Highly variable (designed using empirical formulae, not a fixed percentage). - **Engineering Significance:** A short-duration, **very high-intensity** demand (often 2 to 10 times the normal demand for a brief 2–4 hour window). #### **2.1.6 Losses and Wastages** - **Definition:** Unaccounted-for water (UFW) lost through leaking pipes, unauthorised connections, illegal tapping, meter inaccuracies, and reservoir overflows. - **Typical Range:** 10% to 20% of total demand in well-managed systems; can exceed 40% in older systems. - **Key Drivers:** Pipe material, age of network, maintenance quality, and governance. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Classification > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Engineering Philosophy:** > Each demand category has a unique **temporal pattern** (when the water is used) and **pressure requirement** (how much pressure is needed). For instance, domestic demand peaks in the morning (6–9 AM) and evening (6–9 PM), while fire demand is a sudden unpredictable spike. An engineer **cannot** design a single uniform reservoir for all categories — instead, separate balancing tanks, elevated service reservoirs (ESRs), and pumping stations are designed for each demand head. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Demand Type | Empirical Formula / Standard | Typical Range | Unit | |---|-------------|------------------------------|---------------|------| | 1 | Domestic | IS 1172: 135 to 200 | 40 – 50% of total | lpcd | | 2 | Industrial | 20% to 25% of total | 20 – 25% of total | % of total | | 3 | Institutional / Commercial | 10% to 15% of total | 10 – 15% of total | % of total | | 4 | Public Use | 5% to 10% of total | 5 – 10% of total | % of total | | 5 | Fire (Kuichling's Formula) | $Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$ | 50 – 100 kL/min | L/min | | 6 | Fire (Buston's Formula) | $Q_f = 5663 \sqrt{P}$ | — | L/min | | 7 | Fire (National Board, USA) | $Q_f = 4637 \sqrt{P} \cdot \left[1 - 0.01\sqrt{P}\right]$ | — | L/min | | 8 | Total Demand (Kucheria's Estimate) | $Q_{total} = 100 P^{5/4}$ | — | L/day | | 9 | Total Demand (Freeman's Estimate) | $Q_{total} = 220 P$ | — | L/day | | 10 | Conversion to MLD | $\text{MLD} = \dfrac{\text{lpcd} \times P}{10^6}$ | — | MLD | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Pipe-Free Notation:** In the formulas above, $P$ is the population in **thousands**. The square root symbol applies only to the population term. In the conversion formula, the numerator uses raw population $P$ (not in thousands) and the denominator $10^6$ converts litres to million litres. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This classification directly informs the **design of water treatment plants (WTPs), distribution network diameters, pumping main capacity, and elevated service reservoir (ESR) sizing**. For example, in Kochi (a coastal Kerala city), industrial demand is unusually high due to the presence of coir, spice processing, and IT parks — therefore the Kerala Water Authority (KWA) sizes the WTP to handle ~190 lpcd, well above the national average of 135 lpcd. A student who masters this classification can design a **city-scale water distribution system** for any Indian municipality. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Total Demand from Component Demands The total water demand of a city is the linear superposition (summation) of all six categorical demands. Let us derive this step by step. **Step 1: Define each demand component in terms of population.** Let $P$ be the design population of the city. The per capita demand (lpcd) for each category can be defined as: - $d_d$ = domestic per capita demand (lpcd) - $d_i$ = industrial per capita demand (lpcd) - $d_{in}$ = institutional per capita demand (lpcd) - $d_p$ = public use per capita demand (lpcd) - $d_f$ = fire per capita equivalent demand (lpcd) - $d_l$ = losses per capita equivalent demand (lpcd) **Step 2: Express each component's daily volume.** The total volume of water required per day for category $k$ is: $$Q_k = d_k \times P \quad \text{(litres/day)}$$ **Step 3: Sum all component demands.** $$Q_{total} = Q_d + Q_i + Q_{in} + Q_p + Q_f + Q_l$$ $$Q_{total} = (d_d + d_i + d_{in} + d_p + d_f + d_l) \times P$$ **Step 4: Express in terms of the average per capita demand $q$.** The average per capita demand $q$ (lpcd) is the weighted mean: $$q = d_d + d_i + d_{in} + d_p + d_f + d_l$$ Therefore, the total daily demand in litres is: $$Q_{total} = q \times P$$ **Step 5: Convert to MLD (Million Litres per Day).** Since $1 \text{ MLD} = 10^6 \text{ L/day}$, we divide by $10^6$: $$Q_{total, MLD} = \dfrac{q \times P}{10^6}$$ This is the master equation used in **every KTU numerical** on water supply design. ### 3.2 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sample Problem (Modelled on KTU 2024 Exam Pattern):** > *"A town has a design population of 1,50,000. The estimated per capita demands are: Domestic = 120 lpcd, Industrial = 35 lpcd, Institutional = 20 lpcd, Public use = 10 lpcd, and losses = 15 lpcd. Fire demand is calculated as 1,50,000 L/day. Calculate the total water demand in MLD and the total in litres per day."* **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1: List the given data.** $$P = 1{,}50{,}000 \quad \text{(design population)}$$ $$d_d = 120 \text{ lpcd}, \quad d_i = 35 \text{ lpcd}, \quad d_{in} = 20 \text{ lpcd}$$ $$d_p = 10 \text{ lpcd}, \quad d_l = 15 \text{ lpcd}, \quad Q_f = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$ **Step 2: Compute the population-based demand for each category.** $$Q_d = d_d \times P = 120 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 1.8 \times 10^7 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_i = 35 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 5.25 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_{in} = 20 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_p = 10 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_l = 15 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 2.25 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_f = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$ **Step 3: Sum all components.** $$Q_{total} = (1.8 \times 10^7) + (5.25 \times 10^6) + (3.0 \times 10^6) + (1.5 \times 10^6) + (2.25 \times 10^6) + (1.5 \times 10^5)$$ Let us group the terms by powers of $10^6$: $$Q_{total} = 18.0 \times 10^6 + 5.25 \times 10^6 + 3.0 \times 10^6 + 1.5 \times 10^6 + 2.25 \times 10^6 + 0.15 \times 10^6$$ $$Q_{total} = (18.0 + 5.25 + 3.0 + 1.5 + 2.25 + 0.15) \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_{total} = 30.15 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ **Step 4: Convert to MLD.** $$Q_{total, MLD} = \dfrac{30.15 \times 10^6}{10^6} = 30.15 \text{ MLD}$$ **Step 5: State the result with units.** $$\boxed{Q_{total} = 30.15 \text{ MLD} = 3.015 \times 10^7 \text{ L/day}}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Percentage Contribution Table For a typical KTU 2024 numerical, the following matrix summarises the percentage contribution of each demand category: | Demand Category | Per Capita (lpcd) | Daily Volume (L/day) | % of Total | |------------------|-------------------|----------------------|------------| | Domestic | 120 | $1.80 \times 10^7$ | 59.7% | | Industrial | 35 | $5.25 \times 10^6$ | 17.4% | | Institutional | 20 | $3.00 \times 10^6$ | 9.95% | | Public Use | 10 | $1.50 \times 10^6$ | 4.98% | | Losses | 15 | $2.25 \times 10^6$ | 7.46% | | Fire | — | $1.50 \times 10^5$ | 0.50% | | **Total** | **200 (avg)** | **$3.015 \times 10^7$** | **100%** | ### 3.4 Derivation of Kuichling's Fire Demand Formula (Background) The **Kuichling's Formula** is derived empirically based on historical fire incident data in American cities. The classical statement is: $$Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$$ Where: - $Q_f$ = fire demand in **litres per minute** - $P$ = population in **thousands** **Why $3182$?** This empirical constant arises from a least-squares regression of fire flow data collected by the **National Board of Fire Underwriters (NBFU)** in the late 1800s. The square root reflects the **non-linear** relationship between population and fire risk — doubling the population does not double the fire demand because fire hydrants can serve overlapping zones. **Quick Verification:** For $P = 100$ (thousand i.e. 1 lakh population): $$Q_f = 3182 \times \sqrt{100} = 3182 \times 10 = 31{,}820 \text{ L/min} \approx 31.82 \text{ kL/min}$$ This is well within the typical 50–100 kL/min range for residential areas, confirming the formula's validity for medium-sized cities. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Water Demand Allocation Flow The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates how raw water is logically allocated to each demand category by a municipal water supply engineer: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Water Source
River or Reservoir] --> B[Water Treatment Plant WTP] B --> C[Master Balancing Reservoir MBR] C --> D{Allocation Logic
Based on Demand Categories} D --> E[Domestic Demand
40 to 50 percent
lpcd 135 to 200] D --> F[Industrial Demand
20 to 25 percent
Factories and Plants] D --> G[Institutional Demand
10 to 15 percent
Hospitals and Schools] D --> H[Public Use Demand
5 to 10 percent
Gardens and Street Washing] D --> I[Fire Demand
Empirical Formulae
Kuichling Buston NBFU] D --> J[Losses and Wastage
10 to 20 percent
Leaks and Unauthorised Use] E --> K[Elevated Service Reservoir ESR] F --> K G --> K H --> K I --> L[Fire Hydrant Network
Direct Pumping Main] J --> M[Unaccounted For Water UFW
Reported as Non Revenue Water] K --> N[Distribution Network
Households and Consumers] L --> N ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagram shows that fire demand (I) is served via a **separate direct pumping main** (L) bypassing the ESR, because fires require immediate high-pressure supply. Domestic, industrial, institutional, and public demands (E, F, G, H) are routed through the ESR (K) for pressure stabilisation. Losses (J) are tracked separately as **Non-Revenue Water (NRW)** for audit purposes. ### 4.2 Hierarchical Demand Categorisation Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Water Demand Q total] --> B[Domestic] A --> C[Industrial] A --> D[Institutional] A --> E[Public Use] A --> F[Fire] A --> G[Losses] B --> B1[Drinking and Cooking] B --> B2[Bathing and Washing] B --> B3[Toilet Flushing] B --> B4[Gardening] C --> C1[Process Water] C --> C2[Cooling and Boiler] C --> C3[Cleaning] D --> D1[Hospitals] D --> D2[Schools and Colleges] D --> D3[Offices and Hotels] E --> E1[Street Washing] E --> E2[Public Parks] E --> E3[Public Fountains] F --> F1[Kuichling] F --> F2[Buston] F --> F3[National Board NBFU] G --> G1[Pipe Leakage] G --> G2[Illegal Tapping] G --> G3[Meter Errors] ``` ### 4.3 Temporal Demand Variation Graph (Conceptual) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Reading:** The graph below (rendered as a Mermaid qualitative plot) shows the **time-of-day variation** for different demand categories. The y-axis represents demand intensity (0 to peak), and the x-axis represents the 24-hour clock. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[00:00 to 05:00
Low Demand
Trickle Flow] --> B[06:00 to 09:00
Morning Peak
Domestic Dominant] B --> C[09:00 to 16:00
Industrial Plateau
Steady Mid Level] C --> D[18:00 to 21:00
Evening Peak
Domestic Dominant] D --> E[21:00 to 24:00
Decline
Trickle to Off Peak] E --> A ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that domestic demand creates **two peaks** (morning and evening), while industrial demand creates a **broad plateau** (9 AM to 6 PM). Fire demand is **stochastic** (random spikes) and is therefore designed as a *contingency overlay* on top of the peak demand, not a base load. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** **Q1. List and briefly explain any three types of water demand in a municipal water supply system.** **Model Answer:** The three major types of water demand in a municipal water supply system are: 1. **Domestic Demand:** Water used in private households for drinking, cooking, bathing, washing clothes, toilet flushing, and gardening. It forms the largest single category, typically 40% to 50% of total demand. 2. **Industrial Demand:** Water used in factories, manufacturing plants, and processing units for cooling, boiling, and product manufacture. It accounts for 20% to 25% of total demand and depends on the type of industry. 3. **Public Use Demand:** Water used for municipal services such as street washing, road sprinkling, public parks, and public fountains. It accounts for 5% to 10% of total demand. > **[3 Marks — 1 for listing, 2 for brief explanation with percentage ranges]** > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Q2. What is fire demand? State Kuichling's formula for estimating fire demand.** **Model Answer:** **Fire demand** is the quantity of water required to extinguish fires in residential, commercial, and industrial areas of a city. It is a short-duration, high-intensity demand used to size fire hydrants and storage. **Kuichling's Formula:** $$Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$$ where $Q_f$ is the fire demand in **litres per minute** and $P$ is the population in **thousands**. > **[3 Marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for units and notation]** --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** ### **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a)** Classify the various types of water demands in a town. Explain in detail the factors affecting per capita demand. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Classification of Water Demands (4 Marks):** The water demands of a town are classified into the following categories: 1. **Domestic Demand** — Water used in private residences for drinking, cooking, bathing, washing, toilet flushing, and gardening. Forms 40% to 50% of total demand. 2. **Industrial Demand** — Water consumed by manufacturing units, cooling towers, boilers, and processing plants. Forms 20% to 25% of total demand. 3. **Institutional / Commercial Demand** — Water used by offices, schools, hospitals, hotels, and airports. Forms 10% to 15% of total demand. 4. **Public Use Demand** — Water used for street washing, road sprinkling, public parks, and public fountains. Forms 5% to 10% of total demand. 5. **Fire Demand** — Water required for extinguishing fires, estimated using empirical formulae such as Kuichling's, Buston's, or National Board formula. 6. **Losses and Wastages** — Unaccounted-for water (UFW) lost through pipe leakage, illegal tapping, and meter inaccuracies. Forms 10% to 20% of total demand. **Factors Affecting Per Capita Demand (3 Marks):** 1. **Size of the city:** Larger cities have higher per capita demand due to higher living standards and greater industrial activity. 2. **Climate:** Hotter and drier climates increase per capita demand (more bathing, gardening, and cooling). 3. **Standard of living:** Affluent communities consume more water per capita. 4. **Cost of water:** Lower water tariffs encourage higher consumption; metered systems reduce waste. 5. **Industrial activity:** Cities with heavy industries have higher industrial demand. 6. **Quality of water supply:** Poor quality reduces consumer trust and effective usage. 7. **Pressure in the distribution system:** Higher pressure increases wastage at fixtures. **(b)** The population of a city is 5,00,000. The various demands are: Domestic = 140 lpcd, Industrial = 30 lpcd, Institutional = 15 lpcd, Public use = 10 lpcd, and Losses = 15 lpcd. Fire demand is estimated as 2,00,000 L/day. Calculate the total water demand in MLD. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify the data.** - Population $P = 5{,}00{,}000$ - $d_d = 140$ lpcd, $d_i = 30$ lpcd, $d_{in} = 15$ lpcd, $d_p = 10$ lpcd, $d_l = 15$ lpcd - $Q_f = 2{,}00{,}000$ L/day **Step 2: Compute population-based demand for each category.** $$Q_d = 140 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 7.0 \times 10^7 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_i = 30 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_{in} = 15 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 7.5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_p = 10 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 5.0 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ $$Q_l = 15 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 7.5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ **Step 3: Add all components.** $$Q_{total} = 7.0 \times 10^7 + 1.5 \times 10^7 + 7.5 \times 10^6 + 5.0 \times 10^6 + 7.5 \times 10^6 + 2.0 \times 10^5$$ $$Q_{total} = 70.0 \times 10^6 + 15.0 \times 10^6 + 7.5 \times 10^6 + 5.0 \times 10^6 + 7.5 \times 10^6 + 0.2 \times 10^6$$ $$Q_{total} = (70.0 + 15.0 + 7.5 + 5.0 + 7.5 + 0.2) \times 10^6 = 105.2 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ **Step 4: Convert to MLD.** $$Q_{total, MLD} = \dfrac{105.2 \times 10^6}{10^6} = 105.2 \text{ MLD}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{total} = 105.2 \text{ MLD}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Explain the various empirical formulae used for the estimation of fire demand in a city. Compare Kuichling's, Buston's, and the National Board formulae. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** Fire demand estimation uses empirical formulae derived from historical fire incident statistics. The three most commonly used formulae are: **1. Kuichling's Formula (2 Marks):** $$Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$$ where $Q_f$ is in L/min and $P$ is population in thousands. This formula is based on data from American cities of the 19th century and is suitable for medium-sized cities. **2. Buston's Formula (2 Marks):** $$Q_f = 5663 \sqrt{P}$$ This formula gives higher fire demand values than Kuichling's and is recommended for densely populated commercial zones. Buston derived it considering that fire risk grows faster than the square root of population in congested areas. **3. National Board of Fire Underwriters (NBFU), USA (2 Marks):** $$Q_f = 4637 \sqrt{P} \cdot \left[1 - 0.01\sqrt{P}\right]$$ This formula includes a correction factor that reduces the fire demand for very large populations, accounting for the fact that extremely large cities have distributed fire stations and faster response times. **Comparison Table (Bonus, for full marks):** | Criterion | Kuichling | Buston | NBFU | |-----------|-----------|--------|------| | Conservatism | Moderate | Highly conservative | Adaptive | | Best for | Medium cities | Congested zones | Large cities | | Empirical basis | 19th century USA | USA | USA | **(b)** Estimate the fire demand for a town of population 2,00,000 using all three formulae. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Convert population to thousands.** $$P = 2{,}00{,}000 = 200 \text{ (in thousands)}$$ **Step 2: Apply Kuichling's Formula.** $$Q_{f1} = 3182 \times \sqrt{200} = 3182 \times 14.142 = 45{,}000 \text{ L/min (approx.)}$$ **Step 3: Apply Buston's Formula.** $$Q_{f2} = 5663 \times \sqrt{200} = 5663 \times 14.142 = 80{,}082 \text{ L/min (approx.)}$$ **Step 4: Apply NBFU Formula.** $$Q_{f3} = 4637 \times \sqrt{200} \times \left[1 - 0.01 \times \sqrt{200}\right]$$ $$= 4637 \times 14.142 \times \left[1 - 0.1414\right]$$ $$= 65{,}576 \times 0.8586$$ $$= 56{,}303 \text{ L/min (approx.)}$$ **Step 5: Tabulate the results.** | Formula | Fire Demand (L/min) | Fire Demand (kL/min) | |---------|----------------------|----------------------| | Kuichling | 45,000 | 45.0 | | Buston | 80,082 | 80.1 | | NBFU | 56,303 | 56.3 | > **[7 Marks — 1 each for the three calculations, 1 for final tabulation, 2 for correct application of square root and units]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to convert population to **thousands** before applying fire demand formulae. For $P = 2{,}00{,}000$, you must use $P = 200$ (in thousands). Using raw population $P = 2{,}00{,}000$ gives a wildly incorrect answer. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Unit mismatch — Kuichling's formula gives $Q_f$ in **L/min**, not L/day or L/s. To convert to kL/min, divide by 1000. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to add the **fire demand as a separate non-population-based term** in the total demand calculation (Step 3 in the worked example). Fire demand is typically given in absolute L/day, not as lpcd. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **Per Capita Demand (PCD)** with **Total Demand**. PCD is in lpcd, while Total Demand is in MLD. Always show the conversion step explicitly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - **Total Water Demand** = Domestic + Industrial + Institutional + Public Use + Fire + Losses. - **Domestic demand** is the largest category (40% to 50%); governed by IS 1172 standards (135 to 200 lpcd for Indian cities). - **Industrial demand** (20% to 25%) depends on the type of industry — heavy industries consume far more than IT parks. - **Institutional / Commercial demand** (10% to 15%) is driven by schools, hospitals, offices, and hotels. - **Public use demand** (5% to 10%) is for street washing, parks, and public fountains. - **Fire demand** is calculated using **empirical formulae** (Kuichling's, Buston's, NBFU) — not as a fixed percentage. - **Kuichling's formula:** $Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$ (L/min, $P$ in thousands). - **Buston's formula:** $Q_f = 5663 \sqrt{P}$ (more conservative, for congested zones). - **NBFU formula:** $Q_f = 4637 \sqrt{P} \cdot \left[1 - 0.01\sqrt{P}\right]$ (self-correcting for very large populations). - **Losses** (10% to 20%) are called **Unaccounted-for Water (UFW)** or **Non-Revenue Water (NRW)**. - **Master Conversion Formula:** $\text{MLD} = \dfrac{\text{lpcd} \times P}{10^6}$ — this is the most-asked conversion in KTU exams. - **Fire demand** is a short-duration, high-intensity demand — design with a contingency overlay on top of the peak demand. - **ESR (Elevated Service Reservoir)** is used for domestic, industrial, institutional, and public demands — **not** for fire (fire is served by direct pumping mains). - **7 Key Factors Affecting Per Capita Demand:** population size, climate, standard of living, water cost, industrial activity, water quality, and distribution pressure. - **Always check units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units. ---

topics.title: Demand Fluctuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Demand Fluctuation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Demand Fluctuation** refers to the variation in the rate of water consumption by a community over different time intervals — hourly, daily, seasonally, and annually. In KTU environmental engineering parlance, the rate of water demand is **not constant**; it oscillates around an average value, and these oscillations must be quantified to size storage reservoirs, pumps, and distribution mains correctly.

The water demand of a town is commonly expressed in three equivalent units:

- **Volume per unit time** (e.g., **million litres per day, MLD** or **litres per second, LPS**)
- **Per capita demand** (e.g., **lpcd** – litres per capita per day)
- **Equivalent per capita demand** — which folds in institutional, commercial, industrial, and unaccounted-for water into a single "effective" per-head figure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The unit emphasizes that the design of every component — from the intake well to the elevated service reservoir (ESR) — depends not on the *average* demand, but on the **maximum instantaneous / coincident draft**.

## 1.2 Types of Fluctuations in Water Demand

Water demand varies over multiple time scales. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following classes of fluctuations:

| # | Type of Variation | Time Scale | Engineering Importance |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Hourly variation** | Hours within a single day | Sizes service reservoirs, pumps |
| 2 | **Daily variation** | Day-to-day in a week | Affects treatment plant operation |
| 3 | **Seasonal variation** | Summer / Winter / Monsoon | Decides pumping schedule & source yield |
| 4 | **Annual / Yearly variation** | Long-term trends | Population forecasting, capacity expansion |
| 5 | **Fire demand** | Emergency (rare, intense) | Determines storage volume & main diameter |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of demand fluctuation as the **"breathing pattern" of a city**:

- During a **hot summer afternoon**, the city "inhales" a huge gulp of water — gardens, showers, air-coolers, swimming pools all switch on simultaneously. This is the **peak demand**.
- During a **cool monsoon midnight**, the city barely "breathes" — almost every tap is closed. This is the **minimum demand**.

> A water supply system is like the **lungs** of this city: the treatment plant, pumps, and pipes are the breathing apparatus. If you size the system for *average* breathing, it will **choke during peaks** (people will get trickles from their taps) and **waste energy** during valleys.

The **elevated service reservoir (ESR)** acts like the city's **diaphragm** — it *stores water* when demand is low and *releases* it when demand peaks, smoothing out the breathing cycle.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Convention)

- **Average Daily Demand (ADD):** Total water supplied in a year ÷ 365.
- **Maximum Daily Demand (MDD):** Highest demand on any single day of the year.
- **Peak Hourly Demand (PHD):** Maximum demand in any single hour of the year.
- **Fire Demand:** Sudden, large demand during a fire emergency.
- **Coincident Draft:** The draft on the treatment plant / pumping main when the **peak hourly demand** coincides with the **maximum daily demand** — the worst-case load the system must handle (except fire).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Co-incident draft** is the single most important design load in KTU problems. It is the demand placed on the treatment plant when the *worst day* meets the *worst hour* — and it is what sizes the pumps, the clear-water reservoir, and the transmission main.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Causing Demand Fluctuation

The KTU 2024 module lists the following primary causes of variation in water demand. They are best understood in three groups:

### 2.1.1 Climatic & Geographic Factors
- **Climate:** Hot, dry climates raise per capita demand (showers, cooling, irrigation).
- **Temperature & humidity:** Even within the same season, a +5 °C swing alters domestic use.
- **Rainfall:** A good monsoon lowers lawn-watering and bathing frequency.

### 2.1.2 Socio-Economic & Demographic Factors
- **Standard of living:** Higher income → more fixtures → higher per capita demand.
- **Population:** Pure scaling; demand is roughly proportional to population.
- **Habits & culture:** Religious festivals, cricket matches, weddings can cause *spikes*.
- **Cost of water:** Metered supply discourages waste; flat-rate supply inflates demand.

### 2.1.3 Engineering & Operational Factors
- **Quality of supply:** Reliable, clean supply reduces storage at the consumer's end.
- **Pressure in mains:** Low pressure → consumer stores more → effective demand lowers.
- **System leaks / UFW (Unaccounted-For Water):** Often the single largest contributor to fluctuation in Indian towns.
- **Industrial pattern:** A city with a large factory has a *different* demand profile than a residential town — flatter during the day, but huge during shift changes.

## 2.2 Operational Steps — How Fluctuation is Quantified

The following logical sequence is exactly what KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark answer:

1. **Estimate population** at the design year (using arithmetic / geometric / incremental increase methods).
2. **Compute average daily demand (ADD)** = Population × Per Capita Demand (in lpcd).
3. **Identify peak factors** (ratios of peak to average) for hourly, daily, and seasonal variations.
4. **Apply peak factors** to ADD to obtain MDD, PHD, and seasonal maxima.
5. **Add fire demand** to the coincident draft when sizing storage and trunk mains.
6. **Deduct fire demand** from coincident draft when treating it as non-coincident for design of consumer-end pipes.
7. **Sum up hourly demand** to confirm that total volume over 24 h equals ADD × 24.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Tip:** The phrase "design the capacity of the pumping main" almost always means "size it for **coincident draft + fire demand**", because the question setter wants the *worst-case combined load*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarises every formula required to solve Module-1 problems on Demand Fluctuation. Note the consistent use of $\vert$ and $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax with vertical bars.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value (KTU / CPHEEO) | Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Average Daily Demand (ADD) | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{P \times q}{1000}$ | $q = 135$ (lpcd, class-I city) | MLD |
| 2 | Maximum Daily Demand | $Q_{max,day} = 1.5 \times Q_{avg}$ | Factor $\approx 1.5$ | MLD |
| 3 | Maximum Hourly Demand | $Q_{max,hr} = 1.5 \times Q_{max,day}$ | Factor $\approx 1.5$ | MLD |
| 4 | Peak Hourly Demand (PHD) | $Q_{peak,hr} = 2.25 \times Q_{avg}$ | Combined factor $\approx 2.25$ | MLD |
| 5 | Coincident Draft | $Q_{coincident} = \dfrac{2.25 \times P \times q}{1000}$ | For treatment plant design | MLD |
| 6 | Fire demand (Kuchling's formula) | $Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$ | $P$ in thousands, $Q_f$ in L/min | L/min |
| 7 | Fire demand (Buston's formula) | $Q_f = 5663 \sqrt{P}$ | $P$ in thousands, $Q_f$ in L/min | L/min |
| 8 | Fire demand (Ministry of Works) | $Q_f = \left( 100 \text{ to } 1000 \right) \sqrt{P} \times 60$ | Per minute basis | L/min |
| 9 | Maximum Daily Demand in LPS | $Q_{LPS} = \dfrac{1.5 \times P \times q}{86400}$ | For pipe flow | LPS |
| 10 | Peak Factor (Hourly) | $k_h = \dfrac{Q_{peak,hr}}{Q_{avg}}$ | $1.5$ to $3.0$ (KTU range) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPHEEO 1999 (Manual on Water Supply & Treatment) Convention** — used by KTU as the de-facto standard: per capita demand for **Class-I cities** (population $\geq 100{,}000$) is **135 lpcd** at the consumer's tap, but rises to **220 lpcd** at the *source* once unaccounted-for water (UFW, 15–25 %) is added.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Pipe sizing:** A pipe designed for the *average* will have excessive head loss at peak hour, leaving upper floors dry.
- **Pump selection:** A pump rated at *average* discharge will be overloaded at peak hour, leading to cavitation and motor burnouts.
- **Reservoir sizing:** The balancing reservoir (ESR) volume is determined precisely by the *hourly* fluctuation curve.
- **Energy cost:** Off-peak pumping during low-demand night hours saves substantial electricity tariffs — a direct application of demand-curve knowledge.
- **Fire safety:** Ignoring fire demand can make the entire network legally non-compliant under the National Building Code of India.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **mathematical derivations** of the most commonly-tested KTU formulas. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly; no step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Factor and Coincident Draft

Let:
- $P$ = design population (number of persons)
- $q$ = per capita demand (lpcd = litres per capita per day)
- $k_d$ = ratio of max-day demand to average-day demand $\left( \dfrac{Q_{max,day}}{Q_{avg}} \right)$
- $k_h$ = ratio of max-hourly demand to average-hourly demand on the max-day

**Step 1 — Express the average daily demand (ADD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= \frac{\text{Total water used per day}}{1} \\
Q_{avg} &= \frac{P \times q}{10^6} \;\; \text{[in million litres per day, MLD]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $10^6$ converts litres to million litres. The KTU board expects this conversion in the **first line** of every numerical answer.

**Step 2 — Express the maximum daily demand (MDD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max,day} &= k_d \times Q_{avg} \\
Q_{max,day} &= k_d \times \frac{P \times q}{10^6} \;\; \text{[in MLD]}
\end{aligned}
$$

For most KTU problems, the **default** value is $k_d = 1.5$, unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.

**Step 3 — Express the maximum hourly demand on a normal day:**

The average hourly demand on an average day is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg,hr} &= \frac{Q_{avg} \times 10^6}{24} \;\; \text{[in litres per hour]} \\
Q_{avg,hr} &= \frac{P \times q}{24} \;\; \text{[in litres per hour]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum hourly demand on the maximum day is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max,hr} &= k_h \times Q_{avg,hr} \\
Q_{max,hr} &= k_h \times \frac{P \times q}{24}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the coincident draft (worst hour of worst day):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{coincident} &= k_d \times k_h \times \frac{P \times q}{10^6} \;\; \text{[in MLD]} \\
Q_{coincident} &= 1.5 \times 1.5 \times \frac{P \times q}{10^6} \\
Q_{coincident} &= 2.25 \times \frac{P \times q}{10^6}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **2.25** is the *combined peak factor* for the KTU default. Memorise this — it is the single most-frequently-quoted number in Module 1.

## 3.2 Derivation: Conversion Between MLD and LPS (Litres Per Second)

Many KTU numerical problems mix MLD and LPS, so the conversion must be second-nature.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of MLD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ MLD} &= 1 \times 10^6 \text{ litres per day} \\
1 \text{ MLD} &= \frac{10^6}{24 \times 60 \times 60} \text{ litres per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
24 \times 60 \times 60 &= 86{,}400 \text{ seconds per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final conversion factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ MLD} &= \frac{10^6}{86{,}400} \text{ LPS} \\
1 \text{ MLD} &\approx 11.574 \text{ LPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Inverse conversion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ LPS} &= \frac{86{,}400}{10^6} \text{ MLD} \\
1 \text{ LPS} &= 0.0864 \text{ MLD}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Kuchling's Fire Demand Formula

The empirical basis of Kuchling's formula is that fire demand scales with the **square root** of population, not linearly. The reasoning: a larger town has bigger buildings and a denser fire-load, but the *concurrent* number of fires is statistically smaller per unit area.

**Step 1 — Empirical (Kuchling) relation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_f = K \sqrt{P_{\text{thousands}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K = 3182$ L/min and $P_{\text{thousands}} = P/1000$ when $P$ is expressed in **thousands**.

**Step 2 — Substitute a typical $P$:**

For a town with $P = 50{,}000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{thousands}} &= \frac{50{,}000}{1{,}000} = 50 \\
Q_f &= 3182 \times \sqrt{50} \\
Q_f &= 3182 \times 7.0711 \\
Q_f &\approx 22{,}500 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to LPS for direct use in pipe-flow problems:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_f \text{ (LPS)} &= \frac{22{,}500}{60} \\
Q_f &\approx 375 \text{ LPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

> **Problem.** A KTU-supplied town has a present population of $80{,}000$, expected to grow to $120{,}000$ in 20 years. Per capita demand at the consumer is **135 lpcd**. UFW = **15 %**. Find:
> (a) Average daily demand at the source in MLD.
> (b) Maximum daily demand in MLD.
> (c) Peak hourly demand in MLD.
> (d) Coincident draft in MLD.

**Solution:**

Given: $P = 120{,}000$, $q = 135$ lpcd, $k_d = 1.5$, $k_h = 1.5$, UFW = 15 %.

**Step 1 — Gross per capita demand at source:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{gross}} &= \frac{q}{1 - \text{UFW}} \\
q_{\text{gross}} &= \frac{135}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{135}{0.85} \\
q_{\text{gross}} &\approx 158.82 \text{ lpcd}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average daily demand (ADD) at source:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= \frac{P \times q_{\text{gross}}}{10^6} \\
Q_{avg} &= \frac{120{,}000 \times 158.82}{10^6} \\
Q_{avg} &= \frac{19{,}058{,}400}{10^6} \\
Q_{avg} &\approx 19.06 \text{ MLD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum daily demand (MDD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max,day} &= k_d \times Q_{avg} \\
Q_{max,day} &= 1.5 \times 19.06 \\
Q_{max,day} &\approx 28.59 \text{ MLD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Peak hourly demand (PHD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max,hr} &= k_d \times k_h \times Q_{avg} \\
Q_{max,hr} &= 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 19.06 \\
Q_{max,hr} &\approx 42.88 \text{ MLD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Coincident draft:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{coincident} &= Q_{max,hr} = 42.88 \text{ MLD} \\
Q_{coincident} \text{ (in LPS)} &= 42.88 \times 11.574 \\
Q_{coincident} &\approx 496.30 \text{ LPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-check trick:** 1 MLD $\approx$ **11.574 LPS**. The number 11.574 is the most-memorised constant in any KTU Environmental Engineering paper.
