# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Water Demand Hierarchy

The diagram below maps the entire demand-fluctuation hierarchy. Each block shows how the lower-stage demand is mathematically transformed into the higher-stage load used for component sizing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef baseBox fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E,stroke-width:2px
    classDef derivedBox fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D,stroke-width:2px
    classDef peakBox fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F,stroke-width:2px
    classDef designBox fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:2px

    basePop["Population P at design year"]:::baseBox
    basePCD["Per Capita Demand q in lpcd"]:::baseBox
    baseUFW["Unaccounted For Water UFW in percent"]:::baseBox

    derivedGross["Gross q = q divided by one minus UFW"]:::derivedBox
    derivedADD["Average Daily Demand in MLD"]:::derivedBox
    derivedMLDADD["ADD in LPS equals ADD times 11.574"]:::derivedBox

    peakMDD["MDD equals 1.5 times ADD"]:::peakBox
    peakPHD["PHD equals 1.5 times MDD equals 2.25 times ADD"]:::peakBox
    peakFire["Fire Demand Qf from Kuchling or Buston formula"]:::peakBox

    designSource["Source capacity in MLD equals ADD plus losses"]:::designBox
    designTP["Treatment Plant sized for Coincident Draft"]:::designBox
    designESR["ESR volume from hourly demand mass curve"]:::designBox
    designMain["Pumping main sized for Coincident Draft plus Fire"]:::designBox

    basePop --> derivedADD
    basePCD --> derivedGross
    baseUFW --> derivedGross
    derivedGross --> derivedADD
    derivedADD --> derivedMLDADD
    derivedADD --> peakMDD
    peakMDD --> peakPHD
    derivedADD --> designSource
    peakPHD --> designTP
    peakFire --> designMain
    peakPHD --> designMain
    peakPHD --> designESR
```

## 4.2 Hourly Variation Mass-Curve (Typical Indian City)

The following diagram shows the *classic 24-hour demand profile* for a residential Indian town. The **X-axis** is the hour of the day, the **Y-axis** is the demand expressed as a *multiple of the average hourly rate* (i.e., the hourly peak factor). This curve is exactly the kind of mass-curve that KTU examiners ask students to draw when sizing the ESR.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef morning fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#B45309,color:#7C2D12,stroke-width:1px
    classDef afternoon fill:#FFF7CC,stroke:#A16207,color:#713F12,stroke-width:1px
    classDef evening fill:#FECACA,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:2px
    classDef night fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:1px
    classDef flat fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151,color:#111827,stroke-width:1px

    n0["Hour 0\n0.30 x avg"]:::night
    n3["Hour 3\n0.25 x avg"]:::night
    n6["Hour 6\n1.30 x avg\nmorning peak"]:::morning
    n8["Hour 8\n1.80 x avg\nhighest"]:::morning
    n10["Hour 10\n1.20 x avg"]:::flat
    n12["Hour 12\n1.40 x avg\nlunch"]:::afternoon
    n14["Hour 14\n1.10 x avg"]:::afternoon
    n17["Hour 17\n1.50 x avg\nevening rise"]:::evening
    n19["Hour 19\n1.70 x avg\nsecond peak"]:::evening
    n22["Hour 22\n0.50 x avg"]:::night

    n0 --> n3 --> n6 --> n8 --> n10 --> n12 --> n14 --> n17 --> n19 --> n22
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the curve:** The total *area* under the demand curve equals 24 × (average hourly rate). The balancing reservoir fills in the **valleys** (00:00 – 05:00) and empties in the **peaks** (07:00 – 09:00 and 18:00 – 20:00). The maximum drawdown defines the **required ESR volume**.

## 4.3 Schematic of the Demand–Supply Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef sourceBox fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,color:#064E3B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef treatBox fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#1E1B4B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef storeBox fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F,stroke-width:2px
    classDef distBox fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843,stroke-width:2px
    classDef demandBox fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151,color:#111827,stroke-width:1px

    intake["Source\nRiver or Reservoir"]:::sourceBox
    treat["Treatment Plant\nSized for Coincident Draft"]:::treatBox
    sump["Clear Water Sump\nStores treated water"]:::storeBox
    pump["Pumping Main\nPumps 24 x 7 at constant rate"]:::distBox
    esr["Elevated Service Reservoir\nBalances hourly fluctuation"]:::storeBox
    dist["Distribution Network\nSized for Peak Hourly Demand"]:::distBox
    consumer["Consumer Taps\nVariable demand through day"]:::demandBox
    fire["Fire Hydrant\nSudden fire demand"]:::demandBox

    intake --> treat
    treat --> sump
    sump --> pump
    pump --> esr
    esr --> dist
    dist --> consumer
    dist -.-> fire
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **treatment plant + pumping main** work at an almost *constant rate* of 24 h operation at the average daily demand, but the **ESR + distribution network** handle the *fluctuation*. This two-tier design philosophy is the central reason engineers must master demand-fluctuation mathematics.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define **coincident draft**. Why is it used for sizing the treatment plant instead of the average daily demand?

**Model Answer:**

Coincident draft is the rate of water demand that occurs when the **maximum daily demand** and the **peak hourly demand** happen at the same time. Mathematically,

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{coincident} &= k_d \times k_h \times Q_{avg} \\
Q_{coincident} &= 1.5 \times 1.5 \times Q_{avg} = 2.25 \times Q_{avg}
\end{aligned}
$$

It is used for sizing the treatment plant because the plant must be capable of meeting the **worst combined load** placed by the community. If the plant were sized for the *average* daily demand, it would be overwhelmed on the worst day during the worst hour, leading to **incomplete treatment** and **health hazards**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> List any **six factors** that cause fluctuation in water demand.

**Model Answer:**

The six factors are:
1. **Climate and temperature** of the region.
2. **Standard of living** and per-capita income.
3. **Habits, customs and festivals** of the community.
4. **Cost of water** (metered vs. flat-rate supply).
5. **Industrial and commercial activity** profile.
6. **Unaccounted-for water (UFW)** through leaks and theft.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Question (14 Marks, Module-1 Internal Choice)

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> (a) Explain the various types of fluctuations in water demand and the factors affecting them. *(7 marks)*
> (b) A town has a present population of $50{,}000$ with an average per capita demand of $135$ lpcd. The population is expected to grow at a **geometric** rate of $2.5\%$ per annum. Compute the **average daily demand**, **maximum daily demand**, and **peak hourly demand** at the **end of 30 years**. Take $k_d = 1.5$ and $k_h = 1.5$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)**

The water demand of a community is **never constant**. It fluctuates over multiple time scales:

1. **Hourly variation** — Within a single day, demand is low at night, peaks in the morning (07:00 – 09:00) and again in the evening (18:00 – 20:00).
2. **Daily variation** — Demand is lower on Sundays and holidays; higher on working days.
3. **Seasonal variation** — Demand is highest in **summer**, lowest in the **monsoon**.
4. **Annual variation** — Long-term changes due to population growth, urbanisation, and rising standard of living.
5. **Fire demand** — Sudden, large, occasional demand during a fire.

**Factors affecting fluctuation** (any six to be explained):
- Climate (temperature, rainfall)
- Standard of living
- Cost of water (metered / unmetered)
- Population size and density
- Industrial / commercial profile
- Public habits, customs, and festivals
- Quality of supply and system pressure
- Unaccounted-for water (leakage)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

Given: $P_0 = 50{,}000$, $r = 0.025$, $n = 30$ years, $q = 135$ lpcd, $k_d = 1.5$, $k_h = 1.5$.

**Step 1 — Population forecast using geometric increase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^n \\
P_n &= 50{,}000 \times (1.025)^{30}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(1.025)^{30}$ step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.025)^2 &= 1.050625 \\
(1.025)^4 &= (1.050625)^2 = 1.103812890 \\
(1.025)^8 &= (1.103812890)^2 = 1.218402906 \\
(1.025)^{16} &= (1.218402906)^2 = 1.484505617 \\
(1.025)^{30} &= (1.025)^{16} \times (1.025)^{8} \times (1.025)^{4} \times (1.025)^{2} \\
&= 1.484505617 \times 1.218402906 \times 1.103812890 \times 1.050625
\end{aligned}
$$

Continuing the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.025)^{30} &\approx 2.0976
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Final design population:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= 50{,}000 \times 2.0976 \\
P_n &\approx 104{,}880 \;\text{(round to 105,000)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average daily demand (ADD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= \frac{P_n \times q}{10^6} \\
Q_{avg} &= \frac{105{,}000 \times 135}{10^6} \\
Q_{avg} &= \frac{14{,}175{,}000}{10^6} \\
Q_{avg} &\approx 14.18 \text{ MLD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum daily demand (MDD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max,day} &= 1.5 \times 14.18 \\
Q_{max,day} &\approx 21.26 \text{ MLD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Peak hourly demand (PHD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max,hr} &= 1.5 \times 21.26 \\
Q_{max,hr} &\approx 31.89 \text{ MLD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $P_{30} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{5}$ persons
- $Q_{avg} \approx 14.18$ MLD
- $Q_{max,day} \approx 21.26$ MLD
- $Q_{max,hr} \approx 31.89$ MLD

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2022]**
> (a) Differentiate between **average daily demand**, **maximum daily demand**, and **peak hourly demand**. Explain the concept of **fire demand** and the **Kuchling formula**. *(7 marks)*
> (b) For a residential colony of $25{,}000$ persons, compute the fire demand using **Kuchling's formula**. Also, determine the **size of the elevated service reservoir (ESR)** required to balance the hourly fluctuation if the daily demand is $3.0$ MLD and the demand factor pattern (multiples of average hourly demand) is as follows: 06:00–09:00 → **1.5**, 09:00–18:00 → **1.0**, 18:00–21:00 → **1.7**, 21:00–06:00 → **0.5**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)**

| # | Parameter | Definition | Used for |
|---|-----------|------------|----------|
| 1 | **ADD** | Total water in a year ÷ 365 | Source & pumping main |
| 2 | **MDD** | Highest demand on any single day (1.5 × ADD) | Treatment plant capacity |
| 3 | **PHD** | Highest demand in any single hour (1.5 × MDD) | Distribution mains & ESR |
| 4 | **Fire demand** | Sudden demand during a fire | Trunk mains, storage volume |

**Kuchling's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Q_f$ is in **L/min** and $P$ is the **population in thousands**. The formula is empirical and based on the square-root relationship between population and fire-load.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Fire demand by Kuchling:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 25{,}000 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; P_{\text{thousands}} = 25 \\
Q_f &= 3182 \times \sqrt{25} \\
Q_f &= 3182 \times 5 \\
Q_f &= 15{,}910 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to LPS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_f \text{ (LPS)} &= \frac{15{,}910}{60} \\
Q_f &\approx 265.17 \text{ LPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average hourly demand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg,hr} &= \frac{Q_{avg} \times 10^6}{24} \\
Q_{avg,hr} &= \frac{3.0 \times 10^6}{24} \\
Q_{avg,hr} &= 125{,}000 \text{ L/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Hourly demand in each block (multiples of average):**

| Time block | Duration (h) | Factor | Demand (L/hr) | Volume (L) |
|------------|--------------|--------|---------------|------------|
| 06:00 – 09:00 | 3 | 1.5 | $187{,}500$ | $562{,}500$ |
| 09:00 – 18:00 | 9 | 1.0 | $125{,}000$ | $1{,}125{,}000$ |
| 18:00 – 21:00 | 3 | 1.7 | $212{,}500$ | $637{,}500$ |
| 21:00 – 06:00 | 9 | 0.5 | $62{,}500$ | $562{,}500$ |
| **Total** | **24** | — | — | $2{,}887{,}500$ |

(Note: 3.0 MLD × 1 day = 3,000,000 L; the small discrepancy is rounding in the demand factors — this is acceptable in KTU board solutions.)

**Step 4 — Cumulative supply vs cumulative demand (mass curve):**

Assume a constant supply rate of $125{,}000$ L/hr (i.e., the average hourly rate).

| Time (h) | Cumulative supply (L) | Cumulative demand (L) | Deficit $(+)$ / Surplus $(-)$ |
|----------|-----------------------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 0 (06:00) | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 (09:00) | $375{,}000$ | $562{,}500$ | $+187{,}500$ |
| 12 (18:00) | $1{,}500{,}000$ | $1{,}687{,}500$ | $+187{,}500$ |
| 15 (21:00) | $1{,}875{,}000$ | $2{,}325{,}000$ | $+450{,}000$ |
| 24 (06:00 next day) | $3{,}000{,}000$ | $2{,}887{,}500$ | $-112{,}500$ |

**Step 5 — Required ESR capacity:**

The maximum deficit occurs at the **end of the 18:00–21:00 block** = **$450{,}000$ L** = **$0.45$ ML** = **$450 \text{ m}^3$**.

**Final Answers:**
- Fire demand $\approx 15{,}910$ L/min $\approx 265.17$ LPS
- Required ESR volume $\approx 450 \text{ m}^3$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting UFW.** If the problem says per-capita demand is 135 lpcd and UFW is 15%, students use 135 *directly* and lose the full 1–2 mark "gross demand" step.
> 2. **Using the wrong peak factor.** MDD = 1.5 × ADD, but students sometimes use 2.25 × ADD for MDD (that's actually PHD).
> 3. **Converting MLD to LPS incorrectly.** Memorise 1 MLD = 11.574 LPS, not 10 or 12.
> 4. **Forgetting units in fire-demand formulas.** Kuchling's $Q_f$ is in **L/min**, not LPS; divide by 60 to get LPS.
> 5. **Not drawing the mass curve.** The mass-curve (cumulative demand vs. time) is mandatory for the ESR-volume problem; without it, even a numerically-correct answer gets **partial credit only**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for "Demand Fluctuation":**

- **Three canonical demands:** ADD (design source & pumps), MDD = 1.5 × ADD (design treatment plant), PHD = 2.25 × ADD (design distribution network).
- **Coincident draft** = **2.25 × ADD** (default KTU combined factor); always use this for treatment plant and pumping-main design.
- **Fire demand** is **separate** and **additive** to coincident draft; it is **not** multiplied by peak factors.
- **Conversion constants:** 1 MLD = 11.574 LPS; 1 LPS = 0.0864 MLD; 1 m³ = 1000 L.
- **Kuchling's $Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$** uses $P$ in **thousands** and returns $Q_f$ in **L/min** — never forget the /60 to get LPS.
- **Buston's $Q_f = 5663 \sqrt{P}$** is the alternative formula; Buston gives a more conservative (larger) fire demand.
- **Ministry of Works** uses $Q_f = (100 \text{ to } 1000) \sqrt{P} \times 60$ L/min — choose 1000 for very congested old-city areas.
- **UFW** of 15 – 25 % must be added at the **source**, not at the consumer; gross $q = q_{\text{net}} / (1 - \text{UFW})$.
- **CPHEEO 1999** per capita demand — Class-I city: **135 lpcd (net)**, **220 lpcd (gross, with UFW)**; Class-II: 100 lpcd; rural: 40 lpcd.
- **ESR volume** is determined by the *hourly* demand mass curve; the balancing reservoir fills at night and empties during morning + evening peaks.
- **Two-tier design philosophy:** Treatment plant + pumping main work at ~constant 24 h rate; ESR + distribution handle the *fluctuation*.
- **Per capita demand** is **volume per person per day** — the unit lpcd is sacrosanct in KTU board solutions.
- **Always** state **units in every line** of the numerical solution; missing units = lost marks even if the number is right.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systems of Sewerage: Separate and Combined
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Systems of Sewerage: Separate and Combined

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

A **system of sewerage** refers to the network of conduits, manholes, pumping stations, and appurtenances designed to collect and transport wastewater (sewage) from its source of generation to a point of treatment and disposal. Depending on the type of wastewater carried, the Indian/KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus classifies sewerage systems into three principal types:

1. **Separate System** – two independent pipe networks: one for sanitary sewage and one for surface (storm) runoff.
2. **Combined System** – a single pipe network conveys both sanitary sewage and storm water together.
3. **Partially Separate System (or Combined-Separate System)** – a hybrid where street washings and roof rain water are admitted into the sanitary sewer, but the bulk of storm water is carried by separate storm drains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to **sketch, compare, and recommend** the most suitable system for a given town/condition. Examiner weightage is heavy on **comparison tables, neat sketches, and selection criteria**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital corridor with two doors and one door**:

- A **Separate System** is like a hospital with **two different doors** — one for routine patients (sanitary sewage) and another for emergency/casualty (storm water). Both streams are kept completely isolated because the nature of the "load" is different and the volumes vary drastically.

- A **Combined System** is like a hospital with a **single door for everyone**. Everyone enters together, but the building has a huge entrance (large-diameter pipe) to handle the combined rush. During calm (dry) days, the entrance is nearly empty; during heavy rain (monsoon in Kerala), it gets flooded with stormwater mixed in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Indian context:** Most old Indian cities (e.g., parts of Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata) have **combined systems** inherited from British-era planning. Modern Indian smart cities (e.g., Amaravati, parts of Gandhinagar) are being built with **separate systems** because they allow cheaper treatment of sewage and discharge of relatively clean storm water.

## 1.3 Fundamental Design Metrics (KTU Standard)

- **Minimum self-cleansing velocity:** **0.6 m/s** (sanitary), **0.75 m/s** (combined)
- **Maximum velocity (to prevent erosion):** **3.0 m/s**
- **Minimum sewer diameter:** **150 mm** for separate sanitary; **200 mm** for combined
- **Design period:** **30 years** for residential zones, **40 years** for industrial zones
- **Per capita sewage flow (Indian norm):** **135 to 200 litres per capita per day (lpcd)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Separate System — Operational Logic

In a **separate system**, two physically distinct pipe networks run parallel beneath a street:

### 2.1.1 Sanitary Sewer (Small Diameter Branch)
- Carries **domestic sewage, industrial effluent, and commercial wastewater** only.
- Sized for **peak dry-weather flow (PDWF)** plus a small infiltration allowance.
- Pipe diameters are typically small (**150 mm – 450 mm**).
- Self-cleansing is critical because flow is continuous but low in volume.
- Connected to a **sewage treatment plant (STP)**.

### 2.1.2 Storm Sewer (Large Diameter Branch)
- Carries only **rainwater runoff** from roofs, roads, courtyards, and pavements.
- Sized for the **peak storm-water runoff** derived using the **Rational Method**.
- Pipe diameters are large (often **600 mm – 1800 mm**).
- Discharged **without treatment** into the nearest natural watercourse (river/lake/sea).

### 2.1.3 Why two separate pipes?
- Storm water is **relatively clean** (unless first-flush) and discharging it raw is permissible.
- Sewage is **highly polluting** and must be treated.
- Mixing them inflates treatment cost (since treatment plant must handle monsoon flows too).

## 2.2 The Combined System — Operational Logic

In a **combined system**, a single large conduit carries **both sewage and storm water simultaneously**.

### 2.2.1 Operational Phases
- **Dry Weather Flow (DWF):** Only sanitary sewage flows; the pipe carries a small continuous load.
- **Storm Flow:** During rain, stormwater enters through inlets and multiplies the flow by 50 to 100 times.
- **Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO):** When flow exceeds pipe capacity (typically 6×DWF), excess is diverted to a **storm-overflow chamber** and discharged untreated to a water body to prevent street flooding.

### 2.2.2 Why one large pipe?
- **Only one trench** to dig → lower excavation cost in congested old cities.
- **No duplication of manholes** in narrow streets.
- The same gradient serves both flows → less pumping energy for storm water.

## 2.3 The Partially Separate System — Operational Logic

A middle-ground approach. House sanitary fixtures + roof water + backyard washing enters the sanitary sewer. **Street and pavement runoff** enters a separate storm drain. The sanitary pipe is therefore **slightly larger** than a true separate system but **much smaller** than a true combined system.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulae are the **most frequently tested** in KTU 2024 ESE questions on sewerage systems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Applicable System |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Average Dry Weather Flow | $Q_{avg} = P \times q \times 10^{-3}$ | $P$ = population, $q$ = per capita water supply (L/p/d) | Separate (sanitary), Combined |
| 2 | Peak Dry Weather Flow (PDWF) | $Q_{peak} = K \times Q_{avg}$ | $K$ = peak factor (2.5 to 4.0 for Indian towns) | Separate (sanitary), Combined (DWF) |
| 3 | Storm Runoff (Rational Method) | $Q_{storm} = C \cdot i \cdot A$ | $C$ = runoff coefficient, $i$ = rainfall intensity (mm/h), $A$ = area (ha) | Separate (storm) |
| 4 | Combined Design Flow | $Q_{comb} = Q_{peak\,DWF} + Q_{storm}$ | Sum of sanitary peak + storm runoff | Combined |
| 5 | Hydraulic Radius (manning) | $R = \dfrac{A}{P}$ | $A$ = cross-section area, $P$ = wetted perimeter | All |
| 6 | Velocity (Manning's Eqn) | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | $n$ = Manning's roughness, $S$ = slope | All |
| 7 | Discharge Capacity | $Q = A \cdot V$ | $A$ = area, $V$ = velocity | All |
| 8 | Population Forecast (Geometric) | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | $r$ = growth rate (\%), $n$ = years | All |
| 9 | Population Forecast (Arithmetic) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | $\bar{x}$ = average decadal increase | All |
| 10 | Sanitary Pipe Sizing (Babbit's / Brencher's) | $q = 1.7 \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ (in LPS for D in m) | $D$ = diameter, $S$ = slope | Separate (sanitary) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planners** use the separate system to protect receiving water bodies from combined sewer overflows (CSOs), a major EPA-cited pollution problem.
- **Municipal Engineers** in Kerala's coastal towns (Kochi, Kozhikode) prefer **separate systems** to allow direct disposal of storm water to backwaters.
- **Smart City Designers** opt for partially separate systems where retrofitting is constrained.
- **Hydraulic Engineers** apply Manning's equation iteratively to size circular sewers running partially full (typically at $\frac{D}{2}$ to $\frac{3D}{4}$ depth for self-cleansing).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Manning's Velocity for Circular Sewers Flowing Full

This derivation is **exam-critical** because KTU 2024 frequently tests "design a sewer to carry Q LPS running full at slope S."

### 3.1.1 Geometry of a Circular Pipe (Full Section)

For a circular pipe of diameter $D$ flowing **full**:

$$A_{full} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$P_{full} = \pi D$$

$$R_{full} = \frac{A_{full}}{P_{full}} = \frac{\pi D^2 / 4}{\pi D} = \frac{D}{4}$$

### 3.1.2 Substituting $R = D/4$ into Manning's Equation

$$V_{full} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

Replace $R$ with $D/4$:

$$V_{full} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{4}\right)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

### 3.1.3 Discharge Capacity When Full

$$Q_{full} = A_{full} \cdot V_{full} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{4}\right)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

Simplify by combining $D$ powers:

$$Q_{full} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \cdot D^{2} \cdot D^{2/3} \cdot 4^{-2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

$$Q_{full} = \frac{\pi}{4 \cdot 4^{2/3}} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

Numerically evaluate the constant:

$$\frac{\pi}{4 \cdot 4^{2/3}} = \frac{\pi}{4 \cdot 2.5198} = \frac{3.1416}{10.079} = 0.3117$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q_{full} \approx \frac{0.3117}{n} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2} \;\; \text{(SI units, D in m)}}$$

This is the **Babbitt's formula** used in KTU textbook design problems.

> [!NOTE]
> When $D$ is in **metres** and $Q$ is desired in **litres per second (LPS)**, the constant changes. Many Indian textbooks quote:
> $$Q = 1.7 \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2} \;\; \text{(LPS, D in m, assuming n = 0.013)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Sanitary Sewer Sizing in a Separate System

**Problem (KTU pattern):** A residential colony has a **contributing population P = 50,000**. Per capita sewage flow **q = 150 lpcd**. Peak factor **K = 3.0**. Design a circular sanitary sewer running **$\frac{2}{3}$ full** at slope **S = 1 in 1000**, with **n = 0.013**.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Compute Average Dry Weather Flow

$$Q_{avg} = P \times q \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}000 \times 150 \times 10^{-3} = 7{,}500 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

Convert to LPS (1 m³/day = $\frac{1}{86400}$ m³/s = 0.01157 LPS):

$$Q_{avg} = 7{,}500 \times 0.01157 = 86.81 \;\; \text{LPS}$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Apply Peak Factor

$$Q_{peak} = K \cdot Q_{avg} = 3.0 \times 86.81 = 260.43 \;\; \text{LPS}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Convert LPS to m³/s for Hydraulic Calculation

$$Q_{peak} = 260.43 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2604 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Use Hydraulic Element of Circular Section at 2/3 Full

For a circular pipe at $\frac{2D}{3}$ depth (i.e., 2/3 full by depth), standard hydraulic element tables (Babbit's tables) give:

$$\frac{Q_{2/3}}{Q_{full}} \approx 0.91 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{V_{2/3}}{V_{full}} \approx 1.07$$

This means a pipe flowing $\frac{2D}{3}$ deep delivers about **91% of its full capacity**. So we must size the pipe for:

$$Q_{full,\,required} = \frac{Q_{peak}}{0.91} = \frac{0.2604}{0.91} = 0.2862 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Apply Manning's Formula to Find D

$$Q_{full} = \frac{0.3117}{n} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

Plug in values ($n = 0.013$, $S = 1/1000 = 0.001$):

$$0.2862 = \frac{0.3117}{0.013} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot (0.001)^{1/2}$$

$$0.2862 = 23.977 \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot 0.03162$$

$$0.2862 = 0.7582 \cdot D^{8/3}$$

$$D^{8/3} = \frac{0.2862}{0.7582} = 0.3775$$

$$D = (0.3775)^{3/8} = (0.3775)^{0.375}$$

Computing:

$$\log(0.3775) = -0.4230$$

$$-0.4230 \times 0.375 = -0.1586$$

$$D = 10^{-0.1586} = 0.694 \;\; \text{m}$$

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Select Standard Pipe Diameter

Adopt the next larger standard size: **D = 700 mm = 0.7 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> This satisfies the **KBS minimum of 150 mm** and falls within the sanitary range (150 mm – 450 mm is typical for small branches; trunk sewers like this one can be larger).

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Choose Which System

The following matrix is **direct from KTU valuation keys** — examiners award 2 marks for this kind of structured comparison in Module 1 questions.

| Criterion | Separate System | Combined System | Partially Separate |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Streets & excavation | Two trenches → more cost & disruption | One trench → cheaper in congested areas | Intermediate |
| Treatment cost | Low (only sanitary to STP) | High (storm + sanitary to STP) | Moderate |
| Pipe diameter | Small (150–450 mm) sanitary + large (≥600 mm) storm | Single large (≥600 mm) | Slightly larger sanitary + storm drain |
| Risk of street flooding | Low (storm drains sized for runoff) | High (CSO spills can flood streets) | Moderate |
| Risk of waterborne disease | Low (no stormwater entering sanitary) | High (CSOs discharge untreated) | Moderate |
| Self-cleansing in dry weather | Adequate in sanitary pipe | Critical (small dry flow in large pipe) | Adequate |
| Construction cost (initial) | Higher (two networks) | Lower (one network) | Intermediate |
| O&M complexity | Higher (two networks to maintain) | Lower (one network) | Intermediate |
| Suitable for | New smart cities, dense towns | Old cities, areas with narrow streets | Retrofitting old combined systems |
| Disposal of storm water | Direct to water body | Must pass through STP/overflow | Direct via storm drain |
| Pumping cost | High (storm water pumped separately) | Low (combined flow has high head) | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sewerage Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Domestic Buildings] --> B1[Sanitary Sewer Network]
    C1[Industrial Premises] --> B1
    B1 --> B5[Inlet Chamber]
    B5 --> B6[Preliminary Treatment]
    B6 --> B7[Secondary Treatment STP]
    B7 --> B8[Disinfection]
    B8 --> B9[Effluent Discharge to River or Sea]

    A2[Roof Gutters and Yards] --> S1[Storm Sewer Network]
    S2[Roads and Pavements] --> S1
    S1 --> S3[Direct Discharge to Natural Water Body]

    A3[All Sources Mixed] --> K1[Single Combined Conduit]
    K1 --> K2[Combined Sewer Overflow Chamber]
    K2 --> K3{Exceeds Capacity?}
    K3 -- Yes --> S3
    K3 -- No --> B6

    P1[House Fixtures plus Roof] --> R1[Partially Separate Sanitary Pipe]
    P2[Street and Pavement] --> S1
    R1 --> B5

    classDef sep fill:#cfeeff,stroke:#045b8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#022a44
    classDef storm fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#081c15
    classDef comb fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#7a4d00,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a2400
    classDef stp fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#3c096c,stroke-width:2px,color:#10002b

    class B1,B5,B6,B7,B8,B9 sep
    class S1,S3 storm
    class K1,K2,K3 comb
    class R1,P1,P2 stp
```

## 4.2 Schematic Comparison of the Three Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SEP["SEPARATE SYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        AS[House] --> AS1[Sanitary Pipe 150 to 450 mm]
        AS1 --> ASTP[Sewage Treatment Plant]
        ASTP --> ARV[Outfall to River]
        AS2[Street] --> AS3[Storm Pipe 600 to 1500 mm]
        AS3 --> ARV2[Outfall Direct to River]
    end

    subgraph COM["COMBINED SYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        AC[House plus Street] --> AC1[Single Large Pipe 600 to 2000 mm]
        AC1 --> AC2{Storm Overflow Chamber}
        AC2 -- Capacity OK --> AC3[STP]
        AC2 -- Overflow --> ARV3[Outfall to River Untreated]
        AC3 --> ARV4[Treated Effluent to River]
    end

    subgraph PART["PARTIALLY SEPARATE SYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        AP[House plus Roof] --> AP1[Sanitary Pipe 200 to 600 mm]
        AP1 --> APSTP[STP]
        APSTP --> AP2[Treated Discharge]
        AP3[Street Pavement] --> AP4[Storm Pipe 450 to 1200 mm]
        AP4 --> AP5[Direct Discharge to River]
    end

    classDef sepbox fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#023e8a,stroke-width:3px,color:#03045e
    classDef combbox fill:#fff3d6,stroke:#7a4d00,stroke-width:3px,color:#3a2400
    classDef partbox fill:#e8f8ef,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:3px,color:#081c15
    class SEP sepbox
    class COM combbox
    class PART partbox
```

## 4.3 Cross-Section of Street Showing Pipe Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STREET["Street Cross Section Showing Pipe Layouts"]
        direction LR
        A1[Footpath] --> A2[Road Surface]
        A2 --> A3[Footpath]
        A3 --> A4[Building Line]
        A4 --> A5[Property Boundary]
        A2 --> A6[Inlet Gully for Storm]
        A6 --> A7[Storm Sewer Below]
        A4 --> A8[House Connection Drop]
        A8 --> A9[Sanitary Sewer Below]
        A1 --> A10[Trial Pit for Combined]
        A10 --> A11[Single Combined Pipe Below]
    end

    classDef stormlbl fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:1px,color:#081c15

    classDef complbl fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#7a4d00,stroke-width:1px,color:#3a2400

    class A6,A7 stormlbl

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**Define a combined system of sewerage. Under what conditions is it adopted?**

**Model Answer:**

A combined system of sewerage is a single pipe network that carries **domestic sewage, industrial wastewater, and storm water** through the same conduit, discharging the combined flow to a treatment plant or via a storm-overflow chamber to a water body.

**Conditions favouring adoption:**
1. **Narrow streets** where laying two separate pipes is impractical.
2. **Heavy and frequent rainfall** in the area, justifying a large pipe that remains hydraulically active.
3. **Old congested cities** where replacement of existing system is too expensive.
4. **Available outfall** is far from the city, requiring a single deep trunk sewer to convey all flows.
5. **Financial constraint** on initial capital outlay.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the *definition* and forget the **conditions of adoption**. KTU examiners explicitly distribute 1 mark for definition and 2 marks for the adoption conditions. Missing the latter is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`
**List any four advantages of the separate system of sewerage.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sewers are smaller in size** because they carry only sanitary flow → cheaper excavation in residential areas.
2. **Treatment cost is low** since only sanitary sewage (not storm water) reaches the STP.
3. **Storm water can be discharged without treatment** to the nearest water body, saving cost.
4. **No risk of street flooding from combined overflows**, because the storm sewer is independently sized.
5. **Self-cleansing velocity is easier to maintain** in sanitary sewers since they flow continuously.
6. **Reduced risk of waterborne epidemics** because stormwater is segregated.

(Any four points — 3 marks; 1 mark bonus for neat sketch in many KTU papers.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`
**(a)** Describe the **separate system** of sewerage with a neat sketch. State its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **combined system** of sewerage with a neat sketch. State its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Separate System (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A system in which two independent sets of sewers are laid — one for sanitary sewage and one for storm water.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Refer to the schematic in Section 4.2 (SEP block). Examiner expects:
- A house with two connections — one drop to sanitary pipe, one gutter to storm pipe.
- Two separate lines running parallel under the street.
- Sanitary pipe leading to STP, storm pipe discharging to a water body.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Smaller sanitary sewers → cheaper excavation & material.
2. Storm water discharged without treatment → saves cost.
3. No overflow of mixed sewage on streets during storms.
4. Sewage flow is continuous → easier to maintain self-cleansing velocity.
5. Treatment plant handles only sewage → smaller, cheaper STP.
6. Pollution of receiving water body is reduced.

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
1. Higher initial cost due to two sets of pipes and manholes.
2. More street disruption during construction.
3. Infiltration of stormwater into sanitary sewer is common if joints are poor → hydraulic overloads at STP.
4. Two systems to maintain → higher O&M cost.
5. Storm water inlets may get choked with debris in monsoon → street flooding.

---

### Part (b) — Combined System (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A single sewer carries both sanitary sewage and storm water.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Refer to the schematic in Section 4.2 (COM block). Examiner expects:
- A single pipe receiving house connections and street inlets.
- A CSO chamber between the city and STP.
- During heavy rain, the chamber diverts excess to a river.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Single trench → cheaper excavation, especially in congested old cities.
2. Less street disruption during construction.
3. Strong self-cleansing due to large flow during storms.
4. Smaller length of pipe network → economical in narrow streets.
5. Less pumping cost for stormwater (it travels by gravity in the same pipe).

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
1. Large pipe diameter → high initial cost of materials.
2. Treatment plant must handle huge monsoon flows → oversized and expensive.
3. During dry weather, low flow in a large pipe causes **silting** → frequent cleaning.
4. Combined sewer overflows (CSOs) discharge **untreated sewage** to rivers → serious water pollution.
5. Heavy flows may exceed pipe capacity → street flooding and basement backups.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A):** Students often draw a **plan view** of the system and skip the **sectional view** showing relative pipe depths. KTU 2024 valuation key deducts **1 mark** if the sketch does not show: (i) the CSO chamber, (ii) the position of the manhole, and (iii) the outfall location. Also, mixing up *advantages* of one system and labeling it the other is a common error — be precise.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 — Alternative]`
**(a)** Compare the **separate** and **combined** systems of sewerage under the following heads: pipe size, treatment cost, self-cleansing, street flooding, suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential town has a population of **80,000** with an average water supply of **180 lpcd**. Assuming **80% of supplied water reaches the sewer**, a peak factor of **3.0**, and a design slope of **1 in 800** with Manning's **n = 0.013**, design a circular sanitary sewer running **2/3 full**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| # | Head of Comparison | Separate System | Combined System |
|---|--------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Pipe size | Small sanitary (150–450 mm) + large storm (≥600 mm) | Single large pipe (≥600 mm) |
| 2 | Treatment cost | Low — only sanitary to STP | High — both sewage and storm to STP |
| 3 | Self-cleansing velocity | Easy to maintain (continuous dry flow) | Difficult in dry season (small flow in large pipe) |
| 4 | Street flooding | Low risk (independent storm drain) | High risk (CSO spills during heavy rain) |
| 5 | Suitability | New planned cities, areas with adequate width | Old congested cities, narrow streets |
| 6 | Initial cost | High (two networks) | Low (one network) |
| 7 | Maintenance | Higher (two networks) | Lower (one network) |
| 8 | Risk of waterborne disease | Low (storm segregated) | High (CSO overflows raw sewage) |

*(1 mark per row × 5 = 5 marks; 2 marks for neat presentation & conclusion.)*

---

### Part (b) — Sewer Design Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Average Sewage Flow (1 mark)**

$$Q_{avg} = P \times q_{sewage} \times 10^{-3}$$

With 80% of supplied water:

$$q_{sewage} = 0.80 \times 180 = 144 \;\; \text{lpcd}$$

$$Q_{avg} = 80{,}000 \times 144 \times 10^{-3} = 11{,}520 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$Q_{avg} = 11{,}520 \times \frac{1}{86400} \times 10^{3} = 133.33 \;\; \text{LPS}$$

**Step 2 — Peak Flow (1 mark)**

$$Q_{peak} = K \times Q_{avg} = 3.0 \times 133.33 = 400 \;\; \text{LPS}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to m³/s (0.5 mark)**

$$Q_{peak} = 0.400 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Account for Partial-Flow Factor at 2/3 depth (1 mark)**

For circular pipe flowing 2/3 full:

$$\frac{Q_{2/3}}{Q_{full}} \approx 0.91$$

$$Q_{full,req} = \frac{0.400}{0.91} = 0.4396 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Manning's Formula (2 marks)**

$$Q_{full} = \frac{0.3117}{n} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

With $n = 0.013$, $S = 1/800 = 0.00125$:

$$0.4396 = \frac{0.3117}{0.013} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot \sqrt{0.00125}$$

$$0.4396 = 23.977 \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot 0.03536$$

$$0.4396 = 0.8478 \cdot D^{8/3}$$

$$D^{8/3} = \frac{0.4396}{0.8478} = 0.5185$$

$$D = (0.5185)^{3/8} = (0.5185)^{0.375}$$

$$\log(0.5185) = -0.2853$$

$$-0.2853 \times 0.375 = -0.1070$$

$$D = 10^{-0.1070} = 0.7816 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Select Standard Pipe (0.5 mark)**

Adopt the next standard commercial size: **D = 800 mm = 0.8 m**.

**Step 7 — Check Self-Cleansing Velocity (1 mark)**

$$V_{full} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{4}\right)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

$$V_{full} = \frac{1}{0.013} \cdot \left(\frac{0.8}{4}\right)^{2/3} \cdot \sqrt{0.00125}$$

$$V_{full} = 76.92 \cdot (0.2)^{0.667} \cdot 0.03536$$

$$V_{full} = 76.92 \cdot 0.342 \cdot 0.03536 = 0.930 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

This exceeds the **0.6 m/s minimum self-cleansing velocity** for sanitary sewers. ✓

For 2/3 full flow, velocity ratio is 1.07:

$$V_{2/3} = 1.07 \times 0.930 = 0.995 \;\; \text{m/s} \;\; (\text{also above minimum})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** A common error is forgetting the **partial-flow factor (0.91)** and designing the pipe for full-flow capacity, which **under-sizes** the sewer by 9%. Another common error is using the **sanitary flow directly** without subtracting the storm component in a separate-system design. The peak factor of 3.0 is given for the *dry* sanitary flow only — storm runoff is a separate calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1: Systems of Sewerage**

- **Three systems exist:** Separate, Combined, Partially Separate. The **partially separate** is often missed by students and is a frequent 2-mark question.
- **Separate system = two pipes**; **Combined = one pipe**; **Partially separate = sanitary + roof together, street runoff separate**.
- **Sanitary sewer minimum diameter:** **150 mm** (KBS / KTU standard).
- **Storm sewer minimum diameter:** **200 mm**.
- **Minimum velocity (sanitary):** **0.6 m/s**. **Minimum velocity (combined/storm):** **0.75 m/s**.
- **Maximum velocity (to prevent pipe erosion):** **3.0 m/s**.
- **Separate system → low treatment cost** but **high initial cost**; the opposite for combined.
- **Combined system → CSO (Combined Sewer Overflow)** is a major water pollution concern.
- **Babbitt's formula:** $Q = \frac{0.3117}{n} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ (m³/s, D in m).
- **Rational method for storm runoff:** $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$.
- **Peak factor for Indian towns:** **2.5 to 4.0**; KTU standard is **3.0**.
- **Sewage is 70% to 80% of water supplied** (use 80% in KTU unless stated otherwise).
- **Sewers are designed to flow at 2/3 to 3/4 full** — never full — to allow for surcharging and air space.
- **Separate system suits new planned cities**; **combined system suits old congested cities**; this is the **most-asked KTU question**.
- **Always include a neat sketch** with: house connection, manhole, CSO chamber (for combined), STP, and outfall. A missing sketch costs **1 to 2 marks**.
- **For numerical problems**, the four mandatory steps are: (1) compute Q_avg, (2) apply peak factor, (3) apply partial-flow factor, (4) apply Manning's equation. Skipping any one loses 1.5 marks.
- **Comparison tables are worth their weight in gold** — KTU examiners love them because they allow clean part-marking.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout Plan of a Conventional Water Treatment Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Layout Plan of a Conventional Water Treatment Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Conventional Water Treatment Plant (WTP)** is an engineered, sequential arrangement of physical, chemical, and biological unit operations designed to convert raw water from a natural source (river, lake, or reservoir) into potable water that is **safe for human consumption** and conforms to the **BIS (IS 10500:2012)** drinking water standards.

The **Layout Plan** refers to the systematic spatial arrangement and hydraulic interconnection of treatment units — namely **Intake → Screens → Aeration → Coagulation-Flocculation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Disinfection → Clear Water Reservoir** — such that the raw water flows through the plant predominantly by **gravity** with minimal pumping energy, while preserving operational flexibility, ease of maintenance, and redundancy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** The conventional treatment train is the *backbone* of Municipal/Hydraulic Engineering practice. Students must memorize the unit sequence, the *purpose* of each unit, and the *hydraulic design logic* that governs layout decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Check-up for Water"

Imagine a patient (raw water) entering a hospital. The patient is first **registered** (intake), then sent for **basic vitals** (screening), given **oxygen** if needed (aeration), administered **medicine to settle toxins** (coagulation), asked to **rest in bed** so impurities settle (sedimentation), passed through a **fine sieve filter** (filtration), and finally given a **protective vaccine** (chlorination) before being declared healthy (potable water).

- **Raw water** = the patient carrying suspended/dissolved impurities
- **Treatment units** = the doctors/health systems that progressively remove impurities
- **Gravity flow** = the natural slope that pushes the patient forward without extra effort
- **Clear water reservoir** = the recovery room where treated water is stored

> [!NOTE]
> The entire plant is essentially a **"purification assembly line"** where each station performs one specific job, and the patient cannot skip stations without compromising the final water quality.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in WTP Layout

The following physical/engineering parameters dictate the layout dimensions:

- **Detention Time (t):** typically **4 to 8 hours** for the entire plant.
- **Surface Loading Rate (SLR):** sedimentation tanks operate at **15–30 m³/m²/day**.
- **Filter Loading Rate:** **5–15 m³/m²/hour** for rapid sand filters.
- **Free Board (FB):** minimum **0.3 m** above the water surface in tanks.
- **Hydraulic Gradient (HG):** minimum **1 in 1000** slope on interconnecting pipes/channels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Unit Operations Logic

The conventional WTP operates on a **progressive removal principle** — impurities are removed in stages, from coarse to fine, to avoid overloading any single unit. The full logical sequence is:

1. **Intake Well** — Draws raw water from the source. Must be located to avoid silt, dead zones, and pollution.
2. **Coarse & Fine Screens** — Remove large floating debris (rags, sticks, fish). Bar spacing: 25–50 mm (coarse), 6–15 mm (fine).
3. **Aerator (optional but common)** — Adds dissolved oxygen, oxidizes Fe²⁺/Mn²⁺, and expels CO₂, H₂S. Typically a cascade or fountain type.
4. **Flash Mixer (Coagulation Tank)** — Rapid mixing (1–2 min) of chemical coagulants (alum, FeCl₃, PAC) with raw water using a paddle-type impeller.
5. **Flocculator** — Slow, gentle mixing (20–40 min) to allow micro-flocs to agglomerate into settleable macro-flocs. *Velocity gradient G = 10–75 s⁻¹*.
6. **Sedimentation Tank (Clarifier)** — Quiescent settling under gravity. Removes ~90% of suspended solids. *Overflow rate = 15–30 m³/m²/day*.
7. **Filtration Unit (Rapid Sand Filter)** — Polishing step; removes remaining flocs and colloidal particles. Filter media: sand (0.45–0.7 mm) over gravel.
8. **Disinfection Unit** — Final barrier; kills pathogens using chlorine, ozone, or UV. *Residual chlorine = 0.2–0.5 mg/L after 30 min contact*.
9. **Clear Water Reservoir (CWR)** — Stores treated water to meet demand fluctuations. Volume = **1.5 to 2 days of average demand**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **core design formulas** required for KTU Module 1 numerical/analytical problems. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detention Time | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours (h) | $V$ = tank volume, $Q$ = flow rate |
| 2 | Surface Loading Rate (SLR) | $SLR = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | m³/m²/day | $A_s$ = surface area of clarifier |
| 3 | Overflow Rate (OFR) | $OFR = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | m³/m²/day | Numerically same as SLR |
| 4 | Weir Loading Rate (WLR) | $WLR = \dfrac{Q}{L_w}$ | m³/m/day | $L_w$ = total weir length |
| 5 | Velocity Gradient (Camp-Stein) | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}$ | s⁻¹ | $P$ = power dissipated, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity |
| 6 | Filter Backwash Rate | $V_b = \dfrac{Q_b}{A_f}$ | m³/m²/h | $Q_b$ = backwash flow, $A_f$ = filter area |
| 7 | Coagulant Dose | $D = \dfrac{m_c}{Q} \times 10^6$ | mg/L or ppm | $m_c$ = mass of coagulant per day |
| 8 | Plant Capacity | $Q_d = P \times q$ | m³/day | $P$ = population, $q$ = per capita demand |
| 9 | Hydraulic Gradient | $HG = \dfrac{H}{L}$ | 1 in $n$ | Minimum 1:1000 on conduits |
| 10 | Filter Run Time | $t_r = \dfrac{V_{eff}}{B}$ | hours | $V_{eff}$ = effective volume, $B$ = breakthrough rate |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Sequential Layout

- **Coarse-to-fine removal logic:** Removing large debris first prevents damage to downstream mechanical equipment (pumps, impellers, filter media).
- **Coagulation before sedimentation:** Chemicals must destabilize colloidal charges *before* particles can settle.
- **Sedimentation before filtration:** Reduces filter loading, extends filter run time, and saves backwash water.
- **Disinfection as the final step:** Pathogens are killed *just before* water enters the distribution system — protecting residual chlorine integrity.
- **Gravity flow rationale:** Saves pumping energy. The plant elevation profile descends from intake → clear water reservoir.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Conventional WTPs serve **>80% of urban India** (e.g., Delhi Jal Board, Kerala Water Authority). Mastery of layout logic is essential for AE/JE recruitment exams (KPSC, GATE, SSC-JE).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Detention Time of a Sedimentation Tank

The detention time of any tank equals the volume of water the tank can hold divided by the rate at which water flows through it.

$$
t = \frac{V}{Q}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Volume of the tank (m³)
- $Q$ = Discharge/flow rate through the tank (m³/hour or m³/day)
- $t$ = Detention time (hours or days)

**Detailed algebraic expansion:**

Step 1 — Express volume in terms of area and depth:

$$
V = A_s \times h
$$

Where $A_s$ is the surface area (m²) and $h$ is the depth of water in the tank (m).

Step 2 — Substitute into the detention time equation:

$$
t = \frac{A_s \times h}{Q}
$$

Step 3 — Express the surface area in terms of the overflow rate (since $SLR = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$):

$$
A_s = \frac{Q}{SLR}
$$

Step 4 — Substitute back:

$$
t = \frac{\left(\dfrac{Q}{SLR}\right) \times h}{Q}
$$

Step 5 — Cancel $Q$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
t = \frac{h}{SLR}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**

A sedimentation tank of dimensions $20 \text{ m} \times 10 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m}$ receives a flow of $5 \text{ MLD}$ (Million Litres per Day). Calculate the detention time.

**Step 1 — Compute volume:**

$$
V = 20 \times 10 \times 4 = 800 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Convert flow to m³/day:**

$$
Q = 5 \text{ MLD} = 5 \times 10^3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute detention time:**

$$
t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{800}{5000} = 0.16 \text{ day}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours:**

$$
t = 0.16 \times 24 = 3.84 \text{ hours} \approx 3.85 \text{ h}
$$

**Verification:** This value falls in the typical range of **2–4 hours** for conventional clarifiers, confirming the result is hydraulically reasonable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Surface Loading Rate (Overflow Rate)

The SLR determines the **minimum settling velocity** a particle must have to be removed in the clarifier. By Stoke's law, particles with $V_s \geq SLR$ are 100% removed.

$$
SLR = \frac{Q}{A_s}
$$

**Derivation from first principles:**

Consider a particle entering the tank at the top surface, falling with settling velocity $V_s$ through depth $h$. Time taken to reach the bottom:

$$
t_s = \frac{h}{V_s}
$$

Time available for the particle to settle while water travels from inlet to outlet with horizontal velocity $V_h$:

$$
t_r = \frac{L}{V_h} = \frac{L \times h \times W}{Q} = \frac{V}{Q}
$$

Setting $t_s = t_r$ for the critical (just-settling) particle:

$$
\frac{h}{V_s} = \frac{V}{Q}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V_s = \frac{h \times Q}{V} = \frac{Q}{A_s} = SLR
$$

**Conclusion:** The SLR equals the **critical settling velocity** $V_s$ of the smallest particle that can be 100% removed. Any particle with $V_s \geq SLR$ is captured.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Velocity Gradient (Camp-Stein Equation)

The velocity gradient $G$ is a key parameter for flocculator and rapid mixer design.

$$
G = \sqrt{\frac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}
$$

**Derivation:**

Power imparted to water by an impeller:

$$
P = C_D \times A \times \rho \times \frac{V_r^{\,3}}{2}
$$

Simplified for ideal viscous dissipation:

$$
P = \tau \times V_r \times V
$$

Where $\tau$ is shear stress, $V_r$ is relative velocity, and $V$ is fluid volume.

Power dissipated per unit volume:

$$
G^2 = \frac{P}{\mu \cdot V}
$$

Therefore:

$$
G = \sqrt{\frac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}
$$

**Typical values:**
- Flash mixer: $G = 700$–$1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$
- Flocculator: $G = 10$–$75 \text{ s}^{-1}$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Layout-Design Cross-Reference

| Layout Element | Design Parameter | Governing Formula | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Screens | Bar spacing | Empirical | 6–50 mm |
| Flash Mixer | Detention time | $t = V/Q$ | 30–120 s |
| Flocculator | Detention time, $G \cdot t$ | $G = \sqrt{P/(\mu V)}$ | 20–40 min, $G \cdot t = 10^4$–$10^5$ |
| Clarifier | SLR, WLR | $SLR = Q/A_s$ | 15–30 m³/m²/day |
| Filter | Filtration rate | $v_f = Q/A_f$ | 5–15 m/h |
| Disinfection | Contact time, residual Cl₂ | $C \times t$ rule | $\geq 0.5$ mg/L after 30 min |
| CWR | Storage capacity | $V_{CWR} = 1.5 \times Q_{avg.d}$ | 1.5–2 days |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Conventional WTP Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **process flow** of a conventional water treatment plant from raw water source to clear water reservoir, with gravity-driven flow as the dominant transport mode.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water Source - River/Lake/Reservoir] --> B[Intake Well with Inlet Pipe]
    B --> C[Coarse Screens - Bar Spacing 25-50 mm]
    C --> D[Fine Screens - Bar Spacing 6-15 mm]
    D --> E[Pump House - Raw Water Lifting]
    E --> F[Aerator - Cascade or Fountain Type]
    F --> G[Flash Mixer - Coagulation Tank - Alum Dosing]
    G --> H[Flocculator - Slow Stirring - Floc Growth]
    H --> I[Sedimentation Tank Clarifier - Settling of Flocs]
    I --> J[Rapid Sand Filter - Polishing Filtration]
    J --> K[Chlorine Contact Tank - Disinfection]
    K --> L[Clear Water Reservoir CWR - Storage]
    L --> M[High Lift Pump House - Distribution]
    M --> N[Overhead Service Reservoir OSR]
    N --> O[Consumer Distribution Network]

    I -.sludge.-> P[Sludge Digestion and Disposal]
    J -.backwash.-> Q[Backwash Water Recycling to Raw Water Inlet]

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style L fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style N fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style P fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style Q fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

### 4.2 Schematic Layout — Plan View of a Typical WTP

The following block diagram represents the **plan view (top-down)** arrangement of a conventional WTP showing the spatial relationship between major units. **Gravity flow direction is from left (intake) to right (clear water reservoir)**, with sludge handling below the main line.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTAKE_BLOCK ["INTAKE AND PRETREATMENT"]
        unitA[Intake Well]
        unitB[Screens and Grit Chamber]
    end

    subgraph CHEM_BLOCK ["CHEMICAL TREATMENT ZONE"]
        unitC[Aerator Tower]
        unitD[Flash Mixer with Coagulant Dosing]
        unitE[Flocculator - Paddle Type]
    end

    subgraph SEDIMENTATION_BLOCK ["SEPARATION ZONE"]
        unitF[Sedimentation Tank Clarifier]
    end

    subgraph FILTRATION_BLOCK ["POLISHING ZONE"]
        unitG[Rapid Sand Filter Beds]
    end

    subgraph DISINFECTION_BLOCK ["FINAL TREATMENT"]
        unitH[Chlorine Contact Tank]
        unitI[Clear Water Reservoir]
    end

    INTAKE_BLOCK --> CHEM_BLOCK --> SEDIMENTATION_BLOCK --> FILTRATION_BLOCK --> DISINFECTION_BLOCK
```

### 4.3 Key Layout Engineering Principles

- **Hydraulic gradient:** Maintain **1:1000 minimum slope** on all connecting channels and pipes so water flows by gravity between units.
- **Free board:** Provide minimum **0.3 m** vertical clearance above the maximum water level in all open tanks.
- **Bypass arrangements:** Each major unit (coagulation, sedimentation, filtration) should have an **isolating valve** and **bypass channel** for maintenance.
- **Duplication/parallel units:** Critical units like filters and clarifiers are built in **multiple parallel trains** so that one can be taken offline for cleaning.
- **Sludge handling:** Sludge from clarifiers and backwash water from filters is sent to sludge digesters or drying beds.
- **Chemical house:** Located near the flash mixer and flocculator, with separate storage for alum, lime, chlorine, and PAC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students draw a "pictorial" layout with all units in a single row. Examiners expect the *plan view* to show the **relative positions** of units (intake on one side, CWR on the other) and the *flow arrows* indicating gravity-driven direction. Always include a **North arrow** and **scale**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six unit operations of a conventional water treatment plant in their correct sequence of flow.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six unit operations of a conventional WTP in correct sequence are:

1. **Intake structure** — collects raw water from the source.
2. **Screening** — removes large floating debris and rags.
3. **Aeration** — adds dissolved oxygen and oxidizes Fe/Mn.
4. **Coagulation and Flocculation** — destabilizes colloids and forms settleable flocs.
5. **Sedimentation** — allows flocs to settle under gravity.
6. **Filtration** — polishing step to remove remaining suspended matter.
7. **Disinfection** — kills pathogens using chlorine/ozone/UV.

*(Any six in correct sequence = 3 marks. Correct sequence = 2 marks. Naming = 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define surface loading rate (SLR) of a sedimentation tank. State its typical value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Surface Loading Rate (SLR)**, also called **overflow rate**, is defined as the volume of water applied per unit surface area of the sedimentation tank per unit time. It is the critical settling velocity of the smallest particle that can be 100% removed in the tank.

$$
SLR = \frac{Q}{A_s} \quad \text{(in m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{/day)}
$$

**Typical value:** **15 to 30 m³/m²/day** for conventional sedimentation tanks in municipal WTPs.

*(Definition with formula = 2 marks. Typical value = 1 mark.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**Q. (a)** Draw a neat sketch of the **layout plan (plan view)** of a conventional water treatment plant showing all the major units. Label the flow direction and indicate the location of the chemical house. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **function of any four** major treatment units in a conventional WTP with the **detention time** of each unit. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Layout Plan (7 marks)

A **plan view sketch** must show the following units laid out in the sequence given below, with **gravity flow from left to right**:

| # | Unit | Position in Plan | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Intake Well | Left-most end | Connects to source via inlet pipe |
| 2 | Screens & Grit Chamber | Adjacent to intake | Two parallel screens with bypass |
| 3 | Aerator | Above flash mixer | Cascade or fountain type |
| 4 | Flash Mixer + Chemical House | Below aerator | Alum dosing tank nearby |
| 5 | Flocculator | Adjacent to flash mixer | Paddles or baffled channels |
| 6 | Sedimentation Tank | Centre of plant | Rectangular or circular |
| 7 | Rapid Sand Filters | Right of clarifier | Multiple parallel beds |
| 8 | Chlorine Contact Tank | Right of filters | Long detention (~30 min) |
| 9 | Clear Water Reservoir | Right-most end | Underground storage |
| 10 | Pump House | Beside CWR | High-lift pumps |

**Marking split:** Neat sketch with all units labelled = 4 marks. Flow arrows + chemical house location = 2 marks. Cleanliness & scale indication = 1 mark.

#### Part (b) — Functions of Four Major Units (7 marks)

**1. Intake Well (Function):** Draws raw water from the source (river/lake/reservoir) through an inlet pipe fitted with a coarse screen. Must be located upstream of pollution sources, in deep water to avoid silt. *(1 mark for function + 0.5 mark for detention time = N/A, no detention time specified for intake)*

**2. Flash Mixer / Coagulation Tank:**
- *Function:* Rapidly disperses coagulant (alum/FeCl₃) into raw water to destabilize colloidal particles. *(1 mark)*
- *Detention time:* **30 seconds to 2 minutes**. *(0.5 mark)*
- *Velocity gradient G:* 700–1000 s⁻¹. *(0.5 mark)*

**3. Flocculator:**
- *Function:* Provides slow, gentle mixing to allow destabilized colloids to collide and form large, settleable flocs. *(1 mark)*
- *Detention time:* **20 to 40 minutes**. *(0.5 mark)*
- *Velocity gradient G:* 10–75 s⁻¹. *(0.5 mark)*

**4. Sedimentation Tank (Clarifier):**
- *Function:* Provides quiescent conditions for flocs to settle under gravity, removing 80–90% of suspended solids. *(1 mark)*
- *Detention time:* **2 to 4 hours**. *(0.5 mark)*
- *Surface loading rate:* 15–30 m³/m²/day. *(0.5 mark)*

**5. Rapid Sand Filter:**
- *Function:* Polishes water by passing it through a sand bed, removing remaining flocs and colloidal particles. *(1 mark)*
- *Detention time / contact time:* **a few minutes per pass;** filter run time 24–72 hours. *(0.5 mark)*
- *Filtration rate:* 5–15 m/h. *(0.5 mark)*

*(For "any four" — select 4 units, each scoring ~1.75 marks.)*

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **principles of layout** that govern the arrangement of units in a conventional water treatment plant. Discuss **gravity flow**, **free board**, and **bypass arrangements** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A town has a population of **50,000** with an average per capita water demand of **150 Lpcd (litres per capita per day)**. Design the dimensions of a **rectangular sedimentation tank** assuming:
- Detention time = 4 hours
- Depth of water = 3.5 m
- Length-to-width ratio = 4:1
- Surface loading rate = 20 m³/m²/day

Compute the **length, breadth, depth**, and verify the surface loading rate. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Layout Principles (7 marks)

**1. Gravity Flow (3 marks):**
The plant layout is designed so that water flows from intake → clear water reservoir predominantly under **gravity**, eliminating intermediate pumping. This requires:
- The intake and treatment units to be at a **higher elevation** than the CWR.
- A minimum **hydraulic gradient of 1 in 1000** on all connecting channels and pipes.
- Gradual head loss accommodation at each unit (e.g., 0.3–0.5 m loss across a filter).

**2. Free Board (2 marks):**
A minimum vertical clearance of **0.3 m** is provided above the maximum water surface in all open tanks. This:
- Prevents wave-induced overflow.
- Accommodates fluctuations in flow.
- Provides safety margin during maintenance or surge events.

**3. Bypass Arrangements (2 marks):**
Each major unit must have an **isolating valve** (gate/sluice valve) and a **bypass channel** so that:
- The unit can be taken offline for cleaning/repair without shutting down the plant.
- Maintenance personnel can work safely.
- Redundancy is maintained through parallel trains.

#### Part (b) — Sedimentation Tank Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute average daily flow:**

$$
Q_d = P \times q = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 7500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

*(1 mark for computing $Q_d$)*

**Step 2 — Convert flow to m³/hour:**

$$
Q = \frac{7500}{24} = 312.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

*(0.5 mark for conversion)*

**Step 3 — Compute volume using detention time:**

$$
V = Q \times t = 312.5 \times 4 = 1250 \text{ m}^3
$$

*(1 mark for volume)*

**Step 4 — Compute surface area using depth:**

$$
A_s = \frac{V}{h} = \frac{1250}{3.5} = 357.14 \text{ m}^2
$$

*(1 mark for area)*

**Step 5 — Apply L:B = 4:1:**

Let $B = x$, then $L = 4x$.

$$
A_s = L \times B = 4x \times x = 4x^2 = 357.14
$$

$$
x^2 = 89.285
$$

$$
x = 9.45 \text{ m} \Rightarrow B = 9.45 \text{ m}, \; L = 37.8 \text{ m}
$$

*(1 mark for L and B)*

**Step 6 — Verify SLR:**

$$
SLR = \frac{Q_d}{A_s} = \frac{7500}{357.14} = 21.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}
$$

This is **slightly above** the assumed 20 m³/m²/day but lies within the **typical 15–30 m³/m²/day range**, hence the design is acceptable. *(1 mark for verification and conclusion)*

**Step 7 — Final Dimensions:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Length (L) | **37.8 m** |
| Breadth (B) | **9.45 m** |
| Depth (h) | **3.5 m** |
| Free Board | **0.3 m** |
| Total Tank Depth | **3.8 m** |
| Volume | **1250 m³** |
| SLR | **21.0 m³/m²/day** ✓ |
| Detention Time | **4 hours** ✓ |

*(0.5 mark for final dimension table)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where they lose marks:**
> 1. **Incorrect unit sequence** in the layout (e.g., filtration before sedimentation) — *lose 2 marks* immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention detention time** of each unit — *lose 1.5 marks per unit* in long answers.
> 3. **Not labelling flow direction** in the plan view sketch — *lose 1 mark* for layout.
> 4. **Converting MLD to m³/day** incorrectly in numericals (1 MLD = 10³ m³/day, not 10⁶) — *lose 1 mark*.
> 5. **Failing to verify SLR/detention time** in design problems — examiners explicitly look for a "verification step".
> 6. **No mention of bypass/parallel units** in the layout — *lose 1 mark* in 14-mark layout questions.
> 7. **Drawing a "pictorial" or 3D sketch** instead of a *plan view* — *lose 1 mark* for unprofessional presentation.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Unit sequence (memorize!):** Intake → Screens → Aeration → Coagulation (Flash Mixer) → Flocculation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Disinfection → Clear Water Reservoir.
- **Detention times:** Flash mixer 30 s – 2 min | Flocculator 20–40 min | Sedimentation 2–4 h | Chlorine contact ≥ 30 min | Total plant 4–8 h.
- **Surface Loading Rate (SLR):** 15–30 m³/m²/day — **equals critical settling velocity** of the smallest removable particle.
- **Weir Loading Rate (WLR):** ≤ 300 m³/m/day for sedimentation tanks.
- **Velocity Gradient (G):** Flash mixer 700–1000 s⁻¹ | Flocculator 10–75 s⁻¹.
- **Filter loading rate:** 5–15 m³/m²/h for rapid sand filters.
- **Clear Water Reservoir capacity:** 1.5 to 2 days of average daily demand.
- **Free board:** minimum 0.3 m above MWL in all open tanks.
- **Hydraulic gradient:** minimum 1:1000 on interconnecting conduits.
- **Bypass & parallel units:** mandatory for sedimentation and filtration.
- **Residual chlorine:** 0.2–0.5 mg/L after 30 min contact time (IS 10500:2012).
- **Plant capacity formula:** $Q_d = P \times q$ where $q$ = 135–150 Lpcd (India urban).
- **Sludge handling:** sludge from clarifiers → sludge digesters → drying beds → landfill.
- **Layout principle:** *gravity flow* from intake to CWR; *pumping only at raw water intake* and at *high-lift pump house* post-CWR.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** Module 1 typically carries **15–20%** of the full syllabus marks, with at least one 14-mark layout/numerical question per exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Site Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Site Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Site Selection** in Environmental Engineering refers to the systematic, multi-criteria engineering decision-making process of identifying and evaluating the most technically viable, economically optimal, socially acceptable, and environmentally sustainable geographic location for the construction of environmental infrastructure facilities — such as **intake structures**, **water treatment plants (WTPs)**, **sewage treatment plants (STPs)**, **sanitary landfills**, or **effluent disposal sites**.

As per KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCET503), the process integrates principles of **public health engineering**, **hydrology**, **environmental science**, and **urban planning** to ensure the chosen site delivers long-term serviceability, hydraulic efficiency, and minimal ecological disruption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly stresses that site selection is **NOT a single-parameter decision** — it is a weighted multi-criteria evaluation involving **technical, economic, environmental, and social factors**. Examiners frequently test whether students can list the factors AND justify their relative importance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House-Buying" Intuition

Imagine you are buying a house for your family. You wouldn't just pick the cheapest one — you would check:

- **Distance from work** (proximity to demand)
- **Water supply & drainage** (availability of utilities)
- **Flood-prone area or not** (geological/topographical safety)
- **Nearby polluting industry** (environmental safety)
- **Road connectivity** (accessibility)
- **Resale value** (long-term economy)

**Site selection for an environmental engineering project is exactly the same logic — applied to a treatment plant or intake well.** Just as you would not build a house in a swamp, an engineer should not place a water intake downstream of a sewage outfall.

### 1.3 Why Site Selection Matters

A poorly chosen site leads to:

| Consequence | Real-World Impact |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic inefficiency** | Excessive pumping head → higher energy bills |
| **Water quality deterioration** | Intake near pollution sources → costly treatment |
| **Structural failure** | Site on weak soil / floodplain → foundation collapse |
| **Social conflict** | Land acquisition disputes, NIMBY (Not-In-My-Back-Yard) issues |
| **Future expansion crisis** | Site too small → plant cannot be upgraded |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Key Insight:** Always mention the **"Three E's"** in any KTU answer on site selection:
> 1. **Engineering feasibility** (hydraulics, geology, topography)
> 2. **Economic viability** (capital + O&M cost)
> 3. **Environmental & social sustainability** (NEIAA compliance, public acceptance)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Site Selection Problems in Environmental Engineering

Site selection in Module 1 broadly covers **three infrastructure categories**:

1. **Source Selection (Intake Sites)** — for raw water abstraction from rivers, lakes, reservoirs
2. **Treatment Plant Sites** — for WTP / STP location within the city/town
3. **Disposal Sites** — for solid waste landfill or effluent outfall location

### 2.2 Factors Affecting Site Selection — The Comprehensive KTU Framework

#### A. Factors for Source/Intake Site Selection

1. **Quantity of water available** — Yield must exceed demand (with safety factor).
2. **Quality of water** — Prefer pristine zones; avoid downstream of polluting outfalls.
3. **Distance from the point of use** — Shorter conveyance reduces pumping cost & pipe leakage.
4. **Accessibility of the site** — Approach road for construction material, O&M vehicles, and chlorination staff.
5. **Geological stability of riverbed / lakebed** — Avoid silting zones and eroding banks.
6. **Hydraulic gradient** — Site must permit gravity flow across the treatment-train.
7. **Flood levels** — Intake must be above **HFL (Highest Flood Level)** of a 100-year return period.
8. **Navigation & fishing rights** — Avoid interference with existing waterway users.
9. **Future expansion** — Adjacent land must be available for additional units.

#### B. Factors for Treatment Plant Site Selection

1. **Location relative to the city** — Prefer **downstream** of the city (for WTP raw source) and **upstream** of the city (for STP outfall).
2. **Topography** — Gentle slope allows **gravity flow** through successive treatment units.
3. **Geotechnical suitability** — Bearing capacity > **100 kN/m²** preferred; avoid reclaimed/marshy land.
4. **Distance from the community** — Minimum **100–200 m buffer** to control odor and noise.
5. **Flood safety** — Plant finished floor level must be at least **0.5 m above HFL**.
6. **Availability of utilities** — Power, water for plant use, drainage for backwash.
7. **Land availability & cost** — Approximately **0.5–1.0 hectare per MLD** of treatment capacity (as a thumb rule).
8. **Environmental clearance** — Distance from ecologically sensitive zones (forests, wetlands).
9. **Approach road & connectivity** — All-weather road access.
10. **Sewer / receiving water availability** — For backwash and sludge disposal.

#### C. Factors for Disposal Site (Landfill / Outfall) Selection

1. Distance from habitation — Minimum **500 m to 1 km** (as per CPCB/MoEFCC norms).
2. **Hydrology & groundwater depth** — Water table must be at least **1.5–2 m below the base liner**.
3. **Geology** — Impervious clayey subsoil preferred for natural attenuation.
4. **Accessibility** — Haul distance for waste collection vehicles.
5. **Prevailing wind direction** — Landfill must lie **downwind** of the settlement.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Factor — Engineering Logic

| Factor | Why It Matters (Engineering Reason) |
|---|---|
| **Topography** | Gravity flow eliminates pumping cost (saves **30–50%** of O&M energy) |
| **Geology** | Weak soil → deep/raft foundations → 2× to 3× civil cost |
| **Flood level** | Submergence destroys electrical panels & aerators |
| **Distance from town** | Reduces conveyance cost (pipe cost ∝ √distance approximately) |
| **Buffer distance** | Controls odor complaints & litigation risk |
| **Land availability** | Future capacity expansion (population grows 1.5–2% per year) |
| **Utility access** | Plant cannot operate without continuous power & wash-water |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Criterion | Expression | Standard / Typical Value | Use in Site Selection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Area required for WTP** | $A = Q \times a$ | $a = 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \; \text{m}^2 / \text{MLD}$ | Land acquisition sizing |
| 2 | **Pipe cost (very approximate)** | $C_p \propto D^2 \cdot L$ | — | Distance penalty justification |
| 3 | **Pumping power** | $P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta}$ | $\eta = 0.70 \text{ to } 0.85$ | Justifies gravity flow preference |
| 4 | **Population forecast (arithmetic increase)** | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | $\bar{x} = $ decadal average increment | Future capacity planning |
| 5 | **Geometric increase method** | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | $r$ = growth rate % | Future demand for sizing site |
| 6 | **Buffer distance (WTP)** | $d_{buffer}$ | $\geq 100 \text{ m}$ (urban) | Site boundary clearance |
| 7 | **Buffer distance (landfill)** | $d_{landfill}$ | $\geq 500 \text{ m to 1 km}$ | Site boundary clearance |
| 8 | **Flood protection freeboard** | $FFL = HFL + FB$ | $FB = 0.5 \text{ m}$ minimum | Plant floor elevation |
| 9 | **Bearing capacity rule of thumb** | $q_u$ | $\geq 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$ | Foundation design feasibility |
| 10 | **Demand projection (per capita)** | $D_{total} = P_n \times q_{pc}$ | $q_{pc} = 135 \text{ LPCD (India)}$ | Site sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** For 14-mark Part-B questions, examiners expect you to **derive or justify** at least one of the above formulas. A simple definition list fetches only **8–9 marks**; combining it with a quantitative justification pushes you to **12–14 marks**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

The site selection framework is the **first deliverable** in any consultancy DPR (Detailed Project Report) submitted to the **Public Health Engineering Department (PHED)**, **Kerala Water Authority (KWA)**, or **Smart City Mission** projects. It precedes:

1. **Population forecasting** (to size the plant)
2. **Hydraulic design** (treatment-train layout)
3. **Cost estimation** (DPR financial closure)
4. **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** — mandatory for projects > 20 MLD or landfills > 5 TPD

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Area Required for a Water Treatment Plant

A common KTU problem statement:

> *"Design the area of land required for a WTP serving a town whose design population is $P_n$ and per capita water demand is $q$ LPCD. The plant operates for $t$ hours per day. Assume the specific land area coefficient is $a$ m²/MLD."*

**Step 1 — Convert population and demand to total daily volume**

$$
Q_{MLD} = \dfrac{P_n \times q \times 10^{-3}}{1000}
$$

where $P_n$ is in persons and $q$ is in LPCD (litres per capita per day).

Simplifying:

$$
Q_{MLD} = \dfrac{P_n \times q}{10^6} \;\; [\text{million litres per day}]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the specific land area coefficient**

$$
A_{required} = Q_{MLD} \times a
$$

**Step 3 — Add margin for future expansion (typically 33–50%)**

$$
A_{final} = A_{required} \times (1 + f)
$$

where $f = 0.33$ to $0.50$.

$$
A_{final} = Q_{MLD} \times a \times (1 + f)
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A KTU Board question (Dec 2023 pattern):
