> A town has a present population of **50,000**. The per capita water demand is **135 LPCD**. The growth rate observed over the last three decades is **30%, 25%, 22%** respectively. Using the **geometric increase method**, design for the year **2050** (base year 2020, so $n = 30$ years) and compute the land area required for the WTP at a specific land coefficient of **$a = 0.8$ m²/MLD**, with a future expansion margin of **33%**.

**Step 1 — Average growth rate**

$$
r_{avg} = \dfrac{30 + 25 + 22}{3} = 25.67 \; \% \approx 25.7\%
$$

**Step 2 — Projected population by geometric method**

$$
P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n
$$

$$
P_{2050} = 50{,}000 \times (1.257)^{30}
$$

Taking logarithms:

$$
\log P_{2050} = \log 50000 + 30 \times \log 1.257
$$

$$
\log P_{2050} = 4.6990 + 30 \times 0.0993
$$

$$
\log P_{2050} = 4.6990 + 2.9790 = 7.6780
$$

$$
P_{2050} = 10^{7.6780} = 4.77 \times 10^{7} \approx 4.77 \; \text{lakh persons}
$$

> **Reality check:** A 25% per decade growth is extremely high. In a KTU answer, you should also **comment** that such exponential forecasts are unrealistic for mature towns and suggest using **logistic / saturation curve** methods.

**Step 3 — Total daily water demand**

$$
Q_{MLD} = \dfrac{4.77 \times 10^5 \times 135}{10^6} = 64.4 \; \text{MLD}
$$

**Step 4 — Required land area**

$$
A_{required} = 64.4 \times 0.8 = 51.5 \; \text{m}^2
$$

> **⚠ Examiner Trap:** The unit here is **m²**, not hectares. Students commonly convert incorrectly.

Converting to hectares:

$$
A_{required} = \dfrac{51.5}{10{,}000} = 0.00515 \; \text{ha}
$$

**Step 5 — Add expansion margin**

$$
A_{final} = 51.5 \times 1.33 = 68.5 \; \text{m}^2 \approx 0.00685 \; \text{ha}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check vs typical WTP practice:** A 64 MLD plant in real engineering would need **8–10 acres** (3.2–4.0 ha). The figure 0.0068 ha from the formula is **unrealistic**, proving the formula $a = 0.8$ m²/MLD is meant only for **rough conceptual sizing** in a tutorial context. In an exam, you should state this limitation.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Site Selection Criteria Ranking

A frequent KTU Part-A question asks you to "rank the factors" or "compare river intake vs reservoir intake site selection."

| Criterion | River Intake | Lake/Reservoir Intake | Groundwater Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water quality variability | **High** (turbidity spikes in monsoon) | Low to Moderate | Generally stable |
| Treatment cost | **High** (coagulation, filtration) | Moderate | Low (often disinfection only) |
| Land acquisition difficulty | Moderate | High (submergence) | Low (borewell) |
| Seasonal yield reliability | **Low** (lean-season scarcity) | **High** (regulated release) | Moderate (overdraft risk) |
| Construction complexity | High (river training) | Moderate | Low |
| Ecological impact | High (river morphology) | High (reservoir) | Low to Moderate |
| Recommended for | Large cities on perennial rivers | Towns with seasonal rivers | Small villages, isolated buildings |

### 3.4 Decision Logic Flow for Site Selection (Analytical Steps)

The following structured logic should appear in any KTU 14-mark answer:

$$
\text{Step 1: Define the project scope} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{capacity, design life, future demand}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Shortlist candidate sites (3–5)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{desk study + GIS overlay}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Field reconnaissance} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{geotechnical, topographical, hydrological survey}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Multi-criteria weighted scoring} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{assign weights to technical/economic/social factors}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: Comparative cost analysis} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{civil + mechanical + electrical + O&M for 30 years}
$$

$$
\text{Step 6: Environmental & social impact screening} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{EIA, public hearing, CRZ clearance}
$$

$$
\text{Step 7: Final selection + statutory approvals} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{PHED, KWA, KSPCB, MoEFCC}

$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-yielding step:** KTU examiners award full marks only when the answer shows a **systematic, ranked justification** of factors, not just a bullet-list dump. Always include the "Why" and "How much weight" for at least 3–4 factors.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Site Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Need Identified] --> B[Step 1: Define Capacity & Demand Horizon]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify 3-5 Candidate Sites]
    C --> D[Step 3: Field Reconnaissance]
    D --> E[Step 4: Multi Criteria Weighted Scoring]
    E --> F{Score Threshold Met}
    F -- No --> G[Reject Site - Return to Step 2]
    F -- Yes --> H[Step 5: Comparative Cost Analysis 30 yr LCC]
    H --> I[Step 6: Environmental and Social Screening]
    I --> J{EIA and Public Clearance OK}
    J -- No --> G
    J -- Yes --> K[Step 7: Statutory Approvals PHED KSPCB MoEFCC]
    K --> L[Final Site Selected for Design and Construction]
    L --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Site Selection Inputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DATA LAYER]
        A1[Topographic Survey Data]
        A2[Geotechnical Soil Report]
        A3[Hydrological Yield Records]
        A4[Water Quality Test Results]
        A5[Land Use Land Cover Map]
        A6[Distance to Demand Centres]
        A7[Utility Availability Power Water]
    end
    subgraph PROC[PROCESSING AND EVALUATION LAYER]
        B1[Multi Criteria Weighted Matrix]
        B2[Life Cycle Cost Analysis]
        B3[Hydraulic Gradient Verification]
        B4[Environmental Impact Screening]
        B5[Social Acceptance Index]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT DECISION LAYER]
        C1[Ranked Shortlist of Sites]
        C2[Recommended Site with Justification]
        C3[Risk Register and Mitigation Plan]
        C4[Final Statutory Approval Dossier]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B4
    A6 --> B2
    A7 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B5 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Spatial Relationship of Plant to City

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RIVER[UPSTREAM DIRECTION]
        S1[Intake Source: River or Reservoir]
    end
    S1 -- Raw Water Rising Main --> WTP[Water Treatment Plant: WTP]
    WTP -- Clear Water Pumping Main --> CT[City Distribution Network]
    CT -- Used Water Sewer Network --> STP[Sewage Treatment Plant: STP]
    STP -- Treated Effluent --> RIVER2[Discharge to River Downstream of Intake]
    subgraph BUFFER[REQUIRED BUFFER DISTANCES]
        B1[100 to 200 m: WTP to Habitation]
        B2[500 m to 1 km: STP to Habitation]
        B3[STP must be DOWNSTREAM of WTP intake]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** Always include a labelled diagram in your 14-mark answer. Even a hand-drawn box-arrow sketch showing the **relative position of WTP, city, STP, and water body** can earn **1–2 extra marks** for visual communication.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> List any **six** important factors governing the **selection of a site for a Water Treatment Plant** in an urban area.

**Model Answer (6 factors × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Location downstream of the city** relative to the raw water source, to avoid contamination from municipal sewage.
2. **Adequate and stable land area** — typically **0.5 to 1.0 m² per MLD** of installed capacity, with **33% additional area** kept reserved for future expansion.
3. **Suitable topography** — gentle natural slope to enable **gravity flow** through the treatment units, reducing pumping energy.
4. **Flood-safe elevation** — finished floor level at least **0.5 m above the Highest Flood Level (HFL)** of the area.
5. **Sufficient bearing capacity of soil** — preferably **≥ 100 kN/m²** to permit standard isolated footings and avoid expensive pile foundations.
6. **Adequate buffer distance (≥ 100 m)** from residential habitation to control odor, noise, and public health complaints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note:** Any 6 correct factors with **one-line justification** each = full 3 marks. A bare list without justification fetches only **2 marks**.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Differentiate between the **site selection criteria for a surface water intake** and a **groundwater source (borewell)**. State two advantages of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Surface Water Intake | Groundwater Borewell |
|---|---|---|
| **Key criterion 1** | Site must be on a stable, deep section of the river with adequate depth in all seasons | Site must be in an aquifer recharge zone with **low water-table fluctuation** |
| **Key criterion 2** | U/s of city; away from industrial/municipal outfalls | Away from septic tanks, soak pits, landfills (**≥ 30 m**) |
| **Key criterion 3** | Approach road for heavy cranes and screens | Compact site; only electrical panel room needed |
| **Advantage 1** | Higher yield, can serve large cities | **Superior microbial quality**, often needs only chlorination |
| **Advantage 2** | Reliable for perennial rivers; can be regulated with a weir/barrage | **No treatment for turbidity**, no coagulation cost |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Enumerate, with justification, the **twelve most important factors** that govern the selection of a site for a **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** in a coastal town. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A satellite town of KTU district is to be provided with a water supply scheme. Present population is **80,000**; average decadal growth rates for the last three decades are **32%, 28%, 24%**. Per capita demand = **150 LPCD**. Using the **geometric increase method**, project the population for the design year **2055** (base year 2025, $n = 3$ decades). Determine the **land area required for the WTP** assuming a specific land coefficient of **1.0 m²/MLD** with **40% future expansion margin**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Twelve Factors for STP Site Selection (7 marks — 0.5 per factor with justification)**

1. **Location downstream of the city** with respect to the river — so that no sewage can ever contaminate the raw water intake. Justification: prevents recycle of pathogens and floating debris.
2. **Low-lying land** to allow gravity flow through screens, grit chamber, primary clarifier, aeration tank, and secondary clarifier. Justification: eliminates pumping cost between units.
3. **Flood-safe elevation** — finished floor level **0.5 m above HFL**. Justification: protects electrical blowers, diffusers, and SCADA panels from submersion.
4. **Sufficient bearing capacity** of soil (**≥ 100 kN/m²**) for tank foundations. Justification: tanks have heavy hydrostatic load; weak soil leads to differential settlement and cracks.
5. **Buffer distance of at least 500 m to 1 km** from the nearest habitation. Justification: STP emits H₂S, NH₃, and volatile organics causing odor complaints and litigation under **Water Act, 1974**.
6. **Adequate land area** — typically **0.4 to 0.8 ha per MLD**. Justification: sludge drying beds, staff quarters, and lab occupy significant area.
7. **Distance from the outfall point** — at least **1 km from any water intake point**. Justification: prevents microbial recycle and ensures dissolved oxygen recovery in the receiving water.
8. **Availability of receiving water body** (river/sea) with adequate assimilative capacity. Justification: CPCB requires BOD/flow ratio compliance before disposal.
9. **Geological stability** — away from reclaimed land, marshy zones, and seismic fault lines. Justification: structural safety of reinforced concrete tanks.
10. **Availability of power supply** for continuous aeration (24×7). Justification: facultative conditions in aeration tank if power fails cause bulking sludge and process failure.
11. **Approach road connectivity** for sludge and grit disposal trucks. Justification: O&M depends on reliable vehicle access in all seasons.
12. **Compliance with KSPCB/MoEFCC clearance** — distance from CRZ, wetlands, and forest boundary. Justification: statutory requirement; project halts without Environmental Clearance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (a):**
> - **Common loss (–1 mark):** Listing factors without the **"why"** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests justification, not recall.
> - **Common loss (–1 mark):** Confusing WTP factors with STP factors (e.g., stating *"upstream of city"* for STP — it is **downstream**).
> - **Common loss (–1 mark):** Omitting statutory clearances (KSPCB, CRZ) — this is a **favourite 2024-scheme question hook**.

---

**(b) Population Forecast + Land Area Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Average decadal growth rate**

$$
r = \dfrac{32 + 28 + 24}{3} = 28 \; \%  \text{ per decade}
$$

**Step 2 — Projected population for 2055 using geometric method**

$$
P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n
$$

$$
P_{2055} = 80{,}000 \times (1.28)^3
$$

Computing stepwise:

$$
(1.28)^2 = 1.6384
$$

$$
(1.28)^3 = 1.6384 \times 1.28 = 2.0972
$$

$$
P_{2055} = 80{,}000 \times 2.0972 = 167{,}776 \approx 1.68 \; \text{lakh persons}
$$

**Step 3 — Total water demand in MLD**

$$
Q_{MLD} = \dfrac{P_{2055} \times q_{pc}}{10^6} = \dfrac{167{,}776 \times 150}{10^6}
$$

$$
Q_{MLD} = 25.166 \approx 25.17 \; \text{MLD}
$$

**Step 4 — Required land area**

$$
A_{required} = Q_{MLD} \times a = 25.17 \times 1.0 = 25.17 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Add 40% expansion margin**

$$
A_{final} = 25.17 \times 1.40 = 35.24 \; \text{m}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> - Using the **arithmetic method** when the question states **geometric method** — instant loss of 2 marks.
> - Forgetting to convert population × LPCD into MLD (i.e., dividing by 10⁶) — common arithmetic error.
> - Adding the expansion margin to the **wrong base value** (always add to the **initial required area**, not to the demand).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Compare and justify the **suitability of a river intake, a reservoir intake, and a lake intake** as water supply sources for a fast-growing town of 2 lakh population. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain, with a **neat sketch**, the **general layout of an intake well** on a river. Label its components and state the **design criteria for the bell-mouth opening**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Source Suitability (7 marks — table + 1-line justification each)**

| Feature | River Intake | Reservoir Intake | Lake Intake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yield reliability | Season-dependent | **Highly reliable** (regulated) | Moderate |
| Water quality | Turbidity, BOD, coliform spikes | Stratification issues, algal blooms | Generally good |
| Treatment cost | **High** | Moderate | Low to moderate |
| Civil cost | Moderate | High (dam) | Low |
| Best suited for | Large perennial rivers | Seasonal rivers, urban supply | Small communities |

**Justification paragraph (1 mark):**
For a 2-lakh population requiring **~30 MLD**, a **reservoir intake** is optimal because it guarantees **regulated yield even in lean season**, permits gravity flow to the WTP, and the catchment can be protected against upstream pollution through administrative zoning. A river intake would expose the town to **monsoon turbidity shocks** and **lean-season scarcity**, increasing both treatment and pumping cost.

---

**(b) Intake Well Layout + Bell-Mouth Design (7 marks)**

**Sketch description (textual):**

```
        River Water
    ─────────────────► (flow direction)
            │
   ┌────────┴────────┐
   │   SCREEN BAR    │  ← Coarse trash rack (vertical bars, 50–100 mm spacing)
   │  (perforated)   │
   └────────┬────────┘
            │
   ┌────────┴────────┐
   │   BELL MOUTH    │  ← Smoothly tapered entry, 8° to 15° flare
   │   (entry)       │     Velocity at entry ≤ 0.6 m/s to avoid fish entrainment
   └────────┬────────┘
            │
   ┌────────┴────────┐
   │  INTAKE PIPE /  │  ← Connects to intake well (caisson)
   │   APPROACH      │
   └────────┬────────┘
            │
   ┌────────┴────────┐
   │  INTAKE WELL    │  ← Cylindrical RCC caisson, wet well
   │   (wet well)    │     Min. 2 compartments for maintenance isolation
   └────────┬────────┘
            │
   ┌────────┴────────┐
   │   FINE SCREENS  │  ← Removable mesh, 6–10 mm aperture
   │   (mech.)       │
   └────────┬────────┘
            │
   ┌────────┴────────┐
   │   PUMP HOUSE    │  ← Submersible / vertical turbine pumps
   │   (dry well)    │     Pumps raw water to WTP via rising main
   └─────────────────┘
```

**Design criteria for bell-mouth opening (1 mark each, 3 marks):**

1. **Entry velocity ≤ 0.6 m/s** — to prevent fish entrainment and minimize head loss.
2. **Flare angle 8° to 15°** — for smooth, swirl-free entry and uniform velocity distribution.
3. **Submergence depth** — bell-mouth center must be at least **1.0–1.5 m below LWL (Lowest Water Level)** to prevent vortex formation and air entrainment.
4. **Two-bay design** — to permit isolation of one bay for desilting/grit removal while the other continues operation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Site selection is multi-criteria**, not a single-parameter decision — it must integrate **engineering, economic, environmental, and social** dimensions.
- **Three prime categories** exist: **(i) Source/Intake site, (ii) Treatment plant site, (iii) Disposal site** — each has its own factor matrix.
- **WTP must be located downstream of the city** (with respect to the river) and **upstream of the STP outfall**, so that raw water and treated sewage never meet.
- **STP must be located downstream of the WTP intake** by a minimum of **1 km** to prevent microbial recycle.
- **Topography** is the single most important site factor — **gentle slope enables gravity flow**, saving up to **30–50% of O&M energy** through eliminated pumping.
- **Flood safety:** Finished floor level of any treatment unit must be at least **0.5 m above HFL**.
- **Buffer distances:** WTP ≥ **100–200 m** from habitation; STP ≥ **500 m–1 km**; Landfill ≥ **1 km**.
- **Land area rule of thumb:** **0.5–1.0 m² per MLD** (conceptual) — always add a **33–50% future expansion margin**.
- **Bearing capacity ≥ 100 kN/m²** is the minimum acceptable soil criterion for tank foundations.
- **Three E's to always mention** in KTU answers: **Engineering, Economic, Environmental/Social**.
- **Always include a sketch** in 14-mark answers — a labelled box-arrow diagram showing WTP–city–STP–river relationship fetches **1–2 visual-communication marks**.
- **Statutory clearances** (KSPCB, MoEFCC, CRZ) are **mandatory in the KTU 2024 scheme** — omitting them is a guaranteed mark-loser.
- **Numerical conversion traps:** LPCD × population ÷ 10⁶ = MLD; m² ÷ 10,000 = hectares.
- **For population projection**, the **geometric increase method** is preferred over arithmetic for towns with growth rate **> 5% per decade** — but comment on the **limitations** of extrapolation.
- **Decision logic** must be presented in **7 systematic steps** for full marks: define scope → shortlist → field survey → multi-criteria scoring → cost analysis → EIA → statutory approval.
- **Unit consistency** — examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark for mixed units (e.g., writing land area in m² when context requires hectares).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intake Structure (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An **intake** is a civil engineering structure constructed on or within a surface water source (river, lake, reservoir, or canal) to safely withdraw raw water, control its quality, regulate its flow, and deliver it to the conveyance system (pumping main or gravity conduit) leading to the water treatment plant.

The intake is the **first component** of any municipal water supply scheme that draws water from a natural surface source. It is the interface between the natural water body and the engineered water supply network.

### 1.2 Classification of Intakes (As Per KTU Syllabus)

| S.No | Classification Basis | Type of Intake |
|------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Position in water source | **Submerged Intake** / **Wet Intake** |
| 2 | Source characteristics | **River Intake** / **Lake Intake** / **Reservoir Intake** / **Canal Intake** |
| 3 | Structural form | **Simple Intake** / **Intake Well** / **Intake Tower** |
| 4 | Hydrological condition | **Gravity Intake** / **Pumped Intake** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — The "Water Tap of a City":**
> Imagine your home's water tap connected to a municipal pipe. The tap is a small control device that lets you draw water on demand. Now, scale this concept up massively — instead of a ½-inch pipe, you are drawing thousands of liters per minute from a river. Instead of a small valve, you need a robust concrete structure. That structure is the **intake**. Just as the tap:
> 1. Screens out large debris (the tap has a small mesh strainer),
> 2. Controls the flow rate (the tap has a valve),
> 3. Connects to a delivery pipe (the riser pipe),
> So does an intake, but at a civil engineering scale with screens, gates, and pump wells.

### 1.4 Why Intakes Are Necessary

- **Quality control**: Allows water to be drawn from the **best stratum** (typically below the surface scum but above the bottom sediment).
- **Quantity assurance**: Permits withdrawal of required quantity even when source water level fluctuates seasonally.
- **System reliability**: Provides a controlled entry point with isolation valves for maintenance.
- **Protection**: Prevents entry of floating debris, fish, and large suspended objects via screens.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Reminder:**
> - The design velocity at the intake entry is limited to **0.6 m/s to 1.2 m/s** to prevent sediment entrainment and excessive head loss.
> - The intake is generally designed to draw water from a depth of **0.5 m to 1.0 m below the minimum water level (MWL)** of the source.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Essential Components of an Intake Structure

A fully designed intake comprises the following functional elements:

1. **Bar Screen (Trash Rack / Coarse Screen)**
   - Function: Removes large floating matter (logs, branches, fish, plastics).
   - Bar spacing: **20 mm to 100 mm** (coarse) depending on downstream pump capacity.
   - Bars are typically **flat steel sections** of size 10 mm × 50 mm or larger.

2. **Fine Screen / Mesh Screen**
   - Function: Removes finer suspended matter.
   - Aperture: **5 mm to 10 mm**.
   - Always placed downstream of the bar screen.

3. **Sump / Wet Well / Pump Well**
   - Function: Temporarily stores water before pumping.
   - Provides adequate submergence to the suction pipes of pumps.
   - Designed for a retention time of **2 to 5 minutes** (to allow sediment settling).

4. **Gates / Stop Logs**
   - Function: Allow isolation of the intake for inspection and maintenance.
   - Provided at the entry opening, screen, and outlet pipe.

5. **Riser Pipes / Conduits**
   - Function: Convey water from the intake to the treatment plant.
   - Designed for self-cleansing velocity (≥ **0.6 m/s**) to prevent silt deposition.

6. **Ventilation and Lighting Arrangements**
   - Function: Provide aeration and access to interior for inspection.

7. **Cathodic Protection / Coatings**
   - Function: Prevent corrosion of submerged metallic parts.

### 2.2 Site Selection Criteria for Intakes (High-Yield KTU Point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Enumerate the factors governing the selection of site for an intake."*

The following conditions must be satisfied:

- The site must be located **upstream** of the city (to prevent contamination from city sewage).
- It must be at a location where the river is **deep, straight, and has stable banks**.
- The bed and banks should be free from **heavy scouring or silting**.
- The site should have **firm, rocky, or impervious strata** for foundation stability.
- It must ensure **ample supply throughout the year**, even during dry seasons.
- It should be **accessible** for operation, maintenance, and construction.
- It must be **above the headworks** of any downstream obstruction (e.g., weirs).
- The site should be **away from bends, confluences, and mouths of tributaries** (to avoid turbulence and sediment).
- Pollution sources (such as sewage outfalls) must be located **downstream** of the intake.

### 2.3 Design Considerations — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Estimation of Quantity**
The intake must be designed to withdraw the **maximum daily demand** (MDD) of the town, which is typically **1.8 × Average Daily Demand (ADD)**.

$$Q_{design} = 1.8 \times Q_{avg} \quad \text{(in m}^3\text{/day)}$$

**Step 2: Determination of Pipe Diameter**
Using **Manning's equation** or the discharge formula:

$$Q = A \times V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times V$$

Solving for the diameter:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = design discharge in m³/s
- $V$ = velocity of flow (0.6 to 1.2 m/s)
- $D$ = diameter in meters

**Step 3: Area of Screen Openings**
The effective open area must be sufficient to keep the approach velocity low:

$$A_{screen} = \frac{Q}{V_{screen}}$$

Where $V_{screen}$ is limited to **0.6 m/s to 1.2 m/s**.

**Step 4: Sump / Wet Well Volume**
The sump volume is computed based on pump operation cycle:

$$V_{sump} = Q_{pump} \times t_{cycle} \times \frac{1}{4}$$

Where $t_{cycle}$ is the pump cycle time (typically 5 to 30 minutes).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|------|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $Q = A \times V$ | Discharge = Area × Velocity | m³/s |
| 2 | $D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V}}$ | Diameter of intake pipe | m |
| 3 | $A_{screen} = \frac{Q}{V_{screen}}$ | Net screen area required | m² |
| 4 | $h_{L} = K \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Head loss through screen (K = 0.5 to 1.0) | m |
| 5 | $N = \frac{b+s}{s} = \frac{\text{bar pitch}}{\text{spacing}}$ | Number of bars in screen | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_{self-cleansing} \geq 0.6$ m/s | Minimum velocity to avoid siltation | m/s |
| 7 | $Q_{design} = 1.8 \times Q_{avg}$ | Design discharge (1.8 × ADD) | m³/day |
| 8 | $V_{sump} = \text{pump capacity} \times t_{cycle} \times \frac{1}{4}$ | Wet well volume | m³ |
| 9 | $R = \frac{A}{P}$ | Hydraulic radius of pipe | m |
| 10 | $S = \left(\frac{V \cdot n}{R^{2/3}}\right)^2$ | Slope required to maintain velocity | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students use $\vert$ symbols in formula tables. Use `\vert` in LaTeX or simply words like "absolute" / "magnitude of" to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Intake structures are vital in:

- **Municipal water supply schemes** for cities drawing water from rivers or reservoirs.
- **Hydroelectric power plants** (where intakes feed penstocks to turbines).
- **Industrial cooling water systems** (thermal power stations).
- **Irrigation canal off-takes** in large dam projects.
- **Desalination plants** drawing seawater from coastal intakes.

The structural form, materials (RCC, steel, masonry), and depth of submergence vary depending on source characteristics, but the underlying hydraulics remains the same — **control quality, regulate quantity, and protect downstream equipment**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Diameter of the Intake Pipe

**Given:**
- Design discharge $Q = 0.5$ m³/s
- Permissible velocity $V = 1.0$ m/s

**Step 1: Recall the fundamental continuity equation**

$$Q = A \times V$$

The cross-sectional area of a circular pipe is:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the discharge equation**

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times V$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to solve for D**

$$\frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{Q}{V}$$

$$D^2 = \frac{4Q}{\pi V}$$

**Step 4: Take the square root**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the numerical values**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.5}{\pi \times 1.0}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{2.0}{3.1416}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{0.6366}$$

$$D = 0.7979 \text{ m} \approx 0.80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Round up to the nearest standard commercial size**

Therefore, use a **standard 800 mm (0.80 m)** diameter pipe. This is a common KTU 2-mark and 3-mark sub-question.

### 3.2 Derivation: Net Area Required for the Bar Screen

**Given:**
- Discharge $Q = 0.5$ m³/s
- Permissible approach velocity $V_{screen} = 0.6$ m/s

**Step 1: Apply the discharge equation to the screen**

$$Q = A_{net} \times V_{screen}$$

**Step 2: Solve for the net open area**

$$A_{net} = \frac{Q}{V_{screen}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values**

$$A_{net} = \frac{0.5}{0.6}$$

$$A_{net} = 0.8333 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Account for the gross screen area (bars occupy space too)**

If the bar efficiency is 70% (typical for coarse screens):

$$A_{gross} = \frac{A_{net}}{0.70} = \frac{0.8333}{0.70} = 1.190 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Determine screen dimensions**

If the screen is rectangular with width 1.5 m, then:

$$L_{screen} = \frac{A_{gross}}{\text{width}} = \frac{1.190}{1.5} = 0.793 \text{ m} \approx 0.80 \text{ m}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Head Loss Across the Bar Screen

**Formula (Kirschmer's Equation, simplified):**

$$h_L = \beta \cdot \left(\frac{s}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} \cdot \sin \theta$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = bar shape factor (2.42 for sharp-edged rectangular bars, 1.83 for rectangular bars with semicircular upstream face, 1.0 for circular bars)
- $s$ = bar width
- $b$ = clear spacing between bars
- $V$ = approach velocity
- $\theta$ = angle of bars with horizontal (typically 60°)

**Numerical Example:**

**Given:**
- Bar width $s = 10$ mm = 0.01 m
- Clear spacing $b = 50$ mm = 0.05 m
- Velocity $V = 0.6$ m/s
- $\beta = 1.83$ (rectangular bars with semicircular face)
- $\theta = 60°$

**Step 1: Compute the bar geometry ratio**

$$\frac{s}{b} = \frac{0.01}{0.05} = 0.2$$

**Step 2: Compute the bar shape effect**

$$\left(\frac{s}{b}\right)^{4/3} = (0.2)^{4/3} = (0.2)^{1.3333}$$

Using logarithms:

$$\log(0.2)^{1.3333} = 1.3333 \times \log(0.2) = 1.3333 \times (-0.6990) = -0.9320$$

$$(0.2)^{4/3} = 10^{-0.9320} = 0.1169$$

**Step 3: Compute the velocity head**

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.6)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.36}{19.62} = 0.01835 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compute the sine of the angle**

$$\sin(60°) = 0.8660$$

**Step 5: Combine all terms**

$$h_L = 1.83 \times 0.1169 \times 0.01835 \times 0.8660$$

$$h_L = 1.83 \times 0.1169 \times 0.01589$$

$$h_L = 1.83 \times 0.001858$$

$$h_L = 0.00340 \text{ m} = 3.40 \text{ mm}$$

This is the **head loss** across the bar screen — a very small but significant value for design verification.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Intake Types

| Parameter | Submerged (Wet) Intake | Intake Tower (Wet) | Simple River Intake | Canal Intake |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Source** | River / Lake with fluctuating levels | Reservoir with great depth variation | River with stable banks and water level | Irrigation canal |
| **Construction** | Built underwater, no dry access | Built as vertical tower with multi-port inlets | Built on banks extending into river | Built into canal masonry |
| **Dry Maintenance** | Difficult (requires divers) | Possible (with stop logs) | Easy (accessible from land) | Easy |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Highest | Lowest | Low |
| **Use Case** | Deep rivers, deep reservoirs | Large dams and reservoirs | Shallow rivers, small towns | Small towns along canals |
| **Drawback** | Hard to inspect | Expensive | Limited to specific conditions | Limited flow control |
| **Multi-level Drawoff** | Difficult (single entry) | Easy (ports at various levels) | Difficult | Difficult |

### 3.5 Numerical Practice: Sump Volume Calculation

**Given:**
- Pump capacity = 90 m³/hour
- Cycle time = 10 minutes

**Step 1: Convert pump capacity to m³/min**

$$Q_{pump} = \frac{90}{60} = 1.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 2: Compute the water delivered per cycle**

$$V_{per \, cycle} = Q_{pump} \times t_{cycle} = 1.5 \times 10 = 15 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Apply the wet well design factor (1/4 of cycle volume)**

$$V_{sump} = \frac{1}{4} \times 15 = 3.75 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Choose sump dimensions**
For a square sump of side $L$ and depth $H$:

$$L \times B \times H = 3.75 \text{ m}^3$$

If $L = B = 1.5$ m, then:

$$H = \frac{3.75}{1.5 \times 1.5} = \frac{3.75}{2.25} = 1.67 \text{ m}$$

Use a sump of **1.5 m × 1.5 m × 1.7 m**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Intake Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[River or Reservoir Source] --> B[Bar Screen Coarse Screening 20 to 100 mm]
    B --> C[Fine Screen Mesh 5 to 10 mm Aperture]
    C --> D[Stop Logs and Gates for Isolation]
    D --> E[Sump or Wet Well Storage 2 to 5 min Retention]
    E --> F[Pump Suction Chamber]
    F --> G[Pump House Centrifugal Pumps]
    G --> H[Rising Main to Treatment Plant]
    E --> I[Overflow and Ventilation Pipe]
    E --> J[Drain Sump for Cleaning]
    H --> K[Water Treatment Plant Coagulation Filtration Disinfection]
    K --> L[Clear Water Storage]
    L --> M[Distribution Network to City]
    subgraph AuxiliarySystems [Auxiliary Systems]
        I
        J
    end
    subgraph FlowPath [Main Flow Path]
        A
        B
        C
        D
        E
        F
        G
        H
    end
    subgraph TreatmentAndSupply [Treatment and Supply]
        K
        L
        M
    end
```

### 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Cross-Section of a Typical Intake Tower

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AboveWater [Above Water Level]
        AT[Top Deck with Access Staircase]
        OP[Operator Room and Control Panel]
    end
    subgraph AtWater [At Water Level Zone]
        UP[Upper Port Inlet High Flood Level]
        MP[Middle Port Inlet Normal Level]
        LP[Lower Port Inlet Dry Season]
    end
    subgraph BelowWater [Below Water Level]
        BS1[Bar Screen at Port Inlet]
        RP[Riser Pipe Vertical Conduit]
        SUMP[Sump or Wet Well at Base]
        PS[Pump Suction Pipe]
    end
    subgraph Foundation [Foundation Zone]
        FND[Pile or Raft Foundation on Rock]
    end
    AT --> UP
    AT --> MP
    AT --> LP
    UP --> BS1
    MP --> BS1
    LP --> BS1
    BS1 --> RP
    RP --> SUMP
    SUMP --> PS
    PS --> FND
    OP -.Controls.-> PS
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Selection of Intake Type

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[What is the source type?] --> R{River with stable level}
    Q1 --> L{Lake or reservoir with depth variation}
    Q1 --> C{Canal off take}
    R -->|Yes| SI[Simple River Intake on bank]
    R -->|No| FL[Check for fluctuating level]
    FL -->|Fluctuates| SUB[Submerged Wet Intake]
    L -->|Yes| IT[Intake Tower with multi level ports]
    C -->|Yes| CI[Canal Off Take with Masonry Headwall]
    SI --> DONE[Selected Intake Type]
    SUB --> DONE
    IT --> DONE
    CI --> DONE
```

### 4.4 System-Level Architecture Map — Intake to Consumer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Intake Structure] --> P2[Raw Water Pumping Station]
    P2 --> P3[Water Treatment Plant]
    P3 --> P4[Clear Water Reservoir]
    P4 --> P5[Booster Pumping Station]
    P5 --> P6[Overhead Tank ESR]
    P6 --> P7[Distribution Network]
    P7 --> P8[Domestic Consumer]
    P7 --> P9[Industrial Consumer]
    P7 --> P10[Commercial Consumer]
```

### 4.5 Design Data Summary Table (Visible at a Glance)

| Component | Design Velocity | Spacing or Aperture | Material |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|----------|
| Coarse Bar Screen | 0.6 to 0.9 m/s | 20 to 100 mm clear spacing | Mild steel flats |
| Fine Mesh Screen | 0.6 to 1.2 m/s | 5 to 10 mm | Galvanized wire mesh |
| Intake Riser Pipe | ≥ 0.6 m/s (self-cleansing) | Diameter computed from Q and V | RCC or MS |
| Sump / Wet Well | 2 to 5 min retention | Storage volume = Q × t / 4 | RCC |
| Pump Suction Pipe | 0.6 to 1.5 m/s | Diameter from Q and V | MS or DI |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define an intake. List the essential requirements of a good intake structure.**

**Model Answer:**

An **intake** is a hydraulic structure constructed on a surface water source to draw raw water and convey it to the water treatment plant.

**Essential requirements of a good intake:**

1. It should be located at a position that ensures **good quality water** (above pollution sources, free from turbulence).
2. It should draw water from a **sufficient depth** to avoid floating debris and bottom sediment.
3. It must be capable of drawing the **maximum required quantity** even during dry seasons.
4. It should be **economical**, durable, and easy to construct.
5. It should permit **easy maintenance** with isolation facilities (gates, stop logs).
6. It must remain **operational under all flood and drought conditions**.

*(Answer length: 3–4 sentences, 80–100 words — fits within 3-mark answer space.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What are the factors governing the selection of site for a river intake?**

**Model Answer:**

The following factors govern site selection:

1. **Location upstream** of the city to avoid sewage contamination.
2. **Stable, deep, and straight reach** of the river with firm banks.
3. **Year-round availability** of adequate water (sufficient submergence during dry season).
4. **Firm and impervious foundation** strata (rock or stiff clay preferred).
5. **Accessibility** for construction, operation, and maintenance.
6. **Free from scouring, silting, and flood damage**.
7. **Away from bends, confluences, and tributaries** to avoid sediment and turbulence.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, typically divided into **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Either Q1A or Q1B is to be attempted.

---

### **Question 1A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **classification of intakes** based on their position, source, and structural form. Draw suitable sketches.

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Intakes:**

**(I) Based on Position:**

1. **Submerged Intake (Wet Intake):**
   - Constructed under the water surface, no part visible above water.
   - Used in deep rivers or reservoirs.
   - Difficult to maintain (requires divers).

2. **Exposed Intake (Dry Intake):**
   - Has a portion visible above the water surface (e.g., intake tower).
   - Easier to access for maintenance.
   - Used in reservoirs with large depth variation.

**(II) Based on Source:**

| Source Type | Suitable Intake Form |
|-------------|----------------------|
| River (shallow, stable) | Simple River Intake on bank |
| River (deep, fluctuating) | Submerged Intake with crib structure |
| Lake / Reservoir | Intake Tower with multiple port inlets |
| Canal | Canal Off-Take with masonry head regulator |

**(III) Based on Structural Form:**

1. **Simple Intake** — A masonry/concrete block on the river bank, with a coarse screen and a single inlet pipe.
2. **Crib Intake** — A wooden or RCC crib is sunk at the river bed, with a riser pipe and screen at the top.
3. **Intake Well (Jack Well)** — A large circular or rectangular well sunk near the river bank, connected to the source via a pipe.
4. **Intake Tower** — A tall vertical tower in a reservoir with multiple port inlets at different elevations; best for deep reservoirs.

**Sketch (verbal description for answer sheet):**
- Show a simple intake: river bank → coarse screen → sump → pump → rising main.
- Show an intake tower: water levels (HFL, NWL, LWL) → ports at each level → screens → riser pipe → wet well → pumps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A town draws **50,000 m³/day** of water from a river. Design the **diameter of the intake pipe** assuming a permissible velocity of **1.0 m/s**. Also calculate the **net screen area** required if the permissible approach velocity through the screen is **0.6 m/s**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$
- $V_{pipe} = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$
- $V_{screen} = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1: Convert Q to m³/s**

$$Q = \frac{50{,}000}{24 \times 3600} = \frac{50{,}000}{86{,}400} = 0.5787 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Compute pipe diameter**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V_{pipe}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.5787}{\pi \times 1.0}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{2.3148}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{0.7368} = 0.8584 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Round up to commercial size**

Use **D = 900 mm (0.90 m)** standard pipe.

**Step 4: Compute net screen area**

$$A_{screen} = \frac{Q}{V_{screen}} = \frac{0.5787}{0.6} = 0.9645 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Add 30% for bar area obstruction**

$$A_{gross} = \frac{0.9645}{0.70} = 1.378 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answer:**
- Pipe diameter = **0.90 m (900 mm)**
- Net screen area = **0.965 m²**
- Gross screen area = **1.38 m²**

---

### **Question 1B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **site selection criteria** for an intake with reference to:
(i) Quality of water
(ii) Quantity of water
(iii) Structural stability

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Quality of Water:**

- The intake must be located **upstream of the city** to avoid contamination from urban sewage and industrial waste.
- It should be at a point where the water is **free from stagnation**, excessive algal growth, and floating debris.
- The site should be **away from the confluence of tributaries** carrying silt or pollutants.
- Depth of withdrawal should be such that the best quality water (middle stratum) is drawn — avoiding both surface scum and bottom sediment.

**(ii) Quantity of Water:**

- The site must ensure **year-round availability** of the required quantity.
- The water level should remain **above the intake inlet** even during the **lean (dry) season**.
- The river reach should have **adequate cross-section** to supply the required discharge.
- A **reservoir-based intake** should be located in the deepest part of the impoundment for maximum submergence.

**(iii) Structural Stability:**

- The foundation must rest on **firm, impervious strata** (rock, hard clay, or compacted soil) to prevent settlement.
- The site should be **away from active meander bends** and zones of **scouring**.
- Banks should be **stable** and protected against erosion.
- The location should be **safe from flood damage** and free from subsoil water pressure issues.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **head loss** across a bar screen with the following data:
- Bar width = 10 mm
- Clear spacing between bars = 50 mm
- Approach velocity = 0.6 m/s
- Bar shape factor β = 1.83 (rectangular bars with semicircular upstream face)
- Angle of bars with horizontal = 60°

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $s = 0.01$ m, $b = 0.05$ m, $V = 0.6$ m/s, $\beta = 1.83$, $\theta = 60°$

**Step 1: Apply Kirschmer's equation**

$$h_L = \beta \cdot \left(\frac{s}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} \cdot \sin \theta$$

**Step 2: Compute bar geometry term**

$$\left(\frac{s}{b}\right)^{4/3} = \left(\frac{0.01}{0.05}\right)^{4/3} = (0.2)^{4/3} = 0.1169$$

**Step 3: Compute velocity head**

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.6)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.36}{19.62} = 0.01835 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compute sin θ**

$$\sin(60°) = 0.8660$$

**Step 5: Substitute all values**

$$h_L = 1.83 \times 0.1169 \times 0.01835 \times 0.8660$$

$$h_L = 3.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.40 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** The head loss across the bar screen = **3.40 mm of water column**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget to convert mm to m** when computing the bar ratio (s/b). Always work in SI units throughout the derivation.
> - The bar shape factor β is **not the same** for all bar shapes. Use 2.42 for sharp-edged rectangular bars, 1.83 for rounded-edge bars, and 1.0 for circular bars. Reading the question carefully and selecting the correct β is critical.
> - Do **not use vertical pipes (\vert)** inside formula tables — they break the markdown structure. Use `\vert` or simply write "magnitude of" instead.
> - Many students lose marks by **not drawing neat labelled sketches** in design questions. Always include a schematic diagram of the intake even if a Mermaid block is not permitted in the answer sheet — draw it manually and label all components.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Intake Definition:** Structure to withdraw surface water → convey to treatment plant.
- **Two Main Categories:** Submerged (wet) and Exposed (dry / tower) intakes.
- **Site Selection — Top 5 Points:**
  (i) Upstream of city
  (ii) Firm foundation
  (iii) Deep and straight river reach
  (iv) Year-round water availability
  (v) Accessible for O&M
- **Components:** Bar screen → Fine screen → Gates → Sump → Pump → Rising main.
- **Design Velocity Limits:** 0.6 m/s (min, self-cleansing) to 1.2 m/s (max, head loss limit).
- **Pipe Diameter Formula:** $D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V}}$ — use commercial size, round UP.
- **Screen Area Formula:** $A_{screen} = \frac{Q}{V_{screen}}$ — multiply by 1/0.7 for gross area.
- **Head Loss Formula (Kirschmer):** $h_L = \beta \cdot \left(\frac{s}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} \cdot \sin\theta$.
- **Intake Tower Advantage:** Multi-level port inlets → allows selection of best water quality stratum → best for deep reservoirs.
- **Submerged Intake Limitation:** No dry access → maintenance requires divers → costlier inspection.
- **Design Discharge Rule:** Use **1.8 × Average Daily Demand** for intake design (MDD basis).
- **Standard Depth of Draw:** 0.5 m to 1.0 m below the Minimum Water Level (MWL).
- **Sump Volume Rule:** $\frac{1}{4} \times$ (pump capacity × cycle time) → 2 to 5 min retention.
- **Common KTU Question Types:** "Enumerate site selection criteria" (3 marks), "Classify intakes with sketches" (7 marks), "Design pipe diameter and screen area" (7 marks), "Compute head loss using Kirschmer's equation" (7 marks).
- **Examiner's Trap:** Using the **maximum daily demand** in formulas without the 1.8 factor explicitly written; forgetting to **add 30% gross area** for screen obstruction; using **wrong bar shape factor** β.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:**
> Always define the term, list the types/classification, write the design formulas with units, substitute values with unit conversions, and present the final answer with appropriate rounding and a neat labelled diagram. This structure is what KTU examiners award full marks for.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Screening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screening** is the unit operation in preliminary wastewater treatment that involves the **physical removal of large floating, suspended, and entrained solids** (such as rags, sticks, plastics, leaves, and other coarse debris) from the influent sewage by passing it through a **perforated or slotted device** known as a *screen*.

The device used is called a **screen**, and the retained material on the screen is termed **screenings**. The process is purely **physical (mechanical)** and constitutes the *first* treatment step at almost every municipal sewage treatment plant (STP).

According to the **CPHEEO Manual on Sewerage and Sewage Treatment (2013)** and standard KTU prescribed references, screening is classified as a **preliminary treatment process** that protects:
- **Pumps** from clogging and abrasion
- **Pipes, valves, and downstream units** from fouling
- **Biological treatment units** (like activated sludge / trickling filters) from heavy solids loading
- **Disinfection units** (chlorinators) from interference

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of screening like a kitchen sink strainer.** When you wash rice or vegetables, the strainer (screen) holds back large unwanted particles (rice husks, leaves) while letting the water (effluent) flow through. The same logic applies in a sewage treatment plant — except the "rice" is now real waste like plastics, rags, and sticks, and the strainer is a much larger industrial-grade metal grid.

A student reading this for the first time should understand: **Screening is the very first "defensive line" of a treatment plant** — it is **not** designed to remove dissolved pollutants, fine silt, or organic matter. Its sole job is to remove **visible, large, non-biodegradable solids** that would otherwise damage the rest of the plant.

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metric

The classification of screens is based on the **size of the openings (clear spacing between bars)**, as tabulated below:

| Screen Type | Opening Size (mm) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Coarse Screens** | $\geq 50$ mm | Protection of pumps in deep sewers |
| **Medium Screens** | $6 - 50$ mm | Standard municipal STP inlet |
| **Fine Screens** | $0.25 - 6$ mm | Pre-treatment before biological units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The most common opening size in Indian municipal STPs is **$\mathbf{20\text{ mm}}$**, classified as a *medium screen*, used to protect the downstream grit chamber and primary clarifier.

The standard unit of screen measurement is **millimetres (mm)** for opening size and **metres per second (m/s)** for approach velocity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Working Logic of a Screening Unit

1. **Influent Entry:** Raw sewage enters the screen chamber with an **approach velocity** ($v_a$) typically between **$0.6 - 1.2 \text{ m/s}$** to prevent grit settling in the channel itself.
2. **Flow Through Bars:** Sewage is forced to pass between parallel bars (or through perforations). The clear spacing ($b$) between bars governs what gets caught.
3. **Capture of Solids:** Particles larger than the spacing ($b$) are retained on the upstream face of the screen — these are the **screenings**.
4. **Cleaned Effluent:** The sewage that passes through, now largely free of coarse solids, flows onward to the **grit chamber** (next unit operation).
5. **Cleaning of Screen:** Screenings are removed either **manually** (small plants) or by **mechanically raked rakes / brushes** (large plants) to prevent clogging.
6. **Disposal of Screenings:** Collected screenings are buried in landfills, incinerated, or composted (rare for municipal STP screenings due to hygiene concerns).

## 2.2 Why is Screening Required? (The "Why" Behind the Process)

- **Protects Pumps:** Pumps (especially centrifugal pumps) are highly susceptible to clogging by rags and plastics.
- **Reduces BOD Loading Marginally:** Although primarily a *physical* process, it removes some organic solids, slightly reducing the BOD load.
- **Prevents Floating Scum Issues:** Rags and plastics can form scum blankets in primary clarifiers, reducing efficiency.
- **Reduces Sludge Volume:** Removes bulky non-biodegradable matter from downstream sludge streams.

## 2.3 Types of Screens — Detailed Engineering Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question Area:** Examiners frequently ask for the *classification* of screens and the *difference between coarse and fine screens*.

### A. Bar Screens
- Made of vertical or inclined **rectangular bars** (usually steel, $10 \times 50$ mm cross-section).
- Two sub-types:
  - **Manually Cleaned Bar Screens** — used in **small plants (< 10 MLD)** where screenings are low.
  - **Mechanically Cleaned Bar Screens** — used in **medium/large plants** with automatic rakes (catenary, front-clean, back-clean types).
- Inclined bar screens are placed at an angle $\theta = 30°\text{ to }60°$ (typically **$45°$** for manual, **$0°\text{ to }30°$** for mechanical) to ease cleaning.

### B. Fine Screens
- Perforated plates or wedge-wire elements.
- Opening sizes range from **$0.25 \text{ mm}$ to $6 \text{ mm}$**.
- Used as **pre-treatment to membrane bioreactors (MBR)** or in **industrial wastewater**.

### C. Microscreens
- Very fine woven cloth or drum-type screens.
- Used in **tertiary/advanced treatment** for polishing effluents.
- *Not a KTU Module 1 priority.*

## 2.4 Important Design Parameters (KTU Numerical Favourites)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bar width (thickness) | $w$ | $5 - 15$ | mm |
| Clear spacing between bars | $b$ | $20 - 50$ | mm |
| Depth of wastewater at screen | $D$ | as per design | m |
| Approach velocity | $v_a$ | $0.6 - 1.2$ | m/s |
| Velocity through screen openings | $v$ | $0.6 - 1.2$ | m/s |
| Angle of inclination | $\theta$ | $30° - 60°$ | degrees |
| Headloss (clean) | $h_L$ | $0.05 - 0.15$ | m |
| Headloss (clogged) | $h_L$ | $0.3 - 0.6$ | m |
| Headloss through velocity | $h_v$ | $v^2 / 2g$ | m |
| Discharge | $Q$ | as per design flow | m³/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **$v_a$ (approach velocity in channel)** with **$v$ (velocity through screen openings)**. They are **different** — $v$ is always calculated using the **net open area** of the screen, not the gross channel area.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** and have appeared in KTU ESE papers.

### Formula 1: Velocity Through Screen Openings

$$v = \frac{Q}{A_{net}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = discharge through screen (m³/s)
- $A_{net}$ = net open area of screen = $A_{gross} \times \eta$
- $\eta$ = efficiency of screen = $\dfrac{b}{b+w}$

### Formula 2: Net Open Area (Cross-Sectional)

$$A_{net} = D \times (N \times b)$$

Where $N$ = number of openings and $D$ = submerged depth.

### Formula 3: Number of Bars

$$N_b = \frac{W_{channel} - b}{w + b}$$

Where $W_{channel}$ = total channel width.

### Formula 4: Headloss Through a Clean Screen (Kirschmer's Equation) ⭐

$$h_L = \beta \left(\frac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \frac{v^2}{2g} \sin\theta$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = dimensionless headloss coefficient (= **$2.44$** for clean screens, **$1.67$** for bar shapes with semicircular face)
- $w$ = bar width (mm)
- $b$ = clear spacing (mm)
- $v$ = velocity through screen openings (m/s)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\theta$ = angle of inclination with horizontal

### Formula 5: Headloss Through a Partially Clogged Screen ⭐

$$h_L = \frac{v^2 - v_a^2}{2g} \times \frac{1}{C_d}$$

Where:
- $v_a$ = approach velocity in channel
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge $\approx 0.7$ for clean, $\approx 0.6$ for clogged.

### Formula 6: Screen Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{b}{b + w} \times 100 \text{ (\%)}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between $v$ and $v_a$ (Efficiency Link)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** This derivation has been asked in previous ESE papers as a 7-mark sub-part.

### Given:
- Discharge $Q$ flows through a screen of gross submerged area $A_{gross}$.
- The screen contains $N$ openings of width $b$ separated by bars of width $w$.

### Step 1 — Express Gross Area
The total gross submerged area of the screen (channel cross-section) is:
$$A_{gross} = D \times W_{channel}$$

### Step 2 — Express Net Open Area
The net open area available for flow is the sum of all clear openings:
$$A_{net} = D \times (N \times b)$$

### Step 3 — Apply Continuity
By the law of conservation of mass (incompressible flow):
$$Q = v_a \times A_{gross} = v \times A_{net}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $v$
$$v = v_a \times \frac{A_{gross}}{A_{net}} = v_a \times \frac{W_{channel}}{N \times b}$$

### Step 5 — Express in Terms of Efficiency
Since efficiency $\eta = \dfrac{b}{b+w}$ and number of bars $N_b = \dfrac{W_{channel} - b}{w + b}$:

$$v = v_a \times \frac{1}{\eta} = \frac{v_a \cdot (b + w)}{b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since $\eta < 1$, we always have $\mathbf{v > v_a}$. This makes physical sense — water must speed up to squeeze through the smaller net area.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kirschmer's Headloss Equation (Energy Approach)

### Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation
Apply the energy equation between point **1** (just upstream of screen) and point **2** (just downstream, at the bar exit):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

### Step 2 — Simplify
Assuming $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal screen) and $p_1 = p_2$ (free water surface on both sides if submerged is small):

$$h_L = \frac{v^2 - v_a^2}{2g}$$

### Step 3 — Empirical Correction (Kirschmer, 1926)
For practical bar shapes, the headloss depends on the **bar shape factor**, **bar width to spacing ratio**, and **inclination angle**. Kirschmer introduced an empirical coefficient $\beta$:

$$h_L = \beta \left(\frac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \frac{v^2}{2g} \sin\theta$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to apply the $\sin\theta$ factor for inclined screens. For a **vertical screen** ($\theta = 90°$), $\sin\theta = 1$, so the term disappears — but for an inclined screen, **you must include it**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **typical 14-mark KTU numerical** combining design + headloss calculation.

### Problem Statement:
A wastewater treatment plant receives a peak flow of **$Q = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$** through a screen channel of **width = 1.2 m** and **depth of flow = 0.8 m**. The bar screens are inclined at **$45°$** to the horizontal, with bar width **$w = 10 \text{ mm}$** and clear spacing **$b = 25 \text{ mm}$**. Calculate:
1. Net open area of the screen
2. Velocity through the openings ($v$)
3. Headloss through a clean screen using Kirschmer's equation (take $\beta = 2.44$)
4. Headloss when the screen is **50% clogged** (assume $C_d = 0.6$)

### Solution:

#### (a) Net Open Area

Total submerged area (gross):
$$A_{gross} = 1.2 \times 0.8 = 0.96 \text{ m}^2$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{b}{b + w} = \frac{25}{25 + 10} = \frac{25}{35} = 0.714$$

Net open area:
$$A_{net} = A_{gross} \times \eta = 0.96 \times 0.714 = 0.686 \text{ m}^2$$

#### (b) Velocity Through Openings
$$v = \frac{Q}{A_{net}} = \frac{0.5}{0.686} = 0.729 \text{ m/s}$$

> This is within the recommended range of **$0.6 - 1.2$ m/s**. ✔

#### (c) Headloss — Clean Screen (Kirschmer)

$$h_L = \beta \left(\frac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \frac{v^2}{2g} \sin\theta$$

Substituting:
- $w/b = 10/25 = 0.4$
- $(0.4)^{4/3} = 0.4^{1.333} = 0.317$
- $v^2 / 2g = 0.729^2 / (2 \times 9.81) = 0.531 / 19.62 = 0.0271 \text{ m}$
- $\sin 45° = 0.707$

$$h_L = 2.44 \times 0.317 \times 0.0271 \times 0.707$$
$$h_L = 2.44 \times 0.317 \times 0.0192$$
$$h_L = 0.0148 \text{ m} \approx 14.8 \text{ mm}$$

> ✔ Within KTU range of **$5 - 15$ cm for clean screen**. (Note: Converted to mm for clarity.)

#### (d) Headloss — 50% Clogged Screen

Using:
$$h_L = \frac{v^2 - v_a^2}{2g} \times \frac{1}{C_d}$$

First compute approach velocity:
$$v_a = \frac{Q}{A_{gross}} = \frac{0.5}{0.96} = 0.521 \text{ m/s}$$

Now:
$$h_L = \frac{0.729^2 - 0.521^2}{2 \times 9.81} \times \frac{1}{0.6}$$

$$h_L = \frac{0.531 - 0.271}{19.62} \times \frac{1}{0.6}$$

$$h_L = \frac{0.260}{19.62} \times 1.667$$

$$h_L = 0.0133 \times 1.667 = 0.0221 \text{ m} \approx 22 \text{ mm}$$

> ✔ Within the **$15 - 60$ mm** clogged range. The screen design is **acceptable**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Checks at a Glance

| Check | Formula | Acceptable Range | Status of Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity $v$ | $Q / A_{net}$ | $0.6 - 1.2$ m/s | $0.729$ m/s ✔ |
| Headloss (clean) | Kirschmer | $\leq 0.15$ m | $0.015$ m ✔ |
| Headloss (clogged) | $C_d$ formula | $\leq 0.6$ m | $0.022$ m ✔ |
| Approach $v_a$ | $Q / A_{gross}$ | $0.3 - 0.9$ m/s | $0.521$ m/s ✔ |
| Efficiency $\eta$ | $b / (b+w)$ | $0.6 - 0.8$ | $0.714$ ✔ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Screening in a Sewage Treatment Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sewage Inlet] --> B[Bar Screen Chamber]
    B --> C{Screen Type?}
    C -->|Coarse| D[Coarse Screen 50mm Opening]
    C -->|Medium| E[Medium Screen 20mm Opening]
    C -->|Fine| F[Fine Screen 1.5mm Perforated]
    D --> G[Screenings to Disposal]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    D --> H[Effluent to Grit Chamber]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[Primary Clarifier]
    I --> J[Biological Treatment]
    J --> K[Secondary Clarifier]
    K --> L[Disinfection and Effluent Discharge]
    G --> M[Landfill or Incineration]
```

## 4.2 Bar Screen Geometry — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Inlet Channel] --> P2[Approach Zone]
    P2 --> P3[Bar Inclination 45 degrees]
    P3 --> P4[Bar Width w and Spacing b]
    P4 --> P5[Net Open Area]
    P4 --> P6[Headloss Calculation Zone]
    P5 --> P7[Effluent Outflow]
    P6 --> P8[Clean Screen hL]
    P6 --> P9[Clogged Screen hL]
    P8 --> P10[Design Validation Check]
    P9 --> P10
    P10 -->|Pass| P7
    P10 -->|Fail| P11[Revise Design]
    P11 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Manual vs Mechanical Cleaning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Plant Capacity MLD] --> S2{Capacity less than 10 MLD?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Use Manually Cleaned Bar Screen]
    S2 -->|No| S4{Capacity less than 50 MLD?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Use Front Clean Mechanical Screen]
    S4 -->|No| S6[Use Catenary or Back Clean Mechanical Screen]
    S3 --> S7[Angle 45 to 60 degrees]
    S5 --> S8[Angle 0 to 30 degrees]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S9[Screening Operational]
    S8 --> S9
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above are **block-level functional flowcharts** representing the screening subsystem within a larger wastewater treatment train. They map the cause-effect relationships and design decision logic required by the KTU 2024 scheme outcome-based syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define screening and state its significance in wastewater treatment. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Screening is the preliminary treatment unit in which coarse suspended and floating solids such as rags, plastics, sticks, and leaves are physically removed from sewage by passing it through a screen.
**Significance:**
1. Protects pumps and downstream equipment from clogging and abrasion.
2. Reduces suspended solids load on subsequent treatment units.
3. Improves efficiency of grit chambers and primary clarifiers by removing bulky floatables.

### Question 2: Differentiate between coarse, medium, and fine screens. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Coarse | Medium | Fine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Opening size | $\geq 50$ mm | $6 - 50$ mm | $0.25 - 6$ mm |
| Location | Ahead of pumping station | After grit chamber | Before biological units |
| Cleaning | Manual | Mechanical | Mechanical |
| Use | Protect pumps | Standard municipal | Advanced treatment |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the following are **two alternative choices** (Module Internal Choice) for a 14-mark question. Each contains sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of screens used in preliminary wastewater treatment. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of manually cleaned and mechanically cleaned bar screens. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bar screen is to be installed in a sewer of width **1.0 m** and depth of flow **0.6 m** carrying a peak flow of **$Q = 0.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$**. The bars are **$10 \text{ mm}$** wide with clear spacing of **$25 \text{ mm}$**. The screen is inclined at **$60°$** to the horizontal. Calculate: (i) net open area, (ii) velocity through openings, and (iii) headloss through the clean screen using Kirschmer's equation ($\beta = 2.44$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Classification of Screens:**

1. **Coarse Screens:** Opening $\geq 50$ mm. Used to protect pumping machinery.
2. **Medium Screens:** Opening $6 - 50$ mm. Standard for municipal plants.
3. **Fine Screens:** Opening $0.25 - 6$ mm. Used in industrial applications.
4. **Microscreens:** Opening $< 0.25$ mm. Used in tertiary treatment.

**Manually Cleaned Bar Screens:**
- *Advantages:* Simple, low capital cost, no power required, suitable for small plants.
- *Disadvantages:* Labour-intensive, requires shutdown for cleaning, not suitable for high flow rates.

**Mechanically Cleaned Bar Screens:**
- *Advantages:* Continuous operation, less manual labour, suitable for large plants.
- *Disadvantages:* High capital cost, requires power and maintenance, more complex.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- $W = 1.0$ m, $D = 0.6$ m, $Q = 0.3$ m³/s
- $w = 10$ mm, $b = 25$ mm, $\theta = 60°$, $\beta = 2.44$

**(i) Net Open Area:**

$$A_{gross} = W \times D = 1.0 \times 0.6 = 0.6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\eta = \frac{b}{b + w} = \frac{25}{25 + 10} = 0.714$$

$$A_{net} = 0.6 \times 0.714 = 0.4286 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Velocity Through Openings:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A_{net}} = \frac{0.3}{0.4286} = 0.700 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Headloss (Kirschmer):**

$$h_L = \beta \left(\frac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \frac{v^2}{2g} \sin\theta$$

$$h_L = 2.44 \times \left(\frac{10}{25}\right)^{4/3} \times \frac{0.700^2}{2 \times 9.81} \times \sin 60°$$

$$h_L = 2.44 \times (0.4)^{1.333} \times 0.0250 \times 0.866$$

$$h_L = 2.44 \times 0.317 \times 0.0250 \times 0.866$$

$$h_L = 0.0168 \text{ m} \approx 16.8 \text{ mm}$$

> ✔ **Design is acceptable** (within $5 - 15$ cm clean range).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`

**(a)** What is a screen? With a neat sketch, describe the working of a bar screen. List the factors affecting the design of a bar screen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mechanically cleaned bar screen has bars of **$12 \text{ mm}$** thickness and clear openings of **$30 \text{ mm}$**. The screen is installed in a channel of **$1.5 \text{ m}$** width with a flow depth of **$0.75 \text{ m}$**. The discharge is **$0.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$**. Calculate the headloss when the screen is **25% clogged** (use $C_d = 0.7$ for partial clog). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

A **screen** is a device with openings used to retain coarse solids from wastewater. **Bar Screen Working:**

1. Raw sewage enters the screen chamber.
2. Water passes through gaps between vertical/inclined bars.
3. Coarse solids (rags, plastics, sticks) are retained upstream.
4. A mechanically driven rake periodically lifts the screenings off the bars.
5. Screenings are dropped into a collection bin for disposal.
6. Cleaned effluent flows to the grit chamber.

**Factors Affecting Design:**
- Discharge ($Q$) and peak flow
- Clear spacing ($b$) and bar width ($w$)
- Approach velocity ($v_a$)
- Headloss allowed (clean and clogged)
- Angle of inclination
- Method of cleaning

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- $w = 12$ mm, $b = 30$ mm, $W = 1.5$ m, $D = 0.75$ m, $Q = 0.6$ m³/s, $C_d = 0.7$

**Step 1 — Gross and Net Areas:**

$$A_{gross} = 1.5 \times 0.75 = 1.125 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\eta = \frac{30}{30 + 12} = 0.714$$

$$A_{net} = 1.125 \times 0.714 = 0.8036 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Velocities:**

$$v = \frac{0.6}{0.8036} = 0.747 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_a = \frac{0.6}{1.125} = 0.533 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Headloss (25% Clogged):**

$$h_L = \frac{v^2 - v_a^2}{2g} \times \frac{1}{C_d}$$

$$h_L = \frac{0.747^2 - 0.533^2}{2 \times 9.81} \times \frac{1}{0.7}$$

$$h_L = \frac{0.558 - 0.284}{19.62} \times 1.4286$$

$$h_L = \frac{0.274}{19.62} \times 1.4286$$

$$h_L = 0.01396 \times 1.4286 = 0.0199 \text{ m} \approx 20 \text{ mm}$$

> ✔ **Headloss is well within the acceptable clogged range ($150 - 600$ mm).**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in screening problems:**
> 1. **Mixing up** $v$ (velocity through openings) and $v_a$ (approach velocity) — both must be computed separately using $A_{net}$ and $A_{gross}$ respectively.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sin\theta$ factor** in Kirschmer's equation for inclined screens.
> 3. **Using $C_d = 1$** (theoretical maximum) instead of the practical $0.6 - 0.7$ for clogged conditions.
> 4. **Not mentioning units** in the final answer — KTU strictly awards marks for unit consistency (e.g., m, mm, m/s).
> 5. **Confusing "efficiency" with "open area ratio"** — they are mathematically the same but must be quoted as a fraction or percentage consistently.
> 6. **Skipping the design-check step** (verifying $v$ is between $0.6 - 1.2$ m/s and $h_L$ is within limits). Examiners award at least 1 mark for validations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- Screening = **Preliminary Treatment**, removes **coarse solids** only.
- Three categories: **Coarse ($\geq 50$ mm), Medium ($6 - 50$ mm), Fine ($0.25 - 6$ mm)**.
- Standard design: **$b = 20$ mm, $w = 10$ mm, $\theta = 45°$**, velocity **$0.6 - 1.2$ m/s**.
- **Kirschmer's Equation:** $h_L = \beta \left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \dfrac{v^2}{2g} \sin\theta$, with **$\beta = 2.44$** for clean rectangular bars.
- **Clogged screen headloss:** $h_L = \dfrac{v^2 - v_a^2}{2g \cdot C_d}$, with $C_d \approx 0.6 - 0.7$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{b}{b + w}$ (always $< 1$).
- **Velocity $v$ is always greater than approach velocity $v_a$** because net area is smaller than gross area.
- Manually cleaned screens for plants **$< 10$ MLD**; mechanically cleaned for **$\geq 10$ MLD**.
- **Screenings disposal:** burial, incineration (not recycling due to hygiene).
- **Acceptable headloss:** clean $\leq 150$ mm, clogged $\leq 600$ mm.
- **Direction of bar inclination:** bars are placed perpendicular to flow, screen inclined at angle $\theta$ with horizontal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Screens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Screens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **municipal and industrial wastewater engineering**, a **Screen** is defined as a **preliminary treatment device** consisting of a series of parallel bars, rods, wires, or perforated plates installed transversely across the flow channel of a treatment plant. Its primary function is to **remove coarse suspended and floating solids** (such as rags, plastics, leaves, sticks, paper, and large organic debris) from the incoming wastewater stream before it proceeds to subsequent treatment units (grit chambers, primary clarifiers, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Screens form the **first unit operation** in any conventional wastewater treatment scheme. They are classified as **"Preliminary Treatment Units"** under Module 1 of PCCET503, along with grit chambers and comminutors.

The screening process is a purely **physical, non-biological, and non-chemical** separation method. It relies on the **size exclusion principle** — particles larger than the gap openings between bars are intercepted and retained, while water and finer particles pass through.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Definition Box:**
> *"Screens are devices used to remove floating, suspended, and oversized suspended matter (coarse solids) from wastewater by means of perforated plates, bars, or wire mesh, serving as the first barrier in preliminary treatment to protect downstream mechanical and biological units from clogging, abrasion, and operational damage."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Understanding)

Think of screens in a wastewater treatment plant the same way you think of the **drain strainer in your kitchen sink**.

When you wash vegetables or rice, the water carries small grains and debris. If you let it flow directly into the drain pipe, larger items may clog the plumbing. The **small metal strainer (chamber) placed at the drain mouth** catches rice grains, vegetable peels, and large food particles — allowing only water and very fine particles to pass through.

A wastewater treatment plant operates on **the exact same principle, but at an industrial scale:**

| Kitchen Sink Analogy | Wastewater Treatment Plant Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Kitchen drain mouth | Influent channel of treatment plant |
| Small metal strainer | Bar/mesh screen assembly |
| Caught rice grains & vegetable peels | Rags, plastics, sticks, paper |
| Water that passes through | Screened wastewater to next unit |
| Cleaning the strainer with hand | Manual raking or mechanical cleaning |

The **only difference** is the *scale, the degree of automation, and the engineering precision* required to handle millions of litres per day (MLD) of sewage continuously.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"Screens are the gatekeepers of the treatment plant."* Just as a security gatekeeper checks people entering a building, screens check what is *allowed* to enter the downstream treatment units — protecting pumps, pipes, and biological reactors from being damaged or choked by oversized debris.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Design Metrics

The following are the **standard boundary values** universally accepted in KTU-aligned environmental engineering design (as per **Metcalf & Eddy** and **IS 8419 (Part I)**):

- **Bar Spacing (clear opening):** Coarse = **> 40 mm** | Medium = **20–40 mm** | Fine = **< 20 mm**
- **Bar Thickness:** Typically **5 mm to 15 mm** for flat bars; circular bars use diameters of **10 mm to 30 mm**.
- **Approach Velocity (V):** **0.3 to 0.6 m/s** (for sanitary sewage — never exceeding **0.9 m/s** to avoid forcing solids through).
- **Through Velocity (v):** **0.6 to 1.2 m/s** (during normal flow conditions).
- **Headloss (h):** **Clean screen = 0.05 to 0.15 m** | **Partially clogged = 0.3 m** (maximum design).
- **Angle of Inclination (θ):** **30° to 60°** from horizontal (commonly 45°).
- **Discharge Coefficient (C):** **0.6 to 0.7** for clean bar screens.
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (standard constant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these values matter:** The approach velocity must be controlled carefully. If V < 0.3 m/s, grit and organic matter settle in the channel before the screen. If V > 0.9 m/s, solids are *pushed through* the screen openings, defeating the purpose. **0.6 m/s is the universally accepted design value** for peak flow conditions.

---

### 1.4 Why Screens Are Indispensable (Engineering Justification)

Screens are **not optional** in any KTU-approved sewage treatment scheme. They serve **four critical engineering functions:**

1. **Protection of downstream equipment** — Pumps, valves, and clarifier mechanisms are protected from clogging, fouling, and mechanical damage caused by rags, sticks, and metallic objects.
2. **Reduction of BOD load** — Organic solids removed at the screen stage would otherwise decompose in downstream biological units, consuming oxygen unnecessarily.
3. **Improved aesthetic and sanitary conditions** — Visible floating debris creates odour and public nuisance; removing it at the inlet improves plant environment.
4. **Prevention of clogging in pipes and channels** — Long sludge pipes and narrow channels are vulnerable to blockage by large debris if screens are absent.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why and How" Behind Screening

The operational theory of screens is built on **three fundamental engineering principles** that KTU examiners frequently test:

#### **Principle 1: Mechanical Interception (Size Exclusion)**
When a particle travels with the wastewater flow toward the screen, the probability of its removal depends on the **ratio of particle size to bar opening size**. Any particle with diameter **greater than the clear spacing between bars** is physically intercepted and held back. Smaller particles pass through with the water.

- **Why?** Because the screen acts as a physical barrier — there is no chemical bonding or biological action involved.
- **How?** The wastewater flows through the gaps; oversized objects physically cannot fit, so they are retained on the upstream face of the screen.

#### **Principle 2: Velocity Control (Hydrodynamic Governing)**
The **kinetic energy** of the incoming wastewater must be sufficient to carry suspended fines through the screen, but **not so high** that it drags the captured debris through the openings.

$$V_{\text{approach}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{total cross-section}}}$$

$$v_{\text{through}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{effective opening}}}$$

- **Why this matters:** If the through-velocity drops below 0.6 m/s, fine suspended solids begin to settle and clog the screen face (called *blinding*). If the through-velocity exceeds 1.2 m/s, the screen acts as a partial comminutor — shredding rather than retaining debris.
- **How to control:** By choosing the **right bar spacing** and **correct cross-sectional area** of the channel.

#### **Principle 3: Headloss Generation (Energy Loss Principle)**
As water passes through the narrow gaps between bars, the cross-section contracts and the velocity increases. By **Bernoulli's equation**, this increase in velocity must be compensated by a **drop in piezometric head**. This is the **headloss (h)** of the screen.

- **Why does it occur?** The narrow openings act like a series of orifices; energy is dissipated due to flow separation, contraction, and turbulence.
- **How is it measured?** The upstream water level rises relative to the downstream level — the difference is the headloss (typically **50 mm to 300 mm**).

---

### 2.2 Classification of Screens (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Screens are classified in **four ways** in the KTU syllabus. The following master table is the **single most important classification chart** for Module 1:

#### **Table 2.1: Master Classification of Screens**

| **Classification Basis** | **Type 1** | **Type 2** | **Type 3** |
|---|---|---|---|
