The residence time is usually **2–5 minutes** for raw-water applications. *Sketch reference*: Use the Mermaid block 4.3 (sub-block INSU group). *Comparison Table:* | Feature | Diffused-Air Aerator | Mechanical Surface Aerator | |---|---|---| | Energy source | Compressed air (blower) | Rotating impeller (motor) | | OTE | 1.5–2.0 kg O₂/kWh | 1.2–2.4 kg O₂/kWh | | Footprint | Small | Very small | | CO₂ removal | Moderate (50–70 %) | Good (60–85 %) | | Maintenance | Diffuser clogging | Bearing/gearbox wear | | Suitability | Iron/Mn removal, deep tanks | Activated-sludge basins | **(b) Numerical — Air Volume Required (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Compute oxygen deficit to be made up per day.** $$ \Delta C = 6.5 - 1.5 = 5.0 \;\text{mg/L} = 5.0 \;\text{g/m}^{3} $$ **Step 2 — Compute mass of O₂ required per day.** $$ M_{O_2} = Q \cdot \Delta C = 50{,}000 \times 5.0 = 250{,}000 \;\text{g/d} = 250 \;\text{kg/d} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to mass of air required.** $$ M_{air} = \frac{M_{O_2}}{0.23 \times 0.08} = \frac{250}{0.0184} = 13{,}587 \;\text{kg/d} $$ **Step 4 — Convert mass of air to volume per day, then per hour.** $$ V_{air,d} = \frac{13{,}587}{1.184} = 11{,}476 \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{d} $$ $$ V_{air,h} = \frac{11{,}476}{24} \approx 478 \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{h} $$ > **Final Answer:** Approximately **478 m³ of air per hour** must be supplied by the blower. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** > 1. **Missing the diffuser efficiency factor (0.08)** — without it the answer is roughly 12× too low. > 2. **Using 21 % instead of 23 %** for oxygen fraction by weight. KTU exam tables (and CPHEEO manual) give 23 % by weight; 21 % is by *volume*. > 3. **Forgetting to convert g/d to kg/d** before applying the 23 % fraction. > 4. **Not reporting the final answer in m³/h** — examiners explicitly ask for hourly rate; reporting m³/d loses 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Aerator Types (Module 1)** - **Definition:** An aerator maximizes water–air contact to add O₂, remove CO₂/H₂S, oxidize Fe²⁺/Mn²⁺, and strip odour compounds. - **Governing law:** Two-film theory → $\frac{dC}{dt}=K_{L}a\,(C_{s}-C)$; integrated form uses the *deficit ratio*. - **Henry's Law** dictates that high-$K_H$ gases (CO₂, H₂S) escape water easily. - **Five categories:** Cascade (gravity), Spray (atomization), Diffused-air (bubbles), Mechanical surface (impeller), Pressure/Injector. - **Cascade aerator (most-tested):** 3–6 trays, drop 1–3 m, hydraulic loading **30–75 m³/m²·h**, CO₂ removal **60–80 %**, no power. - **Spray aerator:** finest droplets → highest O₂ addition but freezing/drift issues. - **Diffused-air aerator:** uses **blower + porous diffuser**, 2–5 min contact, good for Fe/Mn removal, OTE 1.5–2.0 kg O₂/kWh. - **Mechanical surface aerator:** rotating impeller splashes water, very compact, OTE 1.2–2.4 kg O₂/kWh. - **Standard DO at 25 °C ≈ 8.3 mg/L** — design around this saturation value. - **Numerical must-knows:** (i) flow unit conversion (m³/d → m³/h), (ii) tray-area formula $A=Q/HLR$, (iii) air-volume calculation using 23 % O₂ by weight and diffuser efficiency. - **Cold-climate caveat:** Cascade and spray aerators suffer freezing; diffused and mechanical types do not. - **Placement in plant:** Aerator → Coagulation/Flocculation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Disinfection. - **Avoid these mistakes in exams:** wrong O₂ fraction (21 % vs 23 %), missing diffuser efficiency, skipping tray-count justification, omitting units in final answers.

topics.title: Theory and Principles of Sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Theory and Principles of Sedimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sedimentation** is the physico-chemical unit operation in which suspended solid particles heavier than water are removed from a liquid stream by gravitational settling under quiescent (or near-quiet) hydraulic conditions. It is the most widely employed primary treatment process in water and wastewater engineering and is also integral to secondary clarifiers in activated sludge plants.

In KTU terminology, sedimentation is classified under the broader umbrella of **suspended-solids separation** operations and is governed by the principles of **fluid mechanics**, **Newton's Law of Resistance**, and **Stokes' Law** for discrete particle settling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Muddy River Pond"**
> Imagine throwing a handful of sand into a still pond. The heavier grains plunge to the bottom almost instantly, while the lighter ones drift slowly downward. Now, picture a slow-moving wide river entering a lake — the river suddenly loses its forward velocity, and the silt it was carrying drops to the lakebed, leaving clearer water to flow out. This is exactly what happens inside a **sedimentation tank** at a water-treatment plant: the flow velocity is drastically reduced, giving suspended particles time to fall out of suspension under gravity.

The three dominant forces acting on a particle in water are:
1. **Gravitational (downward) force** $\rightarrow$ pulls the particle down.
2. **Buoyancy (upward) force** $\rightarrow$ pushes the particle up.
3. **Drag (frictional) force** $\rightarrow$ opposes motion through the fluid.

A particle settles when the gravitational force exceeds the sum of buoyancy and drag forces.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water (at 20 °C) | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ N·s/m²** |
| Kinematic viscosity of water (at 20 °C) | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Specific gravity of quartz (typical) | $S_s$ | **2.65** |
| Typical detention time in tank | $t_d$ | **2 – 4 hours** (primary) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 of Environmental Engineering explicitly demands that students master the four **types of settling** (Type I, II, III, IV) and the derivation of **Stokes' Law** along with the design equations for an **ideal settling tank**.

## 1.4 Role of Sedimentation in Environmental Engineering

Sedimentation is used in environmental engineering to:
- **Remove turbidity, suspended solids (SS), and grit** from raw water and wastewater.
- **Thicken sludges** in thickener tanks.
- Act as a **preliminary step** before filtration or disinfection.
- Function as a **secondary clarifier** in biological treatment systems (e.g., activated sludge, trickling filters).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Settling (KTU Mandatory Classification)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to identify and describe four distinct types of settling phenomena, which depend on **particle concentration** and **particle interaction**.

### Type I — Discrete Particle Settling
- Occurs in **dilute suspensions** (concentration < 1000 mg/L).
- Particles settle as **individual entities** without interacting with neighbours.
- Governed entirely by **Stokes' Law**.
- Example: Removal of grit and coarse sand in grit chambers.

### Type II — Flocculent Settling
- Particles **flocculate (coalesce)** as they settle, increasing in size and mass.
- Settling velocity **increases with depth/time**.
- Example: Removal of finer suspended solids in primary clarifiers of water treatment.

### Type III — Zone (Hindered) Settling
- High concentration of particles (e.g., in sludge thickeners).
- Particles settle as a **blanket or zone**; the fluid moves upward between them.
- Example: Settling of activated sludge in secondary clarifiers; sludge thickeners.

### Type IV — Compression Settling
- Occurs at the **bottom of thickeners** where particles physically contact each other.
- Settling is due to **compression** of the sludge mass under its own weight.
- Example: Final consolidation of sludge in a thickener.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Type I and Type II settling are commonly examined in the 14-mark module questions. Type III and IV appear more often in 3-mark short questions.

## 2.2 Stoke's Law — Conceptual Breakdown

For a spherical particle falling under gravity in a viscous fluid, three forces act:

| Force | Formula | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| Gravity (weight) | $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g$ | Downward |
| Buoyancy | $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g$ | Upward |
| Drag (Stokes regime) | $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s$ | Opposite to motion |

At **terminal settling velocity** ($v_s$), the net force = 0.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gravity} &= \text{Buoyancy} + \text{Drag} \\
\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g &= \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g + 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s
\end{aligned}$$

Solving gives the classic **Stokes' Law**:

$$v_s = \frac{g \, d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{18 \mu}$$

In terms of **kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho_w$ and **specific gravity** $S_s$:

$$v_s = \frac{g \, d^2 (S_s - 1)}{18 \nu}$$

## 2.3 Newton's Law (Turbulent Regime)

For larger particles where the Reynolds number is high, Newton's Law applies:

$$v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g \, d (S_s - 1)}{3 C_D}}$$

where $C_D$ is the **drag coefficient** (function of $Re$).

| Regime | Re | CD | Applicable Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laminar (Stokes) | Re < 0.2 | $C_D = 24/Re$ | **Stokes' Law** |
| Transition | 0.2 < Re < 1000 | $C_D = 24/Re + 3/\sqrt{Re} + 0.34$ | Empirical |
| Turbulent (Newton) | Re > 1000 | $C_D \approx 0.4$ | **Newton's Law** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s = \dfrac{g d^2 (S_s - 1)}{18 \nu}$ | Stokes' settling velocity (laminar) | m/s |
| 2 | $v_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d (S_s - 1)}{3 C_D}}$ | Newton's settling velocity (turbulent) | m/s |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{v_s d}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number for particle settling | – |
| 4 | $v_0 = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) | m³/(m²·d) = m/d |
| 5 | $t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot H}{Q}$ | Detention (residence) time | h or s |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{v_s}{v_s + v_h}$ | Efficiency of discrete settling (Hazen) | – |
| 7 | $v_h = \dfrac{Q}{A} = v_0$ | Horizontal velocity of flow | m/s |
| 8 | $v_H = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \beta (S_s - 1) g d}{f}}$ | Scour velocity (Camp's criterion) | m/s |
| 9 | $L = v_h \times t_d$ | Tank length (ideal) | m |
| 10 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{v_s}$ | Surface area required (ideal tank) | m² |

> **Notation Reminder:** $\beta$ = constant (0.04 for unigranular, 0.06 for graded material), $f$ = Darcy-Weisbach friction factor ($\approx 0.03$ for grit), $H$ = depth of tank, $L$ = length of tank, $Q$ = discharge.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Treatment Plants (WTPs):** Primary sedimentation basins remove 50–70% of suspended solids before filtration.
- **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs):** Primary clarifiers reduce SS load on downstream biological units.
- **Industrial Effluents:** Settling ponds remove heavy metals and grit.
- **Stormwater Management:** Detention basins rely on settling to remove road sediments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Stokes' Law

**Step 1 — Define the system**
Consider a single spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_s$ settling in a Newtonian fluid of density $\rho_w$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$ under gravity $g$.

**Step 2 — List the forces acting on the particle**

(a) Weight of the particle (downward):
$$F_g = \text{Volume} \times \text{density} \times g = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g$$

(b) Buoyancy force (upward), per Archimedes' principle:
$$F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g$$

(c) Drag force (opposing motion). In the laminar regime:
$$F_D = 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s$$

**Step 3 — Apply terminal velocity condition**
At terminal velocity, the net force is zero (the particle has zero acceleration):

$$F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the force expressions**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g - \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g - 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s = 0$$

**Step 5 — Factor out common terms**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s$$

**Step 6 — Isolate the settling velocity $v_s$**

$$v_s = \frac{\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{3 \pi \mu d}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the constants**

$$v_s = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to kinematic viscosity form**
Divide numerator and denominator by $\rho_w$ and substitute $S_s = \rho_s / \rho_w$:

$$\boxed{\,v_s = \frac{g \, d^2 (S_s - 1)}{18 \nu}\,}$$

This is the **Stokes' settling velocity** valid for the **laminar regime** ($Re < 0.2$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Efficiency of an Ideal Settling Tank (Hazen's Model)

**Step 1 — Assumptions of the ideal settling tank**
- All particles settle at their **terminal settling velocity** $v_s$ (Type I).
- Flow is **uniform and horizontal** at velocity $v_h = Q/A$.
- Settled particles are **not re-suspended**.
- The flow regime is **steady-state**.

**Step 2 — Define Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)**
The volumetric flow rate per unit surface area is:

$$v_0 = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{B \cdot L}$$

where $B$ = width, $L$ = length. Dimensionally $v_0$ is a velocity [m/d].

**Step 3 — Criterion for a particle to be removed**
A particle entering at the top of the tank at height $H$ settles a depth $H$ in time $t_d = H / v_s$.
It must reach the bottom **before** leaving the tank horizontally:

$$\frac{H}{v_s} \leq \frac{L}{v_h}$$

**Step 4 — Definition of removal efficiency**
A particle will be removed only if its settling velocity $v_s \geq v_0$. The fraction removed is:

$$\eta = \frac{v_s}{v_s + v_0} = \frac{v_s}{v_s + v_h}$$

**Step 5 — Critical settling velocity**
All particles with $v_s \geq v_0$ are **100 % removed**.
Thus, the **critical settling velocity** is $v_c = v_0 = Q/A$, and the design equation is:

$$\boxed{\,A = \frac{Q}{v_c}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The efficiency depends **only on the surface area**, not on the depth. This is why **shallow, wide tanks** are theoretically more efficient than deep, narrow ones for a given area.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Settling Regimes & Design Implications

| Criterion | Type I | Type II | Type III | Type IV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Particle concentration | < 1000 mg/L | 1000–5000 mg/L | 5000–10000 mg/L | Very high |
| Particle interaction | None | Flocculation | Hindered | Compression |
| Governing equation | Stokes' Law | Empirical column data | Kynch's theory | Empirical |
| Removal mechanism | Gravity | Gravity + floc growth | Blanket settling | Weight of overlying sludge |
| Typical application | Grit chamber | Primary clarifier | Secondary clarifier | Sludge thickener |
| KTU exam weightage | **High** | **High** | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sedimentation Tank

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water / Wastewater Influent] --> B[Inlet Zone with Baffles]
    B --> C[Settling Zone - Quiescent Flow]
    C --> D[Sludge Zone - Collection Hopper]
    C --> E[Effluent Launder / Weir System]
    D --> F[Sludge Draw-off Pipe to Sludge Handling]
    E --> G[Treated Effluent Outlet]
    H[Surface Overflow Rate Control] -.-> C
    I[Detention Time Control] -.-> C
    J[Scour Velocity Check] -.-> C
```

**Block Description:**
- **A → B:** Inlet distributes flow uniformly to avoid short-circuiting.
- **B → C:** Baffles reduce inlet velocity and ensure laminar flow conditions for Type I settling.
- **C → D:** Settled sludge accumulates in the hopper at the bottom; periodic or continuous removal is required.
- **C → E:** Clarified water exits over V-notch weirs or launders ensuring even collection.
- **F → Sludge treatment:** Sludge is pumped to digesters or drying beds.
- **H, I, J:** Design parameters — SOR, detention time, and scour velocity — are controlled to optimize performance.

---

## 4.2 Force Balance & Particle Settling — Conceptual Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spherical Particle in Water] --> B{Forces Acting?}
    B -->|Downward| C[Weight Fg]
    B -->|Upward| D[Buoyancy Fb]
    B -->|Resisting| E[Drag Fd]
    C --> F{Net Force = 0?}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|Yes| G[Terminal Settling Velocity vs]
    F -->|No| H[Particle Accelerates / Decelerates]
    G --> I[Apply Stokes Law: vs = g d^2 Ss-1 / 18 nu]
    I --> J{Re < 0.2?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Laminar - Stokes Valid]
    J -->|No| L[Use Newton Law with CD]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The diagram clearly shows the **decision-based logic** for selecting the appropriate settling law.
- The Reynolds number check determines whether the **Stokes regime** or **Newton's regime** governs settling.

---

## 4.3 Ideal Settling Tank Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Influent[Influent End]
        I1[Influent Channel] --> I2[Inlet Baffle Diffuser]
    end
    I2 --> S[Settling Zone of Length L and Depth H]
    S --> SC[Sludge Hopper - Bottom of Tank]
    S --> EW[Effluent Weir - V-Notch Launder]
    SC --> SO[Sludge Outlet Pipe]
    EW --> O[Treated Effluent Outlet]
```

**Key Design Notes:**
- $L$ = length of the settling zone (typically 15–30 m for primary clarifiers).
- $H$ = depth (3–5 m).
- $L/H$ ratio (around 4–6) controls horizontal flow uniformity.
- Weir loading rate is typically **≤ 300 m³/(m·d)** to avoid re-entrainment.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Stokes' Law for settling of discrete particles. State the conditions under which it is valid.**

**Model Answer:**
Stokes' Law gives the terminal settling velocity of a discrete spherical particle falling under gravity in a viscous fluid:

$$v_s = \frac{g \, d^2 (S_s - 1)}{18 \nu}$$

**Conditions of validity:**
1. The particle is **spherical and smooth**.
2. The fluid is **homogeneous, infinite, and Newtonian**.
3. Settling occurs in the **laminar regime** ($Re < 0.2$).
4. The particle concentration is **low** (no interaction between particles — i.e., **Type I settling**).
5. There is **no flocculation** during settling.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the difference between discrete settling and flocculent settling?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Discrete Settling (Type I) | Flocculent Settling (Type II) |
|---|---|---|
| Particle concentration | < 1000 mg/L (dilute) | > 1000 mg/L |
| Particle interaction | Negligible | Significant — particles coalesce |
| Settling velocity | **Constant** with depth | **Increases** with depth/time |
| Governing law | **Stokes' Law** | **Empirical / settling column tests** |
| Example | Grit chamber | Primary clarifier in WTP |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 scheme provides **internal choice** in Part B. Two alternative questions are presented below; both must have 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Stokes' Law for the terminal settling velocity of a discrete particle settling in a viscous fluid. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A spherical sand particle of diameter 0.1 mm and specific gravity 2.65 settles in water at 20 °C. Calculate:
(i) The terminal settling velocity using Stokes' Law.
(ii) The Reynolds number and verify if the laminar regime assumption is valid.

**Given:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Particle is **rigid, smooth, and spherical**.
2. Fluid is **homogeneous, viscous, and Newtonian**.
3. Settling occurs in **quiescent fluid** (no turbulence).
4. The **Reynolds number $Re < 0.2$** (laminar flow around the particle).
5. **No interaction** between adjacent particles.

**Derivation** (as in Section 3.1 of these notes):

At terminal velocity, weight = buoyancy + drag:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g + 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s$$

Rearranging:

$$v_s = \frac{g \, d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{18 \mu} = \frac{g \, d^2 (S_s - 1)}{18 \nu}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**(i) Terminal settling velocity:**

Given: $d = 0.1$ mm $= 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $S_s = 2.65$, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

Substituting into Stokes' Law:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_s &= \frac{9.81 \times (1 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (2.65 - 1)}{18 \times 1.004 \times 10^{-6}} \\
v_s &= \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.65}{1.8072 \times 10^{-5}} \\
v_s &= \frac{1.61865 \times 10^{-7}}{1.8072 \times 10^{-5}} \\
v_s &= 8.956 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \\
v_s &\approx 8.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.00896 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Reynolds number check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{v_s \cdot d}{\nu} = \frac{8.956 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-4}}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} \\
Re &= \frac{8.956 \times 10^{-7}}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} \\
Re &= 0.892
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Re = 0.892 > 0.2$, the laminar regime assumption is **marginally violated**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $v_s$ but **forget to verify the Reynolds number**, losing 1–2 marks. Always state whether $Re < 0.2$ holds. If it does not, mention that the result is an *approximation* and a more rigorous drag-coefficient-based equation would be needed.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the different **types of settling** encountered in water and wastewater treatment. State at least two distinguishing features of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ideal rectangular settling tank treats 5 MLD (megalitres per day) of water. The tank has a length of 20 m, width of 5 m, and depth of 3 m.
(i) Calculate the **surface overflow rate (SOR)**.
(ii) Calculate the **detention time** in hours.
(iii) Determine the **percentage removal efficiency** of particles having a settling velocity of 1.5 m/h.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

The four types of settling as outlined in Section 2.1:

1. **Type I – Discrete Settling:** Particles settle as individual entities without interaction; governed by Stokes' Law; found in grit chambers.
2. **Type II – Flocculent Settling:** Particles coalesce during settling, increasing mass; settling velocity rises with depth; occurs in primary clarifiers.
3. **Type III – Zone (Hindered) Settling:** High concentration leads to mass settling as a blanket; fluid moves upward; Kynch's theory applies; seen in secondary clarifiers and thickeners.
4. **Type IV – Compression Settling:** Particles in contact are compressed under their own weight; occurs in deep sludge blankets in thickeners.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Given:** $Q = 5$ MLD $= 5 \times 10^3$ m³/day, $L = 20$ m, $B = 5$ m, $H = 3$ m, $v_s = 1.5$ m/h.

**(i) Surface Overflow Rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= L \times B = 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ m}^2 \\
v_0 &= \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}{100 \text{ m}^2} = 50 \text{ m/day} \\
v_0 &= \frac{50}{24} = 2.083 \text{ m/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Detention time:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= A \times H = 100 \times 3 = 300 \text{ m}^3 \\
t_d &= \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{300 \text{ m}^3}{5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}} = 0.06 \text{ day} \\
t_d &= 0.06 \times 24 = 1.44 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Removal efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{v_s}{v_s + v_0} = \frac{1.5}{1.5 + 2.083} \\
\eta &= \frac{1.5}{3.583} = 0.4186 \\
\eta &\approx 41.86\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing **MLD** with **L/s** during unit conversion (1 MLD = 1.1574 L/s). Always convert to consistent units first.
> 2. Reporting the **SOR in m/day** but comparing it with $v_s$ in **m/h** — units must match.
> 3. Mixing up **detention time** with **flow-through time** when considering inlet/outlet zones. Use $t_d = V/Q$ **only for the settling volume**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Sedimentation is the **gravity-based removal** of suspended particles from water/wastewater.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law** is valid only for the **laminar regime** ($Re < 0.2$).
- ✅ The **Hazen model** assumes plug flow and ideal conditions; in practice, efficiency is **lower** due to turbulence and short-circuiting.
- ✅ The **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)** is the most critical design parameter — the tank's surface area determines capacity, **not its depth**.
- ✅ The **four types of settling** are: **Discrete (I), Flocculent (II), Zone/Hindered (III), Compression (IV)**.
- ✅ **Camp's scour criterion** must always be checked for grit chambers: $v_H < \sqrt{\frac{8\beta(S_s-1)g d}{f}}$.
- ✅ Detention time for **primary clarifiers** ≈ 2–4 h; for **secondary clarifiers** ≈ 1.5–2.5 h.
- ✅ Typical **weir loading rate** ≤ 300 m³/(m·d) for water treatment.
- ✅ Remember unit conversions: **1 MLD = 1000 m³/day = 1.157 L/s**.
- ✅ Always verify the **Reynolds number** after applying Stokes' Law — examiners love to test this.
- ✅ For **flocculent settling**, conduct **settling column tests** to construct the **percent removal vs SOR curve**.
- ✅ The removal efficiency $\eta = v_s/(v_s + v_0)$ is for **discrete settling only**.

> **Final Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked a design question on settling tanks, always present your answer in the structured format — (1) Given Data, (2) Assumptions, (3) Governing Equation, (4) Calculation, (5) Result with Units, (6) Validation/Check (Re, scour, etc.). This earns **full marks** and demonstrates mastery to the examiner.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stoke's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stoke's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoke's Law (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Stoke's Law states that the terminal settling velocity of a small spherical particle falling under gravity through a viscous fluid is directly proportional to the square of the particle diameter, the acceleration due to gravity, and the difference in densities between the particle and the fluid; and inversely proportional to the dynamic viscosity of the fluid.*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$v_s = \frac{g}{18} \cdot \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{\mu}$$

Where:
- $v_s$ = Terminal settling velocity of the particle (m/s)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho_s$ = Density of the particle (kg/m³)
- $\rho_w$ = Density of water/fluid (kg/m³)
- $d$ = Diameter of the spherical particle (m)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (N·s/m² or Pa·s)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble in Honey" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Falling Marble in Honey**
>
> Imagine dropping a small marble into a tall jar of honey. The marble does not accelerate indefinitely; it quickly reaches a constant speed (a *cruising velocity*). Why? Because as it falls, the honey resists the motion by creating a **drag force** upward. When the downward gravitational pull exactly equals the upward drag, the marble stops accelerating and falls at a steady *terminal velocity*.
>
> **Stoke's Law mathematically describes this exact scenario** for tiny particles in fluids like water — the foundation of every **sedimentation tank** in municipal water treatment!

Think of it this way:
- A **bigger marble** (larger $d$) → falls faster (settles quicker) because gravity wins.
- **Thicker honey** (higher $\mu$) → marble falls slower.
- A **denser marble** (higher $\rho_s$) → falls faster.

## 1.3 Historical Context & Engineering Significance

Stoke's Law was formulated by **Sir George Gabriel Stokes** (Irish mathematician and physicist) in **1851** as part of his work on fluid dynamics (*Stokes' Law* — note the possessive apostrophe in proper nomenclature, though engineering texts commonly use "Stoke's Law"). It is one of the foundational pillars of:

| Engineering Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Water Treatment | Design of primary sedimentation tanks |
| Wastewater Engineering | Grit chambers, primary clarifiers |
| Air Pollution Control | Particle settling in atmospheric science |
| Geotechnical Engineering | Soil sedimentation analysis |
| Chemical Engineering | Solid-liquid separation in clarifiers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Stoke's Law is the **mathematical backbone of Module 1 (Water Treatment)** in PCCET503. Questions on settling velocity, design of sedimentation tanks, and Type I (discrete) settling are directly derived from this single equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Mechanism — What Actually Happens When a Particle Settles

Consider a small spherical particle released in a quiescent (still) fluid. The particle undergoes three distinct stages:

1. **Stage 1 — Initial Acceleration:** The particle begins to fall from rest. Gravity dominates; velocity increases.
2. **Stage 2 — Transient Phase:** As velocity increases, the viscous drag force grows in proportion to velocity. Net force decreases.
3. **Stage 3 — Terminal Settling Velocity ($v_s$):** Drag force equals the net gravitational force. **Net force = 0**; particle falls at constant velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:**
> Stoke's Law is valid only in **Stage 3** (steady-state) AND under the condition that the flow regime around the particle is **laminar (streamline)**, characterized by a particle Reynolds number $Re < 1$.

## 2.2 Forces Acting on a Settling Particle

Three forces govern the motion of a spherical particle settling in a fluid:

| Force | Direction | Magnitude Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight (W)** | Downward | $W = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot V_p$ | Gravitational pull on the particle mass |
| **Buoyancy Force ($F_b$)** | Upward | $F_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_p$ | Fluid displaced by the particle resists sinking |
| **Drag Force ($F_d$)** | Upward (opposes motion) | $F_d = 3 \pi \mu v_s d$ | Viscous resistance of fluid to particle motion |

Where $V_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$ is the volume of a spherical particle.

## 2.3 Assumptions and Validity Conditions of Stoke's Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Frequently Tests These Assumptions:**

- The particle is **spherical** in shape.
- The particle is **rigid** and does not deform during settling.
- The fluid is **homogeneous, isotropic, and infinite in extent** (no wall effects).
- The flow around the particle is **laminar** ($Re < 1$ for strict validity, up to $Re \approx 2$ for practical use).
- The particle settles **individually** (no flocculation, no hindered settling).
- The particle and fluid temperatures are **constant** (no convective currents).
- Settling occurs in the **Stokes' regime** of the $C_D$ vs $Re$ curve.
- The particle is **small** (typically diameter < 0.1 mm in water at 20°C).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the ONLY quick reference you need for Stoke's Law problems in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \, \mu}$ | Stoke's Law (terminal settling velocity) | m/s |
| 2 | $F_d = 3 \pi \mu v_s d$ | Viscous drag force (Stokes' regime) | N |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot v_s \cdot d}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number (validity check) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re}$ | Drag coefficient in Stokes' regime | dimensionless |
| 5 | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \, \nu \, \rho_w}$ | Stoke's Law in kinematic viscosity form ($\nu = \mu/\rho_w$) | m/s |
| 6 | $V_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$ | Volume of spherical particle | m³ |
| 7 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho_w}$ | Kinematic viscosity relation | m²/s |
| 8 | $F_{net} = W - F_b - F_d = 0$ | Force balance at terminal velocity | N |
| 9 | $C_D = \dfrac{4 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d}{3 \rho_w v_s^2}$ | General drag coefficient (used to identify settling regime) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (s - 1) \cdot d^2}{18 \, \nu}$ | Compact form using specific gravity $s = \rho_s / \rho_w$ | m/s |

## 2.5 The Stoke's Regime in the $C_D$–$Re$ Curve

The relationship between the drag coefficient $C_D$ and the Reynolds number $Re$ is divided into distinct settling regimes:

| Regime | $Re$ Range | Applicable Law | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stokes' Regime** | $Re < 1$ (practical up to $\approx 2$) | $C_D = 24/Re$ | ✓ Stoke's Law valid |
| **Allied/AII Regime** | $1 < Re < 10^4$ | $C_D = 24/Re + 3/\sqrt{Re} + 0.34$ | Transition (intermediate) |
| **Newton's Regime** | $10^4 < Re < 10^5$ | $C_D \approx 0.4$ (constant) | Turbulent settling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:**
> *"For what range of Reynolds number is Stoke's Law applicable?"*
> **Answer:** $Re < 1$ (strict mathematical validity) or $Re \le 2$ (practical engineering use).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Environmental Engineering (KTU PCCET503)**, Stoke's Law is critically used to:

- **Design Sedimentation Tanks:** The overflow rate (= surface loading rate) must exceed $v_s$ to ensure particle removal.
- **Determine Particle Removal Efficiency:** Based on settling velocity distribution.
- **Size Grit Chambers:** To separate inorganic grit from organic matter in wastewater.
- **Analyze Type I (Discrete) Settling:** Where particles do not flocculate — purely governed by Stoke's Law.
- **Estimate Suspended Solids Removal:** In raw water treatment plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Stoke's Law

### Step 1: Identify the Forces

Consider a spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_s$ settling at terminal velocity $v_s$ through a fluid of density $\rho_w$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$.

**Forces acting on the particle:**

1. **Weight (acting downward):**
$$W = m \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot V_p \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g$$

2. **Buoyancy Force (acting upward, Archimedes' Principle):**
$$F_b = \rho_w \cdot V_p \cdot g = \rho_w \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g$$

3. **Viscous Drag Force (acting upward, opposing motion):**
$$F_d = 3 \pi \mu v_s d \quad \text{(Stokes' drag for a sphere in laminar flow)}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Equilibrium Condition at Terminal Velocity

At terminal (steady-state) settling velocity, the net force on the particle is zero. The downward weight is balanced by the upward buoyancy and drag forces:

$$W = F_b + F_d$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\rho_s \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g = \rho_w \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g + 3 \pi \mu v_s d$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Equation

Rearrange to isolate the drag term on one side:

$$\rho_s \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g - \rho_w \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g = 3 \pi \mu v_s d$$

Factor out the common terms on the left-hand side:

$$\left(\frac{\pi d^3 g}{6}\right) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \pi \mu v_s d$$

### Step 4: Solve for Terminal Settling Velocity $v_s$

Divide both sides by $3 \pi \mu d$:

$$v_s = \frac{\left(\frac{\pi d^3 g}{6}\right) (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{3 \pi \mu d}$$

Simplify the algebra — note that $\frac{\pi d^3}{6} \div 3 \pi d = \frac{d^2}{18}$:

$$v_s = \frac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{18 \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result — Stoke's Law:**
> $$\boxed{v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}}$$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of Kinematic Viscosity and Specific Gravity

Using $\nu = \mu / \rho_w$ and $s = \rho_s / \rho_w$, we can write:

$$v_s = \frac{g \cdot (s - 1) \cdot d^2}{18 \nu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This alternative form is very useful in KTU numerical problems where specific gravity $s$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu$ (in m²/s or Stokes) are given.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Drag Coefficient Relation $C_D = 24 / Re$

The general drag force equation is:

$$F_d = C_D \cdot A_p \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$$

Where $A_p = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ is the projected (cross-sectional) area of the sphere.

At terminal settling velocity, the net effective gravitational force equals the drag:

$$\left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$$

Rearranging to solve for $C_D$:

$$C_D = \frac{4 \, g \, d \, (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{3 \, \rho_w \, v_s^2}$$

Now, equating this with Stokes' drag ($F_d = 3 \pi \mu v_s d$):

$$C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2} = 3 \pi \mu v_s d$$

Simplify:

$$C_D \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{8} = \frac{3 \mu v_s}{d}$$

$$C_D = \frac{24 \, \mu}{\rho_w v_s d} = \frac{24}{Re}$$

Since $Re = \frac{\rho_w v_s d}{\mu}$:

$$\boxed{C_D = \frac{24}{Re}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This confirms that in the Stokes' regime ($Re < 1$), the drag coefficient is a hyperbolic function of the Reynolds number — the cornerstone of Type I settling analysis.

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem — KTU Pattern (10-Mark Style):**
>
> A spherical sand particle of diameter **0.05 mm** and density **2650 kg/m³** settles in water at **20°C**. Calculate:
> (i) Terminal settling velocity
> (ii) Particle Reynolds number
> (iii) Verify whether Stoke's Law is applicable
>
> **Given Data:**
> - $d = 0.05 \, \text{mm} = 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}$
> - $\rho_s = 2650 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
> - $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (at 20°C)
> - $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{N·s/m}^2$ (water at 20°C)
> - $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

### Solution:

**(i) Terminal Settling Velocity:**

$$v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \, \mu}$$

$$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Compute numerator step-by-step:

$$9.81 \times 1650 = 16186.5$$

$$(5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 25 \times 10^{-10} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 16186.5 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} = 40466.25 \times 10^{-9} = 4.0466 \times 10^{-5}$$

Compute denominator:

$$18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} = 18.036 \times 10^{-3} = 0.018036$$

$$v_s = \frac{4.0466 \times 10^{-5}}{0.018036}$$

$$v_s = 2.244 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s} = 2.244 \, \text{mm/s}$$

**(ii) Particle Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho_w \cdot v_s \cdot d}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2.244 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 2.244 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.122 \times 10^{-4}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = 0.1120$$

**(iii) Validity Check:**

Since $Re = 0.1120 < 1$, the flow regime is **laminar (Stokes' regime)**.

> **∴ Stoke's Law IS APPLICABLE for this problem.** ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Shortcut (KTU):** If you obtain $Re < 1$, write one crisp line: *"Since $Re < 1$, flow is in the Stokes' regime, hence the assumption of Stoke's Law is valid."* This fetches full marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Form of Stoke's Law

| Problem Statement Includes | Use This Form | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity) is given | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$ | Direct form |
| $\nu$ (kinematic viscosity) is given | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (s - 1) \cdot d^2}{18 \nu}$ | Compact, fewer units conversions |
| Only specific gravity $s$ is given | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (s - 1) \cdot d^2}{18 \nu}$ | Need to lookup $\nu$ for water at given T |
| Particle Reynolds number is required | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot v_s \cdot d}{\mu}$ | Standard definition |
| Need to find $d$ for a given $v_s$ | Rearrange: $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu v_s}{g (\rho_s - \rho_w)}}$ | Inverse application |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow: Force Balance on a Settling Particle

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates the three fundamental forces and their interaction in the Stoke's Law derivation, mapped as a functional flow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SPHERICAL PARTICLE RELEASED IN FLUID] --> B{Forces Acting on Particle}
    B --> C[WEIGHT W DOWNWARD]
    B --> D[BUOYANCY Fb UPWARD]
    B --> E[VISCOUS DRAG Fd UPWARD]
    C --> F[Net Effective Force Fn DOWNWARD]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Particle Begins to Accelerate]
    G --> H[Velocity v Increases]
    H --> I[Drag Force Fd Grows Proportional to v]
    I --> J{Net Force equals ZERO?}
    J -->|NO| G
    J -->|YES| K[TERMINAL SETTLING VELOCITY vs REACHED]
    K --> L[Stoke Equation: vs = g rho s minus rho w d squared divided by 18 mu]
```

## 4.2 Settling Regimes — Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> Use this decision tree to identify the correct settling regime during KTU problem-solving.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PARTICLE SETTLES IN FLUID] --> B[CALCULATE Re equals rho w vs d divided by mu]
    B --> C{Re VALUE}
    C -->|Re less than 1| D[STOKES REGIME]
    C -->|1 to 10000| E[TRANSITION REGIME ALLIED LAW]
    C -->|Re greater than 10000| F[NEWTONS REGIME]
    D --> G[Cd equals 24 divided by Re]
    E --> H[Cd equals 24 by Re plus 3 by square root Re plus 0.34]
    F --> I[Cd equals 0.4 CONSTANT]
    G --> J[APPLY STOKES LAW]
    H --> K[USE INTERMEDIATE EQUATIONS]
    I --> L[USE NEWTONS LAW]
```

## 4.3 Application Architecture — Stoke's Law in a Water Treatment Plant

> [!NOTE]
> Block-level functional flow showing how Stoke's Law governs the sedimentation stage in a typical water treatment plant.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RAW WATER INLET] --> B[INTAKE STRUCTURE]
    B --> C[SCREENING]
    C --> D[GRIT REMOVAL CHAMBER]
    D --> E[PRIMARY SEDIMENTATION TANK]
    E --> F[DESIGN GOVERNED BY STOKES LAW]
    F --> G[Terminal Velocity vs Calculation]
    F --> H[Re Validity Check Re less than 1]
    F --> I[Surface Overflow Rate SOR greater than vs]
    G --> J[PARTICLE REMOVAL EFFICIENCY]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[CLARIFIED WATER TO FILTRATION]
    J --> L[SLUDGE TO TREATMENT]
```
