Hybrid Attached Growth"]:::rbc Clarifier["Secondary Clarifier"]:::unit Disinfect["Chlorination / UV"]:::unit Effluent["Treated Effluent
Se <= 20 mg/L BOD"]:::end Sludge["Sludge Digestion
and Drying Bed"]:::sludge Influent --> Primary Primary --> Distribute Distribute -->|Large Land| Pond Distribute -->|Moderate| TF Distribute -->|Small| ASP Distribute -->|Very Small with Skid Mounting| RBC ASP --> Clarifier TF --> Clarifier Pond --> Disinfect RBC --> Clarifier Clarifier --> Disinfect Disinfect --> Effluent Primary -.-> Sludge Clarifier -.-> Sludge ASP -.-> Sludge TF -.-> Sludge RBC -.-> Sludge classDef start fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef end fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef asp fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000 classDef tf fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000 classDef pond fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000 classDef rbc fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,color:#000 classDef unit fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#334155,color:#000 classDef sludge fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Advantages vs. Limitations Radar Block (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ASP A1["✓ High BOD removal 90%"]:::pro A2["✓ Small footprint"]:::pro A3["✗ High energy for aeration"]:::con A4["✗ Bulking sludge risk"]:::con end subgraph TF T1["✓ Low power need"]:::pro T2["✓ Shock load tolerant"]:::pro T3["✗ Larger land"]:::con T4["✗ Filter fly nuisance"]:::con end subgraph POND P1["✓ Lowest cost"]:::pro P2["✓ No skilled operator"]:::pro P3["✗ Very large land"]:::con P4["✗ Climate dependent"]:::con end classDef pro fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d classDef con fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ### 4.3 Process Selection Flow (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["Q1: Is the community
less than 5000 persons?"]:::q Q2["Q2: Is the land
more than 1 ha/MLD?"]:::q Q3["Q3: Is continuous
power available?"]:::q Q4["Q4: Are toxic
shock loads present?"]:::q R1["Recommendation: Septic Tank
or Imhoff Tank"]:::rec R2["Recommendation: Oxidation Pond
with Maturation Pond"]:::rec R3["Recommendation: Trickling Filter
High Rate Type"]:::rec R4["Recommendation: Activated Sludge
Conventional or Extended Aeration"]:::rec R5["Recommendation: RBC
or MBBR"]:::rec Q1 -->|YES| R1 Q1 -->|NO| Q2 Q2 -->|YES| Q3 Q3 -->|NO| R2 Q3 -->|YES| Q4 Q4 -->|YES| R3 Q4 -->|NO| R4 Q2 -->|NO| R5 classDef q fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 classDef rec fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *List any **three advantages** of the Activated Sludge Process over the Trickling Filter.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Higher BOD removal efficiency** — ASP achieves 85–95 % BOD reduction versus 75–90 % for TF, producing a clearer, more stable effluent suitable for discharge into sensitive Kerala receiving waters (backwaters). 2. **Smaller footprint** — ASP requires only 0.05–0.10 ha/MLD versus 0.15–0.25 ha/MLD for TF, making it ideal for space-constrained urban municipalities like Ernakulam. 3. **Greater operational flexibility** — F/M ratio, SRT, and DO can be dynamically adjusted to handle diurnal variations, whereas TF's biofilm responds more slowly to changes. --- **A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *State **three limitations** of Oxidation Pond in the context of Kerala's monsoon climate.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Climate dependence** — Kerala's monsoon (June–September) brings overcast skies and reduced solar irradiance (down from 5.5 to ~2.0 kWh/m²/day), cutting algal photosynthesis and oxygen production by **40–60 %**, sharply dropping BOD removal efficiency. 2. **Large land requirement** — Facultative ponds need ≈ **1 hectare per 100 m³/day**, often unavailable in dense Kerala panchayats. 3. **Overflow and washout risk** — Heavy monsoon inflow (3× dry-weather flow) can cause short-circuiting, hydraulic washout of biomass, and overflow of untreated sewage. --- ### 📕 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 contributes a 14-mark question with **internal choice** between two alternatives. The model answer below uses the **KTU board examiner's valuation key style**: 7 marks per sub-part, with sub-divisions (i), (ii) etc. for granular marking. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** Compare the **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** and the **Trickling Filter (TF)** on the basis of: (i) Energy consumption (ii) Footprint (iii) Sensitivity to shock loads (iv) Effluent quality (v) Sludge production (vi) Skill level required (vii) Capital cost *(7 marks)* **(b)** For a **Kerala municipality** of 60,000 population discharging 8.1 MLD of sewage with $S_0 = 280$ mg/L, justify which of the two processes (ASP or TF) you would recommend. Use relevant formulas. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Answer:** #### (a) Comparative Tabulation — ASP vs TF *(7 marks — 1 mark per criterion)* | Criterion | Activated Sludge Process | Trickling Filter | |---|---|---| | (i) **Energy** | High — 1.0–1.8 kWh/m³ treated (blowers for diffused aeration) | Low–Moderate — 0.3–0.6 kWh/m³ (only rotary distributor + recirculation pump) | | (ii) **Footprint** | Small — 0.05–0.10 ha/MLD | Moderate — 0.15–0.30 ha/MLD | | (iii) **Shock Load Tolerance** | Poor — biomass can be washed out, takes days to recover | **Good** — biofilm acts as a buffer | | (iv) **Effluent Quality** | **Excellent** — BOD ≤ 20 mg/L, suspended solids ≤ 30 mg/L | Good — BOD ≤ 30 mg/L, suspended solids ≤ 40 mg/L | | (v) **Sludge Production** | High — 0.4–0.6 kg/kg BOD removed | Lower — 0.2–0.3 kg/kg BOD removed | | (vi) **Skill Level** | High — requires MLSS, SVI, F/M monitoring daily | Moderate — periodic distributor speed and recirculation checks | | (vii) **Capex (₹ Lakhs/MLD)** | 50–70 | 40–55 | **Conclusion:** ASP gives superior effluent in a compact footprint but at the cost of energy and skilled operation. TF is robust and economical for smaller loads. #### (b) Recommendation for 60,000-population Kerala Municipality *(7 marks)* **Step 1: Design flow and load** *(1 mark)* $$Q = P \times q = 60{,}000 \times 135 = 8.1 \text{ MLD}$$ $$Q \times S_0 = 8.1 \times 10^3 \times 280 = 2{,}268 \text{ kg BOD/day}$$ **Step 2: Volume of aeration tank for ASP** *(2 marks)* Assume conventional loading $F/M = 0.3$/day and $X = 2500$ mg/L $= 2.5$ kg/m³: $$V_{ASP} = \dfrac{Q S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)} = \dfrac{2268}{2.5 \times 0.3} = 3024 \text{ m}^3$$ HRT: $$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{3024}{8100} = 0.373 \text{ day} = 8.95 \text{ hr} \;\; (\text{within 4–8 hr spec, ok})$$ **Step 3: Trickling Filter volume** *(2 marks)* Using standard organic loading $O LR = 0.3$ kg BOD/m³/day: $$V_{TF} = \dfrac{2268}{0.3} = 7560 \text{ m}^3$$ With depth 1.8 m, area required: $$A_{TF} = \dfrac{7560}{1.8} = 4200 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4: Land comparison and recommendation** *(2 marks)* - ASP aeration tank area = $V_{ASP}/H_{depth}$, with $H=4.5$ m: $$A_{ASP} = 3024/4.5 = 672 \text{ m}^2$$ Add clarifier (≈ 1.5×): total ≈ **1000 m² ≈ 0.1 ha** ✓ - TF area = 4200 m² = **0.42 ha**, plus 0.15 ha clarifier ≈ **0.57 ha**. **Both are feasible.** Given that an urban Kerala municipality has **land scarcity** but **continuous KSERC power supply and trained operators** at the ULB, **ASP is recommended** for its: - Smaller footprint (1000 m² vs 4200 m²) - Better effluent quality (BOD ≤ 20 vs ≤ 30 mg/L) — Kerala backwater discharge norms (KSPCB) demand ≤ 20 mg/L - Operational flexibility for future population growth. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. Students often forget to multiply ASP tank area by 1.5 for clarifier → land underestimation. > 2. Confusing HRT (hours) with SRT (days) — examiners specifically look for correct units. > 3. Writing "ASP is best" without comparing **both quantitatively** — at least **2 numerical steps are mandatory** for 7 marks. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** With the help of a **decision matrix**, justify when to select an **oxidation pond** over ASP. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A rural Kerala panchayat (population 8,000) generates 1.0 MLD of domestic sewage with $S_0 = 220$ mg/L. The site has 4.0 hectares of unused land and **no reliable grid power** (average availability 3 hours/day). Design a **facultative oxidation pond** and discuss the **limitations** of this design in the Kerala monsoon context. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Answer (Concise Outline):** #### (a) Decision Matrix — Oxidation Pond vs ASP *(7 marks)* | Criterion | Score: Pond | Score: ASP | Winner | |---|---|---|---| | Land Available > 1 ha/MLD | 10 (passes) | 10 (passes) | Tie | | Power Available < 4 hr/day | **10 (passes)** | 2 (fails) | **Pond** | | Operator Skill Available | 10 (passes) | 3 (fails) | **Pond** | | Effluent BOD Standard ≤ 20 mg/L | 7 (marginal) | **10 (passes)** | ASP | | Capital Cost Constraint | **10 (cheaper)** | 5 | **Pond** | | Sludge Handling Capacity | **9 (low sludge)** | 5 | **Pond** | **Inference:** Pond is preferred when **all four** of: abundant land, limited power, limited skilled manpower, and low budget coincide — typically in **rural Kerala panchayats and tribal hamlets** (Wayanad, Idukki). #### (b) Pond Design for 1.0 MLD, 4.0 ha Land, No Reliable Power *(7 marks)* **Step 1: Pond area using Marais-Shaw / Standard Kerala CPHEEO design** *(2 marks)* For facultative pond with $S_0 = 220$ mg/L: - Detention time $\theta = 25$ days (CPHEEO manual) - Depth $H = 1.2$ m $$V = Q \times \theta = 1000 \times 25 = 25{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$ $$A_{pond} = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{1.2} = 20{,}834 \text{ m}^2 \approx 2.1 \text{ ha}$$ **Available land = 4.0 ha** ✓ (Adequate. Add maturation pond 1.0 ha → total 3.1 ha. Spare land allows future expansion.) **Step 2: Effluent BOD estimate** *(2 marks)* Using first-order areal model: $S_e/S_0 = \exp(-k_a \cdot A/Q \cdot \theta)$ with $k_a = 0.12$ /day at 28 °C: $$\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp(-0.12 \times 25) = \exp(-3.0) = 0.0498$$ $$S_e = 220 \times 0.0498 = 11 \text{ mg/L} \;\; (\text{Excellent effluent!})$$ **Step 3: Limitations in Kerala Monsoon** *(3 marks)* 1. **Dilution + washout** — Influent flow can increase 3–4× during monsoon, reducing $\theta$ from 25 to ~7 days, raising $S_e$ to ~80 mg/L. 2. **Reduced solar irradiance** — Photolytic oxygen drops 50 % during June–August overcast periods; BOD removal efficiency drops to 60–70 %. 3. **Overflow risk** — Pond freeboard (typically 0.5 m) may be insufficient for 100-year flood events; Kerala's 2018 floods demonstrated this. 4. **Mosquito breeding** — Stagnant water with rich organics becomes a *Culex* breeding site; public health issue. 5. **Groundwater contamination** — In Kerala's laterite soil, percolation from unlined pond can carry nitrate + pathogens to shallow wells. **Mitigations:** add 2–3 facultative ponds in series, line pond with HDPE, raise freeboard to 1.0 m, and add maturation pond for pathogen reduction. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall Alert (Question B):** > 1. Many students use $\theta = 5$–$10$ days (wrong for facultative pond — must be **20–30 days** per CPHEEO). > 2. Forgetting that $k_a$ depends on temperature; quoting $k_a = 0.1$ at 20 °C is wrong for Kerala's 28 °C. Use $k_a = 0.12$–$0.15$. > 3. Not mentioning the **monsoon** as a specific limitation in the Kerala context (only generic answers score ≤ 4/7). --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **ASP** → high BOD removal, **small footprint**, but **high energy + skilled operation**. - ✅ **TF** → **low energy**, robust, but **larger land** and **filter fly nuisance**. - ✅ **Oxidation Pond** → **cheapest, simplest, Kerala-friendly**, but **land-hungry and climate-sensitive**. - ✅ **RBC** → hybrid, low sludge, but **high capital** (imported media). - ✅ Always support "advantages and limitations" with **at least one numerical justification** (F/M, HRT, area, or $S_e$ calculation). - ✅ **Kerala context** (land scarcity in cities, abundance in rural; tropical 28 °C; monsoon 4 months) must be explicitly mentioned in design justification. - ✅ **CPHEEO Manual** values to memorize: $F/M = 0.3$/day, MLSS = 2500 mg/L, $\theta = 6$ hr, pond $\theta = 25$ days, pond depth = 1.2 m. - ✅ **Decision mnemonic — "LEAD"** for pond design: **L**and available, **E**nergy absent, **A**lgae sunlight, **D**ilute wastewater (BOD < 300 mg/L). - ✅ Examiner **deducts marks** for: missing units, swapping SRT and HRT, not comparing both processes in same table, ignoring KSPCB discharge norms (BOD ≤ 20 mg/L into inland surface water).

topics.title: Layout of Water Distribution Network - Types - Methods of Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Layout of Water Distribution Network - Types - Methods of Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Water Distribution Network** is the interconnected system of pipes, valves, fittings, storage reservoirs, pumps, and service connections that conveys **potable treated water** from the service/balancing reservoir (or treatment plant) to the individual consumers in a community, ensuring adequate **quantity**, satisfactory **pressure**, and required **quality** at every tap.

The **Layout** of a water distribution network refers to the geometric arrangement or topological configuration of the **mains, sub-mains, branches, and service connections** within the command area of the city/town. The layout is the single most important planning decision in the design of an urban water supply scheme, as it determines hydraulic efficiency, reliability, and economic viability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to (a) sketch and differentiate the four standard pipe network layouts, (b) identify the most suitable layout for a given topography, and (c) justify the selection of distribution method (gravity / pumping / combined) based on elevation and source location.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City as a Human Body" View

Think of a water distribution network as the **human circulatory system**:

- **The Heart** = Service/balancing reservoir (impounds water).
- **The Arteries** = Primary mains (large diameter trunk lines).
- **The Veins** = Secondary and tertiary sub-mains and branches.
- **The Capillaries** = Service pipes connecting to individual houses.
- **The Heart Valves** = Sluice valves, butterfly valves, and air valves that control flow direction and pressure.

Just as a cardiologist must decide whether the arteries run in **branched** (tree-like) or **looped** (net-like) patterns, a public-health/civil engineer decides whether the city pipes will branch out like a tree, loop like a fishing net, radiate from a center, or form concentric rings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** The chosen layout dictates (i) the **head loss** in the system, (ii) the **residual pressure** at consumer ends, (iii) the **redundancy** during pipe bursts, and (iv) the **cost of the project**.

### 1.3 Key Performance Parameters (Standard KTU Metrics)

| Parameter | Standard Value (as per IS 1172 / CPHEEO Manual) |
|---|---|
| Residual pressure at consumer tap | **7 m to 17 m of water head** (recommended **7 m minimum**, **≤ 17 m** to avoid pipe damage) |
| Velocity in distribution main | **0.6 m/s to 1.8 m/s** (optimal ~ 0.9 m/s) |
| Peak factor (max hourly demand / avg hourly demand) | **2.0 to 3.0** |
| Fire demand duration | **2 to 4 hours** continuous supply |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember layout types — "D G C R":** **D**ead-end, **G**rid-iron, **C**ircular (Ring), **R**adial.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Standard Layouts of Water Distribution Networks

#### 2.1.1 Dead-End / Tree / Branched System

**Description:** A single main pipeline runs through the city center, with sub-mains branching off like a tree, and further branches from each sub-main terminating at dead ends.

**How it works:** Water flows in **one direction** only — from the trunk main to the branches. There is **no interconnection** between branch ends.

**Why use it:** It is the **simplest, cheapest, and easiest** to design and lay. Suits old, organic, irregular cities that developed over time without planning.

**Key Drawbacks (must remember for KTU):**
- Water **stagnates at dead ends** → foul smell, color, and bacterial contamination.
- During pipe repair, the **entire downstream branch is cut off**.
- **Unequal pressure** distribution — far ends suffer low pressure.
- **High head loss** due to dead-end friction build-up.
- Disadvantages are so serious that **CPHEEO discourages** this layout for new projects.

#### 2.1.2 Grid-Iron / Reticular / Interlaced System

**Description:** The entire city is divided into a **rectangular/grid network** of mains, with every street having a pipe and all pipes forming a closed mesh of loops (no dead ends).

**How it works:** Water reaches any point from **multiple directions** through the loops. Each sub-main is fed from **two or more sides**.

**Why it is preferred:**
- Water **circulates continuously** → no stagnation, freshness maintained.
- During a pipe burst/repair, the affected section is **isolated by valves**, and supply continues from the other sides of the loop.
- **More uniform pressure** distribution throughout the area.
- Fire hydrants can draw large volumes without collapsing the network pressure.

**Drawbacks:**
- **Costly** due to longer total pipe length and more valves/fittings.
- **Complex hydraulic analysis** required (Hardy Cross method) to determine flow in each loop.
- Needs careful valve placement for effective isolation.

#### 2.1.3 Circular / Ring System

**Description:** A **large-diameter circular main** is laid around the entire city, with branch mains radiating inward from this ring to the city center. Sometimes multiple concentric rings are provided (outer ring + inner ring + radial branches).

**How it works:** Water enters the ring from one or more feed points, then **flows in both directions** around the ring to supply all radial branches.

**Why it is used:**
- Excellent for cities with **circular/oval development patterns**.
- Combines benefits of grid-iron (no dead ends) with reduced pipe length.
- Good **fire-fighting capacity** due to large-diameter ring main.

**Drawbacks:**
- Requires **accurate flow analysis**.
- If a single feed point fails, the entire ring is affected unless multiple feeds are provided.

#### 2.1.4 Radial System

**Description:** The city is divided into **distinct distribution zones**, each served by a **separate distribution reservoir (DRes)** located at the **center of the zone**. From each DRes, mains radiate outward like **spokes of a wheel**.

**How it works:** Water flows **outward radially** from each central reservoir. No interconnections between zones in their mains (though inter-zonal transfer mains may exist for emergencies).

**Why it is used:**
- Best suited for **well-planned modern cities** with clearly defined zones.
- Gives **very high pressures** at zone centers → excellent for tall buildings.
- **Short pipe length per zone** → lower head loss, smaller pump sizes.
- Easy to design — each zone is hydraulically independent.

**Drawbacks:**
- Requires **multiple service reservoirs** → high capital cost.
- Each zone must have its own **elevated storage (ESR/ELSR)** for pressure boosting.
- If the central reservoir of a zone fails, the **entire zone goes dry** (poor redundancy).

### 2.2 Methods of Water Distribution

The **method of distribution** refers to the **hydraulic mechanism** by which water is delivered — i.e., how the necessary pressure head is generated and maintained at consumer ends.

#### Method 1: Gravity Distribution System

- The **service reservoir (SR)** is located at an elevation **sufficiently higher** than the highest consumer point such that the **hydraulic gradient line (HGL)** remains above the rooftops.
- Required condition: **Elevation of SR (R.L.) ≥ Highest consumer elevation + 7 m (min head) + friction losses**.
- **No pumping cost** during distribution — only capital cost of the elevated reservoir.
- **Most economical** where topography permits.

#### Method 2: Pumping (Pumped) Distribution System

- Water is **directly pumped** into the mains from the treatment plant / sump.
- No (or minimal) elevated storage — sometimes a small **ground-level service reservoir (GSR)** acts as a suction tank.
- **Continuous pumping** required → high electricity cost.
- Used when the city is **flat** and there is no high ground nearby.

#### Method 3: Combined Gravity and Pumping System

- This is the **most common system** in practice.
- Water is **pumped** during low-demand hours to fill an **Elevated Service Reservoir (ESR)** located at a suitable elevation.
- During peak demand, water is supplied by **gravity from the ESR**; the pump is also kept running in parallel.
- **Best of both worlds:** lower pumping cost (off-peak electricity tariff can be used) + reliable pressure during emergencies.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \times V$ | $Q$ = discharge, $A$ = pipe area, $V$ = velocity | $m^3/s$, $m^2$, $m/s$ | Basic flow continuity |
| 2 | $h_f = \dfrac{4.73 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}}$ (Hazen-Williams) | $L$ = length, $Q$ = flow, $C$ = HW coefficient, $d$ = diameter | $m$, $m^3/s$, $-$, $m$ | Head loss in distribution mains |
| 3 | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ (Darcy-Weisbach) | $f$ = friction factor, $g$ = 9.81 $m/s^2$ | $m$ | Frictional head loss |
| 4 | $\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$ | Continuity at a node | $m^3/s$ | Hardy Cross / nodal analysis |
| 5 | $\sum h_f = 0$ in each loop | Sum of head losses around any closed loop = 0 | $m$ | Hardy Cross convergence criterion |
| 6 | $H_{residual} = H_{ESR} - h_f - \Delta z$ | Pressure head at consumer | $m$ | Pressure check at consumer |
| 7 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000 \cdot \eta}$ | Power required to lift water | $kW$ | Pump sizing |
| 8 | $V_{storage} = (P_{max} - P_{avg}) \times T$ | Required ESR storage volume | $m^3$ | ESR capacity design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardy Cross Method** is the iterative procedure to solve for **unknown pipe discharges** in a looped network. It assumes an initial distribution of flow that satisfies continuity, then iteratively corrects each loop by $\Delta Q = -\dfrac{\sum h_f}{n \sum \vert h_f \vert / Q}$ until $\sum h_f \approx 0$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban planning:** Municipal corporations choose layout based on **master plan** of the city.
- **Fire engineering:** A looped/grid system is mandatory in **high-fire-risk zones** (old city centers, industrial areas).
- **Disaster resilience:** The **redundancy** in grid/circular layouts means a single pipe break does not cut off water to any zone.
- **Smart water networks:** Modern SCADA-controlled distribution uses **DMA (District Metered Area)** concept, which is essentially a **radial zoning** approach with smart sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the ESR must be at a specific minimum elevation

For gravity supply to the most **unfavorably located** (highest + farthest) consumer, the **hydraulic grade line (HGL)** at the consumer end must be ≥ the consumer's **topmost fixture** (usually a rooftop tank) plus a residual head of **7 m**.

Let us define the elevation datum at **mean sea level (MSL)** and let the various elevations be:

- $R_{ESR}$ = Reduced Level (R.L.) of the bottom of the Elevated Service Reservoir, in $m$.
- $R_c$ = R.L. of the highest consumer's rooftop tank, in $m$.
- $L$ = Total length of the main pipe from ESR to the consumer, in $m$.
- $Q$ = Peak-hour discharge, in $m^3/s$.
- $d$ = Diameter of the main, in $m$.
- $C$ = Hazen-Williams coefficient (≈ 130 for new CI/steel, 100 for old CI).

The **HGL at the ESR** equals $R_{ESR}$ (since the water surface in the ESR is essentially at the same elevation as the pipe invert at entry). As water flows toward the consumer, the HGL drops continuously due to **friction loss** $h_f$ and **minor losses** $h_m$ in valves, bends, etc.

**Step 1 — Write the energy equation** between the ESR water surface (point A) and the consumer entry point (point B):

$$
R_{ESR} = R_c + 7 + h_f + h_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express the friction loss** using Hazen-Williams:

$$
h_f = \frac{4.73 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express minor losses** as a fraction $\Sigma K$ of the velocity head:

$$
h_m = \Sigma K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}, \quad \text{where} \quad V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the required ESR elevation:**

$$
R_{ESR} = R_c + 7 + \frac{4.73 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} + \Sigma K \cdot \frac{\left(4Q / \pi d^2\right)^2}{2g}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- For a fixed consumer elevation $R_c$, **higher $Q$ or longer $L$** → higher required $R_{ESR}$.
- This is why ESR is built on the **highest ground** in a gravity-supplied city, and why **tall buildings require booster pumps**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hardy Cross Correction per Loop

Consider a single closed loop with $N$ pipes. Let $Q_i$ be the assumed flow in pipe $i$, and $h_{f,i}$ the corresponding head loss.

**Step 1 — Define head loss in each pipe** using a generic formula of the form $h_f = K \cdot Q^n$, where $n = 1.85$ for Hazen-Williams and $n = 2$ for Darcy-Weisbach.

$$
h_{f,i} = K_i \cdot Q_i^{\,n}, \quad \text{where} \quad K_i = \frac{4.73 \cdot L_i}{C_i^{1.85} \cdot d_i^{4.87}} \;\; \text{(Hazen-Williams)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the energy conservation criterion** around the loop (taking clockwise as positive):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} h_{f,i} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a small correction** $\Delta Q$ (assumed the same for all pipes in the loop) to the assumed flows. The corrected flow in pipe $i$ is $Q_i + \Delta Q$ with the sign convention respected.

$$
\sum K_i (Q_i + \Delta Q)^n = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Expand using the binomial approximation** $(Q_i + \Delta Q)^n \approx Q_i^n + n Q_i^{n-1} \Delta Q$ (valid for $\Delta Q \ll Q_i$):

$$
\sum K_i Q_i^n + \Delta Q \cdot n \cdot \sum K_i Q_i^{n-1} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize** that $K_i Q_i^n = h_{f,i}$ and $K_i Q_i^{n-1} = h_{f,i}/Q_i$. Rearranging:

$$
\Delta Q = -\frac{\sum h_{f,i}}{n \cdot \sum \left( h_{f,i} / Q_i \right)}
$$

This is the **Hardy Cross correction equation**, applied iteratively to each loop until $|\Delta Q| < \epsilon$ (convergence tolerance, typically $0.001 \cdot Q_{max}$).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Layouts

| Criterion | Dead-End | Grid-Iron | Circular (Ring) | Radial |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Suitability** | Old, unplanned cities | Well-planned rectangular cities | Cities with circular/oval shape | Modern zonal cities |
| **Pipe length required** | **Minimum** | **Maximum** | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Cost** | **Lowest** | **Highest** | Moderate-High | High (multiple reservoirs) |
| **Pressure uniformity** | Poor (far ends low) | Excellent | Good | Excellent (in zone) |
| **Reliability during repair** | **Very poor** (branch cut off) | **Excellent** (valve isolation only) | Good | Poor (entire zone affected) |
| **Water stagnation** | **Severe** at dead ends | None (continuous circulation) | None | None within zone |
| **Fire-fighting capacity** | Poor | Excellent | Very good | Good |
| **Design complexity** | Simple | Complex (Hardy Cross) | Moderate | Simple per zone |
| **CPHEEO recommendation** | Discouraged for new works | **Strongly recommended** | Recommended | Recommended for zonal cities |
| **KTU exam weightage** | High | **Very High** | Moderate | Moderate |

### 3.4 Selection Logic for KTU Problems

A KTU question often asks: *"Which layout is most suitable for a given city?"* Use this decision tree:

$$
\text{If city is old, irregular, low-budget} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Dead-End}
$$

$$
\text{If city is rectangular, planned, dense, high fire-risk} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Grid-Iron}
$$

$$
\text{If city has circular road pattern or topography} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Circular/Ring}
$$

$$
\text{If city is divided into planned zones with separate reservoirs} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Radial}
$$

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Q1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a dead-end system and a grid-iron system of water distribution. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dead-End System | Grid-Iron System |
|---|---|---|
| Pipe arrangement | Tree-like with terminal dead ends | Rectangular mesh of loops, no dead ends |
| Water flow | One-way only | Multi-directional, continuous circulation |
| Cost | Lowest | Highest |
| Reliability | Poor (single pipe failure = dry zone) | Excellent (valve isolation possible) |
| Design | Simple | Complex (needs Hardy Cross analysis) |

**Advantage (Dead-End):** Lowest cost, easy to design and lay.
**Disadvantage (Dead-End):** Stagnation of water at dead ends causes foul smell and contamination.
**Advantage (Grid-Iron):** Uniform pressure and excellent redundancy during repairs.
**Disadvantage (Grid-Iron):** Higher cost and complex hydraulic analysis.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Q2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. What is a radial system of water distribution? In what situation is it most suitable?**

**Model Answer:**

A **radial system** is a layout in which the city is divided into several **independent distribution zones**, each served by a **distribution reservoir located at the geometric center of the zone**. From each central reservoir, **mains radiate outward** like the spokes of a wheel, supplying the surrounding zone.

**Most suitable for:** Newly developed, **well-planned modern cities** with clearly defined sectors/colonies, especially those with **high-rise buildings** requiring strong pressure and where independent zone-wise water accounting is desired (e.g., smart water management with DMA — District Metered Areas).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 Internal Choice, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the four types of layouts of a water distribution network. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A city with a population of 50,000 has an average per capita demand of 150 lpcd. The ground level at the highest point of the city is at R.L. 32.0 m, and the proposed site for the Elevated Service Reservoir is at R.L. 60.0 m. The total length of the main from the ESR to the farthest consumer is 2.5 km. The peak factor is 2.5. Using Hazen-Williams coefficient $C = 130$ and velocity $V = 0.9$ m/s, determine the diameter of the main and check whether the system can supply the consumer by gravity. Assume a residual head of 7 m is required at the consumer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The four layouts are:
1. **Dead-End / Tree System** — single trunk with branches terminating in dead ends.
2. **Grid-Iron / Reticular System** — closed rectangular mesh, no dead ends.
3. **Circular / Ring System** — large ring main with radial sub-mains to the city center.
4. **Radial System** — zonal reservoirs with mains radiating outward to each zone.

(Detailed sketches as per diagrams in Section 4 above must be reproduced in the answer sheet.)

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Average daily demand:**
$$
Q_{avg} = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ L/day} = \frac{7{,}500{,}000}{86400} = 86.81 \text{ L/s} = 0.0868 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Peak hourly demand (apply peak factor 2.5):**
$$
Q_{peak} = 2.5 \times 0.0868 = 0.2170 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Diameter from velocity constraint** $V = 0.9$ m/s:
$$
A = \frac{Q_{peak}}{V} = \frac{0.2170}{0.9} = 0.2411 \text{ m}^2
$$
$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.2411}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{0.3070} = 0.554 \text{ m}
$$

Adopt the next standard size: **$d = 0.6$ m (600 mm)**.

**Step 4 — Recalculate actual velocity with $d = 0.6$ m:**
$$
A_{actual} = \frac{\pi \times 0.6^2}{4} = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2
$$
$$
V_{actual} = \frac{0.2170}{0.2827} = 0.768 \text{ m/s} \;\; \text{(within 0.6 to 1.8 m/s OK)}
$$

**Step 5 — Friction head loss** (Hazen-Williams, $L = 2500$ m, $C = 130$, $Q = 0.2170$ $m^3/s$):
$$
h_f = \frac{4.73 \times 2500 \times (0.2170)^{1.85}}{(130)^{1.85} \times (0.6)^{4.87}}
$$

Compute stepwise:
- $(0.2170)^{1.85}$: $\ln(0.217) = -1.527$, $\times 1.85 = -2.825$, $e^{-2.825} = 0.0593$
- $(130)^{1.85}$: $\ln(130) = 4.868$, $\times 1.85 = 9.005$, $e^{9.005} = 8125$
- $(0.6)^{4.87}$: $\ln(0.6) = -0.5108$, $\times 4.87 = -2.488$, $e^{-2.488} = 0.0834$

$$
h_f = \frac{4.73 \times 2500 \times 0.0593}{8125 \times 0.0834} = \frac{701.4}{677.6} = 1.035 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Available head check** (R.L. of ESR − R.L. of consumer − required residual):
$$
H_{available} = 60.0 - 32.0 - 7.0 = 21.0 \text{ m}
$$
$$
H_{required} = h_f + h_m = 1.035 + \text{(minor losses, assume 10% of } h_f) = 1.035 + 0.10 = 1.14 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
$$
H_{available} = 21.0 \text{ m} \;>\; H_{required} = 1.14 \text{ m} \;\; \checkmark
$$

Since available head (21 m) is **far greater** than the required head (1.14 m), the system can easily supply the consumer by **gravity**. The pipe diameter of **600 mm is adequate**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **three methods of water distribution**. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A combined gravity and pumping system is to be designed for a township. The following data are given: Population = 1,00,000; per capita demand = 135 lpcd; R.L. of lowest consumer = 25.0 m; R.L. of highest consumer = 38.0 m; R.L. of ESR site = 52.0 m; length of main from ESR to farthest point = 3.0 km; Hazen-Williams coefficient $C = 120$; peak factor = 2.0. Determine the **required diameter** of the main assuming velocity = 1.0 m/s and check whether gravity supply is possible. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The three methods are:
1. **Gravity System** — supply by gravity from elevated reservoir; cheap if topography permits.
2. **Pumping System** — direct pumping into mains; expensive in operation, no storage buffer.
3. **Combined Gravity and Pumping System** — pumping to fill ESR during off-peak, gravity supply during peak; most commonly adopted in practice.

| Method | Capital Cost | O&M Cost | Reliability | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gravity | Moderate | **Lowest** | High | Hilly terrain with high ground |
| Pumping | Lowest | **Highest** | Moderate (pump failure risk) | Flat terrain |
| Combined | Highest | Moderate | **Highest** | Universal — preferred for all towns |

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Average demand:**
$$
Q_{avg} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 135 = 1.35 \times 10^7 \text{ L/d} = 156.25 \text{ L/s} = 0.1563 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Peak demand:**
$$
Q_{peak} = 2.0 \times 0.1563 = 0.3125 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Diameter from velocity** $V = 1.0$ m/s:
$$
A = \frac{0.3125}{1.0} = 0.3125 \text{ m}^2 \;\Rightarrow\; d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.3125}{\pi}} = 0.631 \text{ m}
$$

Adopt standard size: **$d = 0.65$ m (650 mm)**.

**Step 4 — Available head** for highest consumer:
$$
H_{available} = 52.0 - 38.0 - 7.0 = 7.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Friction head loss** ($L = 3000$ m, $C = 120$, $Q = 0.3125$ $m^3/s$, $d = 0.65$ m):
$$
h_f = \frac{4.73 \times 3000 \times (0.3125)^{1.85}}{(120)^{1.85} \times (0.65)^{4.87}}
$$

Stepwise:
- $(0.3125)^{1.85}$: $\ln(0.3125) = -1.163$, $\times 1.85 = -2.151$, $e^{-2.151} = 0.1164$
- $(120)^{1.85}$: $\ln(120) = 4.787$, $\times 1.85 = 8.856$, $e^{8.856} = 7047$
- $(0.65)^{4.87}$: $\ln(0.65) = -0.4308$, $\times 4.87 = -2.098$, $e^{-2.098} = 0.1227$

$$
h_f = \frac{4.73 \times 3000 \times 0.1164}{7047 \times 0.1227} = \frac{1652.2}{864.7} = 1.911 \text{ m}
$$

With minor losses at 10%: $H_{required} = 1.911 + 0.191 = 2.10$ m.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
$$
H_{available} = 7.0 \text{ m} \;>\; H_{required} = 2.10 \text{ m} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Gravity supply is possible.** The 650 mm diameter main is adequate, with a margin of $7.0 - 2.10 = 4.9$ m for additional flows (e.g., fire demand).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT use $Q_{avg}$** for pipe sizing — always use **$Q_{peak}$ (or $Q_{max\ hourly}$)** with peak factor 2 to 3.
> 2. **Round UP** the calculated diameter to the next standard size (300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 600, 650, 700, 800, 900, 1000 mm).
> 3. **Forgetting minor losses** (10% of friction loss is a safe estimate) costs 1 mark. Always include a separate $h_m$ term.
> 4. **Sign convention** in Hardy Cross: clockwise flows positive; summing $h_f$ around a closed loop must equal **zero** at convergence.
> 5. Students often quote "residual head 7 m" as 7 m of pipe length — it is **7 m of water column pressure**, not pipe length.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **One-page revision summary for KTU Module 2:**

- [x] **Water distribution network** = pipe system from service reservoir to consumer tap.
- [x] **Four layouts — "D G C R":** Dead-End, Grid-Iron, Circular, Radial.
- [x] **Dead-End:** Cheapest, but stagnation and poor reliability. **Discouraged by CPHEEO** for new works.
- [x] **Grid-Iron:** Best all-rounder; most common in well-planned cities. Requires Hardy Cross analysis.
- [x] **Circular (Ring):** Suits circular/oval cities; large-diameter ring main.
- [x] **Radial:** Suits zonal modern cities; one ESR per zone.
- [x] **Three methods — Gravity / Pumping / Combined.** Combined is the **most widely adopted**.
- [x] **Gravity works only if:** $R_{ESR} \geq R_{consumer} + 7\text{ m} + h_f + h_m$.
- [x] **Standard residual pressure** at consumer = **7 m (min), 17 m (max)** of water head.
- [x] **Velocity in mains** = **0.6 to 1.8 m/s**, optimal ~ 0.9 m/s.
- [x] **Pipe sizing** is always based on **$Q_{peak} = Q_{avg} \times$ peak factor (2 to 3)**.
- [x] **Hardy Cross formula:** $\Delta Q = -\dfrac{\sum h_f}{n \sum (h_f / Q)}$; iterate till $\Delta Q \to 0$.
- [x] **Continuity** at every node: $\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$.
- [x] **Energy conservation** in every loop: $\sum h_f = 0$.
- [x] **ESR** = Elevated Service Reservoir; **GSR** = Ground Service Reservoir.
- [x] **Standard pipe diameters** (CI/DI): 80, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000 mm — always round up.
- [x] **Hazen-Williams** is the preferred formula for water supply networks (C ≈ 130 new, 100 old).
- [x] **CPHEEO Manual** is the Bible for Indian water supply design — frequently cited in KTU answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Analysis** in Environmental Engineering is the systematic application of fluid mechanics principles and hydraulic engineering to determine **discharges (flow rates)** and **pressure heads (piezometric heads)** at every node and pipe in a water distribution network, subject to two governing physical laws: **Continuity of flow at every junction** and **Conservation of energy around every closed loop.**

In the context of KTU Module 2 (Water Supply Engineering), network analysis specifically refers to the hydraulic analysis of **piped water distribution systems** serving a community — determining the flow in each pipe, the head loss across each pipe, and the residual pressure available at every demand node so that the system delivers the design discharge with adequate pressure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a water distribution network as the **"circulatory system of a city"**:
- The **pipes** are like **arteries and veins** carrying water (blood).
- The **reservoir/elevated tank (E.S.R.)** is the **heart**, pumping water into the system using gravitational potential energy.
- The **consumer tap points** are the **body cells** that consume water.
- The **valves** are like **constriction points** that regulate flow.
- The **pressure gauges** at junctions are like **blood-pressure monitors** showing the health of the network.

Just as a cardiologist must know the flow and pressure in every blood vessel to diagnose blockages, an environmental engineer must compute **Q (discharge)** in every pipe and **H (head)** at every node to design a network that is **adequate, equitable, and economical**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Students MUST Know:**
> - **Node (Junction):** Any point where two or more pipes meet, where a demand occurs, or where a reservoir/tank is located.
> - **Pipe Link:** The conduit connecting two nodes.
> - **Loop (Ring):** Any closed circuit of pipes in the network.
> - **Piezometric Head:** The elevation head plus the pressure head at a point (energy grade line elevation).
> - **Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL):** The line joining the piezometric heads at various points along the pipeline.

### 1.3 Types of Distribution Networks (KTU High-Yield)

| Network Type | Schematic Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead-End (Tree/Branched)** | Tree-like, no closed loops | Old cities, narrow irregular streets |
| **Radial (Dendritic)** | Flows outward from a central source/E.S.R. | Small towns, planned colonies |
| **Grid (Ring/Looped)** | Interconnected loops with multiple flow paths | Modern cities, reliable supply |
| **Combined** | Mix of grid + radial branches | Large metropolitan networks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite Question:** *"Compare dead-end and grid distribution systems."* — Always mention **redundancy, water quality (stagnation in dead-ends), pressure maintenance, and repair flexibility** as the four key comparison parameters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Physics of Network Analysis

Every pipe network must satisfy two fundamental laws simultaneously at all times:

1. **Junction Continuity (Kirchhoff's Current Law analogue):**
   The algebraic sum of discharges entering any node equals the sum leaving it. Mathematically, $\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$ at every node. In compact notation:

   $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = 0 \quad \text{(at every node j)}$$

   - **Why?** Because water is incompressible and mass cannot accumulate at a junction under steady-state flow. Whatever flows in must flow out (or be consumed as a nodal demand).

2. **Loop Energy Conservation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law analogue):**
   The algebraic sum of head losses around any closed loop equals zero. In a loop, clockwise head losses are positive and counter-clockwise head losses are negative:

   $$\sum_{i \in loop} h_{f,i} = 0$$

   - **Why?** Because energy is a conservative quantity. If you start at a node and traverse a closed loop returning to the same node, the net change in piezometric head must be zero (assuming no pumps or turbines in the loop).

### 2.2 Head Loss in a Pipe — The Foundation

The head loss in a pipe carrying discharge $Q$ is generally expressed as:

$$h_f = K \cdot Q^n$$

where:
- $K$ = pipe resistance coefficient (depends on diameter, length, roughness, and the chosen formula)
- $Q$ = discharge through the pipe
- $n$ = flow exponent (1.85 for Hazen-Williams, 2.0 for Darcy-Weisbach with constant friction factor)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **exam-ready arsenal**. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every numerical problem.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Darcy-Weisbach** | $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Universal, all flows |
| 2 | **Hazen-Williams** | $V = 0.849 \cdot C_H \cdot R^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}$ | Water networks (most common in KTU) |
| 3 | **Hazen-Williams (SI)** | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C_H^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | Direct $h_f$ vs $Q$ form |
| 4 | **Manning's** | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Open channels (not for networks) |
| 5 | **Continuity** | $Q = A \cdot V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot V$ | Velocity–discharge link |
| 6 | **Equivalent Pipe (Series)** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}$ | Two pipes in **series** |
| 7 | **Equivalent Pipe (Parallel)** | $\sqrt{K_{eq}} = \sum \sqrt{K_i}$ | Two pipes in **parallel** |
| 8 | **Hardy Cross Correction** | $\Delta Q = -\dfrac{\sum h_f}{n \cdot \sum \vert h_f / Q \vert}$ | Iterative loop correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all formula derivations below, the **absolute value** of a quantity is written using the LaTeX command $\vert x \vert$ (e.g., $\vert h_f/Q \vert$) — **never** with the bare pipe symbol `|`, as it would break the markdown table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Network analysis is the **backbone of urban water supply design** and is used for:
- **Municipality planning:** Sizing pipes to ensure minimum residual pressure (typically **1.5 to 2.0 bar = 15 to 20 m of water column**) at the farthest consumer.
- **Fire-fighting design:** Ensuring the network can deliver the required fire flow (e.g., **1500 to 5000 L/min** for commercial zones) at adequate pressure.
- **Pump station design:** Determining the required pumping head from the analysis.
- **Water Audit & Loss Control:** Comparing design vs. actual flows to detect leakage.
- **EPANET / WaterGEMS software validation:** All commercial hydraulic solvers (EPANET, WaterCAD, InfoWater) implement Hardy Cross / Newton-Raphson methods internally.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hardy Cross Method (Most Asked in KTU)

The **Hardy Cross Method** (1936) is an **iterative balancing technique** that adjusts assumed pipe flows to satisfy the loop energy equation $\sum h_f = 0$ one loop at a time, while automatically maintaining continuity at the nodes by assumed flow distribution.

**Step 1 — Start with the head-loss–discharge relation:**

$$h_f = K \cdot Q^n$$

**Step 2 — Define the residual error in a loop.** Let $Q_a$ be the assumed flow in a pipe and let $\Delta Q$ be a small correction applied equally to all pipes in the loop (positive sign convention: clockwise flow). The corrected flow is:

$$Q = Q_a + \Delta Q$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the head-loss equation:**

$$h_f = K \cdot (Q_a + \Delta Q)^n$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Binomial Expansion** (assuming $\Delta Q$ is small compared to $Q_a$, so higher-order terms can be neglected):

$$h_f \approx K \cdot Q_a^n \cdot \left[1 + n \cdot \dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q_a}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Sum the head losses around the entire loop:**

$$\sum h_f = \sum K \cdot Q_a^n + \Delta Q \cdot n \cdot \sum \dfrac{K \cdot Q_a^n}{Q_a}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the loop condition** $\sum h_f = 0$:

$$\sum K \cdot Q_a^n + \Delta Q \cdot n \cdot \sum \dfrac{K \cdot Q_a^n}{Q_a} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the correction $\Delta Q$:**

$$\Delta Q = - \dfrac{\sum K \cdot Q_a^n}{n \cdot \sum \dfrac{K \cdot Q_a^n}{Q_a}}$$

**Step 8 — Recognize the physical meaning.** Note that $K \cdot Q_a^n = h_f$ (the head loss in that pipe) and $\dfrac{K \cdot Q_a^n}{Q_a} = \dfrac{h_f}{Q_a}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Delta Q = - \dfrac{\sum h_f}{n \cdot \sum \vert h_f / Q \vert}}$$

> **Sign Convention:** If $\sum h_f$ is positive (i.e., the assumed clockwise flows are producing net clockwise head loss), the correction $\Delta Q$ comes out **negative**, meaning the assumed flows are too high in the clockwise direction — they must be **reduced clockwise / increased counter-clockwise** to balance the loop.

**Step 9 — Iterate.** Apply $\Delta Q$ to all pipes in the loop (add to clockwise pipes, subtract from counter-clockwise pipes), recompute, and repeat until $\sum h_f$ for every loop is within an acceptable tolerance (typically $\vert \sum h_f \vert \le 0.01$ m or as stipulated in the problem).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Equivalent Pipe — Pipes in Series

Two pipes are in **series** when the same discharge passes through them sequentially. The total head loss is the sum of individual losses.

**Step 1:** For two pipes in series carrying flow $Q$:

$$h_{f,\text{total}} = h_{f,1} + h_{f,2} = K_1 Q^n + K_2 Q^n$$

**Step 2:** For the equivalent pipe carrying the same $Q$ with equivalent resistance $K_{eq}$:

$$h_{f,\text{total}} = K_{eq} \cdot Q^n$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$K_{eq} \cdot Q^n = K_1 Q^n + K_2 Q^n$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $Q^n$ (since $Q \neq 0$):**

$$K_{eq} = K_1 + K_2$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to $n$ pipes in series:**

$$K_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$$

**Step 6 — Recast in the practical form using $h_f = K Q^n$ with $n=2$** (Darcy-Weisbach):

$$\dfrac{L_{eq}}{D_{eq}^5} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{D_i^5} \quad \text{(constant diameter assumed)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Pipe — Pipes in Parallel

Two pipes are in **parallel** when the total discharge splits between them, but the head loss across both is identical (since they connect the same two nodes).

**Step 1:** Total discharge equals the sum of branch discharges:

$$Q = Q_1 + Q_2$$

**Step 2:** Head loss in each branch is equal (same upstream and downstream nodes):

$$h_f = K_1 Q_1^n = K_2 Q_2^n = K_{eq} Q^n$$

**Step 3 — Solve for individual flows:**

$$Q_1 = \left(\dfrac{h_f}{K_1}\right)^{1/n}, \quad Q_2 = \left(\dfrac{h_f}{K_2}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the continuity equation:**

$$Q = h_f^{1/n} \cdot \left(K_1^{-1/n} + K_2^{-1/n}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to find $K_{eq}$:**

$$K_{eq} = \left(K_1^{-1/n} + K_2^{-1/n}\right)^{-n}$$

**Step 6 — Special case for $n = 2$ (Darcy-Weisbach with constant $f$):**

$$K_{eq} = \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_1}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_2}}\right)^{-2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The most exam-friendly way to remember this for **$n=2$** is:
> $$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_1}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_2}}$$
> This is mathematically identical to the **resistance formula in electrical circuits** — a great memory aid for the student.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A pipe network loop has three pipes with assumed clockwise flows: Pipe 1: $Q_1 = 0.05$ m³/s, Pipe 2: $Q_2 = 0.04$ m³/s, Pipe 3: $Q_3 = -0.01$ m³/s (counter-clockwise). Head losses computed: $h_1 = 1.20$ m, $h_2 = 0.80$ m, $h_3 = -0.10$ m. Use $n = 2$. Find $\Delta Q$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\sum h_f$:**

$$\sum h_f = 1.20 + 0.80 + (-0.10) = 1.90 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $h_f/Q$ for each pipe:**

$$\dfrac{h_1}{Q_1} = \dfrac{1.20}{0.05} = 24.0, \quad \dfrac{h_2}{Q_2} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.04} = 20.0, \quad \dfrac{h_3}{Q_3} = \dfrac{-0.10}{-0.01} = 10.0$$

**Step 3 — Sum $\sum \vert h_f/Q \vert$:**

$$\sum \vert h_f/Q \vert = 24.0 + 20.0 + 10.0 = 54.0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Hardy Cross formula:**

$$\Delta Q = - \dfrac{\sum h_f}{n \cdot \sum \vert h_f/Q \vert} = - \dfrac{1.90}{2 \times 54.0} = -0.01759 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret and correct flows:**

Since $\Delta Q$ is **negative**, the assumed clockwise flows were too large. The corrected flows (Pipe 1, 2 reduced; Pipe 3 increased in magnitude as it was counter-clockwise, so we add to it):

$$Q_1' = 0.05 + (-0.01759) = 0.0324 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$Q_2' = 0.04 + (-0.01759) = 0.0224 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$Q_3' = -0.01 + (-0.01759) = -0.0276 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Verify** by recomputing head losses and iterating until $\sum h_f \approx 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Types of Distribution Networks

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1((Reservoir)]
    R2((Reservoir)]
    J1([Node 1])
    J2([Node 2])
    J3([Node 3])
    J4([Node 4])
    J5([Node 5])

    R1 -- Pipe --> J1
    J1 -- Pipe --> J2
    J2 -- Pipe --> J3
    J3 -- Pipe --> J4
    J4 -- Pipe --> J5

    R2 -- Pipe --> J1
    R2 -- Pipe --> J2
    R2 -- Pipe --> J3

    J1 --- J2
    J2 --- J3
    J3 --- J4
    J4 --- J5
```

**Reading the schematic:** The top arm (R1 → J1 → J2 → J3 → J4 → J5) is a **dead-end / radial branch** with no return path. The middle feed (R2 → J1, J2, J3) forms a **sub-grid** that supplies the loop. This combined topology is what is meant by a **Combined Distribution Network** — the most realistic layout for an Indian city.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Hardy Cross Iteration Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA([Start: Assume Q per pipe in each loop])
    nodeChk{Node continuity satisfied?}
    flowChk{K_i and h_f computed for each pipe?}
    loopChk{Sum h_f in every loop approx zero?}
    correctA[Compute Delta Q using Hardy Cross]
    updateA[Update Q = Q_old + Delta Q]
    outputA([Converged: Q and H at every node])
    failA[Adjust assumption and restart]

    startA --> nodeChk
    nodeChk -- No --> failA
    nodeChk -- Yes --> flowChk
    flowChk -- Yes --> loopChk
    loopChk -- No --> correctA
    correctA --> updateA
    updateA --> flowChk
    loopChk -- Yes --> outputA
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Water Distribution Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    intakeP[Intake Well]
    treatP[Water Treatment Plant]
    sumpP[Clear Water Sump]
    pumpP[Pump House]
    esrP[Elevated Service Reservoir ESR]
    mainP[Primary Main Trunk]
    subM[Secondary Mains]
    distM[Distribution Mains]
    consP[Consumer Service Connections]

    intakeP --> treatP
    treatP --> sumpP
    sumpP --> pumpP
    pumpP --> esrP
    esrP --> mainP
    mainP --> subM
    subM --> distM
    distM --> consP
```

**Interpretation of the architecture:**
- The **Treatment Plant** receives raw water from the intake and delivers potable water.
- The **Sump** stores treated water at ground level.
- The **Pump House** raises water into the **ESR (Elevated Service Reservoir)** — this is the gravitational head source.
- The **ESR is the heart of the network**: it sets the HGL (hydraulic gradient line) and the residual pressure at every consumer.
- From the ESR, water flows through **Primary Mains (large diameter) → Secondary Mains (medium) → Distribution Mains (small, street-level) → Service Connections (house taps)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ESR matters in Network Analysis:** In Hardy Cross analysis, the ESR's water surface elevation is treated as a **fixed piezometric head boundary node**. All other nodal heads are computed relative to it. The choice of ESR height (typically **15 m to 25 m above the highest service point**) directly determines whether the network can meet minimum residual pressure constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a "dead-end" distribution system and a "grid (ring)" distribution system. Mention two advantages and one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Dead-End System | Grid (Ring) System |
|---|---|---|
| Layout | Tree / branch; one flow path | Interconnected loops; multiple flow paths |
| **Advantage 1** | Simple to design, lower cost | **Redundancy:** water reaches a point via multiple paths if one pipe fails |
| **Advantage 2** | Easy to lay in narrow streets | **Better pressure:** no stagnation; uniform pressure |
| **Disadvantage** | Stagnation in dead ends → water quality issues; large pipe sizes needed at the trunk | Higher cost; more valves and fittings; complex analysis |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the two fundamental laws governing the analysis of a pipe network. Write the corresponding mathematical equations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Law of Continuity (Junction Rule):** At every node, the sum of inflows equals the sum of outflows (or, equivalently, the algebraic sum of discharges at a node is zero):

$$\sum Q_i = 0 \quad \text{at every node j}$$

2. **Law of Conservation of Energy (Loop Rule):** The algebraic sum of head losses around any closed loop in the network is zero:

$$\sum h_{f,i} = 0 \quad \text{around every loop k}$$

These two laws, applied simultaneously, yield a system of simultaneous equations that can be solved iteratively (Hardy Cross) or directly (Linear Theory / Newton-Raphson) for the unknown flows in every pipe.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — KTU ESE Pattern]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

*Two reservoirs are connected by a pipe system consisting of three pipes in series: Pipe 1 (L₁ = 600 m, D₁ = 0.30 m), Pipe 2 (L₂ = 400 m, D₂ = 0.25 m), and Pipe 3 (L₃ = 800 m, D₃ = 0.20 m). The difference in water surface elevation between the two reservoirs is **12 m**. Take f = 0.025 for all pipes.*

**(a)** *Develop the equivalent pipe expression for the three pipes in series and calculate the equivalent diameter (assuming the equivalent pipe has the same length as the total length).*

**(b)** *Determine the discharge flowing through the system using the Darcy-Weisbach equation.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Recall the Darcy-Weisbach head-loss formula:**

$$h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

**Step 2 — Express the head loss for each pipe** in terms of its resistance coefficient $K_i = \dfrac{8 f L_i}{\pi^2 g D_i^5}$:

$$h_{f,1} = K_1 Q^2, \quad h_{f,2} = K_2 Q^2, \quad h_{f,3} = K_3 Q^2$$

**Step 3 — Total head loss = sum of individual head losses (series condition):**

$$h_{f,\text{total}} = h_{f,1} + h_{f,2} + h_{f,3} = (K_1 + K_2 + K_3) Q^2$$

**Step 4 — Define the equivalent pipe** with resistance $K_{eq}$:

$$K_{eq} = K_1 + K_2 + K_3$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the formula for K and simplify** (note $8f/(\pi^2 g)$ cancels out for all three pipes since $f$ is the same):

$$\dfrac{L_{eq}}{D_{eq}^5} = \dfrac{L_1}{D_1^5} + \dfrac{L_2}{D_2^5} + \dfrac{L_3}{D_3^5}$$

**Step 6 — Compute each term numerically** ($L_{eq} = 600 + 400 + 800 = 1800$ m):

$$\dfrac{L_1}{D_1^5} = \dfrac{600}{(0.30)^5} = \dfrac{600}{0.00243} = 246{,}913.6$$

$$\dfrac{L_2}{D_2^5} = \dfrac{400}{(0.25)^5} = \dfrac{400}{0.0009766} = 409{,}600.0$$

$$\dfrac{L_3}{D_3^5} = \dfrac{800}{(0.20)^5} = \dfrac{800}{0.00032} = 2{,}500{,}000.0$$

**Step 7 — Sum the three terms:**

$$\sum \dfrac{L_i}{D_i^5} = 246{,}913.6 + 409{,}600.0 + 2{,}500{,}000.0 = 3{,}156{,}513.6$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $D_{eq}$:**

$$D_{eq}^5 = \dfrac{1800}{3{,}156{,}513.6} = 5.702 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$D_{eq} = (5.702 \times 10^{-4})^{0.2} = 0.219 \text{ m} \approx 219 \text{ mm}$$

> ✓ **The equivalent diameter is approximately 0.22 m.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Apply the energy equation between the two reservoir surfaces.** The head loss across the system equals the difference in water surface elevations:

$$h_{f,\text{total}} = 12 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Use the equivalent pipe Darcy-Weisbach equation:**

$$h_{f,\text{total}} = \dfrac{8 f L_{eq} Q^2}{\pi^2 g D_{eq}^5}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q^2$:**

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{h_{f,\text{total}} \cdot \pi^2 g D_{eq}^5}{8 f L_{eq}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{12 \times \pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 5.702 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 0.025 \times 1800}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$12 \times \pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 5.702 \times 10^{-4} = 12 \times 9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 5.702 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 12 \times 0.06624 = 0.7949$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$8 \times 0.025 \times 1800 = 360$$

**Step 7 — Final computation:**

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{0.7949}{360} = 2.208 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^6/\text{s}^2$$

$$Q = \sqrt{2.208 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0470 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q \approx 47.0 \text{ L/s}$$

> ✓ **The discharge through the system is approximately 0.047 m³/s (47 L/s).**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

*A pipe network loop consists of four pipes forming a single closed loop. The assumed flows (clockwise positive) and the head loss constant $K$ for each pipe are tabulated below. Use **Hardy Cross Method with n = 2** to determine the corrected flow in each pipe up to **two iterations**.*

| Pipe | Length L (m) | Diameter D (m) | K = 8fL/(π²gD⁵) | Assumed Q (L/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AB | 200 | 0.20 | 1500 | +30 |
| BC | 250 | 0.15 | 4200 | +20 |
| CD | 200 | 0.20 | 1500 | -10 |
| DA | 250 | 0.15 | 4200 | -20 |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks — First Iteration]

**Step 1 — Convert flows to m³/s:**

$$Q_{AB} = +0.030, \quad Q_{BC} = +0.020, \quad Q_{CD} = -0.010, \quad Q_{DA} = -0.020 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute head loss $h_f = K Q^2$ in each pipe:**

$$h_{AB} = 1500 \times (0.030)^2 = 1500 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = 1.350 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{BC} = 4200 \times (0.020)^2 = 4200 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.680 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{CD} = 1500 \times (-0.010)^2 = 1500 \times 1 \times 10^{-4} = 0.150 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{DA} = 4200 \times (-0.020)^2 = 4200 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.680 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the head losses (with sign convention — head loss in the direction of flow):**

$$\sum h_f = 1.350 + 1.680 + 0.150 + 1.680 = 4.860 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h_f/Q$ for each pipe:**

$$\dfrac{h_{AB}}{Q_{AB}} = \dfrac{1.350}{0.030} = 45.00$$

$$\dfrac{h_{BC}}{Q_{BC}} = \dfrac{1.680}{0.020} = 84.00$$

$$\dfrac{h_{CD}}{Q_{CD}} = \dfrac{0.150}{-0.010} = -15.00 \rightarrow \vert -15.00 \vert = 15.00$$

$$\dfrac{h_{DA}}{Q_{DA}} = \dfrac{1.680}{-0.020} = -84.00 \rightarrow \vert -84.00 \vert = 84.00$$

**Step 5 — Sum the absolute values:**

$$\sum \vert h_f/Q \vert = 45.00 + 84.00 + 15.00 + 84.00 = 228.00$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Hardy Cross correction formula:**

$$\Delta Q = -\dfrac{\sum h_f}{n \cdot \sum \vert h_f/Q \vert} = -\dfrac{4.860}{2 \times 228.00}$$

$$\Delta Q = -\dfrac{4.860}{456.00} = -0.01066 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = -10.66 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 7 — Update the flows (add $\Delta Q$ to all clockwise pipes, subtract from all counter-clockwise pipes):**

$$Q_{AB}' = 30 + (-10.66) = 19.34 \text{ L/s}$$

$$Q_{BC}' = 20 + (-10.66) = 9.34 \text{ L/s}$$

$$Q_{CD}' = -10 - (-10.66) = +0.66 \text{ L/s}$$

$$Q_{DA}' = -20 - (-10.66) = -9.34 \text{ L/s}$$

> ✓ **After Iteration 1: $\Delta Q$ = -10.66 L/s.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks — Second Iteration and Final Answer]

**Step 1 — Convert corrected flows to m³/s:**

$$Q_{AB} = +0.01934, \quad Q_{BC} = +0.00934, \quad Q_{CD} = +0.00066, \quad Q_{DA} = -0.00934$$

**Step 2 — Recompute head losses $h_f = K Q^2$:**

$$h_{AB} = 1500 \times (0.01934)^2 = 1500 \times 3.740 \times 10^{-4} = 0.5610 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{BC} = 4200 \times (0.00934)^2 = 4200 \times 8.724 \times 10^{-5} = 0.3664 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{CD} = 1500 \times (0.00066)^2 = 1500 \times 4.356 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.0007 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{DA} = 4200 \times (0.00934)^2 = 4200 \times 8.724 \times 10^{-5} = 0.3664 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the head losses:**

$$\sum h_f = 0.5610 + 0.3664 + 0.0007 + 0.3664 = 1.2945 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute and sum $\vert h_f/Q \vert$:**

$$\dfrac{h_{AB}}{Q_{AB}} = \dfrac{0.5610}{0.01934} = 29.01$$

$$\dfrac{h_{BC}}{Q_{BC}} = \dfrac{0.3664}{0.00934} = 39.23$$

$$\dfrac{h_{CD}}{Q_{CD}} = \dfrac{0.0007}{0.00066} = 1.06$$

$$\dfrac{h_{DA}}{Q_{DA}} = \dfrac{0.3664}{0.00934} = 39.23$$

$$\sum \vert h_f/Q \vert = 29.01 + 39.23 + 1.06 + 39.23 = 108.53$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new correction:**

$$\Delta Q = -\dfrac{1.2945}{2 \times 108.53} = -\dfrac{1.2945}{217.06} = -0.00596 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = -5.96 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 6 — Update the flows:**

$$Q_{AB}'' = 19.34 - 5.96 = 13.38 \text{ L/s}$$

$$Q_{BC}'' = 9.34 - 5.96 = 3.38 \text{ L/s}$$

$$Q_{CD}'' = 0.66 + 5.96 = 6.62 \text{ L/s}$$

$$Q_{DA}'' = -9.34 + 5.96 = -3.38 \text{ L/s}$$

> ✓ **After Iteration 2: $\Delta Q$ = -5.96 L/s.** Notice that $\Delta Q$ is decreasing in magnitude, confirming convergence. A third iteration would yield $\Delta Q \approx -1$ L/s, indicating the final solution is approaching.

> ✓ **Final Corrected Flows (after 2 iterations):**
> Pipe AB: **13.38 L/s**, Pipe BC: **3.38 L/s**, Pipe CD: **6.62 L/s**, Pipe DA: **-3.38 L/s** (i.e., 3.38 L/s counter-clockwise).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — READ CAREFULLY:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $\Delta Q$ (–2 marks):** The Hardy Cross correction is **negative** of $\sum h_f$. If you write $\Delta Q = +\sum h_f / \sum \vert h_f/Q \vert$, you will lose **2 marks outright**. Always include the negative sign and check your correction against the sign of $\sum h_f$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Absolute Values in the Denominator (–1 mark):** The denominator $\sum \vert h_f/Q \vert$ uses the **magnitude** of $h_f/Q$, not its signed value. Since $Q$ may be negative for counter-clockwise assumed flows, students often sum signed values and obtain an incorrect $\Delta Q$.
>
> 3. **Equivalent Pipe Direction Error (–2 marks):** For series pipes, you **add** the resistances. For parallel pipes, you **add the reciprocals of the square roots** (for $n=2$). Mixing these two up is the single most common error in equivalent-pipe questions.
>
> 4. **Not Maintaining Node Continuity in Hardy Cross (–1 to –2 marks):** The assumed flows MUST satisfy continuity at every node from the very first assumption. If they don't, the iteration will never converge to a physically meaningful answer.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency in Hazen-Williams vs Darcy-Weisbach (–1 mark):** Hazen-Williams constants ($C_H$) are dimensionless, but its $K$ coefficient is **formula-specific**. Never mix the $K$ values from two different formulas within the same network.
>
> 6. **Final Answer Without Units (–0.5 mark per missing unit):** Every numerical answer must carry units (m³/s, L/s, m, mm). Examiners deduct half-marks liberally for missing units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section for last-minute revision.**

- **Two laws of network analysis:** (1) Continuity at every node $\sum Q_i = 0$; (2) Energy conservation around every loop $\sum h_f = 0$.
- **Three most common head-loss formulas:** Darcy-Weisbach (universal), Hazen-Williams (water networks), Manning (open channels — not used in networks).
- **Hardy Cross Method:** Iterative; corrects assumed flows using $\Delta Q = -\sum h_f / (n \sum \vert h_f/Q \vert)$. Always iterate until $\sum h_f \to 0$.
- **Hardy Cross $n$ value:** **$n = 2$** for Darcy-Weisbach (constant $f$); **$n = 1.85$** for Hazen-Williams.
- **Equivalent Pipe (Series):** $K_{eq} = \sum K_i$ — resistances **add directly**.
- **Equivalent Pipe (Parallel, $n=2$):** $1/\sqrt{K_{eq}} = \sum 1/\sqrt{K_i}$ — **reciprocals of square roots add**.
- **Sign Convention in Hardy Cross:** Clockwise = positive; $\Delta Q$ is **subtracted** from clockwise assumed flows and **added** to counter-clockwise assumed flows.
- **Network Types:** Dead-end (tree), Radial, Grid (ring), Combined. Grid is preferred for modern cities due to redundancy.
- **HGL (Hydraulic Gradient Line):** Plots piezometric head along the pipeline; ESR surface sets the HGL origin.
- **Minimum residual pressure at consumer:** **7 m to 17 m** (1.5–2 bar) for domestic supply; **17 m to 35 m** for high-rise / fire demand.
- **Newton-Raphson vs Hardy Cross:** Newton-Raphson is faster-converging and used in modern software (EPANET). Hardy Cross is the manual / academic method. Both solve the same system.
- **Limitation of Hardy Cross:** Convergence is **slow** for large networks; manual iterations become impractical beyond 4–5 loops. For larger systems, linear theory (Henshaw, Cornish) or Newton-Raphson is preferred.
- **Continuity MUST hold from the start:** Always begin Hardy Cross by distributing flows at every node such that $\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$. This is the most frequently violated rule by students.
- **The "$K$" coefficient depends on the formula used:** It is not a universal constant. State the formula when computing $K$ in your answer.
- **Hazen-Williams is empirical** and valid for water at ordinary temperatures ($5$ to $25$ °C); for other fluids or extreme temperatures, use Darcy-Weisbach.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardy Cross and Equivalent Pipe Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hardy Cross and Equivalent Pipe Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hardy Cross Method — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardy Cross Method** is an **iterative numerical procedure** used for the **analysis of water distribution pipe networks** (looped networks) in which the direction of flow in various pipes is not known *a priori*. The method is based on the fundamental network equilibrium principle that **the algebraic sum of head losses around any closed loop is zero**, and it achieves solution through **successive corrections of assumed flow distribution**.

Mathematically, the Hardy Cross correction term for a single loop is given by:

$$\Delta Q = - \frac{\sum h_f}{n \sum \left(\dfrac{h_f}{Q}\right)}$$

where:
- $\Delta Q$ = flow correction to be applied to all pipes in the loop
- $h_f$ = head loss in each pipe (assumed sign convention: clockwise positive, anticlockwise negative)
- $Q$ = assumed flow in each pipe
- $n$ = exponent in the head-loss formula used (**n = 1.85** for Hazen-Williams, **n = 2.0** for Darcy-Weisbach)

### 1.2 Equivalent Pipe Method — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Pipe Method** is a network simplification technique that replaces a group of pipes (connected in series, parallel, or any combination) by a **single equivalent pipe** which, under the same head loss, carries the same discharge as the original group. The two fundamental configurations are: (a) Pipes in **Series** and (b) Pipes in **Parallel**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Hardy Cross as a "Ring Road Traffic Solver":**
Imagine a **ring road** connecting four towns. You know the total traffic entering the system, but you don't know how it splits between the two possible paths around the ring. Hardy Cross acts like a traffic engineer: he *guesses* a split, calculates the "congestion delay" (head loss) on each segment, and then systematically shifts vehicles ($\Delta Q$) from the most congested route to the less congested one — repeating until delays balance. That balance point is the **true flow distribution**.

**Analogy 2 — Equivalent Pipe as an "Electrical Resistor Analogy":**
Just as multiple resistors in series/parallel can be replaced by a single equivalent resistor, multiple pipes in series/parallel can be replaced by a single equivalent pipe. **Head loss (h)** is analogous to **voltage drop (V)**, and **discharge (Q)** is analogous to **current (I)**. The hydraulic equivalent of Ohm's Law gives the same Kirchhoff-like laws for networks.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Hazen-Williams Coefficient (C):** Typical values — Cast Iron (old): **C = 80–100**, Cast Iron (new): **C = 100–120**, PVC: **C = 140–150**, Concrete: **C = 120–140**.
> - **Darcy-Weisbach Friction Factor (f):** Function of Reynolds Number and Relative Roughness.
> - **Standard Hazen-Williams Formula:** $V = 0.849 \times C \times R^{0.63} \times S^{0.54}$ (SI Units).
> - **Pipe Roughness (ε):** Commercial Steel = **0.045 mm**, Cast Iron = **0.26 mm**, Concrete = **0.3–3.0 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hardy Cross Method — Underlying Logic (Step-by-Step)

The Hardy Cross method rests on **two fundamental physical laws**:

1. **Continuity (Junction Rule):** The algebraic sum of discharges at any node must equal zero (inflow = outflow).
2. **Energy Conservation (Loop Rule):** The algebraic sum of head losses around any closed loop must equal zero.

**Operational Procedure (Stepwise):**

1. **Sketch the network** — identify all nodes, pipes, loops, and known nodal demands.
2. **Assume initial flow distribution** — satisfy continuity at every node (assumed flows may initially violate head-loss balance, but they MUST satisfy continuity).
3. **Apply sign convention** — typically: **clockwise flow = positive**, **counter-clockwise flow = negative**.
4. **Compute head loss** in each pipe using Hazen-Williams or Darcy-Weisbach:
   - $h_f = K \cdot Q^n$ where $K$ is the pipe resistance coefficient and $n = 1.85$ (H-W) or $2.0$ (D-W).
5. **Compute $\sum h_f$ and $\sum (h_f / Q)$** for the chosen loop.
6. **Calculate correction $\Delta Q$** using the Hardy Cross equation.
7. **Apply $\Delta Q$** to all pipes in the loop (positive $\Delta Q$ added to assumed clockwise positive flows).
8. **Move to the next loop** with the corrected flows; repeat until $|\Delta Q|$ becomes negligible (typically $< 0.01$ L/s or as required by accuracy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Successive Approximation"?** The pipe network equations (continuity + energy) are **non-linear** in $Q$ (because $h_f \propto Q^n$ where $n \neq 1$). Hardy Cross linearizes the problem locally using Taylor series expansion, retaining only the first-order term — yielding the famous $\Delta Q$ formula.

### 2.2 Equivalent Pipe Method — Underlying Logic

**(a) Pipes in Series:**
- Same discharge $Q$ flows through all pipes.
- Total head loss = sum of individual head losses.

$$\boxed{\;H_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i Q^n\;} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\;K_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i\;}$$

**For pipes of different diameters in series** (assuming Hazen-Williams with same C), an equivalent diameter is derived such that:

$$K_{eq} = \frac{10.67 \cdot L_{eq}}{C^{1.85} \cdot D_{eq}^{4.87}}$$

**(b) Pipes in Parallel:**
- Same head loss $h_f$ across all pipes.
- Total discharge = sum of individual discharges.

$$\boxed{\;Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i\;} \quad \text{where} \quad \boxed{\;K_i Q_i^n = K_{eq} Q^n\;}$$

**Equivalent Resistance Formula for Parallel Pipes (n = 2, Darcy-Weisbach):**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}\;}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Hardy Cross Correction | $\Delta Q = -\dfrac{\sum h_f}{n \cdot \sum (h_f / Q)}$ | L/s or m³/s; $n = 1.85$ or $2.0$ |
| 2 | Head Loss (Hazen-Williams) | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | $L$ in m, $D$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 3 | Head Loss (Darcy-Weisbach) | $h_f = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot V^2}{2gD} = \dfrac{8fLQ^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ | SI Units |
| 4 | Pipe Resistance Constant (HW) | $K = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L}{C^{1.85} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | $K Q^{1.85}$ gives $h_f$ |
| 5 | Pipe Resistance Constant (DW) | $K = \dfrac{8fL}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ | $K Q^2$ gives $h_f$ |
| 6 | Series — Equivalent Resistance | $K_{eq} = \sum K_i$ | Same flow, head losses add |
| 7 | Parallel — Equivalent Resistance | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt[n]{K_{eq}}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\sqrt[n]{K_i}}$ | Same head loss, flows add |
| 8 | Continuity at Node | $\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$ | Mass conservation |
| 9 | Loop Energy Equation | $\sum h_f = 0$ (closed loop) | Energy conservation |
| 10 | Sign Convention (Hardy Cross) | Clockwise = +ve, CCW = −ve | Standard KTU convention |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Hardy Cross method is extensively used in **municipal water supply design** by organizations like Kerala Water Authority (KWA) and Public Health Engineering (PHE) departments to design **looped distribution networks** that ensure **redundancy, balanced pressure, and continuous supply** even during pipe maintenance. The Equivalent Pipe method is used in **CAD-based network simulation** (e.g., EPANET software) to reduce computational load when analyzing large networks.
