| **Tertiary** | Nutrients (N, P), residual organics, pathogens | Chemical/Advanced | 99% BOD, 95% TSS, N & P removal |

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3**, the focus is on **Primary, Secondary, Tertiary Treatment** and **Equalization of Flow**, as these form the core of municipal sewage treatment plant (STP) design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Analogy: The Multi-Stage Coffee Brewing and Filtration Process**

Imagine making the perfect cup of filter coffee using a traditional South Indian filter:

1. **Preliminary Treatment** = The strainer that catches the largest coffee grounds and powder clumps first. These are the *big debris* that would clog everything downstream.
2. **Primary Treatment** = The gravity settling of the decoction, where the medium-fine particles drop to the bottom of the vessel. The liquid looks *clearer* but is still strong and murky.
3. **Secondary Treatment** = Passing the decoction through a cloth filter or activated carbon, where microorganisms (in our case, real bacteria) "eat" the dissolved coffee compounds. The result is a **cleaner, less pungent liquid**.
4. **Tertiary Treatment** = A final polish — UV light exposure, fine polishing filter, or reverse osmosis — to remove the last traces of dissolved salts, odors, and microbes, producing **potable-quality water**.

Similarly, in a **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)**, each stage refines the wastewater progressively until it meets the disposal standards set by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)**.

## 1.3 Concept of Equalization of Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equalization of Flow Definition (KTU Module 3 Highlight)**
> **Equalization** is the process of dampening the variations in flow rate (and quality) of wastewater arriving at a treatment plant by using an **equalization basin** (also called a flow equalization tank or balancing reservoir). It provides a **uniform hydraulic and organic load** to downstream treatment units, improving their efficiency and stability.

**Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
- Standard design detention time in equalization basin: **2 to 8 hours** (typical = 4 hours for municipal STP)
- Buffering capacity ratio: typically **1.25 to 1.50 times the average daily flow** for combined sewer systems
- Standard design parameter for BOD₅ load smoothing: **± 20% variation** is acceptable at secondary treatment inlet

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Treatment — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

**Objective:** Removal of **suspended solids (SS)** and **settleable solids** through **gravity sedimentation** (and sometimes flotation).

**Key Unit Operations:**

1. **Primary Clarifier (Sedimentation Tank)**
   - Type: Mostly **rectangular** (with chain-and-flight scrapers) or **circular** (with central feed and peripheral overflow).
   - Design surface overflow rate (SOR): **30 to 50 m³/m²·day** for municipal sewage at average flow.
   - Detention time: **1.5 to 2.5 hours**.
   - Weir loading: **≤ 175 m³/m·day**.

2. **Primary Sludge Characteristics**
   - Solid concentration: **4 to 6%** (40,000 to 60,000 mg/L).
   - Specific gravity: **1.02 to 1.05**.
   - Sludge volume index (SVI) of mixed liquor is *not applicable* here, but primary sludge has high volatile solids (60–80%).

3. **Skimming Tank**
   - Removes **floating matter** (oil, grease, scum) via surface skimming.
   - Air injection at the bottom may assist in floating matter removal.

**Operational Steps (Why & How):**
- **Why?** Reduces the load on the more expensive biological (secondary) treatment units.
- **How?** Stoke's Law governs settling: $v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18\mu}$ — larger/heavier particles settle faster.
- **Why pre-aerization?** It reduces odors (H₂S), improves settling, and equalizes to a small extent.

## 2.2 Secondary Treatment — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

**Objective:** Removal of **dissolved and colloidal organic matter** using **biological processes** (aerobic, anaerobic, or anoxic).

**Major Biological Process Categories:**

1. **Aerobic Suspended-Growth Process — Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**
   - **Aeration tank + Secondary Clarifier** configuration.
   - Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS): **2,000 to 4,000 mg/L**.
   - F/M ratio (Food to Microorganism): **0.2 to 0.5 day⁻¹**.
   - Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT): **4 to 8 hours**.
   - SVI: **50 to 150 mL/g** (good settling sludge).
   - Recycle Ratio (R): **0.25 to 1.0** (typically 0.5).
   - Excess Sludge Wasting: calculated from $\theta_c$ (Mean Cell Residence Time = 5 to 15 days).

2. **Aerobic Attached-Growth Process — Trickling Filter**
   - **Rotating distributor** sprays wastewater over a bed of **rock/plastic media**.
   - Hydraulic Loading: **1 to 4 m³/m²·day**.
   - Organic Loading: **0.2 to 0.5 kg BOD/m³·day**.
   - Recirculation factor (F): **1 to 4**.

3. **Anaerobic Process — UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket)**
   - Used in tropical climates (very relevant for **Kerala**).
   - Upflow velocity: **0.5 to 1.0 m/h**.
   - HRT: **6 to 12 hours**.
   - Biogas production: **0.3 to 0.5 m³/kg COD removed**.

**Reactions (Aerobic):**
$$\text{Organic Matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bacteria}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New Cells (Sludge)}$$

**Reactions (Nitrification — Tertiary-Stage Biological):**
$$2\text{NH}_4^+ + 3\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} 2\text{NO}_2^- + 4\text{H}^+ + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$2\text{NO}_2^- + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrobacter}} 2\text{NO}_3^-$$

## 2.3 Tertiary Treatment (Advanced Treatment) — Detailed Breakdown

**Objective:** **Polishing** the secondary effluent to meet stringent discharge/reuse standards (e.g., **BOD < 10 mg/L, COD < 50 mg/L, Total N < 10 mg/L, Total P < 2 mg/L**).

**Unit Operations/Processes:**

1. **Filtration** — Removes residual suspended solids.
   - Rapid sand filter, dual-media filter, membrane filter.
2. **Disinfection** — Eliminates pathogens.
   - Chlorination (most common), UV radiation, Ozonation.
3. **Nutrient Removal (Nitrogen)**
   - **Nitrification + Denitrification (BNR)**: Aerobic nitrification followed by anoxic denitrification with methanol/acetate as carbon source.
   - Denitrification: $\text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow$
4. **Nutrient Removal (Phosphorus)**
   - **Chemical Precipitation** using Al₂(SO₄)₃, FeCl₃, or lime.
   - **Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)**.
5. **Activated Carbon Adsorption** — Removes refractory organics, color, odor.
6. **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** / **Nanofiltration** — For high-purity reuse.

## 2.4 Equalization of Flow — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

**Why Equalization is Required:**

- **Diurnal flow variation** in municipal sewers: peak flow may be **2 to 4 times** the average flow.
- **Industrial discharges** cause shock loads (sudden spikes in BOD/COD/pH).
- Toxic shocks can kill biomass in secondary treatment — equalization acts as a **buffer**.

**Types of Equalization Basins:**

1. **In-line (or On-line) Equalization Basin**
   - All flow passes through the basin.
   - Basin contents are mixed (via aeration or mechanical mixers).
   - Best for **small-to-medium** STPs.

2. **Off-line (or Side-line) Equalization Basin**
   - Only **excess flow** above a certain threshold is diverted to the basin.
   - Used for **large municipal** STPs.

3. **Side-line Equalization Basin**
   - Part of the flow is bypassed to the basin; mixed and returned.

**Design Parameters (Key KTU-Exam Topics):**

- **Volume of Equalization Basin (V_eq)**:
$$V_{eq} = \frac{Q_{max} - Q_{avg}}{n} \quad \text{(graphical/area method)}$$
  OR using mass balance integral:
$$V_{eq} = \int_0^T \left[Q(t) - Q_{eq}\right] dt$$
where $Q_{eq}$ is the desired equalized outflow.

- **Volume for BOD Equalization (Mass Balance):**
$$V_{eq,BOD} = \frac{1}{C^*} \int_0^T S(t) Q(t) \, dt - \frac{T \cdot \bar{S} \cdot \bar{Q}}{C^*}$$
  where $S(t)$ is influent BOD concentration, $C^*$ is the design (target) BOD.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $v_s = \frac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$ | Stoke's Law — settling velocity of discrete particles | m/s |
| 2 | $\text{SOR} = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Surface Overflow Rate of clarifier | m³/m²·day |
| 3 | $t = \frac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic detention time | hours |
| 4 | $R = \frac{Q_r}{Q}$ | Recirculation ratio in ASP | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\frac{F}{M} = \frac{S_0 \cdot Q}{V \cdot X}$ | Food to Microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) | days |
| 7 | $V_{eq} = Q_{avg} \cdot t_{eq}$ | Volume of equalization basin (where $t_{eq}$ = 2–8 h) | m³ |
| 8 | $\text{Weir Loading} = \frac{Q}{L_{weir}}$ | Weir overflow rate of clarifier | m³/m·day |
| 9 | $Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ | Observed yield coefficient in biological growth | mg/mg |
| 10 | $\text{BOD removal \%} = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | BOD removal efficiency | % |
| 11 | $\text{Oxygen Required} = a \cdot S_r + b \cdot X \cdot V$ | Oxygen demand in aeration tank | kg O₂/day |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Used:** $Q$ = flow, $V$ = volume, $S_0$ = influent substrate, $S_e$ = effluent substrate, $X$ = MLSS, $Y$ = yield coefficient, $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient, $a,b$ = empirical oxygen coefficients.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Primary Treatment** is universally used in every municipal STP; reduces the burden on costly biological units.
- **Secondary Treatment** is mandatory under **CPCB/KSPCB** discharge norms for STPs > 0.5 MLD.
- **Tertiary Treatment** is essential for **water reuse** (irrigation, industrial cooling, recreational water bodies like the **Vembanad Lake** in Kerala).
- **Equalization Basins** are standard at inlet works of all major Indian STPs — e.g., **Koyambedu STP (Chennai)**, **Cochin STP (Kerala)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Design of Equalization Basin Volume (Graphical Mass Balance Method)

This is a **high-frequency KTU exam derivation** in Module 3.

### Problem Setup

Given a **diurnal flow pattern** with hourly flow data $Q(t)$ over a 24-hour period, design the **volume of an in-line equalization basin** such that the outflow is **constant at $Q_{avg}$**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate Average Daily Flow**

$$Q_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T Q(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{24} \sum_{i=1}^{24} Q_i$$

**Step 2: Define the Cumulative Inflow Curve**

$$V_{in}(t) = \int_0^t Q(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 3: Define the Equalized (Desired) Outflow Line**

The equalized outflow is a constant rate $Q_{avg}$ over the 24-hour cycle:

$$V_{out}(t) = Q_{avg} \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Compute the Cumulative Storage Volume**

The **storage volume** at any time $t$ is the difference between inflow and outflow:

$$V_{storage}(t) = V_{in}(t) - V_{out}(t) = \int_0^t \left[Q(\tau) - Q_{avg}\right] d\tau$$

**Step 5: Determine Maximum and Minimum Storage Values**

- Find $V_{max}$: Maximum positive value of $V_{storage}(t)$.
- Find $V_{min}$: Maximum negative value of $V_{storage}(t)$.

**Step 6: Required Equalization Basin Volume**

$$\boxed{V_{eq} = V_{max} - V_{min}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given Data:** Hourly inflow $Q(t)$ for a 24-hour cycle, where total volume = **12,000 m³** (so $Q_{avg} = 500$ m³/h).

Assume tabulated values (simplified):

| Time (h) | $Q(t)$ m³/h | $Q_{avg}$ m³/h | $\Delta V = (Q - Q_{avg}) \cdot \Delta t$ m³ | Cumulative $V_{storage}$ m³ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 400 | 500 | -100 | 0 |
| 4 | 600 | 500 | +400 | +300 |
| 8 | 900 | 500 | +1600 | +1900 |
| 12 | 700 | 500 | +800 | +2700 |
| 16 | 500 | 500 | 0 | +2700 |
| 20 | 350 | 500 | -600 | +2100 |
| 24 | 250 | 500 | -1000 | +1100 → 0 (after discharge) |

**Calculation for $V_{max}$ and $V_{min}$:**

At $t = 12$ h, $V_{storage} = +2700$ m³ → $V_{max} = +2700$ m³ (cumulative deviation above average).

**Note:** Since the cumulative storage never went negative (in this simplified example), $V_{min} = 0$ at the boundary.

Therefore:
$$V_{eq} = V_{max} - V_{min} = 2700 - 0 = 2700 \text{ m}^3$$

This means the basin must hold **2,700 m³** to dampen the peak flows to a constant $Q_{avg} = 500$ m³/h.

**Volume check:** $V_{eq} / Q_{avg} = 2700 / 500 = 5.4$ hours → within the **2 to 8 hour** design window ✓.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Activated Sludge Process F/M Ratio and SRT Relationship

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define F/M Ratio**

The Food-to-Microorganism ratio is the substrate loading rate per unit biomass:

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{S_0 \cdot Q}{V \cdot X}$$

where $S_0$ = influent BOD₅ (mg/L), $Q$ = flow (m³/day), $V$ = aeration tank volume (m³), $X$ = MLSS (mg/L).

**Step 2: Define SRT (Mean Cell Residence Time) $\theta_c$**

$$\theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + (Q - Q_w) \cdot X_e}$$

Assuming effluent suspended solids $X_e \approx 0$ and $Q_w \cdot X_w$ dominates:

$$\theta_c \approx \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$$

**Step 3: Relate F/M to SRT (Activated Sludge Design Equation)**

Rearranging the definition of $\theta_c$ and combining with the biomass mass balance under steady-state:

$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot \frac{F}{M} - k_d$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\frac{F}{M} = \frac{1/\theta_c + k_d}{Y}}$$

**Step 4: Standard Design Numbers (Verify)**

- $Y = 0.4$ to $0.6$ mg VSS/mg BOD₅
- $k_d = 0.04$ to $0.08$ day⁻¹
- For $\theta_c = 10$ days, $Y = 0.5$, $k_d = 0.06$ day⁻¹:
$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{1/10 + 0.06}{0.5} = \frac{0.16}{0.5} = 0.32 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

This falls within the standard **0.2 to 0.5 day⁻¹** conventional ASP range. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — BOD Removal Efficiency in Secondary Treatment

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: First-Order Kinetics for BOD Removal**

For a completely-mixed activated sludge system, substrate removal follows:

$$\frac{S_0 - S_e}{X \cdot t} = \frac{k \cdot S_e}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

**Step 2: Solving for Effluent BOD**

$$S_e = \frac{S_0}{1 + k \cdot X \cdot t \cdot (1 + k_d \theta_c)^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: BOD Removal Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + k \cdot X \cdot t \cdot (1 + k_d \theta_c)^{-1}}$$

For typical values: $\eta \approx 85$ to $95\%$ (consistent with the table in Section 1.1).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Treatment Stage

| Pollutant / Problem | Best Treatment Stage | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Large debris, rags, grit | Preliminary | Screening/grid removal |
| Suspended particles (> 100 µm) | Primary | Gravity settling dominates |
| Dissolved BOD/COD | Secondary | Biological oxidation |
| Ammonia (NH₃-N) | Secondary/Tertiary | Nitrification (biological) |
| Total Nitrogen | Tertiary | Nitrification + Denitrification |
| Total Phosphorus | Tertiary | Chemical or biological P removal |
| Pathogens (E. coli, coliform) | Tertiary | Chlorination, UV |
| Heavy metals | Tertiary | Adsorption, ion exchange |
| Flow variation shock | **Equalization basin (preliminary stage)** | Buffer hydraulic and organic load |
| pH/Toxic shock | **Equalization basin (preliminary stage)** | Mix and neutralize |

---

    J --> L[Sludge Thickener]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Sludge Dewatering]

```

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define primary treatment of wastewater. List any two units used in primary treatment.**

**Model Answer:**
Primary treatment is the **physical separation of suspended and floating solids** from wastewater through sedimentation and/or floatation, typically using a **primary clarifier** and **skimming tank**. It removes **25–40% of BOD** and **50–70% of total suspended solids (TSS)**, reducing the load on the subsequent biological (secondary) treatment stage.

**Two units used in primary treatment:**
1. **Primary Sedimentation Tank (Clarifier)** — uses gravity settling.
2. **Skimming Tank** — removes floating matter (oil, grease, scum) from the surface.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by equalization of flow in a sewage treatment plant? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**
**Equalization of flow** is the process of dampening fluctuations in the **flow rate and pollutant concentration** of influent sewage using an **equalization basin** positioned after preliminary treatment. The basin is typically equipped with **aerators or mechanical mixers** to ensure uniform quality and to prevent septicity.

**Importance:**
1. It provides a **constant hydraulic load** to the downstream treatment units, ensuring stable operation.
2. It **buffers shock loads** of BOD, COD, pH, or toxic substances, protecting the biological population in the secondary treatment.
3. It improves the **efficiency and reliability** of chemical dosing, disinfection, and sludge handling.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of secondary treatment** of wastewater with a neat sketch of the **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**. Discuss the role of **MLSS, F/M ratio, and SRT** in process design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data is obtained for a municipal STP design:

- Average flow: $Q = 20$ MLD
- Influent BOD₅: $S_0 = 250$ mg/L
- Effluent BOD₅: $S_e = 30$ mg/L
- MLSS: $X = 3000$ mg/L
- Yield coefficient: $Y = 0.5$
- Endogenous decay: $k_d = 0.06$ day⁻¹

Calculate the **(i) F/M ratio**, **(ii) SRT (Mean Cell Residence Time)**, and **(iii) Aeration tank volume** $V$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A(a)

**Secondary Treatment — Concept:**

Secondary treatment is the **biological oxidation of dissolved and colloidal organic matter** remaining after primary treatment. It uses **microorganisms** (bacteria, protozoa) under controlled environmental conditions to convert biodegradable organics into **CO₂, H₂O, and new microbial cells (sludge)**.

**Activated Sludge Process (ASP) — Sketch (refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid block):**

Components:
- **Aeration Tank** — where air is supplied and mixed liquor is formed.
- **Secondary Clarifier** — separates biomass from treated effluent.
- **Return Activated Sludge (RAS)** — recycles settled biomass to maintain MLSS.
- **Excess (Waste) Sludge** — wasted to maintain steady-state biomass.

**Key Design Parameters:**

1. **MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids):** Concentration of biomass in the aeration tank. Typical range: **2,000–4,000 mg/L**. Higher MLSS = more treatment capacity but also higher oxygen demand and poorer settling.

2. **F/M Ratio (Food-to-Microorganism):** Mass of substrate (BOD) applied per unit mass of biomass per day. 
   $$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{S_0 \cdot Q}{V \cdot X}$$
   - **Low F/M (< 0.2)**: Extended aeration, more endogenous respiration, less sludge.
   - **High F/M (> 0.5)**: Conventional ASP, more sludge production.
   - **Optimum for conventional ASP**: **0.2 to 0.5 day⁻¹**.

3. **SRT (Sludge Retention Time / Mean Cell Residence Time):** Average time a sludge particle remains in the system.
   $$\theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$$
   - Typical: **5 to 15 days** for conventional ASP.
   - Longer SRT → more complete substrate utilization, more nitrification.

### Model Solution A(b)

**Given:**
- $Q = 20$ MLD = 20,000 m³/day
- $S_0 = 250$ mg/L
- $S_e = 30$ mg/L
- $X = 3000$ mg/L
- $Y = 0.5$
- $k_d = 0.06$ day⁻¹

**(i) F/M Ratio:**

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{S_0 \cdot Q}{V \cdot X}$$

We need $V$ first. Using the biomass mass balance:
$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot \frac{F}{M} - k_d$$

But here, we use a different approach — first compute HRT ($t = V/Q$) and assume a typical HRT of **6 hours = 0.25 day** for design verification.

Then:
$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{S_0}{X \cdot t} = \frac{250 \text{ mg/L}}{3000 \text{ mg/L} \times 0.25 \text{ day}} = \frac{250}{750} = 0.333 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**(ii) SRT ($\theta_c$):**

$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot \frac{F}{M} - k_d = 0.5 \times 0.333 - 0.06 = 0.1665 - 0.06 = 0.1065 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c = \frac{1}{0.1065} \approx 9.39 \text{ days} \approx 9.4 \text{ days}}$$

This is within the **5 to 15 days** conventional ASP range. ✓

**(iii) Aeration Tank Volume $V$:**

$$V = Q \times t = 20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 0.25 \text{ day} = \boxed{5{,}000 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Check BOD Removal Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{250 - 30}{250} \times 100 = 88\%$$
This is within the **80–95%** secondary treatment range. ✓

### Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall: F/M Ratio Unit Errors**
> Students frequently forget to convert **MLD to m³/day** and **hours to days** consistently. The F/M ratio MUST be expressed in **day⁻¹**. Forgetting to convert HRT (in hours) to days is the most common error — it leads to a 24× miscalculation. Always write: $t \text{ (day)} = \text{HRT (h)} / 24$.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper]**

**(a)** What is **tertiary treatment** of wastewater? List any **four unit operations/processes** used in tertiary treatment with a brief note on each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipal STP receives a **variable hourly inflow** as given below. The total daily inflow is **14,400 m³**. Design the **volume of an in-line equalization basin** required to provide a **constant outflow** of $Q_{avg}$. Use the **cumulative storage method**. **(7 marks)**

| Time (h) | 0–4 | 4–8 | 8–12 | 12–16 | 16–20 | 20–24 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $Q(t)$ (m³/h) | 400 | 800 | 1000 | 800 | 400 | 200 |

### Model Solution B(a)

**Definition of Tertiary Treatment:**

Tertiary treatment (also called **advanced or polishing treatment**) refers to the **final treatment processes** applied to secondary effluent to **remove residual suspended solids, dissolved nutrients (N, P), pathogens, and refractory organics** so that the treated water meets **stringent discharge standards** or is suitable for **reuse** (irrigation, industrial cooling, groundwater recharge).

**Four Unit Operations / Processes:**

1. **Filtration (e.g., Rapid Sand Filter or Multimedia Filter):**
   Removes residual SS (5–10 mg/L) from secondary effluent. Filtration rates: **5 to 15 m³/m²·h**. Backwashing is required periodically.

2. **Disinfection (Chlorination / UV / Ozonation):**
   Eliminates pathogenic microorganisms. Standard chlorine dose: **5 to 15 mg/L** with **30 minutes contact time** to achieve ≤ 200 MPN/100 mL fecal coliform.

3. **Nutrient Removal — Nitrogen (Nitrification + Denitrification):**
   Aerobic nitrification converts NH₃ → NO₃⁻; subsequent anoxic denitrification converts NO₃⁻ → N₂ gas. Methanol or acetate is often added as a carbon source in the anoxic stage.

4. **Nutrient Removal — Phosphorus (Chemical Precipitation or EBPR):**
   Phosphorus is precipitated as AlPO₄ or FePO₄ using **alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃]**, **ferric chloride [FeCl₃]**, or **lime [Ca(OH)₂]**. Dose: **5 to 30 mg/L** of coagulant.

### Model Solution B(b)

**Given:**
- Total daily inflow = 14,400 m³
- $Q_{avg} = 14,400 / 24 = 600$ m³/h
- Hourly inflow pattern over 24 hours (4-hour intervals)

**Step 1: Calculate Inflow Volume in Each 4-Hour Interval**

| Interval | $Q(t)$ (m³/h) | Duration (h) | Volume In (m³) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0–4 | 400 | 4 | 1,600 |
| 4–8 | 800 | 4 | 3,200 |
| 8–12 | 1000 | 4 | 4,000 |
| 12–16 | 800 | 4 | 3,200 |
| 16–20 | 400 | 4 | 1,600 |
| 20–24 | 200 | 4 | 800 |
| **Total** | — | **24** | **14,400** ✓ |

**Step 2: Calculate Cumulative Inflow and Cumulative Outflow**

Outflow rate = $Q_{avg} = 600$ m³/h → 2,400 m³ every 4 hours.

| Time (h) | Cum. Inflow (m³) | Cum. Outflow (m³) | Storage $V_s$ (m³) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 1,600 | 2,400 | -800 |
| 8 | 4,800 | 4,800 | 0 |
| 12 | 8,800 | 7,200 | +1,600 |
| 16 | 12,000 | 9,600 | +2,400 |
| 20 | 13,600 | 12,000 | +1,600 |
| 24 | 14,400 | 14,400 | 0 |

**Step 3: Identify Maximum and Minimum Storage**

- $V_{max} = +2,400$ m³ (at $t = 16$ h)
- $V_{min} = -800$ m³ (at $t = 4$ h)

**Step 4: Required Equalization Basin Volume**

$$\boxed{V_{eq} = V_{max} - V_{min} = 2{,}400 - (-800) = 3{,}200 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 5: Verify Detention Time**

$$t_{eq} = \frac{V_{eq}}{Q_{avg}} = \frac{3{,}200}{600} = 5.33 \text{ hours}$$

This is within the **2 to 8 hour** standard design range. ✓

### Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall: Sign Convention in Storage Calculations**
> The most frequent error is **ignoring the negative storage** (when inflow is less than the equalized outflow). A student who only takes the maximum positive value misses the critical fact that the basin must also accommodate the period when **stored water is being released to maintain constant outflow** during low-inflow hours. The correct volume is always $V_{max} - V_{min}$ (NOT $V_{max}$ alone). This is a guaranteed 3–4 mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Primary Treatment** uses **physical processes** (gravity sedimentation) to remove **suspended solids and floating matter**. Typical removal: **25–40% BOD, 50–70% TSS**.

- **Secondary Treatment** uses **biological processes** (activated sludge, trickling filter, UASB) to remove **dissolved organics**. Typical removal: **80–95% BOD, 70–90% TSS**.

- **Tertiary Treatment** is the **polishing stage** for **nutrient removal, disinfection, and advanced filtration**. Includes chlorination, UV, RO, nutrient removal (N & P).

- **Equalization of Flow** uses an **equalization basin** to dampen **diurnal and shock load variations**. Volume = $V_{eq} = V_{max} - V_{min}$ (cumulative storage method). Detention time = **2 to 8 hours**.

- **F/M Ratio** in ASP: **0.2 to 0.5 day⁻¹**; **SRT**: **5 to 15 days**; **MLSS**: **2,000 to 4,000 mg/L**; **Recycle Ratio R**: **0.25 to 1.0**.

- **Design formulas to memorize:**
  - Stoke's Law: $v_s = \frac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$
  - Equalization: $V_{eq} = V_{max} - V_{min}$
  - SRT-F/M Relation: $\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot (F/M) - k_d$
  - Aeration oxygen: $O_2 = a S_r Q + b X V$

- **Kerala-Specific Note:** UASB and constructed wetland systems are widely used in Kerala due to **high ambient temperature** and **land availability**. Mention UASB in KTU answers to score well — examiners appreciate regional context.

- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** of the treatment process. Marks are reserved for **neat sketches with arrow flow direction** as per the valuation key.

- **Unit Conversions to Memorize:**
  - 1 MLD = 1,000 m³/day
  - 1 m³/h = 24 m³/day
  - 1 mg/L = 1 g/m³ = 1 ppm (for water)
  - HRT (hours) / 24 = HRT (days)

- **Do NOT confuse:**
  - **F/M** (substrate loading, day⁻¹) vs. **Volumetric Loading** (kg BOD/m³·day)
  - **SRT** (sludge age, days) vs. **HRT** (hydraulic retention time, hours)
  - **MLSS** (mixed liquor, mg/L) vs. **MLVSS** (volatile fraction, ~70–80% of MLSS)

- **Key CPCB/KSPCB Discharge Standards (For Reference):**
  - BOD₅: ≤ 30 mg/L (general), ≤ 10 mg/L (for sensitive water bodies)
  - TSS: ≤ 100 mg/L
  - Fecal Coliform: ≤ 1,000 MPN/100 mL

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secondary Treatment Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Secondary Treatment Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Secondary Treatment** is the biological stage of wastewater treatment that follows primary sedimentation. It is specifically designed to remove the **dissolved and suspended organic matter** (measured as Biochemical Oxygen Demand, BOD) that escapes primary treatment, using **microbial oxidation** under controlled environmental conditions. As per KTU PCCET503 Module 3, secondary treatment methods are classified into two principal categories based on the mode of microbial growth:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Secondary Treatment (KTU Board Exam Standard)**
> 1. **Suspended Growth Processes** – Microorganisms are kept in suspension by mixing/aeration (e.g., Activated Sludge Process, Aerated Lagoons, Oxidation Ditch).
> 2. **Attached Growth Processes** – Microorganisms grow as a biofilm over a solid support medium (e.g., Trickling Filter, Rotating Biological Contactor, Biological Towers).

The target BOD removal efficiency of secondary treatment is **80 % – 95 %**, and the effluent BOD is typically brought down from 200–250 mg/L to **20–30 mg/L** before disinfection.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Biological Kitchen"**
> Imagine a giant aerated kitchen where millions of microscopic "chefs" (bacteria) are floating around in a broth of wastewater. You keep feeding them organic waste (food). As long as you supply **oxygen** (the gas supply) and keep them **suspended** (stirring), they happily eat the dissolved organic pollutants, convert them into harmless CO₂, water, and new microbial cells (sludge). You then "filter" out the chefs (sludge) using a clarifier, and the clean soup (effluent) flows out. This is essentially the **Activated Sludge Process** — the most widely used secondary treatment method in the world.
>
> Now imagine the same kitchen, but the chefs are stuck to a **sticky wall** (rock media) over which wastewater trickles. Air naturally provides oxygen. The chefs attached to the wall eat the food as it trickles past. This is the **Trickling Filter** — an attached growth system.

---

### 1.3 Purpose of Secondary Treatment (Why It Matters)

- **Pollutant Removal:** Brings down BOD by **80–95 %** and removes **60–90 %** of total suspended solids.
- **Disinfection Pre-requisite:** Reduces organic load so that chlorine demand during tertiary disinfection becomes economically feasible.
- **Nitrification Stage:** Some modifications (e.g., Extended Aeration) can oxidize ammonia to nitrate.
- **Public Health & Ecology:** Prevents oxygen depletion in receiving water bodies, protecting aquatic life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Secondary treatment is a mandatory unit process for any municipal sewage treatment plant (STP) discharging into inland surface waters, as mandated by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Suspended Growth Process — The Activated Sludge Process (ASP)

The **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** is the workhorse of secondary treatment, developed by **Ardern and Lockett (1914)** in Manchester, UK. Wastewater is mixed with a biologically active sludge (biomass) in an aeration tank, supplied with air/oxygen, and the resulting mixture is separated in a secondary clarifier. A portion of the settled sludge is **recycled** back to the aeration tank to maintain a high concentration of active microorganisms.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Mixing Phase:** Influent wastewater (after primary clarification) enters the aeration tank and is mixed with **Return Activated Sludge (RAS)**.
2. **Aeration Phase:** Diffused air or mechanical aerators supply **dissolved oxygen (DO)** typically maintained at **1.5 – 2.0 mg/L** in the aeration tank.
3. **Biological Reaction:** Aerobic heterotrophic bacteria oxidize organic matter:
   $$\text{Organic Matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bacteria}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New Cells (sludge)}$$
4. **Settling Phase:** The mixed liquor flows to a secondary clarifier; clear supernatant exits as effluent.
5. **Sludge Recycle:** A portion of settled sludge is returned (RAS) to maintain **MLSS** in the aeration tank.
6. **Waste Sludge (WAS):** Excess sludge is wasted to maintain food-to-microorganism balance.

---

### 2.2 Modifications of Activated Sludge Process (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Modification | Aeration Time (hr) | MLSS (mg/L) | F/M Ratio | BOD Removal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional | 4 – 6 | 1500 – 3000 | 0.2 – 0.4 | 85 – 95 % |
| Completely Mixed | 3 – 5 | 3000 – 6000 | 0.2 – 0.5 | 85 – 95 % |
| Extended Aeration | 18 – 36 | 3000 – 6000 | 0.05 – 0.15 | 90 – 98 % |
| High-Rate | 1 – 3 | 4000 – 8000 | 0.4 – 1.5 | 60 – 75 % |
| Contact Stabilization | 0.5 – 1.0 (contact) | 1000 – 3000 | 0.2 – 0.5 | 80 – 90 % |

---

### 2.3 Attached Growth Process — Trickling Filter

Wastewater is **sprinkled** over a bed of coarse media (rock, slag, or plastic). Microorganisms grow as a **biofilm (zoogleal film)** of thickness **0.1 – 0.3 mm** on the media surface. As water trickles down, organic matter is adsorbed and metabolized. A natural draft or forced air provides oxygen.

**Types of Trickling Filters (KTU Classification):**

| Type | Loading (m³/m²·d) | BOD Loading (kg/ha·d) | Recirculation | Stone Depth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low / Standard Rate | 1 – 4 | 800 – 4800 | None | 1.8 – 2.5 m |
| High Rate | 10 – 40 | 4800 – 96000 | 1:1 to 4:1 | 0.9 – 1.8 m |
| Super High Rate (Towers) | 40 – 200 | Up to 1,60,000 | Yes | 3 – 12 m |

---

### 2.4 Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)

A series of closely spaced circular **plastic disks** (35–45 % submerged) rotate slowly (1–2 rpm) on a horizontal shaft. Biomass grows on the disk surface; rotation alternately exposes biofilms to wastewater (absorption) and air (oxygenation). RBCs are highly efficient, low-energy systems, suitable for small communities.

> [!NOTE]
> **RBC Design Standards (KTU Quick Reference):**
> - Peripheral velocity: **0.3 – 0.6 m/s**
> - Hydraulic loading: **0.08 – 0.16 m³/m²·d**
> - Organic loading: **5 – 20 g BOD/m²·d**
> - BOD removal efficiency: **80 – 95 %**

---

### 2.5 Secondary Clarifier — The Unsung Hero

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept:** A secondary clarifier performs three functions — (1) solids-liquid separation of mixed liquor, (2) thickening of return sludge, and (3) sludge storage during peak flows. It is **NOT** a simple settling tank; it is a multi-functional unit with a typical overflow rate of **16 – 28 m³/m²·d** and a solids loading of **100 – 150 kg/m²·d** (for activated sludge return).

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F/M Ratio | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ | 0.2 – 0.4 (Conventional) |
| 2 | Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT, $\theta_c$) | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | day | 5 – 15 |
| 3 | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT, $\theta$) | $\theta = V/Q$ | hr | 4 – 8 (Conventional) |
| 4 | Volumetric Loading (VL) | $VL = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·d | 0.3 – 0.7 |
| 5 | Specific substrate utilization rate (U) | $U = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{X \cdot \theta} = \dfrac{F/M}{E / (1 - E)}$ | day⁻¹ | 0.3 – 0.7 |
| 6 | Recirculation Ratio (R) | $R = \dfrac{Q_R}{Q}$ | dimensionless | 0.25 – 1.0 |
| 7 | Sludge Volume Index (SVI) | $SVI = \dfrac{V_{obs} \text{ (mL/L)}}{MLSS \text{ (g/L)}}$ | mL/g | 80 – 150 |
| 8 | Sludge Density Index (SDI) | $SDI = \dfrac{100}{SVI}$ | dimensionless | 0.7 – 1.2 |
| 9 | Return Sludge Concentration $X_R$ | $X_R = \dfrac{10^6}{SVI} \cdot r$ (approx.) | mg/L | 6000 – 12000 |
| 10 | Oxygen Requirement (Stoichiometric) | $O_2 = 1.42 \cdot \Delta X + 0.62 \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot Q$ | kg O₂/d | - |
| 11 | BOD Removal Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | % | 85 – 95 |
| 12 | Trickling Filter — NRC Equation | $E_2 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}}$ | % | - |
| 13 | Trickling Filter — Rankine | $\eta = 1 - (1 - E_1)^n$ | dimensionless | - |
| 14 | Oxygen Transfer Efficiency (OTE) | $OTE = \dfrac{O_2 \text{ actually absorbed}}{O_2 \text{ supplied}}$ | % | 8 – 12 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** In the F/M formula, $X$ is the **MLVSS** (Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids) in many textbooks, NOT MLSS. Always check the question's convention. For conservative design in KTU papers, $X$ is often used as MLSS unless specified otherwise.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal STPs:** Activated Sludge is used in **> 75 %** of Indian urban STPs under the Namami Gange Programme.
- **Industrial Effluents:** Modified ASPs treat effluents from **pharma, dairy, textile, and petrochemical** industries.
- **Decentralized Systems:** RBCs and Packaged ASPs serve housing colonies, hotels, and hospitals.
- **Space-Constrained Cities:** MBBR (Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor), a hybrid of suspended and attached growth, is increasingly used in urban India.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: F/M Ratio and Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT)

The **Food-to-Microorganism Ratio (F/M)** is the most fundamental design parameter in ASP. It represents the daily mass of substrate (food, in terms of BOD) applied per unit mass of active biomass (microorganisms) in the aeration tank.

**Starting Definitions:**

Let:
- $Q$ = Influent wastewater flow rate (m³/d)
- $S_0$ = Influent soluble BOD (mg/L)
- $S$ = Effluent soluble BOD (mg/L)
- $V$ = Volume of aeration tank (m³)
- $X$ = Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS) in aeration tank (mg/L)
- $Q_w$ = Waste activated sludge flow rate (m³/d)
- $X_w$ = Concentration of solids in waste sludge (mg/L)
- $Q_e$ = Effluent flow rate (m³/d)
- $X_e$ = Suspended solids in effluent (mg/L)

**Step 1: Mass of Food (Substrate) applied per day**

$$F = Q \cdot S_0 \quad \text{[g/d or kg/d]}$$

**Step 2: Mass of Microorganisms in the aeration tank**

$$M = V \cdot X \quad \text{[g or kg]}$$

**Step 3: Take the ratio (Food per Microorganism per day)**

$$F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} \quad \text{[day}^{-1}\text{]}$$

This represents the specific substrate loading rate.

---

**Step 4: Derivation of Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT, $\theta_c$)**

SRT is the average time a microorganism spends in the system. It is the key parameter for biological process control.

**Inventory (Mass) of biomass in reactor:**

$$M_{\text{inventory}} = V \cdot X$$

**Rate at which biomass leaves the system (per day):**

- Via wastage: $Q_w \cdot X_w$
- Via effluent: $Q_e \cdot X_e$

Total biomass leaving rate = $Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e$

**Step 5: Apply the definition of SRT (residence time = inventory / outflow)**

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e} \quad \text{[days]}$$

**Step 6: Practical simplification**

In most KTU problems, $Q_e \cdot X_e$ is negligible compared to $Q_w \cdot X_w$, hence:

$$\theta_c \approx \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SRT is the "master variable" in ASP design:** While F/M changes with influent variations, SRT depends only on the operator-controlled wasting rate. Maintaining a constant $\theta_c$ ensures stable BOD removal. For example, $\theta_c$ > 3 days typically yields > 85 % BOD removal.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Substrate Utilization Rate ($U$) and its Relation to F/M

**Step 1: Substrate removed per unit time**

$$\Delta S \cdot Q = (S_0 - S) \cdot Q \quad \text{[g/d]}$$

**Step 2: Substrate utilized per unit mass of biomass per day ($U$)**

$$U = \dfrac{(S_0 - S)}{X \cdot \theta} \quad \text{[day}^{-1}\text{]}$$

where $\theta = V/Q$ is hydraulic retention time.

**Step 3: Express $U$ in terms of F/M and removal efficiency $E$**

By definition: $E = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0}$, hence $S = S_0(1 - E)$.

$$U = \dfrac{S_0 \cdot E}{X \cdot \theta} = \dfrac{S_0 \cdot E}{X \cdot (V/Q)} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0 \cdot E}{V \cdot X} = (F/M) \cdot E$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{U = (F/M) \times E \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad F/M = \dfrac{U}{E}}$$

**Verification with numbers:** If F/M = 0.3 d⁻¹ and $E = 0.9$, then $U = 0.27 \text{ d}^{-1}$. This means each gram of biomass uses 0.27 g of BOD per day.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Return Sludge Ratio from Mass Balance

A mass balance on biomass around the aeration tank gives the relationship between the recirculation ratio ($R$) and the return sludge concentration ($X_R$).

**Step 1: Mass balance on solids**

Mass of biomass entering + produced = Mass leaving

$$Q \cdot X_0 + Q_R \cdot X_R = (Q + Q_R) \cdot X$$

Since $X_0 \approx 0$ (influent MLSS is negligible post-primary treatment):

$$Q_R \cdot X_R = (Q + Q_R) \cdot X$$

**Step 2: Solve for $X_R$**

$$X_R = \dfrac{(Q + Q_R) \cdot X}{Q_R} = X \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{Q}{Q_R}\right) = X \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{R}\right)$$

Since $R = Q_R / Q$:

$$\boxed{X_R = X \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{R}\right) = \dfrac{X \cdot (1 + R)}{R}}$$

**Step 3: Solve for R in terms of $X$ and $X_R$**

$$R = \dfrac{X}{X_R - X}$$

**Numerical example:** If $X$ (MLSS) = 2500 mg/L and desired $X_R$ = 10000 mg/L, then:

$$R = \dfrac{2500}{10000 - 2500} = \dfrac{2500}{7500} = 0.333$$

So 33.3 % of the influent flow must be recirculated as RAS.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: BOD Removal Efficiency of Trickling Filter using Rankine's Equation

**Step 1: Define single-stage efficiency**

For a single contact of wastewater with the filter media, the efficiency is $E_1$.

**Step 2: After n stages (with recirculation considered as additional stages), the overall efficiency $\eta$ is:**

$$\eta = 1 - (1 - E_1)^n$$

**Step 3: Alternatively, using the NRC (National Research Council) Equation:**

$$E_2 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}}$$

where:
- $E_2$ = % BOD removal efficiency of a two-stage filter
- $W$ = BOD loading (kg/d)
- $V$ = Filter volume (m³)
- $F$ = Recirculation factor = $\dfrac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 R)^2}$

**Worked Example:**

Given: $W = 500 \text{ kg BOD/d}$, $V = 800 \text{ m}^3$, $R = 1.5$.

Compute $F$:
$$F = \dfrac{1 + 1.5}{(1 + 0.1 \times 1.5)^2} = \dfrac{2.5}{(1.15)^2} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.3225} = 1.890$$

Compute $E_2$:
$$E_2 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{500}{800 \times 1.890}}}$$

$$= \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{0.3307}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \times 0.5751} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.2548} = \dfrac{100}{1.2548} = 79.7\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A two-stage trickling filter with the given loading and recirculation achieves approximately **79.7 % BOD removal** in a single pass. With secondary clarification, the overall plant removal would be higher.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Secondary Treatment Methods

| Parameter | Activated Sludge | Trickling Filter | RBC | Oxidation Pond |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Land Required | Low | Moderate | Low | Very High |
| Energy | High (aeration) | Low | Low | Negligible |
| Skilled Operation | High | Moderate | Low | Low |
| BOD Removal | 85 – 95 % | 65 – 90 % | 80 – 95 % | 70 – 90 % |
| Sludge Production | High | Moderate | Low | Negligible |
| Nitrification Possible | Yes (Extended) | Limited | Yes | Yes (Maturation) |
| Cost (Capital) | High | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| Suitability | Medium–Large cities | Small–Medium | Small communities | Small towns, rural |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a comparison is asked, always tabulate the comparison — examiners reward structured presentation with **2–3 bonus marks** in long-answer questions.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flow Diagram — Activated Sludge Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Influent Wastewater] --> B[Primary Clarifier]
    B --> C[Aeration Tank]
    D[Return Activated Sludge RAS] --> C
    C --> E[Secondary Clarifier]
    E --> F[Treated Effluent to Disinfection]
    E --> G[Waste Activated Sludge WAS to Sludge Treatment]
    G --> H[Sludge Digestion and Disposal]
    E --> D
    C -.Aeration.-> I[Air Supply Blower Diffuser]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed line shows aeration supply; the solid loop E → D represents the recirculation path of settled sludge back to the aeration tank — a defining feature of ASP.

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Suspended vs Attached Growth Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SG[Suspended Growth Activated Sludge]
        SG1[Influent BOD] --> SG2[Aeration Tank with MLSS]
        SG2 --> SG3[Secondary Clarifier]
        SG3 --> SG4[Return Sludge Recycle]
        SG4 --> SG2
        SG3 --> SG5[Effluent Discharge]
    end
    subgraph AG[Attached Growth Trickling Filter]
        AG1[Influent BOD] --> AG2[Rotating Distributor]
        AG2 --> AG3[Biofilm on Rock Media]
        AG3 --> AG4[Underdrain System]
        AG4 --> AG5[Secondary Clarifier]
        AG5 --> AG6[Effluent Discharge]
        AG5 --> AG7[Recirculation Pump]
        AG7 --> AG2
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Process Flow — Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Influent Sewage] --> Q[Primary Treatment]
    Q --> R[First Stage RBC Disks]
    R --> S[Second Stage RBC Disks]
    S --> T[Third Stage RBC Disks]
    T --> U[Final Stage RBC Disks]
    U --> V[Secondary Clarifier]
    V --> W[Chlorine Contact Tank]
    W --> X[Final Disinfected Effluent]
    V --> Y[Sludge Wasting]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic — Mass Balance Around Aeration Tank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InfluentQ[Q S0] --> Tank
    RAS[QR XR] --> Tank
    Tank[Aeration Tank Volume V with MLSS X] --> Effluent[Q plus QR with X]
    Effluent --> Clarifier
    Clarifier[Secondary Clarifier] --> OutEff[Q Se XQe]
    Clarifier --> WAS[Qw Xw]
    Clarifier --> RAS2[QR XR to RAS Line]
    RAS2 --> Tank
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The aeration tank acts as a completely mixed reactor (CSTR) where influent and RAS are immediately homogenized. The clarifier divides the outflow into three streams: clarified effluent, return sludge, and waste sludge.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the Activated Sludge Process and list any four design parameters.**

**Model Answer:**
The Activated Sludge Process (ASP) is a **suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment** method in which a mixture of wastewater and biologically active sludge (biomass) is aerated in a tank to degrade organic matter, followed by separation in a secondary clarifier. Part of the settled sludge is recycled to maintain biomass concentration.

**Four design parameters:**
1. **F/M Ratio** (typically 0.2 – 0.4 day⁻¹ for conventional ASP)
2. **MLSS** (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids = 1500 – 3000 mg/L)
3. **HRT / Aeration Period** (4 – 6 hours)
4. **SRT / Mean Cell Residence Time** (5 – 15 days)
5. **Recirculation Ratio** (0.25 – 1.0)

*(Any four accepted; 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for parameters)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Solved Paper]**
**Differentiate between suspended growth and attached growth biological treatment systems with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Suspended Growth | Attached Growth |
|---|---|---|
| Microbial state | Free-floating in mixed liquor | Attached as biofilm on media |
| Oxygen supply | Mechanical aeration / diffused air | Natural draft / rotation |
| Examples | Activated Sludge Process, Aerated Lagoons | Trickling Filter, Rotating Biological Contactor |
| Sludge settleability | Critical (SVI check needed) | Less critical (sloughed biomass settles) |
| Energy | High | Low |
| Land area | Compact | Larger footprint |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Each long-answer question offers an OR option. Both options are given below. You are expected to answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

(a) **Design an Activated Sludge Process for a municipal sewage treatment plant with the following data:** **(7 marks)**

- Average flow: $Q = 10 \text{ MLD}$ (Million Litres per Day)
- Influent BOD: $S_0 = 250 \text{ mg/L}$
- Effluent BOD required: $S = 30 \text{ mg/L}$
- F/M ratio adopted: $0.3 \text{ day}^{-1}$
- MLSS: $X = 2500 \text{ mg/L}$
- Recirculation ratio: $R = 0.5$

**Determine:**
(i) Volume of aeration tank
(ii) Hydraulic retention time
(iii) Recirculation flow rate
(iv) Excess sludge production (assume yield coefficient $Y = 0.5$, $k_d = 0.06 \text{ d}^{-1}$)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute influent BOD load (Food)**

$$F = Q \cdot S_0 = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 250 \text{ mg/L} = 2,500,000 \text{ g/d} = 2500 \text{ kg/d}$$

**Step 2: Volume of Aeration Tank from F/M equation**

$$F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} \Rightarrow V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}$$

$$V = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 250}{2500 \times 0.3} = \dfrac{2{,}500{,}000}{750} = 3333.33 \text{ m}^3$$

**Aeration tank volume ≈ 3333 m³** ✓

**Step 3: Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**

$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{3333.33}{10{,}000} \text{ days} = 0.3333 \text{ d} = 8 \text{ hours}$$

**HRT ≈ 8 hours** ✓

**Step 4: Recirculation flow rate**

$$Q_R = R \cdot Q = 0.5 \times 10 \text{ MLD} = 5 \text{ MLD}$$

**Step 5: Excess sludge production rate**

Using the biomass balance equation:

$$\Delta X = Y \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) - k_d \cdot V \cdot X$$

- $Y \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.5 \times 10{,}000 \times (250 - 30) = 0.5 \times 10{,}000 \times 220 = 1{,}100{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 1100 \text{ kg/d}$
- $k_d \cdot V \cdot X = 0.06 \times 3333.33 \times 2500 = 0.06 \times 8{,}333{,}325 \text{ mg/d} \approx 500 \text{ kg/d}$

$$\Delta X = 1100 - 500 = 600 \text{ kg/d}$$

**Excess sludge ≈ 600 kg/d** ✓

---

(b) **Explain the working principle of a Trickling Filter with a neat labelled sketch. Compare its performance with the Activated Sludge Process.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A trickling filter consists of a **circular or rectangular tank** filled with a coarse media (broken stone, slag, or plastic) over which wastewater is **sprinkled** using a rotary distributor. A **biofilm (zoogleal layer)** of aerobic microorganisms grows on the media surface, typically **0.1 – 0.3 mm** thick. As wastewater trickles past:
1. **Sorption:** Organic matter is adsorbed onto the biofilm.
2. **Oxidation:** Microorganisms metabolize the organics using oxygen supplied by natural draft through the underdrain vents.
3. **Sloughing:** Excess biomass sloughs off and is carried away with the effluent to the secondary clarifier.

**Comparison Table (1 mark each parameter):**

| Parameter | Trickling Filter | Activated Sludge |
|---|---|---|
| Energy | Low (no aeration needed) | High (mechanical aeration) |
| Operation | Simple | Skilled |
| Sludge | Less, well-stabilized | High, requires digestion |
| BOD Removal | 65 – 90 % | 85 – 95 % |
| Shock Load | Tolerates moderately | Sensitive |
| Footprint | Large | Compact |
| Cost | Lower OPEX, higher CAPEX | Higher OPEX, lower CAPEX |

*(Neat sketch should be drawn on answer sheet for 2 marks. Total: 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Solved Question Paper]**

(a) **A municipal wastewater with flow $Q = 5 \text{ MLD}$ and BOD $= 200 \text{ mg/L}$ is to be treated in a high-rate trickling filter. The BOD loading is $0.5 \text{ kg/m}^3 \cdot \text{d}$. The recirculation factor $F = 1.5$. Calculate the volume of the filter, the BOD removal efficiency, and the effluent BOD using the NRC equation.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute BOD loading per day**

$$W = Q \cdot S_0 = 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 200 \text{ mg/L} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 1000 \text{ kg/d}$$

**Step 2: Volume of filter from loading rate**

$$V = \dfrac{W}{\text{BOD Loading}} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ kg/d}}{0.5 \text{ kg/m}^3 \cdot \text{d}} = 2000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Volume of filter = 2000 m³** ✓

**Step 3: Apply NRC Equation**

$$E_2 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}}$$

$$E_2 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{2000 \times 1.5}}}$$

$$= \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{0.3333}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \times 0.5774}$$

$$= \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.2558} = \dfrac{100}{1.2558} = 79.62\%$$

**Efficiency ≈ 79.6 %** ✓

**Step 4: Effluent BOD**

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot (1 - E_2) = 200 \times (1 - 0.796) = 200 \times 0.204 = 40.8 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Effluent BOD ≈ 40.8 mg/L** ✓

---

(b) **Explain the Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) with a neat diagram. List its advantages and limitations.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction and Working:**
An RBC consists of a series of closely spaced **polyethylene / polystyrene plastic disks** (typically 1–3 m diameter) mounted on a horizontal rotating shaft. The shaft rotates at **1 – 2 rpm** with **35 – 45 %** of the disk area submerged. Biomass grows on the disk surface; rotation alternately exposes biofilms to:
- **Submerged phase** (wastewater contact) → sorption of organics
- **Air phase** (above water) → oxygen absorption and oxidation

The wastewater flows through multiple stages in series (typically 3 – 4 stages) for progressive BOD reduction.

**Advantages (any 4):**
1. Low energy consumption (only rotation, no aeration)
2. Easy to operate; minimal skilled manpower
3. Compact footprint compared to trickling filters
4. Tolerates shock loads well
5. Less sludge production
6. Quiet operation (no aerator noise)

**Limitations (any 3):**
1. High capital cost (mechanical equipment)
2. Shaft alignment and bearing maintenance
3. Disk fouling in cold weather or high loads
4. Sensitive to toxic influent shocks
5. Limited to small–medium communities

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting flow in MLD to m³/d but forgetting to convert BOD from mg/L to kg/m³. Use: $1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3 = 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^3$.
> 2. **F/M Confusion:** Some students compute $V \cdot X$ in the numerator and $Q \cdot S_0$ in the denominator — which is the **inverse** of the F/M ratio. Memorize the formula: F = Food = $Q \cdot S_0$; M = Microorganisms = $V \cdot X$.
> 3. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Always state the unit (m³, days, mg/L). KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units.
> 4. **Forgetting the 0.62 / 1.42 Stoichiometric Coefficients in Oxygen Demand Calculation:** Oxygen required = $1.42 \cdot \Delta X + 0.62 \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q$. Forgetting these constants is the most common error in oxygenation questions.
> 5. **Trickling Filter Design:** Students often use the **wrong** equation (NRC vs Eckenfelder vs Rankine). Always state the equation name before applying it.
> 6. **Diagrams Without Labels:** A neat sketch without labels gets 0 marks for the figure component. Always label influent, effluent, aerator, RAS, WAS, and sludge line.
> 7. **HRT vs SRT Mix-up:** HRT is hydraulic (V/Q); SRT is biological (VX / wastage). Examiners frequently test this distinction — answer accordingly.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Secondary treatment** removes **80–95 %** BOD biologically using microorganisms.
- ✅ **Two main types:** Suspended Growth (ASP) and Attached Growth (Trickling Filter, RBC).
- ✅ **Activated Sludge Process** = Aeration tank + Secondary clarifier + RAS recycling.
- ✅ **MLSS range:** 1500 – 3000 mg/L (Conventional), up to 6000 mg/L (Extended).
- ✅ **F/M = (Q·S₀)/(V·X)** is the food-to-microorganism ratio, **day⁻¹** units.
- ✅ **SRT (θc) = (V·X)/(Qw·Xw + Qe·Xe)** — the master control variable.
- ✅ **HRT (θ) = V/Q** — typically 4 – 8 hours for conventional ASP.
- ✅ **SVI** ideal range: **80 – 150 mL/g** (SVI > 200 indicates bulking sludge).
- ✅ **Oxygen Demand** = $1.42 \cdot \Delta X + 0.62 \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q$ kg O₂/d.
- ✅ **Trickling Filter** uses **biofilm** on rock/plastic media; works by **sorption and oxidation**.
- ✅ **NRC equation:** $E_2 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{W/(V \cdot F)}}$ for two-stage filter efficiency.
- ✅ **RBC** uses rotating disks (1–2 rpm) with 35–45 % submergence — low energy, small community use.
- ✅ **Recirculation ratio R = Q_R / Q** is essential to maintain MLSS in ASP.
- ✅ **Extended Aeration** has long HRT (18–36 h) and produces stabilized sludge — no separate digestor needed.
- ✅ **Secondary clarifier** is NOT just a settler — it thickens and stores sludge too.
- ✅ **Aeration tank DO** must be maintained at **1.5 – 2.0 mg/L** to keep biomass aerobic.
- ✅ **Bulking sludge** (SVI > 200) is the most common operational problem in ASP — caused by filamentous bacteria.
- ✅ **Compare ASP vs TF** in tabular form during exam — gets you full marks faster.
- ✅ **MBBR, SBR, MBR** are modern modifications; remember MBBR uses floating plastic media in aeration tank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** For numerical problems, write down **Given Data → Formula → Substitution → Answer with Units**. For theory, always structure with **headings, tables, and labelled diagrams**. A well-organized answer sheet in KTU 2024 scheme typically scores **10–15 % higher** than a disorganized one, even with similar content.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Biological Unit Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Biological Unit Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biological Unit Processes** in environmental engineering refer to the controlled, engineered systems that harness the metabolic activities of microorganisms—primarily bacteria—to convert dissolved and suspended organic matter in wastewater into stable end products such as carbon dioxide, water, methane, and excess biological sludge (biomass). These processes are the cornerstone of secondary wastewater treatment and operate on the principles of **biochemistry, microbiology, and reaction engineering**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCET503 – Module 3):**
> *Biological unit processes are treatment methods that utilise microorganisms to decompose or transform organic and inorganic pollutants present in wastewater into harmless or less harmful substances, primarily through bio-oxidation under controlled environmental conditions.*

The process is governed by a fundamental mass balance on the **carbonaceous biochemical oxygen demand (cBOD)** and the **active biomass concentration (X)**. The two overarching categories are:

1. **Suspended-Growth Processes** – Microorganisms are kept in suspension by mixing (e.g., Activated Sludge Process, Aerated Lagoons).
2. **Attached-Growth Processes** – Microorganisms form a biofilm on a solid support medium (e.g., Trickling Filters, Rotating Biological Contactors).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Kitchen Composting" Analogy:**
> Imagine you have a large kitchen bin full of food waste (this is your **wastewater** containing organic pollutants). If you simply close the lid, the food will rot slowly and emit a foul smell (this is **anaerobic decomposition** happening in nature—uncontrolled). However, if you *intentionally add air, maintain a warm temperature, and add a culture of "friendly" bacteria* (think of it as a "starter culture" similar to yogurt), the waste decomposes rapidly, with minimum odour, producing compost (analogous to **excess sludge**) and carbon dioxide.
>
> This is exactly what an **Aerobic Biological Treatment Plant** does! The aeration tank is the "warm kitchen", the diffused air supply is the "mixing and oxygen", and the return activated sludge (RAS) is the "starter culture" of microorganisms. The engineer controls the *food-to-microbe ratio* and the *residence time*—just like a chef controls the recipe to get the perfect compost.

### 1.3 Classification of Microorganisms in Biological Treatment

| Group | Size (μm) | Role in Treatment | Example Genera |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bacteria** | 0.5 – 5.0 | Primary workhorses for BOD removal, nitrification, denitrification | *Pseudomonas, Zoogloea, Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter* |
| **Fungi** | 5 – 10 | Effective for low pH and high carbohydrate wastes; filamentous growth | *Geotrichum, Aspergillus* |
| **Algae** | 5 – 30 | Photosynthesis supplies O₂ in oxidation ponds | *Chlorella, Euglena* |
| **Protozoa** | 10 – 50 | Polish effluent by consuming free-swimming bacteria | *Vorticella, Paramecium* |
| **Rotifers & Worms** | 50 – 500 | Ingest sludge particles; indicator of good effluent | *Rotaria, Aeolosoma* |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for emphasis):**
> - **F/M Ratio (Food-to-Microorganism ratio):** typically **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹** for conventional activated sludge.
> - **MLVSS (Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids):** **1500 – 4000 mg/L** in conventional systems.
> - **Sludge Retention Time (SRT / θc):** **5 – 20 days** for conventional activated sludge.
> - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT / θ):** **4 – 8 hours** for conventional activated sludge.
> - **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** maintained at **1.5 – 2.5 mg/L** in aeration tanks.
> - **Temperature optimum:** **20 – 30 °C** for mesophilic bacteria.
> - **pH optimum:** **6.5 – 8.5** for most biological processes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Biochemical Reactions

The core biochemical transformations occurring in biological unit processes can be summarised by simplified stoichiometric reactions.

**Aerobic Oxidation of Organic Matter:**
$$\text{Organic Matter} + \text{O}_2 + \text{Nutrients} \xrightarrow{\text{Aerobic Bacteria}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{New Cells}$$

**Nitrification (Two-step Oxidation of Ammonia):**
$$\text{NH}_4^+ + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} \text{NO}_2^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{NO}_2^- + 0.5\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrobacter}} \text{NO}_3^-$$

**Denitrification (Anoxic Reduction of Nitrate):**
$$\text{NO}_3^- + \text{Organic Carbon} \xrightarrow{\text{Pseudomonas (anoxic)}} \text{N}_2\uparrow + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Anaerobic Digestion (Methanogenesis):**
$$\text{Organic Matter} \xrightarrow{\text{Acidogens + Methanogens}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{New Cells}$$

### 2.2 The Six-Stage Microbial Growth Curve (Why & How)

A pure culture of bacteria introduced into a fresh substrate exhibits a characteristic growth curve. **Understanding this curve is critical because it dictates reactor design, F/M ratio, and SRT.**

| Phase | Name | Duration / Cause | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I** | **Lag Phase** | Cells adapt to new environment; enzyme synthesis | Start-up period; acclimatisation required |
| **II** | **Acceleration Phase** | Growth rate increases | Controlled seeding helps shorten this |
| **III** | **Log (Exponential) Phase** | Maximum growth rate; substrate abundant | High F/M regime; needed for high rate systems |
| **IV** | **Declining Growth Phase** | Substrate becomes limiting | Most activated sludge plants operate here |
| **V** | **Stationary Phase** | Growth = Decay; substrate exhausted | End of batch; clarifier design region |
| **VI** | **Endogenous / Decay Phase** | Cells consume their own protoplasm | Sludge minimisation; extended aeration regime |

### 2.3 Metabolic Pathways in Wastewater Treatment

The classification of metabolic pathways is vital for the *Why* and *How* of process selection:

- **Aerobic Metabolism:** Oxygen acts as the terminal electron acceptor. Produces maximum energy, hence maximum cell yield. **Result:** Rapid, efficient BOD removal with low sludge production.
- **Anoxic Metabolism:** Nitrate (NO₃⁻) acts as the terminal electron acceptor. Used for **denitrification**. The pathway is facultative—microbes can switch.
- **Anaerobic Metabolism:** No exogenous electron acceptor. Substrate itself is reduced. Produces **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)**. **Result:** Low cell yield, energy recovery, suitable for high-strength wastes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarises every formula a KTU student must memorise for Module 3. Variables in boldface are primary design parameters.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Monod Kinetics** | $\mu = \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | $\mu$ = specific growth rate (d⁻¹); $\mu_m$ = maximum specific growth rate (d⁻¹); $S$ = substrate concentration (mg/L); $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (mg/L) | Substrate-limited growth |
| 2 | **Biomass Growth Rate** | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = Y \cdot \dfrac{dS}{dt} - k_d \cdot X$ | $X$ = biomass (mg/L); $Y$ = true yield coefficient (mg VSS/mg BOD); $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient (d⁻¹) | Reactor mass balance |
| 3 | **Net Specific Growth Rate** | $\mu_{obs} = \mu - k_d = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c}$ | $\theta_c$ = Mean Cell Residence Time (days) | Sludge age calculation |
| 4 | **Substrate Removal** | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu \cdot X}{Y}$ | Sign convention: removal is negative | Effluent quality prediction |
| 5 | **Observed Yield Coefficient** | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | $Y_{obs}$ (mg/mg) | Sludge production estimation |
| 6 | **F/M Ratio** | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | $Q$ = flow (m³/d); $S_0$ = influent BOD (mg/L); $V$ = aeration tank volume (m³); $X$ = MLVSS (mg/L) | Process control |
| 7 | **Hydraulic Retention Time** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | $\theta$ in days or hours | Reactor sizing |
| 8 | **Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT)** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | $Q_w$ = waste sludge flow; $X_w$ = waste sludge conc.; $Q_e$ = effluent flow; $X_e$ = effluent SS | Sludge wastage schedule |
| 9 | **Specific Substrate Utilization Rate (U)** | $U = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{X \cdot \theta}$ | $S$ = effluent substrate (mg/L) | Kinetic interpretation |
| 10 | **Oxygen Requirement** | $\text{O}_2 \text{ req.} = a \cdot (S_0 - S) + b \cdot X \cdot \theta_c$ | $a$ = oxygen per unit BOD removed; $b$ = endogenous oxygen rate | Aeration design |
| 11 | **BOD Removal Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ = percent removal | Performance evaluation |
| 12 | **Stoichiometric O₂ for BOD** | $\text{O}_2 = \dfrac{(S_0 - S)}{1.42}$ (for sludge) | 1.42 = COD/VSS ratio | Sludge oxidation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Identity used in KTU derivations:**
> $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = \mu_{obs} = Y \cdot U - k_d$
> This identity links the *Sludge Age* directly to the *Specific Substrate Utilisation Rate* and is the foundation of activated sludge design.

### 2.5 Why These Formulas Matter (Real-World Utility)

The above equations form the backbone of designing **a municipal sewage treatment plant serving 1 lakh population**. The engineer uses:
- The **F/M ratio** to set aeration tank volume ($V = Q \cdot S_0 / (F/M \cdot X)$).
- The **SRT** to determine daily waste sludge quantity ($Q_w$).
- The **oxygen requirement** to size the blowers and diffusers.
- The **observed yield** to size the sludge handling facilities (thickener, digester, drying beds).

Without these equations, biological treatment plants would be designed by guesswork, leading to either under-designed plants (effluent violations) or over-designed plants (economic burden).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Monod Equation (Substrate-Limited Growth)

**Statement:** *In a substrate-limited environment, the specific growth rate of microorganisms follows hyperbolic (saturation) kinetics with respect to the limiting substrate concentration.*

**Starting Point — Empirical Observations:**
At very high substrate concentrations, the growth rate is constant and equal to $\mu_m$. At very low substrate concentrations, the growth rate becomes directly proportional to $S$. This dual behaviour is mathematically identical to the Michaelis–Menten model in enzyme kinetics.

**Step 1:** Begin with the basic hypothesis that growth is first order at low $S$ and zero order at high $S$:
$$\mu \propto S \quad \text{when} \quad S \ll K_s$$
$$\mu \rightarrow \mu_m \quad \text{when} \quad S \gg K_s$$

**Step 2:** Propose a hyperbolic function that satisfies both limits. The simplest form is the saturation hyperbola:
$$\mu = \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$$

**Step 3:** Verify the low-substrate limit (taking $S \ll K_s$, so $K_s + S \approx K_s$):
$$\mu \approx \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s} = \left(\dfrac{\mu_m}{K_s}\right) \cdot S$$
This is first-order in $S$ ✓

**Step 4:** Verify the high-substrate limit (taking $S \gg K_s$, so $K_s + S \approx S$):
$$\mu \approx \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S}{S} = \mu_m$$
This is zero-order (constant) ✓

**Step 5:** Verify the half-saturation point. Setting $S = K_s$:
$$\mu = \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{K_s}{K_s + K_s} = \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \mu_m$$
This is why $K_s$ is called the *half-saturation constant* ✓

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\mu = \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship between SRT, Yield, and Decay

**Statement:** *The Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) is related to the observed yield, true yield, and endogenous decay coefficient.*

**Step 1:** Mass balance on biomass in a complete-mix reactor (CMAS) at steady state:
$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Net Growth}$$
$$0 = Q \cdot X_0 - Q \cdot X_e + V \cdot \left(\mu \cdot X - k_d \cdot X\right)$$

**Step 2:** Assume influent biomass is negligible ($X_0 \approx 0$). Effluent biomass is small but retained via recycle ($X_e$ is small). Simplification:
$$0 = V \cdot X \cdot (\mu - k_d) - Q_w \cdot X_w$$
(The clarifier underflow $Q_w \cdot X_w$ removes the excess sludge.)

**Step 3:** Rearrange for net growth:
$$\mu - k_d = \dfrac{Q_w \cdot X_w}{V \cdot X} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Monod kinetics for $\mu$:
$$\mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S} - k_d = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the observed yield at steady state. The net biomass production per unit substrate removed equals $Y_{obs}$:
$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

**Step 6 (Verification by dimensional analysis):**
- Numerator: $Y$ has units mg biomass/mg substrate (consistent).
- Denominator: $k_d \cdot \theta_c$ is dimensionless (d⁻¹ × days).
- Result: $Y_{obs}$ has units mg/mg ✓

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot U - k_d \quad \text{and} \quad Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Effluent Substrate Concentration from SRT

**Step 1:** Starting from the substrate mass balance at steady state:
$$Q \cdot S_0 = Q \cdot S + V \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu \cdot X}{Y}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\mu = Y \cdot U$ and $U = (S_0 - S)/(X \cdot \theta)$:
$$Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S = V \cdot X \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu}{Y}\right) = V \cdot X \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu_m \cdot S}{Y \cdot (K_s + S)}\right)$$

**Step 3:** After extensive algebraic manipulation (substituting $\theta_c = V \cdot X / (Q_w \cdot X_w)$), the classical result for effluent substrate becomes:
$$S = \dfrac{K_s \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)}{\theta_c \cdot (Y \cdot \mu_m - k_d) - 1}$$

**Step 4 (Physical Interpretation):**
- If $\theta_c$ is **large** (long sludge age) → $S$ becomes **small** (better effluent quality).
- If $\theta_c$ is **too small** (sludge washed out) → denominator becomes zero or negative → complete failure.
- The minimum $\theta_c$ for steady-state operation is $\theta_c^{min} = 1/(\mu_m - k_d)$ → known as the **"washout" SRT**.

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{K_s \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)}{\theta_c \cdot (Y \cdot \mu_m) - (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Suspended vs. Attached Growth

| Parameter | Suspended Growth (ASP) | Attached Growth (TF/RBC) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Biomass state** | Free-floating flocs | Biofilm on media |
| **Mixing energy** | High (mechanical/aeration) | Low (gravity flow) |
| **Recycle needed** | Yes (RAS / WAS) | No (sludge sloughs naturally) |
| **F/M (day⁻¹)** | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.05 – 0.3 |
| **SRT (days)** | 5 – 20 | 10 – 50 |
| **Effluent quality** | Very high (<30 mg/L BOD) | High (<30 mg/L BOD) |
| **Footprint** | Compact | Larger (media volume) |
| **Shock load tolerance** | Moderate | High (biofilm protects microbes) |
| **Sludge production** | Higher | Lower |
| **Examples** | Conventional ASP, Oxidation Ditch, SBR, MBR | Trickling Filter, RBC, MBBR, BAF |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Biological Wastewater Treatment Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    influent["Raw Sewage Influent"] --> primary["Primary Clarifier"]
    primary --> primEff["Primary Effluent"]
    primEff --> aeration["Aeration Tank Aerobic Zone"]
    aeration --> anoxic["Anoxic Zone Denitrification"]
    anoxic --> return1["Mixed Liquor Recycle"]
    return1 --> aeration
    aeration --> clarifier["Secondary Clarifier"]
    clarifier --> ras["Return Activated Sludge RAS"]
    ras --> aeration
    clarifier --> was["Waste Activated Sludge WAS"]
    was --> digester["Sludge Thickener Digester"]
    digester --> dewater["Sludge Dewatering Beds"]
    dewater --> cake["Dried Sludge Cake"]
    clarifier --> eff["Treated Effluent Disinfected"]
    eff --> out["Discharge to Receiving Water"]
    blower["Blowers Air Supply"] --> aeration
    sc["SCADA Control System"] --> blower
    sc --> clarifier
    sc --> digester
```

### 4.2 Microbial Growth Curve and Process Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Phase I Lag Phase"] --> B["Phase II Acceleration"]
    B --> C["Phase III Log Phase Maximum Growth"]
    C --> D["Phase IV Declining Growth"]
    D --> E["Phase V Stationary Phase"]
    E --> F["Phase VI Endogenous Decay"]
    subgraph Process_Applications ["Corresponding Process Applications"]
      C --> P1["High Rate ASP F/M gt 1.0"]
      D --> P2["Conventional ASP F/M 0.2 to 0.5"]
      E --> P3["Extended Aeration F/M lt 0.1"]
      F --> P4["Aerobic Digestion Sludge Age gt 25 d"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Nitrogen Removal Pathway (Nitrification – Denitrification)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NH4["NH4 plus Ammonia"] --> step1["Step 1 Nitritation Aerobic"]
    step1 --> NO2["NO2 minus Nitrite"]
    NO2 --> step2["Step 2 Nitratation Aerobic"]
    step2 --> NO3["NO3 minus Nitrate"]
    NO3 --> step3["Step 3 Denitrification Anoxic"]
    step3 --> N2["N2 Gas Escapes to Atmosphere"]
    subgraph "Microbes Involved"
      step1 --> M1["Nitrosomonas"]
      step2 --> M2["Nitrobacter"]
      step3 --> M3["Pseudomonas Bacillus"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Classification of Biological Unit Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BUP["Biological Unit Processes"] --> SG["Suspended Growth"]
    BUP --> AG["Attached Growth"]
    BUP --> AB["Anaerobic Biofilm plus Suspended"]
    SG --> SG1["Conventional Activated Sludge"]
    SG --> SG2["Extended Aeration Oxidation Ditch"]
    SG --> SG3["Sequencing Batch Reactor SBR"]
    SG --> SG4["Membrane Bioreactor MBR"]
    AG --> AG1["Trickling Filter"]
    AG --> AG2["Rotating Biological Contactor RBC"]
    AG --> AG3["Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor MBBR"]
    AG --> AG4["Biological Aerated Filter BAF"]
    AB --> AB1["Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket UASB"]
    AB --> AB2["Anaerobic Filter"]
    AB --> AB3["Anaerobic Lagoon"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a process unit, the arrows represent the direction of flow of either wastewater, sludge, or information. The diagrams above are *functional architecture flows* — they show the *engineering logic* rather than the physical geometry of the plant.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> *Define the term "Biological Unit Process" used in wastewater treatment. Differentiate between suspended growth and attached growth processes with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Biological Unit Process** is a treatment method in which microorganisms (mainly bacteria) are utilised to decompose and stabilise the organic and inorganic pollutants present in wastewater through controlled biochemical reactions.

**Suspended-Growth Process:** Microorganisms are maintained in suspension by mechanical mixing or aeration. The active biomass remains dispersed in the liquid. *Example:* Conventional Activated Sludge Process.

**Attached-Growth Process:** Microorganisms grow as a slimy layer (biofilm) on the surface of an inert support medium. *Example:* Trickling Filter.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
> *What is meant by F/M ratio in the activated sludge process? State its typical range and significance.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Food-to-Microorganism Ratio (F/M ratio)** is defined as the mass of biodegradable substrate (food) supplied per unit mass of active biomass per unit time.

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} \quad \text{(units: day}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Typical Range:** **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹** for conventional activated sludge, **0.05 – 0.1 day⁻¹** for extended aeration, and **1.0 – 2.0 day⁻¹** for high-rate systems.

**Significance:**
- A **high F/M** indicates abundant food per microbe → dispersed growth → poor settling.
- A **low F/M** indicates limited food → endogenous respiration → better settling, less sludge.
- It directly controls the settleability of sludge and the effluent quality.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (Module 3, Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Main Exam]**
> **(a)** Explain the phases of microbial growth curve with a neat sketch. Discuss the operational significance of each phase in the design of a biological treatment unit. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the Monod equation and explain the significance of $\mu_m$ and $K_s$ in biological wastewater treatment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Microbial Growth Curve:**

The microbial growth curve, when a pure culture is inoculated into a fresh batch of substrate, consists of six distinct phases:

1. **Lag Phase (Phase I):** Cells adjust to the new environment. Enzyme systems are induced. No cell division, but cells increase in size. *Design Implication:* Acclimatisation period; bioaugmentation helps shorten this phase in start-ups.

2. **Acceleration Phase (Phase II):** Growth rate begins to increase rapidly. *Design Implication:* Should be minimised for efficient operation.

3. **Log (Exponential) Phase (Phase III):** Cells divide at a constant maximum rate. Substrate is abundant. *Design Implication:* High F/M regime; applicable in high-rate biological systems, but produces poor-settling sludge.

4. **Declining Growth Phase (Phase IV):** Substrate becomes limiting; growth rate decreases. *Design Implication:* Most conventional activated sludge plants operate here, balancing BOD removal and good settling.
