5. **Stationary Phase (Phase V):** Growth rate equals death rate; net growth is zero. *Design Implication:* Indicates the maximum sustainable population; useful for batch processes like SBR.

6. **Endogenous/Decay Phase (Phase VI):** Cells oxidise their own cellular material for energy. *Design Implication:* Extended aeration systems operate here, producing minimal excess sludge.

The growth curve is essential for selecting the operating point of the aeration tank.

**Part (b) — Monod Equation Derivation:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** for the complete step-by-step derivation.

**Significance of $\mu_m$ (Maximum Specific Growth Rate):**
- It is the upper limit of growth rate when substrate is in excess.
- Determines the **washout SRT**: $\theta_c^{min} = 1/(\mu_m - k_d)$.
- Temperature-sensitive: $\mu_m$ doubles approximately for every 10 °C rise up to an optimum.

**Significance of $K_s$ (Half-Saturation Constant):**
- The substrate concentration at which $\mu = 0.5 \, \mu_m$.
- Indicates the **affinity** of microorganisms for the substrate: low $K_s$ → high affinity → efficient removal at low concentrations.
- Typical values: $K_s = 10 - 60$ mg/L (BOD) for heterotrophic bacteria; $K_s = 0.6 - 3.6$ mg/L (NH₄⁺-N) for *Nitrosomonas*.

> **Question B (Module 3, Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Main Exam]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the various phases of nitrification and denitrification in biological nutrient removal. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A municipal activated sludge plant treats a flow of $20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$ with an influent BOD of $250 \text{ mg/L}$. The MLVSS in the aeration tank is $3000 \text{ mg/L}$ and the aeration tank volume is $4000 \text{ m}^3$. The yield coefficient is $Y = 0.6$ and $k_d = 0.05 \text{ d}^{-1}$. Calculate the F/M ratio, observed yield, and the daily oxygen requirement. Take $a = 0.5$ and $b = 0.1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Nitrification and Denitrification:**

Refer to **Section 4.3 (Mermaid Diagram)** for the flow diagram. The verbal explanation:

**Stage 1 – Nitrification (Aerobic):**
This is a two-step biological oxidation of ammonia to nitrate, carried out by two distinct genera of chemoautotrophic bacteria.
- **Nitritation:** $\text{NH}_4^+ \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^-$ by *Nitrosomonas*.
- **Nitratation:** $\text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^-$ by *Nitrobacter*.
- Requires DO > 2 mg/L, pH 7.5 – 8.5, temperature > 10 °C, SRT > 10 days.
- Consumes alkalinity: $7.14 \text{ mg CaCO}_3$ per mg NH₄⁺-N oxidised.

**Stage 2 – Denitrification (Anoxic):**
- $\text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow$ by *Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus* (facultative heterotrophs).
- Requires absence of free DO but presence of $\text{NO}_3^-$.
- Requires a carbon source (BOD) → recovers $3 - 4$ mg alkalinity per mg $\text{NO}_3^-$-N.
- Recovers $2.86 \text{ mg O}_2$ per mg $\text{NO}_3^-$-N (credit to aeration).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$
- $S_0 = 250 \text{ mg/L}$
- $X = 3000 \text{ mg/L}$ (MLVSS)
- $V = 4000 \text{ m}^3$
- $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.05 \text{ d}^{-1}$
- $a = 0.5$, $b = 0.1$
- Assume effluent BOD $S \approx 20 \text{ mg/L}$ (typical for conventional ASP)

**Step 1: F/M Ratio Calculation**
$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 250}{4000 \times 3000}$$

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{5{,}000{,}000}{12{,}000{,}000} = 0.4167 \text{ d}^{-1} \approx 0.42 \text{ d}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: SRT (θc) Calculation**
$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{4000}{20{,}000} = 0.2 \text{ d} = 4.8 \text{ h}$$

Using $\mu_{obs} = Y \cdot U - k_d$ and $U = (S_0 - S)/(X \cdot \theta)$:
$$U = \dfrac{(250 - 20)}{3000 \times 0.2} = \dfrac{230}{600} = 0.3833 \text{ d}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = 0.6 \times 0.3833 - 0.05 = 0.23 - 0.05 = 0.18 \text{ d}^{-1}$$
$$\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{0.18} = 5.56 \text{ d}$$

**Step 3: Observed Yield**
$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.6}{1 + 0.05 \times 5.56} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.278} = 0.4695$$

**Step 4: Daily Oxygen Requirement**
$$\text{O}_2 = a \cdot (S_0 - S) + b \cdot X \cdot \theta_c$$
$$\text{O}_2 = 0.5 \times (250 - 20) + 0.1 \times 3000 \times 5.56$$
$$\text{O}_2 = 0.5 \times 230 + 0.1 \times 16{,}680$$
$$\text{O}_2 = 115 + 1668 = 1783 \text{ mg/L of mixed liquor}$$

Converting to kg/day:
$$\text{O}_2 \text{ (kg/d)} = 1783 \times 20{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 35{,}660 \text{ kg/d}$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **F/M Ratio** | **0.42 d⁻¹** |
| **Observed Yield (Y_obs)** | **0.47 mg/mg** |
| **Daily O₂ Requirement** | **35,660 kg/d** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Consistency is Non-Negotiable:** Mixing mg/L and kg/m³ (which are numerically equal) and g/m³ (which is 1000× different) is the single most common cause of mark deduction. Always write units at every step.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** "Removal" must always be reported as positive in the mass balance. The negative sign in $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\mu \cdot X}{Y}$ is *thermodynamic* and indicates the direction; the *magnitude* of removal is used in design.
> 3. **F/M Units:** KTU expects **day⁻¹** in the answer, NOT dimensionless. Students often write "0.42" without units and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **SRT vs HRT:** These are NOT the same. HRT is hydraulic; SRT is biological. SRT > HRT always (typically 5–20× larger). Conflating them is a fatal error.
> 5. **Oxygen Equation Form:** Use the *Metcalf & Eddy* form with $a$ and $b$ constants as given in the problem statement. Do NOT use a different empirical formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Basic Concepts of Biological Unit Processes:**

- [x] **Biological Unit Process** = engineered use of microbes to treat wastewater.
- [x] Two broad classes: **Suspended Growth (ASP)** and **Attached Growth (TF, RBC, MBBR)**.
- [x] Bacteria are the **primary workhorses**; protozoa and rotifers are **bioindicators** of good effluent.
- [x] **Six phases of growth curve:** Lag → Acceleration → Log → Declining → Stationary → Endogenous. Conventional ASP operates in Phase IV.
- [x] **Monod Kinetics:** $\mu = \mu_m \cdot S/(K_s + S)$ — substrate-limited growth, analogous to Michaelis–Menten.
- [x] **$K_s$** = half-saturation constant (low $K_s$ → high microbial affinity).
- [x] **$\mu_m$** = maximum specific growth rate; temperature dependent.
- [x] **F/M Ratio** = $Q S_0 / (V X)$; range **0.2 – 0.5 d⁻¹** for conventional ASP.
- [x] **SRT ($\theta_c$)** = mean cell residence time; controls effluent quality and sludge production.
- [x] **Observed Yield** = $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d \theta_c)$; long SRT → low $Y_{obs}$ → less sludge.
- [x] **Washout condition:** $\theta_c < 1/(\mu_m - k_d)$ → complete process failure.
- [x] **Nitrification** requires DO > 2 mg/L, pH 7.5 – 8.5, SRT > 10 d; consumes 7.14 mg CaCO₃/mg N.
- [x] **Denitrification** requires anoxic conditions (no free DO), COD as electron donor, and recovers 2.86 mg O₂/mg NO₃⁻-N.
- [x] **Anaerobic digestion** produces CH₄ + CO₂; suitable for high-strength wastes; low cell yield.
- [x] **Oxygen Requirement** = $a(S_0 - S) + b X \theta_c$ — must size blowers accordingly.
- [x] **Effluent BOD prediction:** $S = K_s (1 + k_d \theta_c) / [\theta_c Y \mu_m - (1 + k_d \theta_c)]$.
- [x] **Bacteria in attached growth** form **biofilms** — protect microbes from toxic shocks.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Basic Concepts of Biological Unit Processes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerobic and Anaerobic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aerobic and Anaerobic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aerobic Process — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerobic Biological Treatment (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A biological wastewater treatment process in which microorganisms (predominantly bacteria) metabolize dissolved and suspended organic matter in the **presence of free molecular oxygen (O₂)** as the terminal electron acceptor, oxidizing the substrate to stable end products — carbon dioxide, water, and excess biological sludge (biomass).

- **Standard DO range maintained:** $\text{DO} \geq 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \; \text{mg/L}$
- **Energy yield (per mole of glucose):** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -2840 \; \text{kJ/mol}$ (highly exergonic)
- **Examples:** Activated Sludge Process (ASP), Trickling Filters (TF), Rotating Biological Contactors (RBC), Aerated Lagoons, Oxidation Ditches.

## 1.2 Anaerobic Process — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anaerobic Biological Treatment (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A biological treatment process carried out in the **absence of free oxygen**, in which facultative and obligate anaerobic microorganisms break down organic matter through a four-stage sequential process (Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis), yielding biogas (≈ 60–70% CH₄ + 30–40% CO₂) and a small quantity of biological sludge.

- **Operating redox potential:** $E_h < -200 \; \text{mV}$
- **Energy yield (per mole of glucose):** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -390 \; \text{kJ/mol}$ (modest)
- **Examples:** Septic tanks, Imhoff tanks, Anaerobic Lagoons, Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB), Anaerobic Filters, Anaerobic Digesters.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen vs. Compost Pit" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)**
> Imagine a busy **kitchen restaurant**:
> - **Aerobic = Open-flame gas stove.** The cook (bacteria) has unlimited oxygen blowing across the pan. Food (organic waste) burns hot, fast, and completely — leaving behind only CO₂ (smoke), water vapor (steam), and a little char (sludge). This is **fast, efficient, but energy-hungry** because the chef keeps needing a fresh air supply (aerators/blowers).
> - **Anaerobic = Sealed compost pit in your backyard.** No air enters. Microbes slowly ferment kitchen scraps. After weeks, you get biogas (methane + CO₂ — useful fuel!) and rich compost (sludge). It is **slow, but produces a useful energy byproduct and very little excess sludge**.

This analogy is exactly how KTU examiners frame "Compare and contrast aerobic vs anaerobic" questions.

## 1.4 The Microbial Players

| Feature | Aerobic Microbes | Anaerobic Microbes |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen requirement | **Obligate aerobes** (need O₂) | **Obligate anaerobes** (killed by O₂) |
| Examples | *Pseudomonas*, *Zoogloea*, *Nitrosomonas* | *Methanococcus*, *Methanobacterium*, *Methanosarcina* |
| Growth rate | **Fast** (μ = 0.3–0.5 h⁻¹) | **Very slow** (μ = 0.01–0.05 h⁻¹) |
| Energy per kg COD | ~14 MJ | ~3.5 MJ (but recoverable as CH₄) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aerobic Process — Biochemical Mechanism

The overall aerobic oxidation of a generic organic substrate is given by:

$$\text{Organic matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Aerobes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New cells (sludge)} + \text{Energy}$$

Step-by-step operational logic:

1. **Substrate Diffusion:** Dissolved organics diffuse across the cell membrane of the floc-forming bacteria.
2. **Adsorption onto Floc:** Colloidal and suspended particles are adsorbed onto the biofloc surface (biological flocculation).
3. **Enzymatic Oxidation:** Bacteria secrete extracellular enzymes (hydrolases, oxidases) that cleave complex molecules and oxidize them using dissolved O₂.
4. **Energy Capture:** ATP is generated via oxidative phosphorylation, fueling cell synthesis.
5. **Endogenous Decay:** Once substrate is exhausted, microbes consume their own cellular mass (cell lysis), reducing net sludge yield.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The endogenous decay phase is what makes the activated sludge process "self-regulating." It is the reason MLVSS reaches a steady-state in continuous-flow systems.

## 2.2 Anaerobic Process — The Four Sequential Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** Anaerobic digestion is **not** a single reaction. It is a tightly coupled **four-stage microbial consortium process**. If any stage fails, the system "sours" (VFA accumulation, pH crash).

| Stage | Microbe Group | Key Reaction | End Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Hydrolysis | Fermentative bacteria | Proteins, fats, carbs → monomers | Amino acids, sugars, fatty acids |
| 2. Acidogenesis | Acidogenic bacteria | Monomers → volatile fatty acids (VFA) | Acetic, propionic, butyric acids |
| 3. Acetogenesis | Acetogenic bacteria | VFA + H₂ → acetate | Acetic acid, H₂, CO₂ |
| 4. Methanogenesis | Methanogenic archaea | Acetate → CH₄; H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ | **Methane (CH₄)** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important revision card** for Module 3 numericals.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \frac{(S_0 - S) \cdot Q}{V \cdot X}$ | Substrate utilization rate | $\text{g/(L·d)}$ |
| 2 | $Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | Observed yield coefficient (aerobic) | $\text{kg VSS/kg BOD}$ |
| 3 | $\theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{\mu_m - k_d}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT) | days |
| 4 | $G = 0.35 \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q$ | Methane gas yield (aerobic = 0) | $\text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{d}$ at STP |
| 5 | $\text{BOD}_{rem} = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100$ | BOD removal efficiency | % |
| 6 | $\text{O}_2 \text{ demand} = a \cdot S_r + b \cdot X_v$ | Oxygen requirement equation | $\text{kg O}_2/\text{d}$ |
| 7 | $V = \frac{P \cdot \theta}{\text{HRT}}$ | Anaerobic digester volume | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 8 | $\text{Alkalinity required} = 0.5 \cdot \text{VFA}$ (as CaCO₃) | Stability criterion for digesters | $\text{mg/L}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants used in KTU 2024 problems:**
> - $Y$ (aerobic heterotrophs) = $\mathbf{0.4}$ to $\mathbf{0.6}$ kg VSS/kg BOD
> - $k_d$ (endogenous decay) = $\mathbf{0.04}$ to $\mathbf{0.08}$ /d
> - Methane energy = $\mathbf{0.35 \; m^3 \; CH_4}$ per kg COD destroyed at STP
> - Methane energy content = $\mathbf{35{,}800 \; kJ/m^3}$

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Aerobic systems** are used in **municipal sewage treatment plants (STPs)** of Kerala (e.g., Cochin, Thiruvananthapuram STPs) where land is available and stringent BOD/effluent standards must be met (BOD < 30 mg/L for discharge to inland waters).
- **Anaerobic systems** are used in **high-strength industrial wastewater** (distillery spent wash, dairy, slaughterhouse) and **sludge digestion** for biogas recovery — making them **net energy positive**.
- **Hybrid systems** (e.g., Anaerobic + Aerobic) are now the industry standard for sustainability and carbon-neutrality in line with **India's National Mission on Clean Ganga (NMCG)** guidelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Aerobic Oxygen Demand Equation

The oxygen requirement in an activated sludge system comes from two simultaneous demands: (a) oxidation of substrate for energy and synthesis, and (b) endogenous respiration of the biomass.

**Starting point — Mass balance on oxygen:**

$$O_2 = (\text{O}_2 \text{ for substrate oxidation}) + (\text{O}_2 \text{ for endogenous decay})$$

**Step 1:** The fraction of substrate used for energy (and not converted to biomass) is $(1 - Y)$. So oxygen for synthesis:

$$O_{2,\text{syn}} = a \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q$$

where $a$ is the **oxygen demand per unit substrate removed** (typically $a \approx 0.5$).

**Step 2:** Endogenous decay releases CO₂ and consumes O₂. The biomass that decays per day is $k_d \cdot X_v \cdot V$:

$$O_{2,\text{endo}} = b \cdot X_v \cdot V$$

**Step 3:** Combining both into the master KTU equation:

$$\boxed{\;O_2 = a \cdot S_r \cdot Q + b \cdot X_v \cdot V\;}$$

where $S_r = (S_0 - S)$ is the BOD removed per unit time, $Q$ is flow rate, $X_v$ is MLVSS, and $V$ is aeration tank volume.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Observed Sludge Yield

The true yield $Y$ overestimates actual biomass production because of endogenous decay. The **observed yield** is derived by equating biomass growth with biomass loss over one MCRT cycle:

**Step 1:** Net biomass production rate = Growth − Decay:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = Y \cdot \left(\frac{dS}{dt}\right) - k_d \cdot X$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, integrating over the MCRT ($\theta_c$):

$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot \frac{S_r}{X \cdot V} - k_d$$

**Step 3:** Let observed yield be defined as $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{\text{sludge produced per day}}{\text{substrate removed per day}}$:

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{X \cdot V}{\theta_c \cdot S_r \cdot Q}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and simplifying:

$$\boxed{\;Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** As $\theta_c \to \infty$ (extended aeration), $Y_{obs} \to 0$, which is the **design basis of the Extended Aeration / Oxidation Ditch system** (e.g., Krishipat, Muvattupuzha STP).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum MCRT for Anaerobic Digester

For an anaerobic system to retain methanogens (which have a generation time of 2–6 days), the hydraulic/solids retention time must exceed a critical minimum.

**Step 1:** Substrate utilization is given by Monod kinetics:

$$U = \frac{k \cdot S}{K_s + S}$$

**Step 2:** At washout (the limiting condition where microbial growth = washout rate):

$$\mu_m \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} = \frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d$$

**Step 3:** For methanogens, $S$ is very low (efficient conversion), so $S \ll K_s$, leading to $\dfrac{S}{K_s + S} \to 0$ in washout analysis. Setting $S \to 0$:

$$\boxed{\;\theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{\mu_m - k_d}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU Value:** For mesophilic digesters (35°C), $\theta_c^{min}$ is typically **10–15 days**, which is why **anaerobic digesters are large-volume** compared to aerobic tanks of equivalent capacity.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the table that earns 14-mark answers in KTU ESE.** Memorize it.

| Parameter | Aerobic Treatment | Anaerobic Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| Electron acceptor | O₂ (molecular oxygen) | CO₂, sulfate, nitrate, organic intermediates |
| Energy generation | High (-2840 kJ/mol) | Low (-390 kJ/mol) |
| Sludge yield | High (0.3–0.6 kg/kg COD) | Very low (0.05–0.15 kg/kg COD) |
| Biogas production | **None** | **Yes** (0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD) |
| Effluent quality | Excellent (BOD < 30 mg/L) | Moderate (needs post-treatment) |
| Energy balance | **Net consumer** | **Net producer** |
| Footprint / HRT | Short HRT (4–8 h) | Long HRT (15–30 days) |
| Temperature sensitivity | Moderate | Highly sensitive (mesophilic 35°C, thermophilic 55°C) |
| Start-up time | Days to weeks | Weeks to months |
| Capital cost | High (blowers, diffusers) | Low (no aeration) |
| O&M cost | High | Low |
| KTU typical application | Municipal sewage | High-strength industrial WW, sludge digestion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Aerobic Process Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Wastewater] --> B[Screening and Grit Removal]
    B --> C[Primary Clarifier]
    C --> D[Aeration Tank with Diffused Air System]
    D --> E[Secondary Clarifier]
    E --> F[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    E --> G[Return Activated Sludge RAS]
    G --> D
    E --> H[Waste Activated Sludge WAS]
    H --> I[Sludge Thickener]
    I --> J[Sludge Digester]
    J --> K[Sludge Drying Beds]

    L[Blower Air Supply] --> D
    M[Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids MLSS] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The RAS loop is the heart of activated sludge — it concentrates biomass in the aeration tank, ensuring a high MLVSS (2000–4000 mg/L) for rapid BOD removal.

## 4.2 Anaerobic Process Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sludge or High Strength Wastewater] --> B[Sludge Thickener]
    B --> C[Anaerobic Digester Mesophilic 35C]
    C --> D[Sludge Dewatering]
    D --> E[Sludge Cake to Landfill or Use as Manure]

    C --> F[Biogas Collection Dome]
    F --> G[Gas Holder]
    G --> H[Boiler or CHP Engine]
    H --> I[Electricity and Hot Water for Plant]
    G --> J[Flare Stack for Excess Gas]

    C --> K[Supernatant Recycle]
    K --> L[Head of Treatment Plant]
```

## 4.3 Four-Stage Microbial Pathway of Anaerobic Digestion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Complex Organics Proteins Fats Carbohydrates] --> S1[Stage 1 Hydrolysis]
    S1 --> Q1[Monomers Amino Acids Sugars Fatty Acids]
    Q1 --> S2[Stage 2 Acidogenesis]
    S2 --> Q2[Volatile Fatty Acids VFAs H2 CO2]
    Q2 --> S3[Stage 3 Acetogenesis]
    S3 --> Q3[Acetic Acid CH3COOH H2 CO2]
    Q3 --> S4[Stage 4 Methanogenesis]
    S4 --> R1[Methane CH4 60 to 70 percent]
    S4 --> R2[Carbon Dioxide CO2 30 to 40 percent]
```

## 4.4 Aerobic vs Anaerobic — Comparative System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AERO[Aerobic System]
        A1[Wastewater In] --> A2[Aeration Tank O2 supplied by blower]
        A2 --> A3[Sludge Rich in Biomass]
        A3 --> A4[Disposal Cost High]
    end

    subgraph ANAERO[Anaerobic System]
        B1[Wastewater In] --> B2[Sealed Digester No Oxygen]
        B2 --> B3[Biogas CH4 plus CO2]
        B2 --> B4[Low Sludge Yield]
        B3 --> B5[Energy Recovery Revenue]
    end

    style AERO fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1
    style ANAERO fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between aerobic and anaerobic treatment processes with respect to electron acceptor, end products, and energy yield.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Electron Acceptor:** Aerobic processes use free molecular oxygen (O₂) as the terminal electron acceptor, whereas anaerobic processes operate in the complete absence of O₂ and use CO₂, sulfate, or organic intermediates as the electron acceptor.
>
> **End Products:** Aerobic processes yield CO₂, H₂O, and excess biological sludge. Anaerobic processes yield biogas (≈ 60–70% CH₄ and 30–40% CO₂) and a much smaller quantity of digested sludge.
>
> **Energy Yield:** Aerobic oxidation of glucose releases ~2840 kJ/mol of energy, while anaerobic fermentation releases only ~390 kJ/mol. However, the anaerobic energy is recovered as methane, making it net-energy-positive.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the four stages of anaerobic digestion and write the key reaction of each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Hydrolysis:** Complex biopolymers (proteins, fats, carbohydrates) are hydrolyzed by extracellular enzymes into soluble monomers (amino acids, sugars, fatty acids).
> 2. **Acidogenesis:** Monomers are fermented by acidogenic bacteria into volatile fatty acids (acetic, propionic, butyric), H₂, and CO₂.
> 3. **Acetogenesis:** Higher VFA and H₂ are converted into acetic acid (CH₃COOH), H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria.
> 4. **Methanogenesis:** Methanogenic archaea convert acetate and H₂/CO₂ into methane (CH₄). Example reactions:
> $$CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$$
> $$CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**With a neat sketch, describe the activated sludge process. Explain the role of return activated sludge (RAS) and the significance of MLVSS.**

**Model Solution:**

The activated sludge process (ASP) is a **suspended-growth aerobic biological treatment** system in which a mixed microbial culture (activated sludge) is kept in suspension in the aeration tank with continuous oxygen supply.

**Process Flow:**
1. **Primary clarified sewage** enters the **aeration tank**, where it is mixed with **return activated sludge (RAS)** recycled from the secondary clarifier.
2. **Diffused aeration** (or mechanical aerators) supplies oxygen and keeps the mixed liquor suspended.
3. The **mixed liquor** flows to the **secondary clarifier**, where biomass settles.
4. The **clarified effluent** is discharged; a portion of the **settled sludge** is returned as **RAS**; excess sludge (**WAS**) is wasted.

**Role of RAS:**
- Maintains a high concentration of **MLVSS (Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids)** in the aeration tank (typically 2000–4000 mg/L).
- Decouples HRT from SRT (Solids Retention Time), allowing long SRT with short HRT.
- Ensures continuous inoculation of incoming substrate with acclimatized biomass.

**Significance of MLVSS:**
- MLVSS represents the **active microbial mass** responsible for BOD removal.
- The **F/M ratio (Food to Microorganism ratio)** = $F/M = \dfrac{S_0 \cdot Q}{X_v \cdot V}$ governs treatment efficiency.
- Standard F/M ratio for conventional ASP = **0.2–0.5 kg BOD/kg MLVSS·d**.

**Diagram:** (Already shown in SECTION 4.1)

---

#### **Q3(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**A wastewater treatment plant treats 10,000 m³/d of sewage with influent BOD of 250 mg/L. The aeration tank volume is 2000 m³ and MLVSS is 3000 mg/L. Calculate (i) F/M ratio, (ii) BOD loading rate, and (iii) hydraulic retention time.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 10{,}000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}$
- $S_0 = 250 \; \text{mg/L} = 250 \; \text{g/m}^3$
- $V = 2000 \; \text{m}^3$
- $X_v = 3000 \; \text{mg/L} = 3 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

**(i) F/M Ratio:**

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{S_0 \cdot Q}{X_v \cdot V}$$

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{250 \times 10{,}000}{3 \times 2000} = \frac{2{,}500{,}000}{6000}$$

$$\frac{F}{M} = 416.67 \; \text{g BOD/d per kg MLVSS}$$

Converting to standard units:

$$\frac{F}{M} = 0.417 \; \text{kg BOD/kg MLVSS·d}$$

> This is within the **conventional ASP range (0.2–0.5)** ✓

**(ii) BOD Loading Rate (Volumetric):**

$$\text{BLR} = \frac{S_0 \cdot Q}{V} = \frac{250 \times 10{,}000}{2000} = 1250 \; \text{g/m}^3\cdot\text{d}$$

$$\text{BLR} = 1.25 \; \text{kg/m}^3\cdot\text{d}$$

**(iii) Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):**

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{2000}{10{,}000} = 0.2 \; \text{d} = 4.8 \; \text{hours}$$

> **Verdict:** HRT of 4.8 h is typical for **conventional activated sludge** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q4(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Explain the mechanism of anaerobic digestion with a flow diagram. Why is the process sensitive to temperature and pH? How is alkalinity maintained in the digester?**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism (Four Stages):** Already detailed in SECTION 2.2.

**Temperature Sensitivity:**
- Methanogens are **strict mesophiles** (optimum 35°C) or **thermophiles** (optimum 55°C).
- A drop of even 2–3°C below 35°C can reduce methanogenic activity by **>50%**, causing VFA accumulation.
- Temperature fluctuations disrupt the **syntrophic relationship** between acetogens and methanogens.
- Methanogens have a **low growth rate** ($\mu_m \approx 0.01-0.05 \; \text{h}^{-1}$), so they cannot recover quickly from thermal shocks.

**pH Sensitivity:**
- Methanogens operate best at **pH 6.8–7.4**.
- If pH drops below 6.5, methanogens are inhibited, VFA accumulates, and the system "sours" — a runaway acidification.
- The **carbonic acid / bicarbonate buffer system** ($CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HCO_3^- + H^+$) naturally resists pH drops.

**Alkalinity Maintenance:**
- Maintain **VFA : Alkalinity ratio** ≤ 0.5 (both as CaCO₃).
- If VFA exceeds 2000 mg/L, dosing of **lime (Ca(OH)₂)** or **sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃)** is used.
- **Co-digestion** with high-alkalinity waste (e.g., cattle dung) also buffers pH.

---

#### **Q4(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**An anaerobic digester receives 50 m³/d of sludge with 4% total solids (TS) and 70% volatile solids (VS). If 50% of VS is destroyed and methane yield is 0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed, calculate (i) VS destroyed per day, (ii) methane produced per day, and (iii) total biogas volume (assuming CH₄ = 65% of biogas).**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 50 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}$
- $TS = 4\% = 40{,}000 \; \text{mg/L} = 40 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $VS = 70\% \; \text{of TS} = 0.70 \times 40 = 28 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- VS destruction efficiency = **50%**
- Methane yield = $0.35 \; \text{m}^3 \; CH_4/\text{kg VS destroyed}$

**(i) VS Destroyed per Day:**

$$VS_{in} = VS \times Q = 28 \times 50 = 1400 \; \text{kg/d}$$

$$VS_{destroyed} = 0.50 \times 1400 = \boxed{700 \; \text{kg/d}}$$

**(ii) Methane Produced per Day:**

$$G_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times VS_{destroyed} = 0.35 \times 700 = \boxed{245 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}}$$

**(iii) Total Biogas Volume:**

Since CH₄ constitutes 65% of total biogas:

$$G_{biogas} = \frac{G_{CH_4}}{0.65} = \frac{245}{0.65} = \boxed{376.92 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}}$$

> **Bonus Insight:** Energy recovered = $245 \times 35{,}800 = 8.77 \times 10^6 \; \text{kJ/d} = 8.77 \; \text{GJ/d}$, equivalent to burning ~250 kg of LPG per day!

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (most common):** Mixing mg/L with kg/m³. Remember: $1 \; \text{mg/L} = 1 \; \text{g/m}^3$.
> 2. **Confusing F/M Ratio and Volumetric Loading:** The F/M ratio is **per kg MLVSS**, not per m³.
> 3. **Forgetting the difference between TS and VS:** Anaerobic destruction applies to **VS only** (the biodegradable fraction), not TS.
> 4. **Methane yield constant:** Always use $0.35 \; \text{m}^3 \; CH_4$ per kg of **VS destroyed** (some textbooks say COD — KTU uses VS unless otherwise specified).
> 5. **VFA:Alkalinity ratio:** In digester design questions, students forget to convert both parameters to the same basis (CaCO₃).
> 6. **Anaerobic ≠ "no treatment":** A common mistake is to say anaerobic effluent is "clean." It actually requires **post-treatment (aerobic polishing)** for nutrient and residual COD removal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Aerobic vs Anaerobic**

- ✅ Aerobic = **O₂ present**, high energy yield, high sludge, **net energy consumer**.
- ✅ Anaerobic = **O₂ absent**, low energy yield, low sludge, **net energy producer (biogas)**.
- ✅ Aerobic electron acceptor: **O₂**. Anaerobic electron acceptor: **CO₂ / sulfate / organic intermediates**.
- ✅ Four stages of AD: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**.
- ✅ Methanogens are the **rate-limiting step** in AD — they are slow, pH-sensitive, and temperature-sensitive.
- ✅ Optimum digester conditions: **pH 6.8–7.4, 35°C (mesophilic), 15–20 days HRT/SRT**.
- ✅ Methane yield constant: $0.35 \; \text{m}^3 \; CH_4$ per kg VS destroyed at STP.
- ✅ Observed yield formula: $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$.
- ✅ Oxygen demand equation: $O_2 = a \cdot S_r \cdot Q + b \cdot X_v \cdot V$.
- ✅ F/M ratio for conventional ASP: **0.2–0.5 kg BOD/kg MLVSS·d**.
- ✅ MLVSS for conventional ASP: **2000–4000 mg/L**.
- ✅ Aerobic effluent quality: **excellent** (BOD < 30 mg/L).
- ✅ Anaerobic effluent: **needs post-treatment** for residual COD and nutrients.
- ✅ KTU frequently tests: **comparison tables, F/M ratio, methane yield, four stages of AD, digester design**.
- ✅ **Hybrid (Anoxic-Aerobic-Anaerobic) systems** are the modern industry standard for **nutrient (N, P) removal**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attached and Suspended Growth Processes (Concepts Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Attached and Suspended Growth Processes (Concepts Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Biological Wastewater Treatment — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Biological wastewater treatment* is an engineered process that exploits the **metabolic activity of microorganisms** (bacteria, protozoa, fungi) to **stabilise dissolved and suspended organic matter** in sewage, converting it into **harmless end products** (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄) and a small mass of **biological excess sludge** that can be separated by sedimentation.

The two principal families of secondary biological treatment are classified based on **how the microbial biomass physically resides** within the reactor:

| Family | Biomass State | Reactor Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Suspended Growth Processes** | Microbes float freely in mixed liquor | Activated Sludge, SBR, MBR, Oxidation Ditch |
| **Attached Growth Processes** | Microbes stick to a solid media as **biofilm** | Trickling Filter, RBC, Bio-tower |

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Soup vs. Garden"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
>
> - **Suspended Growth** is like a **bowl of fish soup** — the microorganisms (fish) are *swimming freely* inside the wastewater (broth). Everything is well-mixed, the biomass is everywhere, and you must keep the soup aerated and well-stirred, otherwise the fish suffocate and settle.
>
> - **Attached Growth** is like a **vertical garden / moss wall** — the microorganisms form a *thin sticky green film* (biofilm) on the surface of stones, plastic media, or rotating discs. The wastewater is *sprinkled or flowed* over this garden, the microbes eat the food in the water, and the cleaned water drips out the bottom.

---

### 1.3 The Microbial Engine — A Common Foundation

Despite the physical difference, both process families rely on the same **biochemical engine**:

$$\text{Organic Matter (BOD)} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Microbes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New Cells (Sludge)} + \text{Energy}$$

The **oxygen source** differs:
- **Suspended Growth:** Oxygen is bubbled (diffusers) or mechanically beaten into the water.
- **Attached Growth:** Oxygen is naturally drawn into the biofilm from air (trickling filters) or by rotating the media through air (RBC).

---

### 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters for Kerala Engineers

> [!TIP]
> Kerala's terrain (undulating, high water table, space-constrained municipalities like Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut) makes the **choice between suspended and attached growth** a *board-exam favourite* and a *real design decision*. Attached growth (e.g., **DEWATS**, bio-towers) suits decentralized rural panchayats; suspended growth (e.g., **SBRs at Cochin smart-city STP**) suits high-density urban catchments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SUSPENDED GROWTH PROCESSES — The Activated Sludge Family

#### 2.1.1 The Canonical Activated Sludge Process (ASP)

The **Activated Sludge Process** is the *flagship* of suspended growth systems. It was developed in **1914 by Ardern & Lockett** in Manchester, England.

**Operational Philosophy:**
> Microorganisms are kept in a state of **continuous suspension** in the wastewater by mechanical aerators or diffused air. The mixed liquor (wastewater + biomass) flows to a clarifier, where biomass is settled and **returned** (RAS — Return Activated Sludge) to the aeration tank to maintain a high population of active microbes.

**Process Flow (Conceptual):**
Influent → Primary Clarifier → **Aeration Tank** → Secondary Clarifier → Disinfection → Effluent
(With RAS loop and WAS — Waste Activated Sludge — bleeding off the system.)

#### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Process

1. **Step 1 — Aeration Tank Mixing:** Wastewater enters an aerated tank where air is supplied. The oxygen keeps the microbes alive and the turbulence keeps them suspended.
2. **Step 2 — Substrate Contact:** The dissolved organic matter (BOD) contacts the microbial flocs. Adsorption + oxidation begins.
3. **Step 3 — Endogenous Respiration:** After food is consumed, microbes oxidise their own cell mass (maintenance energy), reducing sludge yield.
4. **Step 4 — Settling:** Mixed liquor passes to a clarifier. Biomass flocs settle under gravity as the **sludge blanket**.
5. **Step 5 — Recycling:** A portion of settled sludge (RAS) is pumped back to maintain a constant **MLSS** (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids) concentration.
6. **Step 6 — Wasting:** Excess sludge (WAS) is bled off to keep the system at **steady-state biomass inventory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why recycle sludge?**
> The HRT (hydraulic retention time) of wastewater in the tank is only **4–8 hours**, but microbes need **days to weeks** to grow and acclimatise. Recycling decouples **hydraulic retention time (HRT)** from **solids retention time (SRT)** — the secret to high efficiency.

#### 2.1.3 Variants of Activated Sludge (Board-Favourite Table)

| Variant | Key Feature | F/M Ratio | SRT (days) | HRT (hr) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional** | Plug flow, mid-range loading | 0.2 – 0.4 | 5 – 15 | 4 – 8 | Standard municipal sewage |
| **Complete Mix** | Uniform F/M across tank | 0.2 – 0.5 | 5 – 15 | 4 – 8 | Shock load handling |
| **Step Aeration** | Influent distributed along tank | 0.2 – 0.4 | 5 – 15 | 4 – 8 | High flow / high load |
| **Extended Aeration** | Low load, no primary clarifier | 0.05 – 0.15 | 20 – 40 | 18 – 36 | Small communities, package plants |
| **Oxidation Ditch** | Loop channel, rotor aerators | 0.05 – 0.15 | 20 – 40 | 8 – 36 | Kochi/Kerala municipal STPs |
| **Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)** | Time-based batch (Fill-React-Settle-Idle) | 0.05 – 0.5 | 10 – 30 | 12 – 24 | Variable flow, low footprint |
| **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** | UF membranes replace clarifier | 0.05 – 0.4 | 10 – 30 | 4 – 8 | Reuse-grade effluent |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic — SBR:** Sequencing Batch Reactor is essentially an activated sludge system that performs **all five operations (fill, react, settle, decant, idle) in a single tank in TIME rather than SPACE.** It is a favourite in the Kerala municipal sector (e.g., Smart City STPs at Kochi).

---

### 2.2 ATTACHED GROWTH PROCESSES — The Biofilm Family

#### 2.2.1 The Biofilm Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *biofilm* is a **structured, slimy, layered consortium of microorganisms** embedded in a self-produced Extracellular Polymeric Substance (EPS) matrix, adhered to a **solid support medium**. The biofilm is **concentration-stratified** — aerobes on the outer face, facultative/anaerobes in the deeper anoxic zones.

**Why does biofilm self-form?**
- Microbes secrete a **sticky EPS** (polysaccharides + proteins) within minutes of contact with a surface.
- They multiply, forming **microcolonies** that thicken into a visible film.
- Substrate diffuses **from the bulk water → into the biofilm**, where it is metabolised.

#### 2.2.2 Trickling Filter (TF) — The Classic Attached Growth Unit

Invented in **England in 1890s**, the trickling filter is a **packed bed of rock/plastic media** over which settled wastewater is **trickled** (sprayed) via a rotating distributor arm.

**Operational Logic:**
1. **Distributor arm** rotates slowly (1 revolution per few minutes), spraying wastewater uniformly over the media.
2. Wastewater **coats the biofilm** as it trickles down; air is drawn naturally **upward (counter-current)** through the voids by the temperature differential (warm wastewater → cold air chimney effect).
3. Microbes in the biofilm **adsorb and oxidise** dissolved BOD.
4. **Sloughing:** Excess biomass detaches (sloughs) and is collected in the **secondary clarifier** underneath.
5. **Recirculation:** A fraction of the clarified effluent is **recycled** to dilute the incoming BOD and maintain hydraulic distribution.

**Trickling Filter Classification (KTU 2024):**

| Class | Hydraulic Loading (m³/m²·d) | Organic Loading (kg BOD/m³·d) | BOD Removal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-rate (Standard)** | 1 – 4 | 0.08 – 0.16 | 80 – 90% |
| **High-rate** | 10 – 30 | 0.30 – 0.60 | 60 – 85% |
| **Roughing (Super-high)** | 40 – 200 | 1.0 – 4.0 | 50 – 70% |
| **Two-stage** | Combined series | Combined | 85 – 95% |

#### 2.2.3 Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) — Modern Attached Growth

The RBC is a series of **closely spaced circular plastic discs** mounted on a horizontal shaft, partially submerged (≈ 40%) in a tank of wastewater.

**Operational Logic:**
- Discs rotate slowly (1 – 2 rpm) using an electric motor.
- Biofilm grows on the **submerged portion** (substrate contact) and **emerged portion** (oxygen absorption).
- Rotation is the **aeration mechanism** — no external air blower required.
- **Sloughed biomass** falls into the trough and is removed by a downstream clarifier.

**KTU 2024 Key RBC Data:**
- Standard density: 30 – 50 m² disc area per m³ tank volume.
- Soluble BOD removal: 85 – 95% in 3 – 4 stages.
- Shaft loadings: 5 – 20 g BOD/m²·d.

#### 2.2.4 Hybrid Systems — The Best of Both Worlds

> [!NOTE]
> **MBBR (Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor):** Plastic carrier elements (~ 500 m²/m³ specific surface) are *freely suspended* in an aeration tank. The biomass grows **on the carriers**, but the carriers are kept in **suspension by aeration**. This blends *attached growth biology* with *suspended growth hydraulics* — an emerging favourite in Kerala resort and hospital STPs.

---

### 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CONCEPT-LEVEL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These relationships are essential for KTU 2024 numerical and conceptual questions. Note that the topic is "Concepts Only" — therefore we treat these as **interpretive relations**, not design-grade derivations.

| Parameter | Formula | Typical Range (Municipal) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F/M Ratio** (Food to Microorganism) | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | 0.05 – 0.5 day⁻¹ | Substrate (food) load per unit biomass |
| **Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT / θc)** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | 5 – 40 days | Average time a microbe spends in system |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT / θ)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | 4 – 36 hours | Average time wastewater spends in reactor |
| **MLSS** (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids) | $MLSS = \dfrac{\text{mass of biomass in tank}}{\text{volume of tank}}$ | 1500 – 4000 mg/L | Biomass concentration in aeration tank |
| **MLVSS / MLSS Ratio** | $MLVSS/MLSS \approx 0.7 - 0.8$ | 0.7 – 0.8 | Volatile (active) fraction of biomass |
| **SVI** (Sludge Volume Index) | $SVI = \dfrac{\text{30-min settled volume (mL/L)}}{MLSS \text{ (g/L)}}$ | 50 – 150 mL/g | Settleability indicator (low = good) |
| **Observed Yield (Y_obs)** | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | 0.2 – 0.4 kg/kg | Net sludge produced per kg BOD removed |
| **Oxygen Required** | $O_2 = a \cdot S_r - b \cdot X \cdot \theta_c$ | 1.0 – 1.5 kg O₂/kg BOD | Stoichiometric + endogenous demand |
| **Recirculation Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{Q_{RAS}}{Q_{influent}}$ | 0.25 – 1.0 | RAS flow as fraction of influent flow |
| **Trickling Filter Hydraulic Loading** | $HLR = \dfrac{Q + Q_R}{A}$ | 1 – 200 m/d | Total flow per unit filter area |
| **Trickling Filter Organic Loading** | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | 0.08 – 4.0 kg/m³·d | BOD load per unit filter volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall — Notation Alert:** In Kerala board papers, do **not** confuse the **organic loading** (kg BOD/m³·d) with the **F/M ratio** (day⁻¹). The former is a *volumetric load*; the latter is a *specific substrate load per unit biomass*. Examiners frequently swap these to test depth of understanding.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Suspended Growth Systems** dominate in **municipal STPs > 5 MLD capacity** (Cochin, Trivandrum cluster STPs) because of **smaller footprint, higher effluent quality, and ease of nitrification control**.
- **Attached Growth Systems** dominate in **small communities (< 1 MLD)**, **industrial high-BOD streams**, and **decentralised Kerala grama panchayats** due to **operational simplicity, no aeration blower cost, and tolerance to influent fluctuations**.
- **Hybrid MBBR-SBR-MBR** systems are the *current Kerala municipal trend* (e.g., Alappuzha smart-city cluster STPs) — combining biofilm robustness with membrane-quality effluent.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. The "Concepts Only" framing is preserved by focusing on **interpretive relationships** rather than design-engineering optimisation.

### 3.1 Derivation — The Food-to-Microorganism (F/M) Ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why derive F/M?** Because the F/M ratio is the *single most diagnostic* parameter in the KTU activated-sludge syllabus — it tells you whether a plant is operating as **conventional, extended aeration, or high-rate**.

**Step 1: Define the symbols used in the F/M relation.**

Let:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate of influent (m³/d)
- $S_0$ = influent substrate concentration (kg BOD/m³ or mg/L)
- $V$ = volume of the aeration tank (m³)
- $X$ = concentration of biomass in the aeration tank = MLSS (kg/m³)

**Step 2: Compute the rate at which *food* enters the system.**

The mass of substrate entering the aeration tank per unit time is:

$$F = Q \cdot S_0 \quad \text{[kg BOD / day]}$$

**Step 3: Compute the *active biomass inventory* in the tank.**

The total mass of biomass held in suspension is:

$$M = V \cdot X \quad \text{[kg MLSS]}$$

**Step 4: Form the ratio of food rate to biomass inventory.**

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{F}{M} \;=\; \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} \;\;\left[\text{day}^{-1}\right]\;}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result dimensionally.**

- Numerator has units of **kg/d**.
- Denominator has units of **kg**.
- Their ratio has units of **d⁻¹** (per day) — a *frequency*.
- *Higher F/M* → microbes are *underfed* relative to their numbers → *high effluent quality, low sludge age* (e.g., conventional).
- *Lower F/M* → microbes are *overfed* → *extended aeration*, more endogenous decay, less sludge yield, better nitrification.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pattern:** Numerical problems often ask — *"If the F/M ratio is 0.3 d⁻¹, MLSS is 2500 mg/L, and the tank is 500 m³, what is the influent BOD load?"* Reverse-substitute using the derived formula. Practice both directions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT, θc)

**Step 1: Mass balance on biomass over a steady-state reactor.**

At steady state, *rate of biomass growth* = *rate of biomass loss* from the system.

$$V \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt} = V \cdot r_g - Q_w \cdot X_w - Q_e \cdot X_e = 0$$

where:
- $r_g$ = net biomass growth rate (kg/m³·d)
- $Q_w$ = waste activated sludge flow rate (m³/d)
- $X_w$ = waste sludge concentration (kg/m³)
- $Q_e$ = effluent flow rate (m³/d)
- $X_e$ = effluent suspended solids (kg/m³)

**Step 2: Solve for total biomass in the system.**

Multiply both sides by 1 and recognise the *biomass inventory* $V \cdot X$:

$$V \cdot X \cdot r_g = Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e$$

**Step 3: Form the SRT (also called sludge age) as inventory over wastage rate.**

$$\boxed{\;\theta_c \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Biomass inventory in system}}{\text{Rate of biomass leaving system}} \;=\; \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e} \;\;\text{[days]}\;}$$

**Step 4: Approximate simplification (concept-level).**

In *complete-mix* activated sludge, $X_e \ll X_w$, so the effluent term is often dropped:

$$\theta_c \approx \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical meaning:** A *long SRT* means microbes live in the system for a long time → they are *old, starved, well-acclimatised* → great for *nitrification* and *low sludge yield*. A *short SRT* means young, hungry microbes → fast BOD removal but *no nitrification*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Observed Sludge Yield (Y_obs)

**Step 1: Start from Monod kinetics conceptual relationship.**

The net observed yield relates the true growth yield $Y$ and the endogenous decay coefficient $k_d$:

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{\text{Net biomass produced}}{\text{Substrate consumed}} = \dfrac{\text{Growth} - \text{Decay}}{\text{Substrate}}$$

**Step 2: Express the net growth rate as true growth minus decay.**

$$Y_{obs} \cdot r_s = Y \cdot r_s - k_d \cdot X$$

where $r_s$ is the substrate removal rate.

**Step 3: Divide both sides by $r_s$.**

$$Y_{obs} = Y - \dfrac{k_d \cdot X}{r_s} = Y - k_d \cdot \theta_c$$

(Using the fact that $\theta_c \approx X/r_s$ at steady state.)

**Step 4: Final canonical form.**

$$\boxed{\;Y_{obs} \;=\; \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}\;\;\text{[kg VSS / kg BOD removed]}\;}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the trend.**

- As $\theta_c \to \infty$ (very old sludge), $Y_{obs} \to 0$ → no net sludge (extended aeration, low WAS).
- As $\theta_c \to 0$ (high-rate), $Y_{obs} \to Y$ → maximum sludge production.

---

### 3.4 Conceptual Derivation — Oxygen Demand

**Step 1: Oxygen is required for two purposes.**

$$\text{Total } O_2 = O_2^{\text{(synthesis)}} + O_2^{\text{(endogenous)}}$$

**Step 2: Express synthesis oxygen demand (proportional to BOD removed).**

$$O_2^{\text{syn}} = a \cdot (Q \cdot S_r)$$

where $a \approx 0.5$ kg O₂/kg BOD removed (theoretical COD-balance coefficient).

**Step 3: Express endogenous demand (proportional to biomass inventory and SRT).**

$$O_2^{\text{endo}} = b \cdot X \cdot V \cdot \theta_c$$

where $b \approx 0.05 - 0.1$ kg O₂/kg MLVSS·d (endogenous coefficient).

**Step 4: Combine to canonical oxygen equation.**

$$\boxed{\;O_2 = a \cdot S_r \cdot Q - b \cdot X \cdot V \cdot \theta_c\;\;\text{[kg O}_2\text{/d]}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** The negative term indicates that *endogenous decay consumes oxygen* — a useful oxygen credit. In practice, this is why *extended aeration plants need MORE aeration capacity* than conventional plants, despite removing the same BOD, because the long SRT multiplies the second term.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Suspended vs. Attached Growth

| Parameter | Suspended Growth (ASP Family) | Attached Growth (TF / RBC Family) |
|---|---|---|
| **Biomass State** | Suspended flocs (MLSS) | Biofilm on media |
| **Biomass Hold-up** | 1500 – 4000 mg/L (MLSS) | 5000 – 15000 mg/L (as biofilm) |
| **HRT (hydraulic)** | 4 – 36 hours | 1 – 4 hours (faster) |
| **SRT (sludge age)** | 5 – 40 days | 10 – 100 days (much longer) |
| **F/M Ratio** | 0.05 – 0.5 d⁻¹ | N/A (biofilm kinetics differ) |
| **O₂ Source** | Blower / mechanical aerator | Natural draft / shaft rotation |
| **BOD Removal Efficiency** | 85 – 95% | 60 – 90% (per stage) |
| **Effluent Quality** | High, suitable for reuse | Moderate, polishing often needed |
| **Sludge Production** | Higher (Y_obs ≈ 0.3 – 0.5) | Lower (biofilm sloughs less) |
| **Footprint** | Small (compact tankage) | Large (filter area or disc area) |
| **Energy Demand** | High (blowers dominate) | Low (no aeration blower) |
| **O&M Complexity** | Moderate – high | Low – moderate |
| **Tolerance to Shock Loads** | Moderate (with complete mix) | High (biofilm acts as buffer) |
| **Nitrification Capability** | Yes (long SRT required) | Yes (natural, often simultaneous) |
| **Kerala Typical Application** | Kochi / Trivandrum metro STPs | Rural panchayats, resorts, hospitals |
| **Capital Cost (per MLD)** | ₹ 30 – 50 Lakh | ₹ 20 – 35 Lakh |
| **O&M Cost (per MLD·yr)** | Higher (electricity for blowers) | Lower |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favourite Question Type:**
> *"Compare the activated sludge process and the trickling filter in terms of: (i) biomass retention, (ii) oxygen transfer, (iii) shock load tolerance, (iv) sludge yield, (v) footprint, and (vi) Kerala-specific applicability."* — This single question alone can fetch **14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Activated Sludge Process (Suspended Growth) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INFLOW Sewage] --> B[Bar Screen]
    B --> C[Grit Chamber]
    C --> D[Primary Clarifier]
    D --> E[Aeration Tank<br/>MLSS 2000-3000 mg/L<br/>F:M 0.2-0.4 d-1]
    E --> F[Secondary Clarifier]
    F --> G[Disinfection]
    G --> H[EFFLUENT Discharge or Reuse]
    F --> I[RAS Return Activated Sludge]
    I --> E
    F --> J[WAS Waste Activated Sludge]
    J --> K[Sludge Digestion and Dewatering]
    E -.Diffused Air.-> L[Air Blower]
    L --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as:** Influent → preliminary treatment → primary settling → aeration tank (the heart) → secondary clarifier → disinfected effluent. The *recirculation loop* (RAS) and *wasting line* (WAS) are the keys to the whole system. The blower is the energy centre.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Trickling Filter (Attached Growth) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INFLOW Sewage] --> B[Primary Clarifier]
    B --> C[Settling Tank Influent]
    C --> D[Trickling Filter<br/>Rock or Plastic Media<br/>Biofilm on surface]
    D --> E[Secondary Clarifier]
    E --> F[Disinfection]
    F --> G[EFFLUENT Discharge]
    E --> H[Recirculation Pump]
    H --> D
    D -.Natural Draft.-> I[Air Inlet Plenum]
    I --> D
    D --> J[Sloughed Biofilm]
    J --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as:** Influent → primary clarifier → sprinkler/distributor trickles wastewater over biofilm-covered media → air flows counter-current through the void spaces → sloughed biofilm settles in the secondary clarifier → part of clarified effluent is **recirculated** to maintain hydraulic wetting and dilute incoming BOD.

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Shaft Drive Motor] --> B[Horizontal Shaft]
    B --> C[Disc Stage 1<br/>40 percent submerged]
    B --> D[Disc Stage 2]
    B --> E[Disc Stage 3]
    B --> F[Disc Stage 4]
    G[Inlet Wastewater] --> H[Trough Submerges Bottom 40 percent of discs]
    H --> I[Biofilm on disc surfaces]
    I --> J[Sloughed biomass falls into trough]
    J --> K[Outlet to Secondary Clarifier]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as:** A motor rotates a horizontal shaft on which 3–4 stages of closely-spaced plastic discs are mounted. Biofilm grows on the discs. The lower 40% of the discs dip into flowing wastewater; the upper 60% drains and absorbs oxygen from the air. Rotation = simultaneous aeration + substrate contact.

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Tree: Which Process to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New STP Design] --> B{What is the design capacity?}
    B -- > 5 MLD --> C{Space available?}
    B -- 1 to 5 MLD --> D[Consider ASP, SBR, MBR]
    B -- Less than 1 MLD --> E[Consider Trickling Filter, RBC, MBBR, DEWATS]
    C -- Yes --> F[Use Activated Sludge or MBR]
    C -- No --> G[Use MBBR or Bio-tower vertical]
    A --> H{Land cost high?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Suspended Growth ASP family]
    H -- No --> J[Attached Growth TF or RBC]
    A --> K{Energy cost high?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Attached Growth minimal blower]
    K -- No --> M[Suspended Growth with diffused aeration]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 — Conceptual Process Selection Question:**
> *"For a 50 MLD STP to be built in a space-constrained coastal Kerala municipality, propose and justify a treatment scheme."* — This decision tree structure earns full marks when cited in the answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define the term "Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS)" and state its typical concentration range in a conventional activated sludge process. Why is MLSS maintenance critical to process operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** MLSS is the dry-weight concentration of suspended solids in the mixed liquor of the aeration tank, expressed in mg/L, and represents the *biomass inventory* (living microbes + inert particulates) in a suspended growth reactor.
- **Typical Range (1 mark):** For a conventional activated sludge process, MLSS is maintained between **1500 – 4000 mg/L** (typical design value: 2000 – 3000 mg/L).
- **Criticality (1 mark):** MLSS directly determines the F/M ratio and the sludge age (SRT). Adequate MLSS ensures sufficient biomass to metabolise the incoming BOD load; too low MLSS causes effluent quality deterioration, while too high MLSS causes clarifier overload, sludge bulking, and high solids in the effluent.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**Differentiate between "suspended growth" and "attached growth" biological treatment processes with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Suspended Growth (1 mark):** Microbial biomass is maintained in **free suspension** in the wastewater being treated; aeration provides mixing and oxygen. *Example:* Activated Sludge Process.
- **Attached Growth (1 mark):** Microbial biomass grows as a **biofilm on a stationary or rotating support medium**; wastewater flows over the biofilm. *Example:* Trickling Filter.
- **Key Distinction (1 mark):** Suspended growth decouples HRT from SRT via sludge recycling, allowing very high effluent quality but requiring continuous energy input for aeration; attached growth relies on biofilm longevity (high SRT naturally), with lower energy demand but larger footprint and lower per-stage BOD removal.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**. Explain the function of the aeration tank, the secondary clarifier, and the return activated sludge (RAS) line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Food-to-Microorganism (F/M) ratio** and the **Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT)**. A conventional activated sludge plant has the following data: flow $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d, influent BOD $S_0 = 250$ mg/L, aeration tank volume $V = 500$ m³, MLSS $X = 2500$ mg/L. Compute the F/M ratio and comment on whether the plant is conventionally loaded, under-loaded, or over-loaded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Description of ASP (7 marks):**

- **Flow Diagram (2 marks):** The activated sludge process consists of:
  Influent → Primary Clarifier → Aeration Tank (with diffuser aeration) → Secondary Clarifier → Disinfected Effluent.
  A **RAS line** recycles settled sludge from the secondary clarifier back to the aeration tank; a **WAS line** wastes excess sludge to sludge processing.

- **Aeration Tank Function (2 marks):**
  The aeration tank is the **biological reactor** where dissolved organic matter (BOD) contacts suspended microbial flocs under aerobic conditions. It performs:
  (i) Substrate adsorption and oxidation,
  (ii) Endogenous respiration of biomass,
  (iii) Mixing to keep biomass in suspension,
  (iv) Oxygen transfer via diffused air or mechanical aerators.

- **Secondary Clarifier Function (1.5 marks):**
  The secondary clarifier **separates the biomass from the treated wastewater** by gravity settling. It produces a clarified supernatant (effluent) and a thickened underflow (sludge) which is split into RAS (recycled) and WAS (wasted).

- **RAS Line Function (1.5 marks):**
  The return activated sludge line recycles a fraction (typically 25 – 100% of the influent flow) of the settled biomass back to the aeration tank, ensuring that the **MLSS concentration is maintained** at design levels. This decouples HRT (hours) from SRT (days), enabling the biomass inventory to be much higher than what would be carried by the hydraulic flow alone.

**(b) F/M Ratio and SRT — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

- **Definition of F/M (1.5 marks):**
  The Food-to-Microorganism ratio is the **daily substrate (food) load applied per unit mass of biomass** in the aeration tank, expressed in d⁻¹:
  $$F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$$

- **Definition of SRT (1.5 marks):**
  The Mean Cell Residence Time (sludge age) is the **average time a unit of biomass remains in the system** under steady state, defined as biomass inventory divided by the rate of biomass wastage:
  $$\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$$

- **Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

  $$F/M = \dfrac{10{,}000 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 250 \;\text{g/m}^3}{500 \;\text{m}^3 \times 2500 \;\text{g/m}^3}$$

  $$\boxed{\;F/M = \dfrac{2{,}500{,}000}{1{,}250{,}000} = 2.0 \;\text{d}^{-1}\;}$$

- **Comment (1 mark):**
  This F/M value of **2.0 d⁻¹** is **much higher than the conventional range (0.2 – 0.4 d⁻¹)** — the plant is **heavily overloaded** (high-rate condition). The operator must either (i) reduce influent BOD, (ii) increase MLSS by reducing sludge wasting, or (iii) increase aeration tank volume. A high F/M leads to poor effluent quality, dispersed growth, and no nitrification.

---

#### **Question B (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Trickling Filter** as an attached growth process. Distinguish between **low-rate, high-rate, and roughing trickling filters** in terms of hydraulic and organic loading rates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)**. Discuss its merits and demerits compared to the activated sludge process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Trickling Filter Description (7 marks):**

- **Flow Diagram (2 marks):**
  Influent (settled sewage) → Rotating Distributor → Trickling Filter Bed (rock/plastic media with biofilm) → Secondary Clarifier → Disinfection → Effluent.
  A **recirculation line** returns clarified effluent back to the filter inlet for dilution; air enters from the bottom plenum by natural draft.

- **Process Description (2 marks):**
  Wastewater is sprayed over the filter media. Microbes grow on the media surface as a **biofilm**. As wastewater trickles down, substrate diffuses into the biofilm where it is metabolised aerobically. Air flows counter-current through the bed voids. Excess biomass **sloughs off** periodically and is collected in the secondary clarifier.

- **Low-Rate Trickling Filter (1 mark):**
  Hydraulic Loading $\approx 1 - 4$ m³/m²·d, Organic Loading $\approx 0.08 - 0.16$ kg BOD/m³·d, BOD removal 80 – 90%, intermittent dosing, partial recirculation. Robust but large footprint.

- **High-Rate Trickling Filter (1 mark):**
  Hydraulic Loading $\approx 10 - 30$ m³/m²·d, Organic Loading $\approx 0.30 - 0.60$ kg BOD/m³·d, BOD removal 60 – 85%, continuous dosing, high recirculation. Smaller footprint but lower per-stage efficiency.

- **Roughing Trickling Filter (1 mark):**
  Hydraulic Loading $\approx 40 - 200$ m³/m²·d, Organic Loading $\approx 1.0 - 4.0$ kg BOD/m³·d, BOD removal 50 – 70%. Used as a *roughing* stage ahead of another secondary treatment to handle very strong industrial wastewater.

**(b) Rotating Biological Contactor (7 marks):**

- **Construction (2 marks):**
  A RBC consists of a **horizontal shaft** (rotated slowly at 1–2 rpm by a motor) on which a series of **closely-spaced circular plastic or FRP discs** are mounted. The shaft and discs are installed in a **concrete or steel trough** filled with wastewater. The discs are typically 1–3 m in diameter, and the assembly may have 3–4 (or more) stages in series.

- **Working (2 marks):**
  As the discs rotate, the lower 40% of the disc surface is **submerged** in wastewater, where biofilm contacts the substrate. The upper 60% is **exposed to air**, where the biofilm absorbs oxygen. Rotation therefore provides **simultaneous substrate contact and aeration**. Sloughed biomass is collected in the trough and flows out to a secondary clarifier.

- **Merits vs. ASP (1.5 marks):**
  *(i)* Low energy (no blower required); *(ii)* simple operation and maintenance; *(iii)* tolerance to shock loads; *(iv)* high nitrification efficiency at low temperatures; *(v)* no sludge bulking issues; *(vi)* modular — capacity increased by adding more stages.

- **Demerits vs. ASP (1.5 marks):**
  *(i)* Large footprint per MLD; *(ii)* disc material cost is high; *(iii)* shaft/structural loads limit scaling to large MLDs; *(iv)* biofilm overgrowth can cause imbalance; *(v)* sensitive to low pH or toxic shocks in influent; *(vi)* less effective for BOD removal above 200 mg/L in a single stage.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing F/M ratio (d⁻¹) with Organic Loading Rate (kg/m³·d).** They are NOT the same. F/M is *substrate per unit biomass per day*; OLR is *substrate per unit reactor volume per day*. Examiners swap these deliberately.
>
> 2. **Writing "RAS" without explaining the recycling ratio R = Q_RAS/Q_inf.** Always state that R is typically 0.25 – 1.0.
>
> 3. **Confusing SRT (sludge age) with HRT (hydraulic retention time).** HRT = V/Q (hours); SRT = V·X / (Q_w·X_w) (days). They differ by 2–3 orders of magnitude in ASP.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the role of biofilm in attached growth.** Simply stating "microbes attach to a media" is not enough — explain EPS matrix, stratification, diffusion, and sloughing.
>
> 5. **In numerical problems, mixing units.** Keep Q in m³/d, S₀ in mg/L = g/m³, V in m³, X in mg/L = g/m³ — be consistent, or convert everything to g/m³ first.
>
> 6. **Skipping the sludge wastage line (WAS) in the ASP flow diagram.** This is a *guaranteed 1-mark loss*. The WAS line is *conceptually essential* for steady-state operation.
>
> 7. **Omitting the recirculation line in the trickling filter diagram.** This is a *guaranteed 1-mark loss*. Recirculation is *what differentiates high-rate from low-rate TF*.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section on the last page of your answer booklet as a last-minute revision pass before the KTU 2024 exam.**

✅ **Biological Treatment** uses microbes to convert BOD into CO₂ + H₂O + new cells (sludge).
✅ **Suspended Growth** keeps biomass in free suspension; **Attached Growth** grows biomass as a biofilm on a solid support.
✅ **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** is the *flagship* suspended growth process — invented 1914, Ardern & Lockett.
✅ ASP flow: Primary Clarifier → Aeration Tank → Secondary Clarifier → Effluent; with **RAS** (recycle) and **WAS** (waste) lines.
✅ **F/M ratio** = $Q \cdot S_0 / (V \cdot X)$; range 0.05 – 0.5 d⁻¹.
✅ **SRT (θc)** = $V \cdot X / (Q_w \cdot X_w)$; range 5 – 40 days; controls nitrification.
✅ **HRT (θ)** = $V / Q$; range 4 – 36 hours; controls substrate contact time.
✅ **MLSS** = biomass concentration in aeration tank; range 1500 – 4000 mg/L.
✅ **SVI** = 30-min settled volume per unit MLSS; range 50 – 150 mL/g; high SVI → bulking sludge.
✅ **Y_obs** = $Y / (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)$; long SRT reduces net sludge yield.
✅ **SBR (Sequencing Batch Reactor)** performs all five operations (Fill, React, Settle, Decant, Idle) in TIME in a single tank.
✅ **MBR (Membrane Bioreactor)** replaces secondary clarifier with UF membrane; produces reuse-grade effluent.
✅ **MBBR (Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor)** is a *hybrid* of suspended and attached growth.
✅ **Trickling Filter (TF)** = biofilm on rock/plastic media; wastewater trickled by rotating distributor; air drawn by natural draft.
✅ **TF classes:** Low-rate (1–4 m³/m²·d), High-rate (10–30), Roughing (40–200).
✅ **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)** = biofilm on rotating plastic discs; 40% submerged; rotation provides both substrate contact and aeration.
✅ **Biofilm** = structured microbial consortium embedded in EPS matrix; stratified (aerobic exterior, anoxic interior).
✅ **Sloughing** = periodic detachment of excess biofilm; collected in secondary clarifier.
✅ **Suspended vs. Attached growth comparison:** Suspended = higher efficiency, smaller footprint, higher energy, more sludge. Attached = lower efficiency, larger footprint, lower energy, less sludge, more shock-tolerant.
✅ **Kerala-specific context:** Attached growth (TF, RBC, MBBR) suits rural panchayats and resorts; suspended growth (ASP, SBR, MBR) suits urban municipal STPs (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut clusters).
✅ **Energy rule of thumb:** Suspended Growth ≈ 0.3 – 0.5 kWh/m³; Attached Growth ≈ 0.05 – 0.15 kWh/m³.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — ATTACHED AND SUSPENDED GROWTH PROCESSES (CONCEPTS ONLY)**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework. All notations follow IS 10500 / CPCB conventions and standard environmental engineering textbooks.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trickling Filter (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Trickling Filter (Concept Only) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trickling Filter (TF):** A fixed-film biological reactor used in **secondary treatment of municipal wastewater**, in which wastewater is **distributed over a bed of coarse, inert packing media** (rock, slag, plastic) and allowed to trickle downward by gravity. As the wastewater flows over the media, a **slime layer (biofilm)** of aerobic microorganisms attached to the media surface metabolizes the dissolved organic matter. Air is supplied naturally (draft) or forced through the bed to maintain aerobic conditions. The trickling filter is one of the oldest and most reliable biofilm reactors in sanitary engineering, generally classified as a **Secondary Wastewater Treatment Unit** operating under **Attached Growth (Biofilm) Process** conditions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy — "The Artificial Rocky River"** > Imagine a hollow stone tower filled with loose rocks. Dirty kitchen water is sprayed gently from the top using a rotating sprinkler arm (like a lawn sprinkler). As the water drips down over the thousands of wet rock surfaces, a thin **green-brown slime** (bacteria) coats every stone. The bacteria "eat" the dirt in the water as it passes. By the time the water reaches the bottom, it is much cleaner, and the slime-coated rocks have air naturally sucked up through them (like a chimney effect) to keep the bacteria alive and hungry. This analogy captures the **three simultaneous processes** occurring inside a trickling filter: - **Distribution** of wastewater (rotary distributor) - **Attachment and growth of biofilm** on media - **Natural or forced aeration** through the void spaces ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics & Standard Constants | Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Unit | |---|---|---| | Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR) | **1 – 30** | m³/m²·d | | Organic Loading Rate (OLR) | **0.08 – 0.4** (low rate) / **0.4 – 4.8** (high rate) | kg BOD/m³·d | | Typical BOD Removal Efficiency | **65 % – 90 %** | % | | Media Depth | **1.0 – 3.0 m** | m | | Media Size (rock) | **25 – 75 mm** | mm | | Dosing Interval | **5 – 60 seconds** | s | > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Pearl:** Always remember the keyword triad — **"Distribute, Attach, Aerate"** — this is exactly how a 3-mark KTU question on TF definition is phrased. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Component-wise Operational Architecture A trickling filter is **not just a bed of stones** — it is a structured reactor with **six critical components**, each serving a unique hydraulic or biochemical function: ### (a) Rotary Distributor - A **slowly rotating arm** (1–2 revolutions/min) mounted above the filter bed. - Has **orifices/nozzles** that spray wastewater uniformly over the media. - Hydraulic head (typically **0.6 – 2.0 m**) drives rotation; **reaction torque** from jets propels the arm. - Ensures **even dosing** → prevents dry zones and clogged zones. ### (b) Filter Media - The "engine room" of the TF. - Materials: **Crushed rock** (historically), **slag**, **plastic rings/modules** (modern). - Provides **large specific surface area** for biofilm attachment. - Must have **high void ratio (≥ 40 %)** for airflow. ### (c) Underdrain System - Located at the **bottom** of the filter. - Collects treated effluent (effluent + sloughed biofilm). - Channels **air upward** through the bed (natural draft via Stack effect). - Drains into the **final clarifier (humus tank)**. ### (d) Biofilm (Zoogleal Slime Layer) - A **mixed culture of bacteria, fungi, protozoa, algae**, and higher organisms. - Outer aerobic layer → facultative layer → anaerobic layer at media interface. - Responsible for **BOD removal, nitrification, and denitrification** (latter under specific conditions). ### (e) Ventilation System - **Natural draft** via the chimney effect of warm biofilm and rising air. - **Forced draft** using blowers (for deep or high-rate filters). - Maintains dissolved oxygen **≥ 2 mg/L** throughout the bed. ### (f) Recirculation System - A portion of **clarifier effluent is returned** to the filter inlet. - **Purpose:** Maintain continuous dosing, dilute shock loads, improve wetting, boost effluent quality. - **Recirculation Ratio (R) = Q_r / Q** (dimensionless). ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Trickling Filter Operation The biochemical process can be decomposed into **four sequential physical-chemical steps**: 1. **Adsorption** — Organic molecules diffuse from bulk liquid into the **gel layer** of the biofilm. 2. **Absorption & Diffusion** — Substrates penetrate the **stratified biofilm layers** to reach active cells. 3. **Metabolism (Aerobic Oxidation)** — Microorganisms oxidize organics using dissolved O₂: $$C_5H_7NO_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 5CO_2 + 2H_2O + NH_3$$ 4. **Sloughing** — As the biofilm thickens (> 0.1 mm), it detaches and is **washed out** to the humus tank for separation. ## 2.3 Classification of Trickling Filters (High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table is THE most-asked TF classification in KTU exams — memorize it.** | Type | Hydraulic Loading (m³/m²·d) | Organic Loading (kg BOD/m³·d) | Recirculation | BOD Removal (%) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Standard / Low Rate** | **1 – 4** | **0.08 – 0.2** | ❌ None | **80 – 90** | | **High Rate** | **10 – 30** | **0.4 – 2.4** | ✅ Yes | **65 – 85** | | **Roughing / Super High Rate** | **40 – 200** | **> 2.4** | ✅ Yes | **40 – 65** | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All standard trickling filter design formulas are summarized below. **Memorize these five equations — they appear in every KTU numerical.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $$\eta = \dfrac{S_i - S_e}{S_i} \times 100$$ | BOD Removal Efficiency | % | | 2 | $$HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^2}$$ | Hydraulic Loading Rate | m³/m²·d | | 3 | $$OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_i}{V}$$ | Volumetric Organic Loading | kg BOD/m³·d | | 4 | $$E_2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \sqrt{W/V}}$$ | **NRC Formula** for single-stage TF efficiency | fraction | | 5 | $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561}{\sqrt[4]{R}} \cdot \sqrt{W/V}}$$ | NRC Formula **with recirculation** | fraction | > **Where:** > - $Q$ = Sewage flow (m³/d) > - $A$ = Filter surface area (m²) > - $V$ = Filter volume (m³) > - $S_i$ = Influent BOD, $S_e$ = Effluent BOD (mg/L) > - $W$ = Total BOD loading (kg/d) = $Q \cdot S_i / 1000$ > - $R$ = Recirculation Ratio = $Q_r / Q$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Trickling filters are deployed where: - **Energy efficiency** is critical (consume **40 – 70 % less power** than activated sludge). - **Shock load tolerance** is required (biofilm re-establishes quickly). - **Skilled operators are scarce** (rural Kerala panchayat STPs). - Combined with **Activated Sludge (AS-TF, TF-AS, or ABF)** for nitrification and BOD polishing. > [!TIP] > Examples: Aluva, Thiruvananthapuram Corporation, and most KSUDP-funded municipal STPs in Kerala use **trickling filters as a primary biological unit**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the NRC Trickling Filter Efficiency Equation The **NRC (National Research Council, USA, 1946)** equation is a **purely empirical** correlation. It is derived from operating data of standard-rate filters and is *not* a first-principles derivation. We therefore present it as a **calibrated regression model** and then show how to apply it in KTU-style problems. ### Step 1: Empirical Formulation The NRC committee observed that for a **single-stage filter without recirculation**, the BOD removal efficiency $E_2$ correlates with the dimensionless BOD-loading parameter $\dfrac{W}{V}$ as: $$E_2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{V}\right)^n}$$ where $k$ and $n$ are empirical constants fitted from operating data of **standard-rate filters**. ### Step 2: Statistical Fitting of Constants By regression on data from **34 trickling filter plants**, the NRC committee reported: $$k = 0.0561 \quad \text{and} \quad n = 0.5$$ Substituting these calibrated values: $$E_2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{V}\right)^{0.5}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \cdot \sqrt{W/V}}$$ > **Boundary Conditions of the NRC Equation:** > - Valid for: $W/V$ between **0.2 and 1.0 kg BOD/m³·d** > - Filter depth: **1.8 – 2.4 m** > - Media: **Rock** (25 – 75 mm) > - Single-stage, **no recirculation** ### Step 3: Modified NRC Equation with Recirculation Ratio When **recirculation ratio $R$** is introduced, the effective BOD load applied to the filter is reduced because the recycled effluent is blended with raw influent. The NRC introduced a correction term: $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561}{(R+1)^{0.5}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V}}}$$ Expanding $(R+1)^{0.5}$ as a fourth-root correction in many KTU textbooks: $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561}{\sqrt[4]{R+1}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on KTU Conventions:** Different textbooks write the recirculation correction slightly differently. The most commonly used form in KTU question papers is: > $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot R^{0.5}}}}$$ > Always **state the formula explicitly** before using it in your answer script. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Standard KTU Problem > **Problem:** A circular trickling filter of diameter **20 m** and depth **2.5 m** receives a sewage flow of **5000 m³/d** with an influent BOD of **250 mg/L**. There is **no recirculation**. Compute: (a) Hydraulic Loading Rate, (b) Organic Loading Rate, (c) BOD removal efficiency using NRC formula, (d) Effluent BOD. ### Solution (Step-by-Step) **Given Data:** - $D = 20$ m, $H = 2.5$ m - $Q = 5000$ m³/d - $S_i = 250$ mg/L = **0.250 kg/m³** ### (a) Hydraulic Loading Rate $$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} = 100\pi = 314.16 \text{ m}^2$$ $$HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{5000}{314.16} = 15.92 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{d}$$ ### (b) Organic Loading Rate $$V = A \times H = 314.16 \times 2.5 = 785.4 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W = Q \times S_i / 1000 = 5000 \times 0.250 = 1250 \text{ kg/d}$$ $$OLR = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{1250}{785.4} = 1.591 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3\cdot\text{d}$$ > **Interpretation:** This OLR classifies the unit as a **High-Rate Trickling Filter** (since OLR > 0.4 kg BOD/m³·d). ### (c) BOD Removal Efficiency (NRC Formula) $$\dfrac{W}{V} = 1.591 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{W/V} = \sqrt{1.591} = 1.261$$ $$E_2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \times 1.261} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0707} = \dfrac{1}{1.0707} = 0.934$$ $$\boxed{E_2 = 93.4\%}$$ ### (d) Effluent BOD $$S_e = S_i (1 - E_2) = 250 \times (1 - 0.934) = 250 \times 0.066 = 16.5 \text{ mg/L}$$ $$\boxed{S_e \approx 16.5 \text{ mg/L}}$$ > [!TIP] > This effluent BOD is well within the **KSPCB discharge norm of ≤ 30 mg/L** for STPs discharging to inland surface water. # 4.
