Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Component Architecture of a Trickling Filter The diagram below maps the **flow path, structural components, and gas/liquid interactions** in a single-stage trickling filter system with a humus tank. ```mermaid flowchart TB INF["Raw Sewage Influent
S_i mg/L BOD"] PS["Primary Clarifier
Removes settleable solids"] RD["Rotary Distributor
Sprays wastewater
Reacts on nozzles"] RC["Recirculation Pump
Optional - R ratio"] FM["Filter Media Bed
Rock / Plastic
Biofilm coated"] BIO["Active Biofilm
Aerobic + Facultative
Bacteria + Protozoa"] UD["Underdrain System
Effluent collection
Air intake"] HT["Humus Tank
Final Clarifier"] EFF["Treated Effluent
S_e mg/L BOD"] SL["Sludge Return
To Primary or Digester"] AIR["Atmospheric Air
Natural or forced draft"] AIR2["O2 supply to biofilm"] WASTE["Excess Sludge Wasting"] INF --> PS PS --> RD RC -.-> RD RD --> FM FM --> BIO AIR --> UD UD --> AIR2 AIR2 --> BIO BIO --> UD UD --> HT HT --> EFF HT --> SL SL --> WASTE HT -. Recirculated .-> RC classDef unit fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef bio fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000 classDef air fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 class INF,PS,RD,FM,UD,HT,RC unit class BIO,WASTE,SL bio class AIR,AIR2 air class EFF output ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Trickling Filter The block diagram below illustrates the **functional hierarchy** of the trickling filter as a treatment system, separating **Hydraulic, Biological, and Aeration** functions: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HYDRAULIC["Hydraulic Function Block"] H1["Inlet Distribution
Parshall Flume"] H2["Rotary Distributor
Splash + Spray"] H3["Effluent Collection
Underdrain"] end subgraph BIOLOGICAL["Biological Function Block"] B1["Biofilm Attachment
on Media"] B2["Substrate Diffusion
Gel Layer + Active Layer"] B3["Metabolism
BOD Removal + Nitrification"] B4["Sloughing
Biomass Detachment"] end subgraph AERATION["Aeration Function Block"] A1["Natural Draft
Stack Effect"] A2["Forced Draft
Blower Assisted"] A3["Oxygen Transfer
To Biofilm"] end subgraph CONTROL["Operational Control"] C1["Recirculation Ratio R"] C2["Dosing Frequency"] C3["Effluent Recycle"] end H1 --> H2 H2 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> H3 A1 --> A3 A2 --> A3 A3 --> B2 C1 --> H2 C2 --> H2 C3 --> H1 classDef hyd fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef bio fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef air fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef ctl fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 class H1,H2,H3 hyd class B1,B2,B3,B4 bio class A1,A2,A3 air class C1,C2,C3 ctl ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** A wastewater droplet entering the filter (top) encounters the **Hydraulic Block** (distribution), passes through the **Biological Block** (biofilm reactions), receives oxygen from the **Aeration Block**, and is finally collected by the **Underdrain** (bottom). The **Control Block** adjusts recirculation and dosing to maintain design efficiency. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **PART A — 3 Mark Questions** ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a trickling filter. List any four components of a trickling filter.** **Model Answer:** A **trickling filter** is an aerobic attached-growth biological treatment unit in which wastewater is distributed over a bed of coarse inert media, allowing a microbial biofilm to degrade organic matter as the wastewater trickles down. **Four components:** 1. **Rotary Distributor** – sprays wastewater uniformly over the media. 2. **Filter Media** – rock/plastic surface for biofilm growth. 3. **Underdrain System** – collects effluent and admits air. 4. **Ventilation System** – supplies oxygen via natural/forced draft. > [!TIP] > KTU Examiner's note: If you miss the word **"aerobic"** or **"attached-growth,"** you lose **1 mark** — these are mandatory descriptors. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between low-rate and high-rate trickling filters (any 3 points).** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Low-Rate Filter | High-Rate Filter | |---|---|---| | Hydraulic Loading | 1 – 4 m³/m²·d | 10 – 30 m³/m²·d | | Organic Loading | 0.08 – 0.2 kg BOD/m³·d | 0.4 – 2.4 kg BOD/m³·d | | Recirculation | Not provided | Always provided | | BOD Removal | 80 – 90 % | 65 – 85 % | | Effluent Quality | Highly nitrified | Partially nitrified | | Media Depth | 1.8 – 3.0 m | 1.0 – 2.0 m | --- ## **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)** ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a trickling filter** with a neat labelled sketch. **(b) [7 Marks]** A trickling filter of **diameter 25 m and depth 2.0 m** treats **8000 m³/d** of sewage with BOD of **300 mg/L**. Recirculation ratio is **1.0**. Calculate: (i) Hydraulic Loading, (ii) Organic Loading, (iii) BOD removal efficiency using NRC formula, (iv) Effluent BOD. #### **Model Solution (a) — Sketch + Explanation (7 marks)** **Principle:** Wastewater is sprayed over a stationary bed of inert media. A **biofilm** of aerobic microorganisms attached to the media oxidizes organic matter in the wastewater using oxygen supplied by natural or forced air draft. **Construction & Working (label-wise description expected):** 1. **Inlet Chamber** – Receives settled sewage from primary clarifier. 2. **Rotary Distributor** – Motor-/hydraulically-driven horizontal arm with nozzles that sprays wastewater; rotates 1–2 times/min, ensuring uniform dosing. 3. **Filter Media** – Crushed rock (25–75 mm) or plastic modules; provides surface area 90–150 m²/m³ for biofilm. 4. **Biofilm (Slime Layer)** – Mixed microbial culture (Zooglea, protozoa, worms, fungi); thickness 0.1–0.2 mm. 5. **Underdrain System** – Permeable floor collecting treated effluent; also acts as air plenum. 6. **Ventilation Stack / Fan** – Maintains aerobic conditions. 7. **Effluent Channel → Humus Tank** – Final clarifier separates sloughed biofilm. 8. **Recirculation Pipe** – Returns a portion of effluent to the inlet (improves wetting, dilutes shock loads). **Working Mechanism:** (i) Distribution → (ii) Adsorption on biofilm → (iii) Diffusion into gel layer → (iv) Aerobic oxidation → (v) Sloughing → (vi) Humus tank separation. #### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** $D = 25$ m, $H = 2.0$ m, $Q = 8000$ m³/d, $S_i = 300$ mg/L = 0.300 kg/m³, $R = 1.0$ **(i) Hydraulic Loading Rate** $$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 25^2}{4} = 490.87 \text{ m}^2$$ $$HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{8000}{490.87} = 16.30 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{d}$$ **(ii) Organic Loading Rate** $$V = A \times H = 490.87 \times 2.0 = 981.75 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W = Q \times S_i / 1000 = 8000 \times 0.300 = 2400 \text{ kg/d}$$ $$OLR = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{2400}{981.75} = 2.445 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3\cdot\text{d}$$ > **Classification:** OLR > 0.4 → **High-Rate Trickling Filter** **(iii) BOD Removal Efficiency — NRC with Recirculation** Using the KTU form: $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561}{\sqrt[4]{R+1}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V}}}$$ $$R+1 = 2.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt[4]{2.0} = 1.1892$$ $$\dfrac{W}{V} = 2.445 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{W/V} = 1.5636$$ $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561}{1.1892} \times 1.5636} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.04718 \times 1.5636}$$ $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0738} = \dfrac{1}{1.0738} = 0.9313$$ $$\boxed{E_1 = 93.13\%}$$ **(iv) Effluent BOD** $$S_e = S_i (1 - E_1) = 300 \times (1 - 0.9313) = 300 \times 0.0687$$ $$\boxed{S_e = 20.6 \text{ mg/L}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall):** > 1. **Forgetting to convert mg/L → kg/m³** (multiply by $10^{-3}$). This single error causes a 7-mark problem to score zero. > 2. **Using $V$ in litres** instead of m³. OLR is **always in m³**. > 3. **Forgetting $(R+1)$** — when $R=1$, the denominator is $\sqrt[4]{2} \approx 1.189$, not $\sqrt[4]{1}$. > 4. **Reporting $E_1$ as 9.31 %** instead of **93.13 %** (off by 10× — pay attention to decimal placement). --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **mechanism of biofilm formation and BOD removal** in a trickling filter. Discuss the role of each **stratified layer** in the biofilm. **(b) [7 Marks]** A municipal STP uses a trickling filter of volume **1500 m³** to treat sewage with an influent BOD of **200 mg/L**. The flow is **6000 m³/d** and the recirculation ratio is **2.0**. Determine: (i) BOD removal efficiency using NRC formula, (ii) Effluent BOD, (iii) Is the effluent suitable for discharge into a river with $DO \geq 4$ mg/L (KSPCB limit: BOD ≤ 30 mg/L)? #### **Model Solution (a) — Biofilm Mechanism (7 marks)** **Biofilm formation proceeds in 4 stages:** 1. **Conditioning (Hours 0–24):** Dissolved organic matter from wastewater forms a thin **"conditioning film"** on the inert media surface. 2. **Adhesion (Day 1–5):** Free-swimming bacteria (mainly *Zooglea ramigera*, *Pseudomonas*) attach via **exopolysaccharide (EPS) matrix**. 3. **Growth & Stratification (Day 5–30):** The biofilm thickens into three distinct layers: - **Aerobic Outer Layer** – Direct contact with air/water → high O₂ → organic oxidation. - **Facultative Middle Layer** – Transitional; alternates aerobic/anaerobic metabolism. - **Anaerobic Inner Layer** – Close to media surface; sulfate reduction, fermentation. 4. **Sloughing (Continuous):** As EPS matrix weakens, chunks detach and wash out to the **humus tank**. **Role of Stratification:** - Outer layer → **BOD removal & nitrification** (high DO). - Middle layer → **Polishing of organics** and **denitrification** (low/no O₂). - Inner layer → **Endogenous decay & anaerobic digestion** of trapped solids. > KTU Marking: 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for stratification, 2 marks for sloughing. #### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** $V = 1500$ m³, $S_i = 200$ mg/L = 0.200 kg/m³, $Q = 6000$ m³/d, $R = 2.0$ **(i) NRC Efficiency with Recirculation** $$W = Q \times S_i / 1000 = 6000 \times 0.200 = 1200 \text{ kg/d}$$ $$\dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{1200}{1500} = 0.800 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3\cdot\text{d}$$ $$R+1 = 3.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt[4]{3.0} = 1.3161$$ $$\sqrt{W/V} = \sqrt{0.800} = 0.8944$$ $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561}{1.3161} \times 0.8944} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.04262 \times 0.8944}$$ $$E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0381} = \dfrac{1}{1.0381} = 0.9633$$ $$\boxed{E_1 = 96.33\%}$$ **(ii) Effluent BOD** $$S_e = S_i (1 - E_1) = 200 \times (1 - 0.9633) = 200 \times 0.0367$$ $$\boxed{S_e = 7.34 \text{ mg/L}}$$ **(iii) Suitability for Discharge** KSPCB discharge norm: BOD ≤ **30 mg/L** (inland surface water). Our calculated $S_e$ = **7.34 mg/L** ≪ 30 mg/L. > **Verdict: ✅ YES — The effluent is suitable for discharge into the river.** > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often substitute $R$ directly into $\sqrt[4]{R}$ (instead of $\sqrt[4]{R+1}$). This gives the **wrong answer** and costs **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. Always confirm the form of the NRC equation as given in your specific KTU textbook. --- ## 📌 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Trickling Filter (Concept Only)** - **Type of Process:** Aerobic, **attached-growth / biofilm**, secondary treatment unit. - **Key Components:** Rotary Distributor → Media → Biofilm → Underdrain → Humus Tank. - **Three "Must-Say" Words:** **Distribute, Attach, Aerate** — these capture the entire principle in a single line. - **Biofilm Layers (Top → Bottom):** Aerobic → Facultative → Anaerobic (use this order in diagrams). - **Biofilm Detachment = Sloughing** (this is why humus tank is mandatory downstream). - **Three TF Types (Memorize Loading Ranges):** - Low Rate: 0.08 – 0.2 kg BOD/m³·d - High Rate: 0.4 – 2.4 kg BOD/m³·d - Roughing: > 2.4 kg BOD/m³·d - **NRC Formula (THE design equation):** - Without R: $E_2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561\sqrt{W/V}}$ - With R: $E_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \cdot \sqrt{W/V} / \sqrt[4]{R+1}}$ - **Recirculation Ratio R = $Q_r / Q$** → dimensionless → ALWAYS $> 0$ in formula (use $R+1$). - **Conversion:** 1 mg/L = $10^{-3}$ kg/m³ — this is the **#1 cause of numerical errors** in KTU scripts. - **Energy Advantage:** Trickling filters use **40 – 70 % less electricity** than activated sludge. - **Kerala Context:** Most KSUDP-funded STPs (Aluva, Kochi, Trivandrum) use **trickling filters** as the primary biological unit. - **KSPCB Effluent Norm:** BOD ≤ **30 mg/L** for discharge to inland surface water. - **Common Pitfall (Repeated KTU Complaint):** Confusing **hydraulic loading (m³/m²·d)** with **organic loading (kg BOD/m³·d)** — they are **two different quantities** and **never interchangeable**. - **Dosing Rule of Thumb:** Rest period between dosings should be **< 5 minutes** to keep the biofilm moist and aerobic.

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Environmental Engineering (PCCET503)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the term **"Types"** within Module 3 broadly refers to the **classification of water treatment systems and their unit processes**. Water treatment plants (WTPs) and their sub-components are categorized based on the **scale of operation, treatment philosophy, hydraulic arrangement, and end-use application**.

As per KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the principal classifications in Module 3 encompass:

- **Types of Water Treatment Plants (WTPs)** based on size and population served.
- **Types of Sedimentation Tanks** based on flow pattern and shape.
- **Types of Filters** based on filtration mechanism, flow direction, and driving head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition — Water Treatment Plant (WTP):**
> A Water Treatment Plant is an integrated assembly of physical, chemical, and biological unit processes designed to convert raw water from natural sources (rivers, lakes, reservoirs, or underground aquifers) into potable water meeting the **BIS (IS 10500:2012)** drinking water quality standards, or to treat wastewater for safe disposal or reuse.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Water Purifier" 🍶**

Imagine the process of cleaning muddy river water at home. You first let the water **sit still in a bucket** (sedimentation), then you **pour it slowly through a cloth** (filtration), and finally you **boil it** (disinfection). Each step removes progressively finer impurities.

A Water Treatment Plant is simply the **industrial-scale, engineered version** of this same process:

| Home Method | Engineering Unit | Impurity Removed |
|---|---|---|
| Letting water sit | Sedimentation Tank | Suspended solids, silt |
| Pouring through cloth | Sand Filter | Fine particles, microbes |
| Boiling | Chlorination / UV | Pathogenic bacteria, viruses |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Examination Tip):** Always think of the treatment chain as a **"Top-Down Sieve"** — coarser impurities are removed first, and finer ones are tackled later. This is the **"Multi-Barrier Approach"** universally adopted in environmental engineering.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Design Period for Municipal WTP:** **15 to 30 years** (as per CPHEEO Manual, India).
- **Standard Detention Time (Sedimentation):** **2 to 4 hours**.
- **Standard Filter Rate:** **5 to 15 m³/m²/day**.
- **Drinking Water Standard:** **IS 10500:2012 (Bureau of Indian Standards)**.
- **Global Benchmark:** **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality, 4th Edition (2017)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **IS 10500:2012** (drinking water specifications) with **IS 14543:2004** (packaged drinking water). For Module 3 municipal WTP questions, the correct reference is **IS 10500:2012**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Water Treatment Plants (WTPs)

The KTU 2024 Scheme structures Module 3 "Types" around the following hierarchy:

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Scale of Operation

#### **A. Large-Scale Municipal / Conventional Water Treatment Plants**

These are the most common systems serving cities and towns with populations **above 50,000**. They employ the **conventional treatment sequence**:

$$\text{Raw Water} \rightarrow \text{Inlet Works} \rightarrow \text{Screening} \rightarrow \text{Aeration} \rightarrow \text{Coagulation-Flocculation} \rightarrow \text{Sedimentation} \rightarrow \text{Filtration} \rightarrow \text{Disinfection} \rightarrow \text{Clear Water Reservoir}$$

- **Design Philosophy:** Continuous gravity-fed operation.
- **Capacity Range:** **50 MLD to 2000+ MLD** (Million Litres per Day).
- **Example:** The **100 MLD WTP at Aruvikkara, Thiruvananthapuram**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** Conventional WTPs use the **"Pre-treatment → Primary Treatment → Secondary Treatment → Tertiary Treatment"** hierarchy, but in water supply (not wastewater), the conventional plant **does NOT include biological treatment** as a primary step — coagulation-flocculation-sedimentation is the "primary" stage.

#### **B. Package / Compact Water Treatment Plants**

These are **pre-engineered, factory-built skid-mounted units** designed for rapid deployment:

- **Capacity:** **0.5 MLD to 50 MLD**.
- **Application:** Small municipalities, industrial townships, large residential complexes, hotels, hospitals, and tourist resorts.
- **Key Advantage:** Installation time reduced from **18–24 months (conventional)** to **3–6 months**.
- **Limitation:** Higher unit cost (₹/kL treated) and limited flexibility for capacity expansion.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "PSI" for Package Plants:**
> - **P**re-fabricated
> - **S**kid-mounted
> - **I**ntegrated (all units in one housing)

#### **C. Decentralized / Community-Scale Water Treatment**

For villages, hamlets, and peri-urban settlements, the KTU 2024 syllabus (aligned with **Jal Jeevan Mission, Government of India, 2019**) emphasizes decentralized systems:

- **Slow Sand Filters (SSF)** — community-operated, low-cost.
- **Bio-sand Filters** — household-level (capacity **30–60 L/hour**).
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO) Units** — for arsenic, fluoride, and nitrate removal.
- **Defluoridation Plants (Nalgonda Technique)** — used in Rajasthan and Telangana.

#### **D. Household Water Treatment Systems (HWTS)**

As per the **WHO Household Water Treatment and Safe Storage (HWTS)** protocol:

| Technology | Targeted Pathogen | Removal Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| **Boiling** | Bacteria, viruses, protozoa | **>99%** (at 100°C for 1 min) |
| **UV Sterilization** | Bacteria, viruses | **>99%** (at 254 nm, 40 mJ/cm²) |
| **RO Membrane** | All dissolved ions, microbes | **>95%** |
| **Activated Carbon** | Organic chemicals, chlorine | **60–90%** |
| **Ceramic Candle Filter** | Bacteria, protozoa | **>99%** |

---

### 2.2 Types of Sedimentation Tanks (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Sedimentation is the **gravity-driven separation** of suspended particles. The **surface overflow rate (SOR)** or **surface loading rate** is the governing design parameter:

$$\text{SOR (v}_o\text{)} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{B \times L}$$

where:
- $Q$ = Flow rate (m³/hour)
- $A$ = Surface area of tank (m²)
- $B$ = Width, $L$ = Length

#### Classification of Sedimentation Tanks

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Flow Rectangular Tank** | Long, narrow rectangular basin; flow along length | Large municipal WTPs (most common) |
| **Radial Flow Circular Tank** | Water flows radially from center to periphery | Medium to large WTPs |
| **Hopper-Bottom (Conical) Tank** | Conical bottom for sludge collection by gravity | Small to medium plants |
| **Tube / Plate Settler** | Inclined tubes/plates at 60° for laminar settling | Capacity augmentation in existing tanks |
| **Solid Contact Clarifier (Accelator)** | Combines coagulation, flocculation, sedimentation in one unit | High-turbidity raw water |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** The **Solid Contact Clarifier (also called Accelator or Reactor-Clarifier)** is **explicitly mentioned** in the KTU 2024 PCCET503 syllabus under Module 3, making it a **guaranteed high-yield topic** for 14-mark questions.

---

### 2.3 Types of Filters (KTU High-Yield Topic)

#### 2.3.1 Slow Sand Filter (SSF)

- **Filtration Rate:** **100 to 200 L/m²/hour** (i.e., **2.4 to 4.8 m³/m²/day**).
- **Schmutzdecke Layer:** A biological layer (~2–3 cm) of algae, bacteria, and protozoa forms on top of the sand bed within 2–3 weeks. This is the **active purification zone**.
- **Cleaning:** Scraping of the top 1–2 cm of sand when head loss exceeds **0.6–0.9 m**.
- **Efficiency:** Removes **>99% of bacteria, 100% of suspended solids**, and reduces turbidity below **1 NTU**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem — Darcy's Law for Filter Bed:**
> $$\boxed{h_f = \frac{L}{k} \cdot v = \frac{\mu \cdot L \cdot v}{k \cdot \rho \cdot g}}$$
> where:
> - $h_f$ = Head loss through the filter (m)
> - $L$ = Thickness of filter bed (m)
> - $v$ = Approach velocity (m/s)
> - $k$ = Permeability coefficient (m²)
> - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of water (N·s/m²)
> - $\rho$ = Density of water (kg/m³)
> - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

#### 2.3.2 Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) — Gravity Type

- **Filtration Rate:** **5000 to 15000 L/m²/hour** (i.e., **120 to 360 m³/m²/day**).
- **Filter Media:** Graded sand + supporting gravel layers.
- **Cleaning:** **Backwashing** with water (and optionally air scour) — takes **10 to 15 minutes**.
- **Application:** All modern municipal WTPs with capacity > 5 MLD.

#### 2.3.3 Pressure Filter

- The filtration occurs inside a **closed, pressurized cylindrical vessel**.
- Operating Pressure: **2 to 5 bar (200–500 kPa)**.
- **Application:** Industrial water treatment, swimming pools, and small community plants.
- **Advantage:** No need for elevated clear water reservoir — pressurized water can be directly pumped to the distribution system.

#### 2.3.4 Dual-Media and Multi-Media Filters

- **Dual-Media:** Anthracite (top) + Sand (bottom) — exploits **stratification after backwash**.
- **Multi-Media:** Anthracite + Sand + Garnet / Ilmenite — provides **coarse-to-fine filtration** in the direction of flow, increasing dirt-holding capacity by **2–3 times**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Compilation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table — KTU 2024 Module 3 Master Formula Sheet**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) | $v_o = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/hour | **1.0 to 2.5 m/hr** |
| 2 | Detention Time (DT) | $T = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q}$ | hours | **2 to 4 hours** |
| 3 | Weir Loading Rate | $WLR = \dfrac{Q}{L_w}$ | m³/m/day | **<300 m³/m/day** |
| 4 | Filter Flow Rate | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A_{filter}}$ | m³/m²/day | **120–360 (RSF), 2.4–4.8 (SSF)** |
| 5 | Head Loss (Darcy) | $h_f = \dfrac{\mu \cdot L \cdot v}{k \cdot \rho \cdot g}$ | m | **<2.5 m (RSF)** |
| 6 | Backwash Rate | $q_{bw} = \dfrac{Q_{bw}}{A_{filter}}$ | m/min | **0.6 to 1.0 m/min (RSF)** |
| 7 | Backwash Expansion | $E = \dfrac{L_e - L}{L} \times 100$ | % | **20 to 50%** |
| 8 | Filter Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{(T_i - T_o)}{T_i} \times 100$ | % | **>90% (turbidity removal)** |
| 9 | Chlorine Dosage | $Cl_2 = D \times Q$ | kg/day | **1 to 5 mg/L (potable)** |
| 10 | Breakpoint Chlorination | $Cl_2:NH_3 \approx 10:1$ | mass ratio | **At breakpoint** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU LaTeX Notation Rule:** In the markdown table above, the absolute value or division is shown using `\dfrac` and `\cdot` to ensure no conflict with the markdown pipe symbol `|`.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Conventional WTP** at **Aruvikkara, Kerala (KWA)** — serves **>70% of Thiruvananthapuram city** with 87 MLD capacity.
- **Vembanad Lake Packaged Plant** — protects a **Ramsar Wetland site** using decentralized treatment.
- **Singapore's NEWater Project** — uses **RO + UV + UF** tertiary treatment to recycle sewage to potable grade — direct application of Module 3 unit processes.
- **Jal Jeevan Mission (India, 2019–)** — India's flagship program providing **55 litres of potable water per capita per day (LPCD)** to every rural household — relies on the **decentralized WTP types** discussed above.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law) for Discrete Particles

The **fundamental physical principle** behind all types of sedimentation tanks is the balance between **gravitational force (downward)** and **drag force (upward)** acting on a settling particle.

### Step 1: Identify the Forces Acting on a Spherical Particle

A spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_s$ settling in a fluid of density $\rho_w$ experiences three forces:

- **Gravitational Force (Weight):** $F_g = m \cdot g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3 \cdot \rho_s \cdot g$

- **Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Upthrust):** $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$

- **Drag Force (Stokes' Regime, Re < 0.5):** $F_d = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s$

### Step 2: Apply the Equilibrium Condition

At terminal settling velocity, the net downward force equals the drag force:

$$F_g - F_b = F_d$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3 \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s$$

### Step 3: Simplify Algebraically

Divide both sides by $3 \pi \cdot d$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g}{18} = \mu \cdot v_s$$

Solve for $v_s$:

$$v_s = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

### Step 4: Final Stokes' Law Equation

$$\boxed{v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu}}$$

where:
- $v_s$ = Terminal settling velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $d$ = Particle diameter (m)
- $\rho_s$ = Particle density (kg/m³)
- $\rho_w$ = Water density = **1000 kg/m³** at 4°C
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of water = **1.002 × 10⁻³ N·s/m²** at 20°C

### Step 5: Validity Range — Reynolds Number Constraint

Stokes' Law is valid **only** for **laminar flow** around the particle, i.e., **Reynolds Number < 0.5** (and ideally < 0.2):

$$Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot v_s \cdot d}{\mu} < 0.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip — "Stokes vs. Newton Zones":**
> - **Re < 0.5** → **Stokes' Law** (laminar settling — fine particles, flocs).
> - **0.5 < Re < 1000** → **Transition Zone** (use empirical Hazen or Camp's formula).
> - **Re > 1000** → **Newton's Law** (turbulent settling — coarse sand).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Removal Efficiency of an Ideal Sedimentation Tank

### Step 1: Define the Geometry

Consider a **horizontal-flow rectangular sedimentation tank** of:
- Length = $L$ (m), along the direction of flow
- Depth (water column height) = $H$ (m), perpendicular to flow
- Width = $B$ (m)
- Flow rate through tank = $Q$ (m³/s)

### Step 2: Express the Mean Horizontal Velocity

$$v_h = \dfrac{Q}{B \cdot H}$$

### Step 3: Express the Theoretical Detention Time

$$T = \dfrac{L}{v_h} = \dfrac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q}$$

### Step 4: Express the Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)

The critical settling velocity needed for 100% removal of a particle entering at the top surface is:

$$v_o = \dfrac{H}{T} = \dfrac{Q}{B \cdot L} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{surface}}$$

### Step 5: Derive the Removal Fraction

For a particle settling at velocity $v_s$ in a tank with overflow rate $v_o$:

- If $v_s \geq v_o$ → **100% removal** (particle settles before reaching outlet).
- If $v_s < v_o$ → **Partial removal**, fraction = $v_s / v_o$.

The **total fraction removed** in an ideal (plug-flow) tank is:

$$\boxed{\eta_{removal} = \dfrac{v_s}{v_o} = \dfrac{v_s \cdot A_{surface}}{Q} = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{v_s}{v_o}\right)^n}$$

where $n$ = number of tanks in series. For $n = 1$ (single tank):

$$\eta = \dfrac{v_s}{v_o}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**SURFACE AREA, not Volume, governs sedimentation efficiency.**" This is the **Hazen's Ideal Sedimentation Tank Theory (1904)** — universally tested in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Standard)

**Problem [KTU 2022 Model — Adapted]:**
A water treatment plant treats **50 MLD** of water. The sedimentation tank is **rectangular**, with:
- Length = **30 m**
- Width = **10 m**
- Depth = **4 m**

Calculate:
1. **Detention Time (in hours)**
2. **Surface Overflow Rate (in m³/m²/day)**
3. **Weir Loading Rate** (assuming a single effluent weir of length **25 m**)

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 50 \text{ MLD} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day} = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/day} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{24 \times 3600} = 0.5787 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$

#### **Part (a): Detention Time**

$$T = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{L \times B \times H}{Q}$$

$$V = 30 \times 10 \times 4 = 1200 \text{ m}^3$$

$$T = \dfrac{1200 \text{ m}^3}{0.5787 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}} = 2073.6 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T = \dfrac{2073.6}{3600} = 0.576 \text{ hours} \approx \mathbf{34.6 \text{ minutes}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This detention time is TOO LOW!** KTU design standard is 2–4 hours. The student must suggest a **wider or deeper tank**.

#### **Part (b): Surface Overflow Rate**

$$\text{SOR} = v_o = \dfrac{Q}{A_{surface}} = \dfrac{Q}{L \times B}$$

$$\text{SOR} = \dfrac{50{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}}{30 \times 10 \text{ m}^2} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{300} = \mathbf{166.67 \text{ m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{/day}}$$

$$v_o = \dfrac{166.67}{24} = 6.94 \text{ m/hr} \quad (\text{Also too high — KTU limit: 1.0 to 2.5 m/hr})$$

#### **Part (c): Weir Loading Rate**

$$\text{WLR} = \dfrac{Q}{L_w} = \dfrac{50{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}}{25 \text{ m}} = \mathbf{2000 \text{ m}^3\text{/m/day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Design Standard:** WLR must be **< 300 m³/m/day**. The current design violates this. Recommendation: increase weir length to **>167 m** (use V-notch weirs along the entire length).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SSF vs. RSF (Guaranteed KTU 14-Mark Question)

| Parameter | Slow Sand Filter (SSF) | Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Filtration Rate** | 2.4–4.8 m³/m²/day | 120–360 m³/m²/day |
| **Filter Media** | Single sand (0.15–0.35 mm) | Graded sand (0.45–0.7 mm) |
| **Bed Depth** | 0.9–1.2 m | 0.6–0.9 m |
| **Cleaning Method** | Scraping top layer | Backwashing with water + air |
| **Cleaning Frequency** | 1–3 months | 24–72 hours |
| **Schmutzdecke** | **Yes (critical)** | No |
| **Pre-treatment Required** | None (raw water directly) | Coagulation + Sedimentation |
| **Bacterial Removal** | >99% (via Schmutzdecke) | 90–99% (mechanical) |
| **Area Required** | Very large | Compact |
| **Suitability for Kerala** | **Excellent (Tropical climate boosts biological activity)** | Good (common in all WTP) |
| **Cost per MLD** | Low (capital) but high (land) | High (capital) but low (land) |
| **KTU Year Typically Asked** | 2019, 2021, 2023 | 2018, 2020, 2022, 2024 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Insight — Kerala-Specific Application:**
> Slow Sand Filters are highly suitable for **Kerala's tropical climate** because the elevated temperature (**25–32°C**) promotes the growth of the **Schmutzdecke biological layer**, which enhances pathogen removal. KTU questions often frame SSF vs. RSF within Kerala-specific rural water supply contexts (e.g., **Kudumbashree-managed community filters in Wayanad and Palakkad**).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Differentiate between Slow Sand Filter (SSF) and Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) in terms of filtration rate and cleaning method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | SSF | RSF |
|---|---|---|
| **Filtration Rate** | 100–200 L/m²/hr (2.4–4.8 m³/m²/day) | 5000–15000 L/m²/hr (120–360 m³/m²/day) |
| **Cleaning Method** | Manual scraping of top 1–2 cm sand (Schmutzdecke) | Backwashing with water + air scour (10–15 min cycle) |
| **Cleaning Frequency** | Once in 1–3 months | Once in 24–72 hours |

**[1 mark each — 3 marks total]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Define Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) and state its typical design value for a municipal sedimentation tank.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition [1 mark]:** SOR is the volumetric flow rate of water per unit surface area of the sedimentation tank, expressed as $v_o = Q/A$. It is numerically equal to the terminal settling velocity of the smallest particle that is 100% removed.
- **Typical Design Value [1 mark]:** **1.0 to 2.5 m³/m²/hr** (i.e., 24 to 60 m³/m²/day).
- **KTU Standard Reference [1 mark]:** As per **CPHEEO Manual, 1999**, SOR should not exceed **30 m³/m²/day** for plain sedimentation and **40 m³/m²/day** for coagulated sedimentation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Pitfall — Internal Choice:**
> KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) mandates that for every 14-mark Module question, **two sub-choices (A and B)** are provided from the SAME module. The student must answer **either A or B in entirety**. Do NOT attempt both.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3 Choice A]`

**(a) Classify the different types of water treatment plants based on the scale of operation. Explain the Conventional Municipal WTP in detail with a labelled flow diagram.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Classification of WTPs based on Scale [3 marks]:**

1. **Municipal / Conventional WTP** (capacity > 5 MLD; serves large populations).
2. **Package / Compact WTP** (capacity 0.5 to 50 MLD; skid-mounted, prefabricated).
3. **Decentralized Community WTP** (Slow Sand Filters, Bio-sand filters for rural areas).
4. **Household Water Treatment Systems** (BO, UV, RO, activated carbon, ceramic candle filters).

**Conventional Municipal WTP — Flow Sequence [4 marks]:**

1. **Inlet Works & Screening** — Removes large debris (sticks, rags, fish). Bar spacing 25–100 mm.
2. **Aeration** — Removes dissolved gases (CO₂, H₂S), oxidizes Fe²⁺ and Mn²⁺.
3. **Flash Mixer (Coagulation)** — Rapid mixing for 30–60 sec to disperse coagulant (alum, FeCl₃, PAC).
4. **Flocculator** — Gentle stirring (G = 10 to 75 s⁻¹) for 20–40 min to grow flocs.
5. **Sedimentation Tank** — Gravity settling; SOR = 1.0–2.5 m/hr; DT = 2–4 hours.
6. **Filtration** — Rapid Sand Filter (rate 120–360 m³/m²/day) or Slow Sand Filter (2.4–4.8 m³/m²/day).
7. **Disinfection** — Chlorination (1–5 mg/L dose, 30 min contact time) or UV (40 mJ/cm²).
8. **Clear Water Reservoir (CWR)** — Storage for 6–12 hours of demand before distribution.

**(b) A municipal WTP has a flow rate of 100 MLD. Design a rectangular sedimentation tank and determine the Detention Time and Surface Overflow Rate, given the following data: tank length = 40 m, width = 12 m, depth = 4.5 m.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Convert Units [1 mark]**
$$Q = 100 \text{ MLD} = 100{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/day} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{24 \times 3600} = 1.157 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Tank Volume [1 mark]**
$$V = L \times B \times H = 40 \times 12 \times 4.5 = 2160 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Detention Time [1 mark]**
$$T = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{2160}{1.157} = 1866 \text{ sec} = 0.5185 \text{ hours} \approx 31.1 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4: Verify Against KTU Standards [1 mark]**
> [!WARNING]
> **Detention Time = 31 minutes is BELOW the KTU design standard of 2–4 hours.** This indicates the tank is undersized.

**Step 5: Recalculate for Compliant Design [2 marks]**
For DT = 3 hours, Required Volume = $Q \times T = 1.157 \times 3 \times 3600 = 12{,}499 \text{ m}^3$.

**Step 6: Calculate SOR [1 mark]**
$$\text{SOR} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{100{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}}{40 \times 12 \text{ m}^2} = 208.3 \text{ m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{/day}$$

$$\text{SOR} = \dfrac{208.3}{24} = 8.68 \text{ m/hr}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **SOR = 8.68 m/hr EXCEEDS the KTU limit of 2.5 m/hr.** Suggestion: Increase the surface area to **>1667 m²** (e.g., 50 m × 35 m).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3 Choice B]`

**(a) Explain the working principle, design criteria, and applications of a Solid Contact Clarifier (Accelator). Compare it with a conventional horizontal-flow rectangular sedimentation tank.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Solid Contact Clarifier (Accelator) [4 marks]:**

- **Working Principle:** Combines **coagulation, flocculation, and sedimentation in a single circular tank.** Raw water enters the central reaction well where coagulant is added. The water moves upward through a **sludge blanket** (pre-formed floc layer), where flocculation is enhanced by contact with the suspended sludge. The clarified water overflows into peripheral launders.
- **Design Criteria:**
  - **Surface Overflow Rate:** 1.5 to 3.0 m/hr.
  - **Detention Time:** 1 to 1.5 hours (much lower than conventional).
  - **Sludge Blanket Depth:** 1.5 to 3 m maintained by periodic blow-down.
- **Applications:** High-turbidity raw water (monsoon-fed rivers), softening plants, and industrial water treatment.

**Comparison with Conventional Rectangular Tank [3 marks]:**

| Feature | Solid Contact Clarifier | Rectangular Tank |
|---|---|---|
| Unit processes combined | Yes (3-in-1) | No (separate coagulator, flocculator, settler) |
| Footprint | Very compact | Large |
| Sludge handling | Internal recirculation | External sludge collection |
| Capital cost | Higher | Lower |
| Suitability for Kerala | Excellent (monsoon turbidity) | Good |

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Slow Sand Filter. Discuss its suitability for Kerala's rural water supply scenario.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Construction [3 marks]:**

A typical SSF has the following layered structure (top to bottom):
1. **Supernatant water layer (1–1.5 m)** — raw water to be filtered.
2. **Schmutzdecke layer (2–3 cm)** — biological layer of algae, bacteria, protozoa, and organic matter — **the actual purification zone**.
3. **Filter sand bed (0.9–1.2 m)** — effective size 0.15–0.35 mm, uniformity coefficient 2–5.
4. **Supporting gravel (0.3–0.5 m)** — graded layers 2–60 mm.
5. **Underdrain system** — collects filtered water.
6. **Controller valve** — regulates outflow to maintain constant head.

**Working [2 marks]:**
Raw water is admitted to the supernatant layer. It percolates slowly downward by gravity. The **Schmutzdecke layer removes 99% of bacteria, 100% of suspended solids, and reduces turbidity below 1 NTU** through **biological predation, mechanical straining, and adsorption**. Filtered water is collected in the underdrain.

**Cleaning [1 mark]:**
When head loss reaches 0.6–0.9 m (or after 1–3 months), the top 1–2 cm of sand is manually scraped, washed, and stored for later re-use.

**Suitability for Kerala [1 mark]:**
> [!TIP]
> **Kerala's tropical climate (25–32°C) accelerates Schmutzdecke development** (within 2–3 weeks vs. 4–6 weeks in temperate climates). The state has **>2500 SSFs** under the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)**, particularly in **Wayanad, Idukki, and Palakkad** districts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Module 3 "Types" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units** — SOR is often reported as m³/m²/day OR m/hr. KTU expects consistency. Always state BOTH.
> 2. **Confusing SSF and RSF cleaning mechanisms** — SSF uses **scraping**, NOT backwashing.
> 3. **Ignoring Schmutzdecke** — 30% of marks in any filter question go to the **biological layer mechanism**; omitting it is a guaranteed -3 marks.
> 4. **Wrong standard citation** — KTU uses **CPHEEO Manual (1999)** and **IS 10500:2012**, NOT AWWA or US-EPA standards.
> 5. **Numerical errors in detention time** — Always convert MLD to m³/s using the divisor **86,400** (not 86,400 ÷ 1000).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 3 "Types" — Last-Minute Revision Bullets**

- ⭐ **Conventional WTP sequence:** Screening → Aeration → Coagulation → Flocculation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Disinfection.
- ⭐ **SOR Formula:** $v_o = Q/A$ (m/hr); **KTU design value: 1.0–2.5 m/hr.**
- ⭐ **Detention Time (Sedimentation):** **2 to 4 hours**; if calculated value < 2 hr, design is non-compliant.
- ⭐ **Weir Loading Rate:** **< 300 m³/m/day** — use V-notch weirs to extend weir length.
- ⭐ **SSF rate:** 2.4–4.8 m³/m²/day; **RSF rate:** 120–360 m³/m²/day.
- ⭐ **Schmutzdecke = biological layer = active purification zone in SSF.**
- ⭐ **Darcy's Law:** $h_f = \dfrac{\mu \cdot L \cdot v}{k \cdot \rho \cdot g}$; head loss limit in RSF = **2.5 m**.
- ⭐ **Backwash rate for RSF:** **0.6 to 1.0 m/min**; bed expansion 20–50%.
- ⭐ **Package WTP capacity:** **0.5 to 50 MLD**; installation in 3–6 months.
- ⭐ **Solid Contact Clarifier (Accelator):** combines coagulation + flocculation + sedimentation — high-yield KTU topic.
- ⭐ **Stokes' Law validity:** Re < 0.5; for flocs, use **modified Stokes** with $\rho_s \approx 1050$ kg/m³.
- ⭐ **Kerala-specific:** SSFs are highly suitable due to tropical climate; **Jal Jeevan Mission** drives decentralized WTP deployment.
- ⭐ **Reference Standards:** **IS 10500:2012** (drinking water), **CPHEEO Manual 1999** (design), **WHO Guidelines 4th Ed. 2017** (global benchmark).
- ⭐ **Chlorine dose:** **1 to 5 mg/L** for potable water; **breakpoint ratio** Cl₂:NH₃ ≈ **10:1** by mass.
- ⭐ **Hazen's Theorem:** "**Surface area, not volume, governs sedimentation efficiency.**"

---
