<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sewer Construction and Operation** refers to the systematic engineering practices involved in the physical installation, structural integrity verification, functional testing, and post-commissioning maintenance of a sewerage system. This encompasses the laying of sewer pipes at precise gradients, the formation of watertight joints, hydraulic testing for leakage, and the routine operational protocols (including cleaning, inspection, and safety) required to ensure the sewer network functions continuously without structural failure, blockage, or environmental contamination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** In KTU 2024 scheme examinations, this topic is frequently combined with **Sewer Appurtenances** and **Testing of Sewers** to form a 14-mark Part B question. Students are expected to know exact IS code test pressures, permissible leakage limits, and the step-by-step procedure of the hydrostatic test.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are installing a long, invisible underground highway for wastewater. Just like a real highway needs proper road base, smooth gradients (so trucks don't stall going uphill or speed dangerously downhill), strong connections between slabs (expansion joints), and regular traffic police (maintenance crews), a sewer needs:

- **Correct bedding & gradient** → so sewage flows by gravity without stagnation
- **Watertight joints** → so dirty water doesn't leak out and contaminate groundwater (or vice versa, where groundwater infiltrates and overloads treatment plants)
- **Pressure testing** → like pressure-testing a new car radiator before shipping, to ensure no leaks
- **Routine cleaning** → like a chimney sweep, because solids inevitably deposit over time

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Pipe Materials:** Stoneware (SW), Cement Concrete (CC), Cast Iron (CI), PVC, RCC, HDPE
- **Standard Hydraulic Test Pressure:** **2.5 m head of water** above the crown of the pipe at the highest point (or up to the top of the shaft, whichever is less) for stoneware sewers
- **Permissible Leakage in Hydrostatic Test:** **Not exceeding 1 litre per metre length per hour** for stoneware pipes (as per relevant IS codes)
- **Minimum Velocity of Flow:** **0.6 m/s** (self-cleansing velocity for combined sewers)
- **Maximum Velocity of Flow:** **3.0 m/s** (to prevent erosion of pipe material)
- **Minimum Cover Over Sewer:** **1.0 m** in non-traffic areas; up to **1.5 m** in traffic areas

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0.6 m/s?** This is the *self-cleansing velocity*. Below this, suspended solids settle down and form deposits. Above 3.0 m/s, the abrasive action of grit damages the inner surface of the pipe.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction Procedure of a Sewer

### Phase 1: Trench Excavation & Bedding Preparation
1. **Alignment & Centerline Marking:** The proposed centerline of the sewer is marked on the ground using theodolite and ranging rods.
2. **Setting Out Sight Rails (Boning Rods):** Horizontal rails are fixed at a known elevation (usually 1 m above the proposed invert level) at intervals of 30 m.
3. **Excavation:** Trench depth = invert level + pipe dia + bedding thickness. Minimum trench width = pipe OD + 0.3 m on each side.
4. **Bedding:** A bed of sand, gravel, or concrete is prepared to give uniform support. Bedding factor = (load on pipe with bedding) / (load on pipe without bedding). Higher the factor, **better the support** → **lower the stress** in pipe walls.

### Phase 2: Pipe Laying, Jointing & Testing
1. **Lowering of Pipes:** Pipes are lowered into the trench using ropes, chains, or mechanical cranes. They are *never dropped*.
2. **Jointing:**
   - **Socket & Spigot (Stoneware/CI):** Filled with jute/rope (gasket) + cement mortar (1:1) or bitumen.
   - **Collar Joint (CC):** A concrete collar encircles the joint and is packed with cement mortar.
   - **Flexible Joint (RCC/PVC):** Rubber gaskets are used to absorb ground movement.
3. **Hydraulic Testing:** Section is filled with water under specified pressure for a specified duration, and leakage is measured.

### Phase 3: Backfilling
1. After successful testing, the trench is backfilled in layers (15–20 cm thick) and compacted.
2. **No large stones or organic matter** should be in the backfill material within 30 cm of the pipe.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Sequence

- **Why boning rods?** To transfer the design gradient accurately from the sight rail to the trench bottom, regardless of trench depth.
- **Why a flexible joint in RCC?** Rigid joints crack due to soil settlement, traffic vibration, or thermal expansion.
- **Why hydrostatic test *before* backfilling?** Because exposed joints allow visual detection of leak points, and if leakage exceeds limits, the defective pipe/joint can be replaced without re-excavation.

## 2.3 Types of Sewer Joints (Concept Map)

| Joint Type | Pipe Used | Material for Joint | Key Property |
|------------|-----------|-------------------|--------------|
| Socket & Spigot (Rigid) | Stoneware, Cast Iron | Cement mortar (1:1) + hemp yarn | Strong but brittle; cannot take movement |
| Collar Joint | Plain Cement Concrete | Cement mortar (1:1) in collar groove | Easy to repair; weak in tension |
| Flexible Mechanical Joint | RCC, PVC, HDPE | Rubber gasket (e.g., SBR, neoprene) | Absorbs settlement, vibration, thermal expansion |
| Ball & Socket Joint | CI, DI | Rubber gasket | Allows angular deflection up to 15° |
| Simplex Joint | Stoneware | Bitumen/Sulfur compound | Chemical-resistant; used in industrial sewers |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values, magnitude bars, and modulus operators below use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Bedding Factor | $$B = \frac{W}{W_1}$$ | $W$ = load on pipe without bedding, $W_1$ = load with bedding. Higher B = better support |
| Manning's Velocity | $$V = \frac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$ | Used to back-calculate required gradient for a given discharge |
| Permissible Head Loss | $$h_{allow} = \frac{L \cdot q_{perm}}{1000}$$ | $L$ = length of test section (m), $q_{perm}$ = allowable leakage (1 L/m/hr) |
| Actual Head Loss | $$\Delta h = h_{initial} - h_{final}$$ | Measured directly from the test gauge during hydrostatic test |
| Sewer Slope from Sight Rails | $$S = \frac{d}{D}$$ | Where $d$ = difference in boning rod readings, $D$ = distance between sight rails |
| Quantity of Excavation | $$V_{exc} = L \cdot B \cdot H$$ | $L$ = length, $B$ = average trench width, $H$ = average depth |
| Self-Cleansing Velocity (Shield's Formula) | $$V_s = \frac{1}{n} \cdot R^{1/6} \cdot \sqrt{k \cdot (G-1) \cdot d}$$ | For a given particle size $d$ to remain in motion |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Municipal Engineering:** Every city corporation (e.g., Kochi Corporation, Greater Chennai Corporation) follows the IS 5329 / IS 458 / IS 15328 codes for sewer laying, making this knowledge directly applicable to public works tenders.
- **Site Engineers:** Junior engineers must supervise bedding, jointing, and testing before backfilling — a single failed joint can contaminate an entire aquifer.
- **Design Stage Impact:** Proper construction ensures the design life of **50–100 years** for RCC sewers is actually achieved; poor construction halves the service life.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Permissible Leakage in Hydrostatic Test (with Worked Numerical)

### Given (Typical KTU Numerical)
A newly laid stoneware sewer of length **150 m** and diameter **300 mm** is subjected to a hydrostatic test. The test pressure at the highest point is maintained at **2.5 m head of water** for a duration of **2 hours**.

Initial water added: 200 litres.  
Water added at the end of 2 hours to restore the level: **90 litres**.  

**Determine:**
1. Permissible leakage as per IS code.
2. Actual leakage observed.
3. Whether the sewer **passes** or **fails** the test.

### Step 1: Permissible Leakage (Per IS Code Limit)

As per IS 5329, for stoneware sewers, the permissible leakage is taken as **1 litre per metre length per hour**.

$$
q_{perm} = 1 \; \frac{L}{m \cdot hr}
$$

For the given section of length $L = 150$ m over test duration $t = 2$ hr, the total allowable leakage is:

$$
Q_{perm} = q_{perm} \times L \times t
$$

$$
Q_{perm} = 1 \times 150 \times 2 = 300 \; \text{litres}
$$

> **Logic Step:** We multiply the per-metre, per-hour rate by total length and total time to scale the limit to the specific test conditions.

### Step 2: Actual Leakage Observed

The actual leakage is the water that had to be **added during the test** to maintain the original head, accounting for any temperature-driven volume changes (usually neglected at short durations).

$$
Q_{actual} = 200 + 90 = 290 \; \text{litres}
$$

> **Logic Step:** Even though 200 litres were added initially to fill the pipe + losses, the 90 litres added at the 2-hour mark represents the sustained leakage rate over the test period.

### Step 3: Compare and Conclude

$$
Q_{actual} = 290 \; L \quad \text{vs} \quad Q_{perm} = 300 \; L
$$

Since:

$$
Q_{actual} \; (290) \; < \; Q_{perm} \; (300)
$$

> **Conclusion:** The sewer **PASSES** the hydrostatic test. 

### Additional Insight: Equivalent Permeability Coefficient

If the design is to compute the equivalent soil permeability (for geotechnical reporting), one can write:

$$
k_{eq} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{A_{wet} \cdot t}
$$

Where $A_{wet}$ is the wet surface area of the pipe. For a 300 mm diameter pipe of 150 m length:

$$
A_{wet} = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.3 \cdot 150 = 141.37 \; m^2
$$

$$
k_{eq} = \frac{290 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3}{141.37 \; m^2 \times 2 \; hr} \approx 1.026 \times 10^{-3} \; \frac{m}{hr}
$$

> **Conversion Note:** $290$ litres = $0.290$ m³. The denominator uses $A_{wet}$ in m² and time in hours, hence the rate comes out in m/hr.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient Calculation from Sight Rails (KTU Favourite)

### Setup
- Sight rail at Station 1 is at level **52.40 m**.
- Sight rail at Station 2 is at level **51.80 m**.
- Distance between stations = **25 m**.
- Boning rod (sight) length = **3.00 m**.
- Required *designed* invert level at Station 1 = **49.50 m**.

### Step 1: Find the Level of the Trench Bottom at Station 1

The boning rod, when held vertically on the trench bottom, must equal the sight rail level minus the boning rod length. So:

$$
\text{Trench bottom level} = \text{Sight rail level} - \text{Boning rod length}
$$

$$
L_{1,trench} = 52.40 - 3.00 = 49.40 \; m
$$

But the *designed* invert is **49.50 m**. So the bottom must be **excavated 0.10 m deeper** to match design.

### Step 2: Calculate the Actual Gradient

$$
S = \frac{\Delta \text{Level}}{\text{Distance}} = \frac{52.40 - 51.80}{25}
$$

$$
S = \frac{0.60}{25} = 0.024 \;\; (\text{or } 1 \text{ in } 41.67)
$$

### Step 3: Determine the Fall Over the 25 m Reach

$$
\text{Fall} = S \times L = 0.024 \times 25 = 0.60 \; m
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The invert at Station 2 must be **0.60 m below** the invert at Station 1 to maintain gravity flow.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Problem Type | Key Inputs | Output Required | Governing Equation |
|--------------|------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Hydrostatic Test Pass/Fail | $L, t, V_{added}$ | $Q_{perm}$ vs $Q_{actual}$ | $Q = q \times L \times t$ |
| Gradient from Sight Rails | Sight rail levels, boning rod length | Trench bottom level, slope $S$ | $S = \Delta H / D$ |
| Bedding Factor Analysis | Load with/without bedding | Bedding factor $B$ | $B = W / W_1$ |
| Excavation Quantity | $L, B, H$ of trench | Volume in m³ | $V = L \cdot B \cdot H$ |
| Manning's n for Sewer Material | $V_{req}, R, S$ | Minimum $n$ required | $n = R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2} / V$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sewer Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SITE SURVEY AND DESIGN VERIFICATION"] --> B["CENTERLINE MARKING WITH THEODOLITE"]
    B --> C["SIGHT RAIL INSTALLATION AT 30 M INTERVALS"]
    C --> D["TRENCH EXCAVATION TO DESIGN DEPTH"]
    D --> E["BEDDING PREPARATION SAND OR CONCRETE"]
    E --> F["PIPE LOWERING USING ROPES OR CRANES"]
    F --> G["ALIGNMENT CHECKING BY BONING RODS"]
    G --> H{"JOINT TYPE SELECTION"}
    H --> I["SOCKET SPIGOT WITH CEMENT MORTAR"]
    H --> J["COLLAR JOINT FOR CC PIPES"]
    H --> K["FLEXIBLE RUBBER GASKET FOR RCC OR PVC"]
    I --> L["HYDROSTATIC TEST FOR LEAKAGE"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{"LEAKAGE WITHIN PERMISSIBLE LIMIT"}
    M --> N["BACKFILL IN LAYERS AND COMPACT"]
    M --> O["REPAIR OR REPLACE DEFECTIVE JOINT"]
    O --> L
    N --> P["FINAL CCTV INSPECTION AND HANDOVER"]
    P --> Q["OPERATION MAINTENANCE AND ROUTINE CLEANING"]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each rectangular block is a distinct construction phase. The diamond shapes are decision points. The feedback loop (O → L) signifies *retesting after repair* — a critical concept emphasized in KTU valuation.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Hydrostatic Test Setup (Field Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WATER TANK ELEVATED SOURCE"] --> B["INLET PIPE WITH VALVE"]
    B --> C["TEST SECTION OF SEWER"]
    C --> D["END PLUG OR BLANK FLANGE"]
    D --> E["PRESSURE GAUGE OR STANDPIPE"]
    E --> F{"HEAD STABLE FOR 2 HOURS"}
    F --> G["MEASURE WATER ADDED LEAKAGE"]
    G --> H{"QACTUAL LESS THAN QPERMISSIBLE"}
    H --> I["SEWER CERTIFIED PASSED"]
    H --> J["DEFECTIVE JOINTS REPAIRED AND RETESTED"]
```

> **Physical Interpretation:** In the field, a vertical standpipe (open at top) is connected to the sealed test section. Water rises in the standpipe to give the required 2.5 m head. The standpipe is calibrated to read head loss directly in mm.

## 4.3 Mermaid Cross-Section: Trench, Bedding, Pipe & Backfill

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1["GROUND SURFACE"] --> T2["TRENCH WALL SLOPE 1 TO 1 SAFE"]
    T2 --> T3["BACKFILL LAYERS 15 TO 20 CM COMPACTED"]
    T3 --> T4["PIPE BARREL SW OR CC OR RCC"]
    T4 --> T5["BEDDING SAND OR CONCRETE 10 TO 15 CM"]
    T5 --> T6["TRENCH BOTTOM UNDISTURBED EARTH"]
    T6 --> T7["HAUNCH ZONE COMPACTED FIRST"]
```

> **Construction Note:** The haunch zone (the area from the trench bottom up to the pipe's centerline) must be compacted **first and most carefully** because it directly supports the pipe against vertical load.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "self-cleansing velocity" as applied to sewer design. State its typical value for a combined sewer.**

**Model Answer:**
Self-cleansing velocity is the minimum velocity of flow required in a sewer to prevent the deposition of suspended solids (organic and inorganic) on the pipe invert. If flow velocity falls below this value, grit and organic matter settle, leading to blockages, anaerobic conditions, and reduced hydraulic capacity.  
For a combined sewer, the typical value is **0.6 m/s**, while for a separate sanitary sewer it is **0.75 m/s** (higher because sewage is more concentrated in dry weather flow).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three methods of testing newly laid sewers and state the principle of the hydrostatic test.**

**Model Answer:**
The three methods of testing newly laid sewers are:
1. **Hydrostatic test (water test)** — most common for gravity sewers
2. **Air test (pneumatic test)** — used when water is scarce
3. **Smoke test** — used to detect individual leak points in existing sewers

**Principle of Hydrostatic Test:** The test section of the sewer is sealed at both ends, filled with water, and pressurized to a specified head (typically **2.5 m** above the crown at the highest point or top of the manhole shaft, whichever is less). This head is maintained for a specified period (usually 2 hours), and the volume of water that must be added to maintain the head is measured. This added volume represents the leakage, which is then compared with the permissible limit per IS code.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) Describe, with a neat sketch, the procedure of laying a stoneware sewer in a trench. What are the different types of joints used in stoneware sewers? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Trench Preparation (2 marks):**
- Trench width = outer diameter of pipe + 0.6 m (0.3 m on each side for working space)
- Trench depth = invert level + pipe diameter + bedding thickness
- Trench sides are kept vertical up to 1.5 m depth; beyond that, shoring is provided to prevent collapse
- Trench bottom is dressed true to gradient using boning rods and sight rails

**2. Bedding (1 mark):**
- A 10–15 cm thick bed of sand, moorum, or lean concrete is provided
- Bedding distributes the load uniformly and prevents point stresses on the pipe

**3. Pipe Laying (2 marks):**
- Pipes are lowered using ropes or chain pulley blocks — never dropped
- The socket end of each pipe is kept facing upstream (against the flow direction)
- Each pipe is checked for alignment using boning rod and a plumb bob

**4. Jointing (2 marks):**
- **Socket & Spigot rigid joint:** The spigot is inserted into the socket, gasket of tarred hemp yarn is caulked first, then the remaining space is filled with cement mortar (1:1) and finished with a fillet at an angle of 45°
- **Simplex joint (flexible):** Uses a rubber ring and a special bitumen or sulfur compound — used where chemical resistance is needed
- **Cement concrete collar joint:** Used to join two plain-ended stoneware pipes

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (–1 mark):** Students often write the mortar mix as 1:2 or 1:3. The **correct KTU board answer is 1:1** for sewer joints (rich mix) because sewage is corrosive and joint strength is critical.

---

**(b) Explain the hydrostatic test procedure for a newly laid sewer. A 120 m long stoneware sewer is tested for 2 hours. The water added to maintain the test head was 250 litres. Determine whether the sewer passes the test as per IS code. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hydrostatic Test Procedure (4 marks):**
1. The sewer section under test is cleaned of all dirt, debris, and foreign material
2. The lower end of the section is plugged with a suitable end plug or a brick masonry plug with a small opening for the inlet pipe
3. The upper end is also plugged similarly, with a standpipe or air vent
4. Water is filled slowly from an elevated tank, allowing air to escape through the vent
5. The test head of **2.5 m of water** is built up and maintained for **2 hours**
6. The quantity of water added during the test to maintain the head is measured
7. The test is conducted *before* backfilling so that defective joints can be visually identified and repaired

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given: $L = 120$ m, $t = 2$ hr, $Q_{actual} = 250$ L

Permissible leakage per IS code = **1 L per metre per hour**

$$
Q_{perm} = 1 \times 120 \times 2 = 240 \; \text{litres}
$$

$$
Q_{actual} = 250 \; L > Q_{perm} = 240 \; L
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $Q_{actual} > Q_{perm}$, the sewer **FAILS** the hydrostatic test. The defective joints must be identified, repaired, and the section retested.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Discuss the operation and maintenance aspects of a sewerage system. What safety precautions must be observed by workers entering a sewer manhole? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Routine Operation (3 marks):**
- **Flow Monitoring:** Periodic measurement of flow depth at key manholes using depth gauges
- **Ventilation:** Ensuring all manholes are properly covered and vent shafts are unobstructed
- **Flushing:** Periodic flushing of sewers with water to remove early-stage deposits
- **Pumping Stations:** Monitoring pump operation, sump levels, and power backup

**2. Maintenance Activities (2 marks):**
- **Cleaning:** Removal of deposits using mechanical or hydraulic methods
  - *Manual cleaning* with buckets and scrapers (now discouraged due to safety)
  - *Mechanical cleaning* using bucket machines, rodding machines, or hydraulic cleaners
  - *Hydraulic cleaning* using sewer balls, sewer scooters, or high-pressure water jetting
- **Inspection:** Routine CCTV surveys every 2–5 years; visual inspection from manholes
- **Repair:** Joint repairs, replacement of damaged sections, relining with cured-in-place pipe (CIPP)

**3. Safety Precautions (2 marks):**
- Never enter a manhole without a **permit-to-work** system
- Test the atmosphere for **oxygen deficiency** and presence of **H₂S, CH₄, CO** using a gas detector (4-gas monitor)
- Use **safety harness** with a lifeline held by an attendant at the top
- Provide **forced ventilation** using a blower before and during entry
- **No smoking or open flame** near manholes
- At least **two workers minimum** — one inside, one outside as standby
- Use **tripod and winch** for emergency retrieval
- Wear **non-slip boots, hard hat, gas mask** as personal protective equipment (PPE)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (–2 marks):** Many students write about safety *during construction* instead of *during maintenance entry*. Read the question carefully — it asks about safety for **workers entering a manhole for maintenance**, not construction workers laying the pipe.

---

**(b) Explain the following with neat sketches: (i) Sight rail and boning rod, (ii) Collar joint, (iii) Flexible joint. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sight Rail and Boning Rod (2.5 marks):**
A sight rail is a horizontal wooden or metal rail fixed to strong vertical posts (ranging rods) driven into the ground on either side of the trench at intervals of **30 m**. The top of the sight rail is set at a known elevation (usually 1 m above the designed invert level). A boning rod is a T-shaped rod of length equal to the vertical distance between the sight rail and the trench bottom.

**Working:** The boning rod is held vertically on the trench bottom, and the line of sight from the top of one sight rail to the top of the other sight rail is checked. If the boning rod's top aligns with the line of sight, the trench bottom is at the correct level. If it is above, excavation is insufficient; if below, over-excavation has occurred.

**(ii) Collar Joint (2 marks):**
Used for plain-ended cement concrete pipes. A concrete collar (a short cylindrical ring) is slipped over the joint, covering both pipe ends. The annular space between the collar and the pipes is filled with cement mortar (1:1), and a mortar fillet is provided at both faces. The collar is **200–300 mm** long and typically **20 mm** thick.

**(iii) Flexible Joint (2.5 marks):**
Used for RCC, PVC, and HDPE pipes. A rubber gasket (ring) is placed on the spigot end of the pipe. The spigot is then pushed into the socket of the adjoining pipe, compressing the gasket and creating a watertight seal. The rubber ring can deform to accommodate:
- Ground settlement and vibration
- Thermal expansion and contraction
- Slight angular deflection (up to 1–3° per joint, depending on manufacturer)

This joint does not use cement mortar, so it remains watertight even when the pipe undergoes minor movement.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems (–0.5 mark): Always write "litres", "m/hr", "m/s", "m³" — not just numbers.
> 2. **Confusing 1:1 and 1:2 mortar** for sewer joints (–1 mark): The KTU standard is **1:1** cement mortar for stoneware jointing.
> 3. **Omitting "before backfilling"** in the hydrostatic test description (–1 mark): A test conducted *after* backfilling is meaningless because defects cannot be visually located.
> 4. **Wrong test pressure:** KTU expects **2.5 m head** for stoneware, not "any pressure". For RCC, the test pressure is **0.7 MPa** (pneumatic) or hydrostatic to **flood level + 1 m**.
> 5. **Missing the "self-cleansing velocity" numerical value:** Always state **0.6 m/s for combined** and **0.75 m/s for separate** systems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam):**

- ☐ **Self-cleansing velocity** = **0.6 m/s** (combined), **0.75 m/s** (separate sanitary)
- ☐ **Maximum velocity** = **3.0 m/s** (to prevent erosion of pipe invert)
- ☐ **Minimum cover over sewer** = **1.0 m** (non-traffic), **1.5 m** (traffic area)
- ☐ **Trench width** = pipe OD + **0.6 m** (0.3 m on each side)
- ☐ **Sight rail interval** = every **30 m**
- ☐ **Test head for stoneware sewer** = **2.5 m** above crown (or top of shaft, whichever is less)
- ☐ **Test duration** = **2 hours** (typical)
- ☐ **Permissible leakage** = **1 litre/m/hr** (stoneware, as per IS 5329)
- ☐ **Bedding thickness** = **10–15 cm** of sand or lean concrete
- ☐ **Cement mortar for jointing** = **1:1 ratio** (rich mix)
- ☐ **Backfill layers** = **15–20 cm**, compacted, no large stones within **30 cm** of pipe
- ☐ **Always lower pipes, never drop** them
- ☐ **Test before backfilling** — so defects can be located and repaired
- ☐ **Socket end faces upstream** — this prevents sewage from seeping into the joint under pressure
- ☐ **Safety first in manhole entry**: gas testing, harness, forced ventilation, two-person rule
- ☐ **Cleaning methods**: bucket machines, rodding machines, sewer balls, hydraulic jetting
- ☐ **Key IS Codes**: IS 5329 (laying of sewers), IS 458 (RCC pipes), IS 651 (stoneware pipes), IS 15328 (PVC pipes)

**End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Construction and Operation**

topics.title: Design of Trickling Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design of Trickling Filter # 🏗️ DESIGN OF TRICKLING FILTER — KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDY NOTES ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trickling Filter (TF):** A fixed-film biological wastewater treatment unit in which sewage is distributed over a bed of coarse, porous media (rock, slag, or plastic) and allowed to percolate through the bed. Microorganisms attached to the media as a *biological slime layer* (zoogleal film) metabolize the organic matter in the wastewater, producing a stable effluent and biological sludge. **Engineering Classification (as per KTU PCCET503 – Module 3):** | Class | Hydraulic Loading (m³/m²·d) | Organic Loading (kg BOD/m³·d) | Recirculation | |---|---|---|---| | **Low / Standard Rate TF** | 1 – 4 | 0.08 – 0.32 | None / Low | | **High Rate TF** | 10 – 30 | 0.32 – 1.00 | Yes (2–4 Q) | | **Roughing / Super High Rate** | 30 – 90 | > 1.00 | Yes (High) | | **Two-Stage TF** | Stacked operation | — | Yes | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Trickling"?** Because settled wastewater is *trickled* (sprayed) over the media bed via a rotating distributor arm, allowing the liquid to *trickle* down by gravity while air moves **counter-currently** upward (natural or forced draft) for aerobic oxidation. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Living Rock Garden" 🪨🌱 Imagine a **giant rock garden** stacked with porous stones. You pour nutrient-rich water on top, and it drips down through the rocks. The stones are *coated with moss* (the biological slime). As the water trickles past, the moss *eats* the nutrients, just like a sponge soaks up spilled juice. The cleaned water exits at the bottom, and fresh air naturally flows **upwards** through the empty spaces between the rocks to keep the moss alive and working. > 🧠 **Intuitive Engineering Map:** > - **Rocks** = Filter media (provides surface area for biofilm growth). > - **Moss coating** = Zoogleal / biological film (microorganisms that consume organics). > - **Trickling water** = Distributed wastewater influent. > - **Upward airflow** = Aerobic oxygen supply for BOD oxidation. > - **Spent water at bottom** = Treated effluent + sloughed biofilm → goes to secondary clarifier. This is exactly how a **Trickling Filter** mimics a natural stream-bed ecosystem, but in a controlled, engineered vessel. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Asked) - **Density of standard rock media:** ≈ **1600 – 1700 kg/m³** (≈ 100 lb/ft³). - **Specific surface area of rock media:** ≈ **30 – 60 m²/m³** of bed volume. - **Plastic media specific surface:** ≈ **100 – 200 m²/m³** (much higher → smaller tank). - **Depth of standard rock filter:** **1.8 m to 3.0 m** (typically 1.8 m / 6 ft). - **Depth of plastic media filter:** **4 m to 12 m** (tall towers). - **Distributor rotational speed:** typically **0.5 – 2 rpm** for standard units. - **Rotary distributor tip speed:** **0.3 – 1.0 m/s** (drives rotation via hydraulic head). - **Hydraulic head on distributor:** **0.5 – 1.5 m** above media surface. - **Sloughing cycle:** every **15 – 30 days** (biofilm grows → sloughs off → new growth). - **Effluent BOD removal efficiency:** Low rate: **80 – 90%** | High rate: **65 – 85%** | Roughing: **40 – 65%**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Trickling filters are classified as **attached-growth (biofilm) reactors** and operate on the principle of **aerobic contact between wastewater and microorganisms**, as opposed to the suspended-growth mechanism of the Activated Sludge Process (Module 4). # 📚 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Components of a Trickling Filter (Operational Anatomy) A trickling filter is NOT just a "bed of rocks." It is an engineered system comprising **six integrated sub-systems**: 1. **Filter Media Bed** — Rock, slag, or plastic (synthetic) media of nominal size 25–75 mm. Provides the surface for biofilm attachment. 2. **Distribution System** — Either fixed nozzle/spray system OR (most common) a **rotary reaction-type distributor** with 2–4 horizontal arms driven by hydraulic head. 3. **Underdrain System** — A false bottom with vitrified clay or plastic blocks, designed to: - Collect treated effluent. - Allow collected sloughings to flow to the humus tank. - Permit **ventilation** of air up through the bed (counter-current oxygen supply). 4. **Dosing Tank / Pump Well** — Stores settled sewage and feeds it to the distributor at a controlled rate. 5. **Ventilation System** — Ducts/fans (if forced draft) or natural stack effect (if natural draft) for oxygen replenishment. 6. **Final (Humus) Clarifier** — Settles out sloughed biofilm to produce clear secondary effluent. --- ## 2.2 Mechanism of Treatment (Step-by-Step Logic) - **Step 1 — Distribution:** Settled wastewater is sprayed uniformly over the media surface via the rotary distributor. - **Step 2 — Adsorption & Diffusion:** As the thin liquid film flows over the biofilm, organic substrates (BOD) diffuse into the slime layer. - **Step 3 — Biological Oxidation:** Aerobic bacteria in the biofilm oxidize the diffused organics: $$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{enzymes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{new microbial cells} $$ - **Step 4 — Oxygen Supply:** Air flows **counter-current** (upward) through the voids, replenishing dissolved oxygen. - **Step 5 — Sloughing:** When the biofilm thickness exceeds ≈ **0.1 – 0.2 mm**, it detaches (sloughs) and is washed out with the effluent to the humus clarifier. - **Step 6 — Re-growth:** A fresh biofilm begins to grow on the newly exposed media surface. --- ## 2.3 Design Parameters — KTU Cheat Sheet (MUST MEMORIZE) > [!NOTE] > Below is the **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet** for trickling filter design. This is the single most tested cluster of formulas in the Module 3 university exam. | # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Hydraulic Loading Rate** | HLR or $H$ | $H = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m³/m²·d | | 2 | **Organic Loading Rate** | OLR or $L_{org}$ | $L_{org} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·d | | 3 | **Filter Volume** | $V$ | $V = A \cdot D$ | m³ | | 4 | **NRC Equation (Efficiency)** | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V F}}}$ | Fraction (×100 for %) | | 5 | **Eckenfelder Equation** | $S_e / S_0$ | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(-k \cdot D / Q^{0.5}\right)$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | **Velz Equation (Modified)** | $S_e$ | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(-k \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{A}{Q}\right)$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | **Recirculation Factor** | $F$ | $F = \dfrac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 R)^2}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | **Recirculation Ratio** | $R$ | $R = \dfrac{Q_R}{Q}$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | **Distributor Flow** | $Q_d$ | $Q_d = Q (1 + R)$ | m³/d | | 10 | **BOD Removed (per day)** | $W$ | $W = Q (S_0 - S_e) \cdot 10^{-3}$ | kg BOD/d | | 11 | **Required Filter Volume** | $V$ | $V = \dfrac{W}{(OLR)}$ | m³ | | 12 | **Required Filter Area** | $A$ | $A = V / D$ | m² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** The most common exam trap is **confusing $W/V$ (organic loading in kg BOD/m³·d) with $W/(V \cdot F)$ used in the NRC equation**. The $F$ factor (recirculation) must be applied ONLY to the $W/V$ term inside the square root. --- ## 2.4 In-Depth Discussion of the Three Governing Design Equations ### 🔹 A. The NRC (National Research Council) Equation > Developed by the **U.S. National Research Council (1946)** for **standard-rate and high-rate rock filters**. It is the most widely used in KTU board exams. $$ \eta = \frac{1}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}} $$ Where: - $\eta$ = fraction of BOD removed (e.g., 0.85 for 85%). - $W$ = BOD applied per day (kg/d) = $Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) / 1000$. - $V$ = filter volume (m³). - $F$ = recirculation factor (if $R = 0$, then $F = 1$). > **Limitation:** Valid only for **rock-media** trickling filters, and assumes *uniform* distribution. ### 🔹 B. The Eckenfelder Equation (Most General) > Used for **plastic-media towers and high-rate filters** with non-standard loadings. $$ \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(-k \cdot \frac{D}{Q^n}\right) $$ Where $n$ ≈ 0.5 for plastic media, $k$ = treatability constant (L/m or m⁻¹, depends on substrate). ### 🔹 C. The Velz (Germain) Equation > Used for **deep towers** with low hydraulic loadings. The exponential form predicts a logarithmic BOD profile with depth. $$ \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(-K \cdot D \cdot \frac{A}{Q}\right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does the Velz equation have $A/Q$?** It expresses the **contact time (residence time)** of liquid in the filter. $A/Q$ has units of (m²/(m³/d)) = d/m. Combined with $D$ (m) and $K$ (m⁻¹), the exponent is dimensionless — confirming mathematical rigor. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Municipal Sewage Treatment:** Trickling filters treat 30–40% of all municipal sewage in India (small to medium towns like those served by Kerala's local bodies). - **Industrial Wastewater:** Used for treating food-processing, brewery, and pulp & paper effluents. - **Decentralized Plants:** Ideal for small communities (≤ 50,000 population) because of **low energy consumption** (no aeration blower required) and **operational simplicity**. - **Retrofit Scenarios:** Used as a *roughing stage* in front of activated sludge plants to reduce BOD loading. # 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Recirculation Factor $F$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **most asked 3-mark sub-question** in the KTU exam — "Define recirculation factor and derive its expression." ### Setup Let: - $Q$ = raw sewage inflow. - $Q_R$ = recirculated flow (from humus tank effluent back to the dosing tank). - $Q_1 = Q + Q_R$ = flow entering the filter. - $S_0$ = influent BOD of raw sewage. - $S_R$ = BOD of recirculated stream (taken to be same as final effluent $S_e$). - $S_1$ = BOD of *combined* flow entering the filter (after mixing). By mass balance at the mixing point (immediately before the filter): $$ Q \cdot S_0 + Q_R \cdot S_R = Q_1 \cdot S_1 $$ Since $Q_R = R \cdot Q$ and $S_R = S_e$: $$ Q \cdot S_0 + R \cdot Q \cdot S_e = Q (1 + R) \cdot S_1 $$ Divide throughout by $Q$: $$ S_0 + R \cdot S_e = (1 + R) S_1 $$ $$ S_1 = \frac{S_0 + R \cdot S_e}{1 + R} $$ ### Apply NRC Equation to the Filter Itself The filter receives flow $Q_1 = Q(1+R)$ and reduces BOD from $S_1$ down to $S_e$. So per unit volume, the effective BOD applied becomes: $$ \frac{W'}{V} = \frac{Q(1+R)\,(S_1 - S_e)}{V} $$ Substituting $S_1$: $$ S_1 - S_e = \frac{S_0 + R S_e}{1+R} - S_e = \frac{S_0 + R S_e - S_e - R S_e}{1+R} = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{1+R} $$ Therefore: $$ \frac{W'}{V} = \frac{Q(1+R) \cdot \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{1+R}}{V} = \frac{Q (S_0 - S_e)}{V} $$ Hmm — the $(1+R)$ cancels! But the NRC researchers observed that the **effective BOD removal rate is enhanced by recirculation** because the **increased hydraulic rate flushes sloughings and re-wets the film**, exposing fresh surface. They introduced an empirical correction $F$ such that: $$ \frac{W}{V \cdot F} = \frac{Q (S_0 - S_e)}{V \cdot F} $$ where the recirculation factor $F$ is given empirically by: $$ F = \frac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 R)^2} $$ > **Verification at $R = 0$ (no recirculation):** > $F = (1 + 0)/(1 + 0)^2 = 1/1 = 1$ ✓ > > **Verification at $R = 1$:** > $F = 2 / (1.1)^2 = 2 / 1.21 = 1.65$ > > **Verification at $R = 2$:** > $F = 3 / (1.2)^2 = 3 / 1.44 = 2.08$ This shows $F$ **grows with $R$**, thereby reducing $W/(VF)$ in the denominator and increasing $\eta$ — the very reason recirculation improves efficiency. --- ## 3.2 Full Worked-Out Design Example (KTU Board Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the standard 14-mark Part-B problem format.** Memorize the structure. ### Problem Statement Design a **high-rate single-stage trickling filter** for the following data: - Average sewage flow, $Q = 6.0$ MLD = **6000 m³/d** - Raw sewage BOD, $S_0 = 250$ mg/L - Required effluent BOD, $S_e = 30$ mg/L - Filter depth, $D = 1.8$ m - Recirculation ratio, $R = 1.5$ - Use the **NRC equation** ### Step 1 — Compute BOD to be Removed per Day ($W$) $$ W = Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m³ basis} $$ $$ W = 6000 \times (250 - 30) \times 10^{-3} = 6000 \times 220 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ W = 1320 \text{ kg BOD/d} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Recirculation Factor $F$ $$ F = \frac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 R)^2} = \frac{1 + 1.5}{(1 + 0.15)^2} = \frac{2.5}{1.3225} $$ $$ F = 1.890 \text{ (dimensionless)} $$ ### Step 3 — Determine Required Filter Volume Using NRC Rearrange the NRC equation for $V$: $$ \eta = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} = \frac{250 - 30}{250} = \frac{220}{250} = 0.88 $$ From the NRC formula: $$ 0.88 = \frac{1}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V F}}} $$ Invert both sides: $$ 1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\frac{W}{V F}} = \frac{1}{0.88} = 1.1364 $$ $$ 0.443 \sqrt{\frac{W}{V F}} = 0.1364 $$ $$ \sqrt{\frac{W}{V F}} = \frac{0.1364}{0.443} = 0.308 $$ Square both sides: $$ \frac{W}{V F} = (0.308)^2 = 0.0949 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3 \cdot \text{d} $$ Solve for $V$: $$ V = \frac{W}{F \times 0.0949} = \frac{1320}{1.890 \times 0.0949} $$ $$ V = \frac{1320}{0.1794} \approx 7359 \text{ m}^3 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Required Filter Area $$ A = \frac{V}{D} = \frac{7359}{1.8} \approx 4088 \text{ m}^2 $$ ### Step 5 — Adopt Practical Dimensions Adopt a **circular filter** (most common in India): $$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \Rightarrow D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 4088}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{5207} $$ $$ D = 72.2 \text{ m} \quad \text{(diameter of the filter tank)} $$ > Use a **standard 75 m diameter** filter. Recalculate actual $V$ and check OLR. ### Step 6 — Compute Actual Organic Loading Rate (Check) $$ OLR = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{1320}{7952} = 0.166 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3 \cdot \text{d} $$ ✅ This lies within the **high-rate range of 0.32 – 1.0** ✓ (actually closer to standard-rate, but acceptable for $D = 1.8$ m). ### Step 7 — Compute Hydraulic Loading Rate $$ HLR = \frac{Q (1 + R)}{A} = \frac{6000 \times 2.5}{7952/1.8} \approx \frac{15000}{4418} $$ $$ HLR = 3.39 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d} \quad \text{(with recirculation)} $$ ### Step 8 — Design the Rotary Distributor Number of arms = **2** (standard for 75 m dia). Flow on distributor = $Q_d = Q(1+R) = 6000 \times 2.5 = 15000$ m³/d = 625 m³/hr. Tip speed of distributor ≈ 0.6 m/s → Peripheral length = $\pi \times 75 = 235.6$ m. Revolutions per minute = $\dfrac{0.6 \times 60}{235.6} = 0.153$ rev/min (✓ typical 0.1–2 rpm). ### ✅ Final Design Summary Table | Parameter | Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Filter volume $V$ | 7359 | m³ | | Filter diameter | 75 | m | | Filter depth | 1.8 | m | | Recirculation ratio $R$ | 1.5 | — | | Recirculation factor $F$ | 1.890 | — | | BOD removed $W$ | 1320 | kg BOD/d | | Organic loading $W/V$ | 0.166 | kg BOD/m³·d | | Hydraulic loading (with recirc.) | 3.39 | m³/m²·d | | Number of distributor arms | 2 | — | | Distributor flow | 15000 | m³/d | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Eckenfelder Equation (Plastic Media Tower) ### Problem Design a **plastic media trickling filter tower** using Eckenfelder's equation: - $Q = 5000$ m³/d - $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, $S_e = 30$ mg/L - Treatability constant $k = 0.25$ (m⁻¹) - $n = 0.5$ for plastic media - Cross-section area assumed $A = 50$ m² ### Step 1 — Compute $S_e / S_0$ $$ \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{30}{300} = 0.10 $$ ### Step 2 — Rearrange Eckenfelder $$ 0.10 = \exp\left(-0.25 \cdot \frac{D}{(Q/A)^{0.5}}\right) $$ Take natural log of both sides: $$ \ln(0.10) = -0.25 \cdot \frac{D}{(Q/A)^{0.5}} $$ $$ -2.3026 = -0.25 \cdot \frac{D}{(Q/A)^{0.5}} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute $(Q/A)^{0.5}$ $$ \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5000}{50} = 100 \text{ m/d} $$ $$ \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right)^{0.5} = (100)^{0.5} = 10 \text{ m}^{0.5}/\text{d}^{0.5} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $D$ $$ 2.3026 = 0.25 \cdot \frac{D}{10} \Rightarrow D = \frac{2.3026 \times 10}{0.25} = \frac{23.026}{0.25} $$ $$ D = 92.1 \text{ m} $$ > **Engineering Judgment:** A 92-m deep tower is impractical. Reduce $A$ or apply a two-stage design. In real practice, plastic towers are 6–12 m deep. The Eckenfelder equation sometimes overshoots; it must be calibrated with the actual treatability constant from pilot studies. > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often forget the units of $k$ and $D$ must be **dimensionally consistent**. If $D$ is in meters, $k$ must be in m⁻¹ when $n = 0.5$ and $Q/A$ is in m/d. # 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Trickling Filter System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Influent Sewage

[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between low-rate and high-rate trickling filters. State any four points.** #### Model Answer (MUST use a TABLE for 3-mark questions): | Parameter | Low Rate TF | High Rate TF | |---|---|---| | Hydraulic loading (m³/m²·d) | 1 – 4 | 10 – 30 | | Organic loading (kg BOD/m³·d) | 0.08 – 0.32 | 0.32 – 1.0 | | Recirculation | Nil | Yes (R = 1–4) | | BOD removal efficiency | 80 – 90 % | 65 – 85 % | | Sludge production | More (nitrifying) | Less | | Effluent quality | Highly nitrified | Less nitrified | --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **State the NRC equation for trickling filter efficiency. Explain the significance of the recirculation factor $F$.** #### Model Answer: The NRC equation is: $$ \eta = \frac{1}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}} $$ **Significance of $F$:** - $F$ is the **recirculation factor**, defined as $F = (1 + R) / (1 + 0.1R)^2$. - It accounts for the **enhanced BOD removal rate** when treated effluent is recirculated. - Recirculation increases the hydraulic shear on the biofilm, causing continuous sloughing and re-growth, which **exposes fresh active biomass**. - $F$ appears in the denominator → larger $F$ → smaller denominator → larger $\eta$. - For $R = 0$, $F = 1$ (no benefit). For $R = 1$, $F ≈ 1.65$ (66% improvement). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Rule:** In Part B, every module-based question carries an internal choice. You must answer EITHER (a) + (b) OR (c) + (d) — each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Q.A (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 7 Marks **Design a single-stage high-rate trickling filter for the following data:** - Average sewage flow $Q = 4.5$ MLD - BOD of raw sewage $S_0 = 280$ mg/L - Required effluent BOD $S_e = 30$ mg/L - Filter depth $D = 1.8$ m - Recirculation ratio $R = 2.0$ - Use NRC equation. **Step 1 — BOD to be removed per day:** $W = Q (S_0 - S_e) \times 10^{-3} = 4500 \times (280 - 30) \times 10^{-3} = 4500 \times 0.250 = 1125$ kg BOD/d. **Step 2 — Recirculation factor:** $F = (1 + 2) / (1 + 0.2)^2 = 3 / 1.44 = 2.083$. **Step 3 — Required efficiency:** $\eta = (280 - 30) / 280 = 0.893$ (i.e., 89.3%). **Step 4 — Apply NRC and solve for $V$:** $\eta = 1 / (1 + 0.443 \sqrt{W/(V F)})$ $\Rightarrow 1 + 0.443 \sqrt{W/(V F)} = 1/0.893 = 1.120$ $\Rightarrow 0.443 \sqrt{W/(V F)} = 0.120$ $\Rightarrow \sqrt{W/(V F)} = 0.2709$ $\Rightarrow W/(V F) = 0.0734$ $\Rightarrow V = W / (F \times 0.0734) = 1125 / (2.083 \times 0.0734) = 1125 / 0.1529 = 7358$ m³. **Step 5 — Filter area:** $A = V / D = 7358 / 1.8 = 4088$ m². **Step 6 — Circular filter diameter:** $D_{tank} = \sqrt{4A/\pi} = \sqrt{4 \times 4088 / 3.1416} = \sqrt{5204} = 72.1$ m → adopt standard **75 m**. **Step 7 — Check organic loading:** Actual $V = \pi \times (75/2)^2 \times 1.8 = 7952$ m³. $OLR = W/V = 1125/7952 = 0.141$ kg BOD/m³·d ✓ (within 0.08–0.32 low-rate–high-rate boundary). #### Q.A (b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 7 Marks **Explain with a neat sketch the components of a trickling filter and the function of the rotary distributor.** **Components:** 1. Filter media (rock or plastic) 2. Distributor (rotary reaction type) 3. Underdrain system 4. Dosing tank 5. Humus clarifier 6. Ventilation ducts **Rotary Distributor Function:** - Distributes settled sewage **uniformly** over the entire media surface. - Driven by the **hydraulic head** of sewage entering the central column — the sewage exits through nozzles on horizontal arms, creating a reaction force that rotates the assembly at 0.5–2 rpm. - Each revolution ensures every portion of the bed is wetted, allowing **alternating wet and dry cycles** essential for oxygen transfer and biofilm respiration. - For very low flows, auxiliary electric motors are provided. *(For full marks, the student should describe the dosing tank and underdrain layout verbally.)* --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE #### Q.B (a) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — 7 Marks **A municipal STP uses a trickling filter with the following data:** - $Q = 7.5$ MLD, $S_0 = 200$ mg/L, $S_e = 25$ mg/L, $D = 2.0$ m - Recirculation ratio $R = 1.0$ - The existing filter has volume $V = 5000$ m³. - **Check whether the existing filter is adequate using the NRC equation. If not, find the additional volume required.** **Step 1 — Recirculation factor:** $F = (1 + 1) / (1 + 0.1)^2 = 2 / 1.21 = 1.653$. **Step 2 — BOD removed:** $W = 7500 \times (200 - 25) \times 10^{-3} = 7500 \times 0.175 = 1312.5$ kg BOD/d. **Step 3 — Predicted efficiency of existing filter:** $\eta = 1 / (1 + 0.443 \sqrt{W/(V F)}) = 1 / (1 + 0.443 \sqrt{1312.5/(5000 \times 1.653)})$ $\sqrt{1312.5 / 8265} = \sqrt{0.1588} = 0.3985$ $\eta = 1 / (1 + 0.443 \times 0.3985) = 1 / (1 + 0.1765) = 1 / 1.1765 = 0.85$ (i.e., 85%). **Step 4 — Required effluent BOD:** Predicted $S_e = 200 \times (1 - 0.85) = 200 \times 0.15 = 30$ mg/L. **Step 5 — Compare with required 25 mg/L:** Since predicted $S_e = 30$ mg/L > required 25 mg/L, the filter is **INADEQUATE**. **Step 6 — Find required V for 25 mg/L:** Required $\eta = (200 - 25)/200 = 0.875$. $1 + 0.443 \sqrt{1312.5/(V \times 1.653)} = 1/0.875 = 1.1429$ $0.443 \sqrt{1312.5/(V \times 1.653)} = 0.1429$ $\sqrt{1312.5/(V \times 1.653)} = 0.3226$ $1312.5 / (V \times 1.653) = 0.1041$ $V = 1312.5 / (0.1041 \times 1.653) = 1312.5 / 0.1721 = 7626$ m³. **Step 7 — Additional volume required:** $\Delta V = 7626 - 5000 = 2626$ m³ → construct a second filter of this volume or expand. #### Q.B (b) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — 7 Marks **Explain the differences between the NRC, Eckenfelder, and Velz equations. When is each preferred?** | Equation | Preferred Media | Type of Plant | Key Form | |---|---|---|---| | **NRC** | Rock media (low/high rate) | Conventional municipal STP | $\eta = 1/(1 + 0.443\sqrt{W/(VF)})$ | | **Eckenfelder** | Plastic media, deep towers | High-rate industrial/municipal | $S_e/S_0 = \exp(-k D / (Q/A)^n)$ | | **Velz** | Deep towers, low HLR | Pilot plant, deep plastic towers | $S_e/S_0 = \exp(-K D \cdot A/Q)$ | **Selection logic:** - Use **NRC** when filter is rock media, shallow (≤ 3 m), and recirculation is being designed. - Use **Eckenfelder** for plastic media or pilot-plant-derived $k$ values. - Use **Velz** when depth > 4 m and BOD profile is logarithmic. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **🚨 COMMON MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern)** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 10⁻³** when converting mg/L to kg/m³ for $W$ — leads to $W$ being 1000× too large. **Loss: 3–4 marks.** > 2. **Using $W/V$ in NRC instead of $W/(V F)$** — even with recirculation. **Loss: 2–3 marks.** > 3. **Computing filter diameter from area without checking** if it matches a standard size (e.g., 25, 30, 45, 60, 75 m). **Loss: 1 mark.** > 4. **Forgetting to write the unit kg BOD/d** in $W$ — examiners deduct **½ mark per incorrect/missing unit** (strict KTU norm). > 5. **Writing $\sqrt{\,}$ with absolute-value vertical bars** in markdown or answer sheets — examiners find it hard to grade, may deduct **1 mark**. Always use square root symbol with a single horizontal bar. > 6. **Skipping the recirculation factor derivation** when asked. **Loss: up to 4 marks** on the 7-mark "explain F" question. > 7. **Confusing OLR and HLR units** (m³/m²·d vs kg BOD/m³·d). **Loss: 1 mark per incorrect parameter.** > 8. **Not stating the design depth assumption** (e.g., $D = 1.8$ m) — examiners expect this to be **explicitly stated**. **Loss: 1 mark.** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist (Important Things to Remember) > [!NOTE] > **📌 MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM:** - ✅ Trickling filter is a **fixed-film (attached-growth) aerobic** biological treatment system. - ✅ The **NRC equation** is the KTU-favourite: $\eta = 1 / (1 + 0.443 \sqrt{W/(V F)})$. - ✅ **Recirculation factor** $F = (1 + R) / (1 + 0.1R)^2$ — always $F \geq 1$. - ✅ **Organic loading** $OLR = Q S_0 / V$ in kg BOD/m³·d. - ✅ **Hydraulic loading** $HLR = Q(1+R) / A$ in m³/m²·d. - ✅ **Standard rock filter depth** = **1.8 m**; **plastic tower depth** = **4 to 12 m**. - ✅ **Standard filter diameters** are circular: **25, 30, 45, 60, 75 m**. - ✅ **BOD to be removed per day** $W = Q (S_0 - S_e) \times 10^{-3}$ in **kg BOD/d**. - ✅ **Eckenfelder** uses $D / (Q/A)^n$ in the exponent (with $n ≈ 0.5$). - ✅ **Velz** uses $D \cdot A / Q$ in the exponent (analogous to contact time). - ✅ Rotary distributor: 2–4 arms, 0.5–2 rpm, 0.5–1.5 m head. - ✅ **Sloughing** occurs when biofilm thickness > 0.15 mm. - ✅ Air flow is **counter-current (upward)**; oxygen transfer happens at the air-water interface over the biofilm. - ✅ Low rate TF efficiency: 80–90% | High rate: 65–85% | Roughing: 40–65%. - ✅ Two-stage TF achieves up to **95% BOD removal** for very strong wastes. - ✅ Trickling filter effluent always requires a **humus tank** (final clarifier) to settle sloughed biofilm. - ✅ For a **Kerala local-body STP**, always check if natural ventilation (stack effect) is sufficient, or whether forced-draft fans are required (typical when bed depth > 3 m or wastewater is strong).

topics.title: Activated Sludge Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activated Sludge Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** is a **suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment process** in which a mixed microbial population (biomass) is maintained in intimate contact with the organic substrate of wastewater in an **aerated reactor**, followed by **solid–liquid separation** in a secondary clarifier, with a portion of the settled biomass continuously recycled back to the reactor to maintain a high and active population of microorganisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Activated sludge is a flocculent suspension of aerobic and facultative microorganisms in wastewater that metabolizes soluble and colloidal organic matter into carbon dioxide, water, and new cellular biomass under controlled dissolved oxygen conditions.*

Formally, the process achieves the following simultaneous engineering objectives:
1. **Oxidation of biodegradable organics** to stable end products (CO₂, H₂O).
2. **Synthesis of new microbial cells** (sludge production).
3. **Energy generation** through endogenous respiration within the biomass.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of an aquarium full of microscopic "workers" (bacteria) who love to eat dirt (organic waste in sewage). Now imagine you pour sewage into the tank, keep stirring the water and pumping air through it (so the workers can breathe and stay awake), and the workers will happily munch the dirt, multiply, and clean the water.

After a while, you siphon out the now-clean water. The workers sink to the bottom as a brown sludge. To keep the tank full of hungry, active workers, you scoop most of the sludge and pour it **back into the tank** (this is called **return activated sludge, RAS**). The surplus sludge is **wasted** (waste activated sludge, WAS).

> [!NOTE]
> The activated sludge process is essentially **"feeding hungry bacteria with sewage in a well-oxygenated tank, settling them out, and recycling them so they keep working efficiently."**

## 1.3 Why This Process is Important

- Removes **85–95% BOD** and suspended solids from municipal wastewater.
- Produces a **clear, low-odor effluent** suitable for tertiary treatment or disposal.
- Forms the **backbone of modern municipal Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** in India and worldwide.
- In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, ASP is the **core of Module 3 (Biological Treatment – Suspended Growth)** and is regularly asked in End Semester Examinations (ESE) and University question papers.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Range |
|---|---|
| Dissolved Oxygen (DO) in aeration tank | **1.5 – 2.0 mg/L** |
| MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids) | **1500 – 4000 mg/L** |
| MLVSS/MLSS ratio | **0.7 – 0.85** |
| F/M ratio | **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹** |
| SRT (Mean Cell Residence Time) | **5 – 15 days** |
| HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time) | **4 – 8 hours** |
| SVI (Sludge Volume Index) | **50 – 150 mL/g** |
| Recirculation ratio (R) | **0.25 – 1.0** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Components and Flow

The activated sludge process has **four key unit operations** connected in a closed loop:

1. **Aeration Tank (Reactor)** – Where air/oxygen is continuously supplied and the mixed liquor (wastewater + biomass) is agitated.
2. **Secondary Clarifier (Settling Tank)** – Where biological flocs are separated from the treated effluent.
3. **Return Sludge Pump (RAS)** – Returns a portion of settled sludge back to the aeration tank.
4. **Waste Sludge Pump (WAS)** – Removes excess biomass to maintain steady-state conditions.

**Step-wise operational logic:**

- **Step 1:** Influent wastewater enters the aeration tank, where it mixes with return activated sludge (RAS).
- **Step 2:** Compressed air is diffused through fine bubble diffusers at the bottom, maintaining **DO between 1.5 and 2.0 mg/L**.
- **Step 3:** Aerobic microorganisms oxidize the dissolved organic matter to **CO₂ + H₂O + new cells**.
- **Step 4:** The mixed liquor flows into a secondary clarifier. Clean supernatant (treated effluent) overflows.
- **Step 5:** Settled sludge is split — most is **recycled (RAS)**, the excess is **wasted (WAS)**.
- **Step 6:** The effluent is disinfected (chlorination/UV) before discharge.

## 2.2 Microbial Ecology

The mixed liquor contains a diverse ecosystem:
- **Bacteria** (floc formers like *Zoogloea*, *Pseudomonas*) — primary BOD removal.
- **Protozoa** (ciliates, flagellates) — polish effluent by consuming free bacteria.
- **Metazoa** (rotifers, nematodes) — indicators of a well-aged sludge.
- **Filamentous organisms** — provide structure to flocs, but overgrowth causes **sludge bulking**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Favourite Concept:** Maintain a healthy balance between **floc-formers** and **filamentous bacteria** to prevent bulking and pinpoint sludge.

## 2.3 Design and Operational Parameters — The "Why" and "How"

| Parameter | Symbol | Governing Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **F/M Ratio** (Food to Microorganism ratio) | U | Controls the type of metabolism (high F/M = dispersed growth; low F/M = endogenous respiration) |
| **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT/SRT)** | $\theta_c$ | Determines sludge age; controls which microorganisms dominate |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta$ | Time wastewater spends in the aeration tank |
| **Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids** | X | Biomass concentration in the reactor |
| **Sludge Volume Index** | SVI | Settling quality of sludge |
| **Recirculation Ratio** | R | Fraction of effluent flow returned as RAS |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | Food to microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | h or day |
| 3 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | Solids Retention Time (SRT / MCRT) | day |
| 4 | $SVI = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{MLSS}$ | Sludge Volume Index | mL/g |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100$ | BOD Removal Efficiency | % |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q}$ | Recirculation Ratio (R = Qᵣ/Q) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $O_2 = a' L_r + b' S_a$ | Oxygen requirement (modified equation) | kg O₂/day |
| 8 | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} - K_d$ | Net specific growth rate | day⁻¹ |
| 9 | $X = \dfrac{\theta_c \cdot Y (S_0 - S)}{\theta(1 + K_d \theta_c)}$ | Biomass concentration in reactor | mg/L |
| 10 | $\dfrac{S_0 - S}{S} = K \cdot \dfrac{\theta_c}{1 + K_d \theta_c} \cdot \dfrac{X}{...}$ (Lawrence-McCarty) | Substrate removal kinetics | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The ASP equations are used by design engineers to size aeration tanks, select blowers, calculate sludge production, and ensure effluent quality compliant with **CPCB norms (BOD ≤ 30 mg/L for discharge to water bodies)**.

## 2.5 Standard Modifications of Activated Sludge Process

| Modification | F/M (day⁻¹) | SRT (days) | HRT (h) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional | 0.2 – 0.4 | 5 – 15 | 4 – 8 | Municipal sewage |
| Completely Mixed | 0.2 – 0.6 | 5 – 30 | 3 – 5 | Industrial + domestic |
| Extended Aeration | 0.05 – 0.15 | 20 – 40 | 18 – 36 | Small communities |
| Oxidation Ditch | 0.05 – 0.10 | 20 – 30 | 24 – 48 | Decentralized plants |
| Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR) | 0.1 – 0.3 | 10 – 30 | 4 – 6 (per cycle) | Variable inflow |
| Membrane Bioreactor (MBR) | 0.1 – 0.4 | 15 – 60 | 4 – 6 | High-quality reuse |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)

**Definition:** HRT is the average time a fluid element spends inside the aeration tank.

**Derivation:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{\text{Volume of tank}}{\text{Flow rate of influent}}$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$$

**Units check:** If $V$ is in m³ and $Q$ is in m³/h, then $\theta$ is in **hours** — correct.

**Real-world meaning:** In a conventional ASP, $\theta \approx 4$ to $8$ hours, so the wastewater stays in the aeration tank long enough for microbes to consume organics but is short enough to prevent endogenous decay dominating.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — F/M Ratio (Substrate Loading Rate)

**Definition:** The F/M ratio is the mass of substrate (BOD) applied per unit mass of microorganisms per day.

**Derivation:**

$$F/M = \dfrac{\text{Mass of BOD applied per day}}{\text{Mass of MLSS in aeration tank}}$$

$$F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$$

**Substituting** $\theta = V/Q$:

$$F/M = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$$

**Significance:** A **high F/M** → rapid growth, dispersed flocs, poor settling. A **low F/M** → endogenous respiration, pinpoint flocs, good settling, low sludge yield.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT / SRT)

**Mass balance on biomass in the system:**

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} - \text{Waste}$$

At **steady state** (no accumulation), the SRT is the ratio of biomass inventory to the rate at which biomass leaves the system.

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$$

Where:
- $Q_w \cdot X_w$ = mass of sludge wasted per day (kg/day)
- $Q_e \cdot X_e$ = mass of biomass lost in the effluent per day (kg/day)

**Standard simplified form** (ignoring effluent losses):

$$\theta_c \approx \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — BOD Removal Efficiency

By mass balance on the substrate across the aeration tank:

$$\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100\%$$

A typical **conventional ASP** achieves $\eta = 85\% - 95\%$. To meet **CPCB discharge norms of BOD ≤ 30 mg/L**, the influent BOD $S_0$ must be such that the residual $S$ falls within limits.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Sludge Volume Index (SVI)

SVI is measured by allowing MLSS to settle for 30 minutes in a 1-litre graduated cylinder.

$$SVI = \dfrac{\text{Settled sludge volume (mL/L)} \times 1000}{\text{MLSS concentration (mg/L)}}$$

**Practical interpretation:**
- $SVI < 100$ mL/g → **Good settling**.
- $SVI = 100 - 200$ mL/g → **Light bulking**.
- $SVI > 200$ mL/g → **Severe bulking** (filamentous growth).

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Oxygen Requirement (Modified Form)

The **theoretical oxygen demand** has two components:

1. **Carbonaceous BOD removal:** $a' L_r$ (where $a' \approx 0.5 - 0.6$)
2. **Endogenous decay of biomass:** $b' S_a$ (where $b' \approx 0.05 - 0.15$)

$$O_2 = a' L_r + b' S_a$$

**Where:**
- $L_r = Q (S_0 - S) \times 10^{-3}$ kg BOD/day (BOD removed per day)
- $S_a = V \cdot X_v \times 10^{-3}$ kg MLVSS in aeration tank
- $a', b'$ are empirical constants depending on wastewater type.

A common thumb rule:
$$\boxed{1.42 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ required per kg BOD removed}}$$

The 1.42 factor comes from the stoichiometric oxidation:
$$\text{C}_5\text{H}_7\text{NO}_2 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 5\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NH}_3$$

(Molecular mass ratio = $160 / 113 \approx 1.42$.)

## 3.7 Comprehensive Numerical Problem (KTU-Standard)

**Problem Statement (Model Question):**
> A conventional activated sludge plant treats **$Q = 5 \text{ MLD}$** of municipal sewage with influent BOD **$S_0 = 250 \text{ mg/L}$**. The desired effluent BOD is **$S = 20 \text{ mg/L}$**. The MLSS in the aeration tank is maintained at **$X = 2500 \text{ mg/L}$**, and the HRT is **$\theta = 6 \text{ hours}$**. Calculate:
>
> (a) Volume of aeration tank
> (b) F/M ratio
> (c) BOD removal efficiency
> (d) Mass of BOD removed per day
> (e) Theoretical oxygen requirement (take $a' = 0.5$, $b' = 0.08$, MLVSS $X_v = 2000$ mg/L)

### Solution

**Given:**
- $Q = 5 \text{ MLD} = 5 \times 10^3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$
- $S_0 = 250 \text{ mg/L}$, $S = 20 \text{ mg/L}$
- $X = 2500 \text{ mg/L}$, $X_v = 2000 \text{ mg/L}$
- $\theta = 6 \text{ h} = 6/24 = 0.25 \text{ day}$

**Part (a) — Volume of aeration tank:**

$$V = Q \times \theta$$

$$V = 5 \times 10^3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 0.25 \text{ day}$$

$$\boxed{V = 1250 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Part (b) — F/M Ratio:**

$$F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$$

$$F/M = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}) \times (250 \text{ mg/L})}{(1250 \text{ m}^3) \times (2500 \text{ mg/L})}$$

Unit conversion: $V \cdot X = 1250 \times 10^3 \text{ L} \times 2500 \text{ mg/L} = 3.125 \times 10^9 \text{ mg}$ of MLSS.

$$F/M = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^9 \text{ mg/day}}{3.125 \times 10^9 \text{ mg}}$$

$$\boxed{F/M = 0.4 \text{ day}^{-1}}$$

**Part (c) — BOD Removal Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{250 - 20}{250} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 92\%}$$

**Part (d) — Mass of BOD removed per day:**

$$L_r = Q \times (S_0 - S)$$

$$L_r = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day} \times (250 - 20) \text{ mg/L}$$

$$L_r = 1.15 \times 10^9 \text{ mg/day} = 1150 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$\boxed{L_r = 1150 \text{ kg BOD/day}}$$

**Part (e) — Theoretical Oxygen Requirement:**

Using the modified equation:
$$O_2 = a' \cdot L_r + b' \cdot S_a$$

Where:
$$S_a = V \cdot X_v = 1250 \text{ m}^3 \times 2000 \text{ g/m}^3 = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ g} = 2500 \text{ kg MLVSS}$$

$$O_2 = (0.5 \times 1150) + (0.08 \times 2500)$$

$$O_2 = 575 + 200$$

$$\boxed{O_2 = 775 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}}$$

**Air supply requirement (assuming 5% oxygen transfer efficiency, density of air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³, oxygen 23% by mass):**

$$O_2 \text{ in air} = 0.232 \times 1.2 = 0.278 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{m}^3 \text{ air}$$

$$\text{Air required} = \dfrac{775}{0.05 \times 0.278} = \dfrac{775}{0.0139}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Air} \approx 55,755 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 0.65 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

## 3.8 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Quantity Computed | Formula Used | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aeration tank volume | $V = Q\theta$ | 1250 m³ |
| 2 | F/M ratio | $F/M = QS_0 / (VX)$ | 0.4 day⁻¹ |
| 3 | BOD removal efficiency | $(S_0 - S)/S_0$ | 92% |
| 4 | BOD removed per day | $Q(S_0 - S)$ | 1150 kg/day |
| 5 | MLVSS in tank | $V X_v$ | 2500 kg |
| 6 | Oxygen demand | $a' L_r + b' S_a$ | 775 kg O₂/day |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of Conventional Activated Sludge Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Influent Sewage] --> B[Screening and Grit Removal]
    B --> C[Primary Clarifier]
    C --> D[Aeration Tank with Diffused Air]
    D --> E[Secondary Clarifier]
    E --> F[Treated Effluent to Disinfection]
    F --> G[Outfall or Reuse]
    E --> H[RAS Pump Return Sludge Line]
    H --> D
    E --> I[WAS Pump Waste Sludge Line]
    I --> J[Sludge Thickener and Digester]
    D --> K[Air Blower and Diffuser Network]
    K --> D
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INFLOW[Influent Substrate S0] --> REACT[Aeration Reactor V]
    REACT --> MIX[Microbial Oxidation DO 1.5 to 2 mg per L]
    MIX --> SETTLE[Secondary Clarifier]
    SETTLE --> EFF[Treated Effluent S]
    SETTLE --> RECYCLE[RAS Recirculation Ratio R]
    RECYCLE --> REACT
    SETTLE --> WASTE[WAS Disposal to Thickener]
    AIR[Atmospheric Air via Blower] --> MIX
    FLOW[Influent Flow Q] --> REACT
    FLOW --> EFF
    SETTLE --> SLOSS[Solids Loss in Effluent Xe]
```

## 4.3 Process Performance Flow (Input–Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STREAMS]
        Q1[Flow Q MLD]
        S0[BOD Influent S0 mg per L]
        XR[Return Sludge Xr mg per L]
    end
    subgraph REACTOR[AERATION TANK]
        V1[Volume V m3]
        X1[MLSS X mg per L]
        T1[HRT theta h]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STREAMS]
        S1[Effluent BOD S mg per L]
        XW1[WAS Xw mg per L]
        QW1[WAS Flow Qw MLD]
    end
    Q1 --> REACTOR
    S0 --> REACTOR
    XR --> REACTOR
    REACTOR --> S1
    REACTOR --> XW1
    REACTOR --> QW1
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** Always label mass flows (Q, $Q_w$, $Q_r$), concentrations (S₀, S, X, $X_r$, $X_w$), and the **direction of sludge recirculation** distinctly from the **effluent** — this is the most commonly asked **labelled sketch** in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *What is the Activated Sludge Process? State the role of return activated sludge (RAS).*

**Model Answer:**
The activated sludge process is a **suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment process** in which a mixed culture of aerobic microorganisms is maintained in an aeration tank to oxidize dissolved and suspended organic matter under controlled DO conditions (1.5–2.0 mg/L). The mixed liquor is then transferred to a secondary clarifier where biomass separates as sludge.

**Role of RAS:** A portion (typically 25–100% of the flow) of the settled sludge from the clarifier is returned to the aeration tank to:
1. Maintain a high and uniform concentration of active biomass.
2. Improve substrate–microbe contact.
3. Stabilize process performance against shock loads.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Differentiate between F/M ratio and SRT. Why is SRT considered a more reliable design parameter?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | F/M Ratio | SRT (θc) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Mass of BOD applied per unit MLSS per day | Average time biomass stays in the system |
| Unit | day⁻¹ | day |
| Control | Affects metabolism type | Controls microbial species dominance |
| Measurement | Direct calculation | Mass balance of biomass |

**SRT is more reliable** because it directly governs:
- Microbial species selection (long SRT favours nitrifiers).
- Sludge production rate.
- Endogenous decay and effluent quality — making it **independent of influent fluctuations** that affect F/M.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **conventional activated sludge process**. Describe the function of each unit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipal STP treats **$Q = 10 \text{ MLD}$** of sewage with $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, $S = 25$ mg/L. MLSS = 3000 mg/L, MLVSS = 2100 mg/L, HRT = 5 hours. Calculate the volume of the aeration tank, F/M ratio, BOD removal efficiency, and theoretical oxygen requirement. Use $a' = 0.5$, $b' = 0.07$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **conventional activated sludge process** consists of:

1. **Aeration Tank** – Where wastewater mixes with return sludge; air is supplied via diffusers. DO is maintained at 1.5–2.0 mg/L. Microorganisms oxidize BOD and produce new cells.
2. **Secondary Clarifier** – Settles the biomass from mixed liquor; supernatant is the treated effluent.
3. **RAS Pump** – Recycles 25–100% of settled sludge back to maintain biomass concentration.
4. **WAS Pump** – Wastes excess biomass to keep the system at steady state.
5. **Air Blower and Diffuser System** – Provides oxygen and mixing.

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Process flow description (3 marks):**
- Influent → Primary clarifier (sketch as pre-treatment) → Aeration tank → Secondary clarifier → Effluent → Disinfection.
- Sludge from secondary clarifier → split into RAS (to aeration tank) and WAS (to sludge handling).

**Functions of each unit (4 marks):**
- Aeration tank → BOD removal + synthesis of biomass.
- Clarifier → solid–liquid separation.
- RAS → biomass concentration control.
- WAS → sludge age (SRT) control.
- Blower → oxygen supply.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $Q = 10 \text{ MLD} = 10^4 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$, $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, $S = 25$ mg/L, $X = 3000$ mg/L, $X_v = 2100$ mg/L, $\theta = 5$ h $= 5/24$ day $\approx 0.2083$ day, $a' = 0.5$, $b' = 0.07$.

**(i) Volume of aeration tank:**

$$V = Q \theta = 10^4 \times 0.2083 = 2083.3 \text{ m}^3 \approx 2084 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) F/M ratio:**

$$F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = \dfrac{10^7 \text{ L/day} \times 300 \text{ mg/L}}{2083.3 \text{ m}^3 \times 3000 \text{ mg/L}}$$

$$F/M = \dfrac{3 \times 10^9}{6.25 \times 10^9} = 0.48 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**(iii) BOD removal efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{300 - 25}{300} \times 100 = 91.67\%$$

**(iv) Oxygen requirement:**

$$L_r = Q(S_0 - S) = 10^4 \text{ m}^3 \times (300 - 25) \text{ g/m}^3 = 2.75 \times 10^6 \text{ g/day} = 2750 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$S_a = V \cdot X_v = 2083.3 \text{ m}^3 \times 2100 \text{ g/m}^3 = 4.375 \times 10^6 \text{ g = 4375 \text{ kg}}$$

$$O_2 = a' L_r + b' S_a = 0.5 \times 2750 + 0.07 \times 4375$$

$$O_2 = 1375 + 306.25 = 1681.25 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{V = 2084 \text{ m}^3, \quad F/M = 0.48 \text{ day}^{-1}, \quad \eta = 91.67\%, \quad O_2 = 1681.25 \text{ kg/day}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT)**? Derive the equation for MCRT in an activated sludge system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the following modifications of ASP: **Conventional, Extended Aeration, Completely Mixed, and SBR**. Tabulate the differences in F/M, SRT, HRT, MLSS, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (3 marks):**
Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT), also called **Solids Retention Time (SRT) or sludge age** ($\theta_c$), is defined as the **average time a unit mass of biological solids remains in the activated sludge system** before being wasted.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Performing a **mass balance on biomass** in the system at steady state:

$$\text{Input of biomass} = \text{Output of biomass}$$

Considering the wasted sludge flow $Q_w$ carrying concentration $X_w$, and effluent losses $Q_e$ carrying $X_e$:

$$V \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} = 0 = -\left(Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e\right)$$

The biomass inventory = $V \cdot X$.

The rate of biomass removal = $Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e$.

Hence:

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$$

In practice, effluent losses are small, so:

$$\theta_c \approx \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$$

**Significance:** $\theta_c$ controls microbial ecology, sludge production, and effluent quality.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | Conventional | Extended Aeration | Completely Mixed | SBR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F/M (day⁻¹)** | 0.2 – 0.4 | 0.05 – 0.15 | 0.2 – 0.6 | 0.1 – 0.3 |
| **SRT (days)** | 5 – 15 | 20 – 40 | 5 – 30 | 10 – 30 |
| **HRT (hours)** | 4 – 8 | 18 – 36 | 3 – 5 | 4 – 6 (per cycle) |
| **MLSS (mg/L)** | 1500 – 3000 | 3000 – 6000 | 2500 – 4000 | 2000 – 5000 |
| **Application** | Medium-large municipalities | Small communities, package plants | Industrial + domestic | Variable inflow, automation |
| **Sludge Production** | High | Very low | High | Moderate |
| **Footprint** | Large | Medium | Medium | Compact (no separate clarifier) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse HRT ($\theta$) with SRT ($\theta_c$). HRT is the **time water stays in the tank**; SRT is the **time sludge stays in the system**. They are NOT equal — SRT is typically 5–10 times HRT in conventional ASP.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert units — $Q$ in MLD must be converted to m³/day or L/day, and concentrations in mg/L must be consistent.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In the $O_2$ equation, students use $X$ (MLSS) instead of $X_v$ (MLVSS) — **always use MLVSS** for endogenous respiration term.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Drawing sketches without arrows showing RAS / WAS / Effluent directions — **mark all flow lines** to get full marks for the diagram.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** In design problems, the 1.42 factor for oxygen stoichiometry is often misapplied — it is the **theoretical** maximum; actual oxygen demand uses the empirical $a', b'$ equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (Module 3 — Activated Sludge Process)**

- **ASP = Suspended-growth aerobic biological treatment.** Microbes remain in suspension in the aeration tank.
- **Core components:** Aeration tank, Secondary clarifier, RAS, WAS, Air blower, Diffusers.
- **DO in aeration tank:** Maintain **1.5 – 2.0 mg/L**; below 1.0 → poor BOD removal; above 3.0 → wasteful energy.
- **F/M ratio** = $QS_0 / (VX)$. Range **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹** for conventional ASP.
- **SRT / MCRT** = $VX / (Q_w X_w)$. **Longer SRT → less sludge, better nitrification.**
- **HRT** = $V / Q$. **Conventional HRT = 4 – 8 hours.**
- **SVI** = $V_{30} \times 1000 / \text{MLSS}$. Good settling: **SVI = 50 – 150 mL/g**.
- **BOD removal efficiency** = $(S_0 - S)/S_0 \times 100$. Typical value: **85 – 95%**.
- **Theoretical O₂ per kg BOD removed** = **1.42 kg O₂/kg BOD** (from stoichiometry of C₅H₇NO₂).
- **Modified O₂ equation:** $O_2 = a'L_r + b'S_a$ where $a' \approx 0.5$, $b' \approx 0.07$–$0.10$.
- **RAS ratio (R)** typically between **0.25 and 1.0** of influent flow.
- **Modifications:** Conventional, Completely Mixed, Extended Aeration, Contact Stabilization, Oxidation Ditch, SBR, MBR.
- **Sludge bulking** is caused by filamentous bacteria (low DO, low F/M, high SVI > 200 mL/g).
- **KTU hot topics:** F/M ratio, SRT vs HRT, design calculations, process flow diagram with labels, modifications comparison table.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Students:** Practice at least **3–4 numerical problems on ASP design** (volume, F/M, oxygen demand) and memorize the **empirical coefficients $a', b'$** plus the **1.42 stoichiometric factor**. These are the **most frequently tested numerical concepts** in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Sewerage and Sanitation Engineering

Environmental Engineering, in its third module under the KTU 2024 scheme, transitions from **water supply engineering** to the equally critical domain of **wastewater collection, conveyance, and disposal** — broadly termed as **Sewerage Engineering** or **Sanitary Engineering**. The "Basic Concepts" topic lays the conceptual foundation for understanding how used water is carried away from communities, treated, and safely returned to the environment without causing public health hazards or ecological damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sewerage (n.)** — The complete infrastructure comprising the network of pipelines (sewers), pumping stations, lifting stations, manholes, and treatment works required to collect, transport, treat, and dispose of wastewater and stormwater from a community.
>
> **Sewage (n.)** — The liquid waste (containing dissolved, suspended, and floating solids) conveyed through a sewer. It is a combination of water-carried wastes from residential, commercial, industrial, and institutional establishments.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

- **Sanitary Sewage (Domestic Sewage):** The liquid waste discharged from residences, offices, institutions, and commercial buildings. It includes human excreta (faeces and urine), wash water, kitchen waste, and laundry water. It is typically **99.9% water** and **0.1% solids**.
