- **Sullage:** The relatively clean wastewater from baths, sinks, washing machines, and kitchens — *excluding* human excreta. It is sometimes called **grey water**.

- **Stormwater (Storm Sewage):** The runoff from rainfall events collected from streets, roofs, and open grounds, conveyed separately or jointly with sanitary sewage.

- **Industrial Wastewater:** Liquid waste discharged from industrial processes, which may contain toxic, hazardous, or non-biodegradable substances.

- **Sewer:** The underground conduit (pipe or closed channel) used to transport sewage from its source to the point of treatment or disposal.

- **Infiltration:** The seepage of groundwater into the sewer through pipe joints, cracks, or manhole walls.

- **Inflow:** The direct entry of water into the sewer from sources like manhole covers, cross-connections, or surface drains.

- **Effluent:** The treated or partially treated liquid outflow from a wastewater treatment plant.

- **Influent:** The untreated wastewater flowing into a treatment plant or process unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Sewage** = The waste liquid *itself* (the substance).
> - **Sewerage** = The *system* of pipes and infrastructure that carries the sewage.
> - **Sewer** = The individual *pipe* or conduit forming part of the sewerage system.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **sewerage as the "circulatory-excretory system" of a city**, much like the human body's venous system and kidneys combined. Just as blood transports waste products from cells to the kidneys for filtration and excretion, the sewerage system transports used water from buildings to the treatment plant (the "kidney") for purification before safely returning it to nature (rivers, lakes, sea — analogous to the bladder). A blockage anywhere in this urban "vein" leads to a sanitary catastrophe, just as a blood clot in a vein causes a medical emergency.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Density of sewage ≈ **1000 kg/m³** (assumed equal to water)
- Per capita sewage generation: **80% – 100%** of per capita water supply (typical KTU design value: **80%**)
- Sewer flow is assumed to follow **Darcy-Weisbach** or **Manning's** equations for open-channel/partial flow conditions.
- Minimum diameter of a sewer (as per IS 5329 / KTU design norms): **150 mm** for separate systems, **200 mm** for combined systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Sewerage Systems

A sewerage system may be configured in one of three principal ways, depending on whether sanitary sewage and stormwater are conveyed through the same pipes or separate ones.

#### 2.1.1 Separate System

In a **separate system**, two independent sets of sewers are laid:

1. **Sanitary Sewers** — carry only domestic and industrial wastewater.
2. **Storm Sewers** — carry only stormwater runoff from rains.

**Operational Logic:**
- Stormwater does not require treatment and is discharged directly into the nearest water body.
- Sanitary sewage is conveyed to the treatment plant (STP).
- Only one pumping station (for sanitary sewage) is needed.

**Why and How it Works:**
- This system is preferred in cities with **high rainfall** (so stormwater load is large and would overload a treatment plant if combined) and in cities with **flat terrain**.
- The system keeps the treatment plant size and operational cost smaller because stormwater bypasses the STP.

**Drawbacks:**
- Requires laying two sets of pipes → higher capital cost.
- Storm sewers may develop stagnation and silting during dry weather due to low velocities.
- Combined street inlets are not feasible.

#### 2.1.2 Combined System

A single sewer carries **both sanitary sewage and stormwater** together.

**Operational Logic:**
- During dry weather → only sanitary sewage flows (low flow, easy to treat).
- During rainy weather → combined flow enters the STP; when this exceeds treatment capacity, the excess is diverted to a **stormwater overflow** (regulator) and discharged untreated into the water body.

**Why and How it Works:**
- Lower initial cost (only one set of pipes).
- Suitable for **old cities** with narrow streets and dense populations.
- Self-cleansing velocity is maintained even during dry weather because dilution by sewage is significant.

**Drawbacks:**
- The STP must be designed for the **maximum combined flow** → high treatment cost.
- Stormwater dilutes sewage during heavy rain, making treatment inefficient.
- Creates pollution in receiving water bodies during overflow events.

#### 2.1.3 Partially Separate (or Composite) System

A hybrid configuration:
- The sanitary sewer receives **domestic sewage + a portion of stormwater** (e.g., from roofs, courtyards, and back yards).
- Street stormwater is carried by a separate storm drain.

This is the **most commonly adopted system in modern Indian cities** (including most KTU model designs).

### 2.2 Components of a Sewerage System

A complete sewerage system comprises the following engineered components:

1. **House Plumbing** — soil and waste pipes from the building to the street sewer.
2. **Lateral Sewer** — small-diameter pipes receiving discharge from house connections.
3. **Sub-main / Branch Sewer** — collects flow from laterals.
4. **Main / Trunk Sewer** — large-diameter conduit carrying flow to the treatment plant.
5. **Outfall Sewer** — the terminal sewer that delivers flow to the STP or disposal point.
6. **Manholes** — access points for inspection, cleaning, and joining sewers.
7. **Drop Manholes** — for sudden drops in elevation to prevent scouring.
8. **Lampholes** — for inspection of straight runs.
9. **Street Inlets (Gully Traps)** — surface-level entry points for stormwater.
10. **Catch Basins** — silt and grit collection chambers.
11. **Inverted Siphons** — used when a sewer must pass below an obstruction (river, another sewer, subway).
12. **Sewage Pumping Stations** — for lifting sewage from low-lying to high-lying zones.
13. **Stormwater Regulators / Overflows** — divert excess flow during heavy rains (in combined systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sewer Hydraulics Assumption:** Sewers are designed to flow **under gravity** and operate as **open channels** (i.e., flow with a free water surface, even inside a closed pipe). Only when the pipe is surcharged (full-bore under pressure) is it considered to behave as a pressure pipe. This is a fundamental KTU design principle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Average Daily Sewage Flow (ADF) | $Q_{avg} = P \times q \times 0.8$ | L/day | $P$ = Population, $q$ = per capita water supply (Lpcd) |
| 2 | Maximum Daily Flow (MDF) | $Q_{max,day} = 1.8 \times Q_{avg}$ | L/day | Peaking factor 1.8 (KTU standard) |
| 3 | Maximum Hourly Flow (MHHF) | $Q_{max,hr} = 2.7 \times Q_{avg}$ | L/day | Peak factor 2.7 used for sewer sizing |
| 4 | Minimum Flow | $Q_{min} = \tfrac{1}{3}$ to $\tfrac{1}{2} \times Q_{avg}$ | L/day | Used for checking self-cleansing velocity |
| 5 | Stormwater Runoff (Rational Method) | $Q_R = C \cdot i \cdot A$ | m³/s | $C$ = runoff coefficient, $i$ = rainfall intensity (m/s), $A$ = area (m²) |
| 6 | Time of Concentration (Kirpich) | $t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$ | min | $L$ = length (m), $S$ = slope |
| 7 | Manning's Equation (Sewer Flow) | $V = \tfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | m/s | $R$ = hydraulic radius, $S$ = bed slope, $n$ = Manning's coefficient |
| 8 | Self-Cleansing Velocity | $V_{min} = 0.6$ m/s (sanitary); $0.75$ m/s (combined) | m/s | To prevent silting |
| 9 | Non-Scouring Velocity | $V_{max} = 2.4$ to $3.0$ m/s | m/s | To prevent pipe erosion |
| 10 | Peak Factor (Babbitt's approximation) | $PF = \tfrac{5}{P^{1/6}}$ | dimensionless | $P$ in thousands |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions** in design:
> - $V_{min} \le V \le V_{max}$
> - Sewer diameter: **≥ 150 mm** (separate), **≥ 200 mm** (combined)
> - Minimum cover above crown: **0.7 m** under roads, **0.5 m** elsewhere.
> - Self-cleansing: $V \ge 0.6$ m/s at **peak hourly flow**, not average.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

The design of sewerage systems governs public health outcomes in urban areas. Misdimensioned sewers cause either **frequent surcharging and street flooding** (undersized) or **permanent silting and blockages** (oversized, with low velocity). KTU-aligned designs ensure that the system remains self-cleansing, hygienic, and economical. These principles apply identically to municipal drainage, hospital effluent systems, industrial effluent networks, and campus townships.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Daily Sewage Flow and Peak Factors

The average daily sewage flow from a community is derived by combining population forecasting with per-capita water demand.

**Step 1 — Establish design population.**

Use any standard population forecasting method (arithmetic, geometric, incremental increase). The KTU-approved formula is:

$$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$$

where:
- $P_n$ = population after $n$ decades
- $P_0$ = initial (present) population
- $\bar{x}$ = average decadal increase
- $\bar{y}$ = average incremental increase

**Step 2 — Convert per-capita water supply to per-capita sewage flow.**

Experience shows that **80% of the supplied water emerges as sewage** (the rest is consumed in cooking, drinking, evaporation, garden watering, leakage, etc.). Therefore:

$$q_{sewage} = 0.8 \times q_{water}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Average Daily Sewage Flow (ADF).**

$$Q_{avg} = P \times q_{sewage} = P \times 0.8 \times q_{water}$$

**Step 4 — Apply peak factors to obtain Maximum Daily and Maximum Hourly Flow.**

Sewage flow is not uniform — it peaks in the morning and evening. Empirical studies (Babbitt, Metcalf & Eddy) yield:

$$Q_{max,day} = 1.8 \times Q_{avg}$$

$$Q_{max,hr} = 2.7 \times Q_{avg}$$

**Step 5 — Convert from litres per day (L/d) to litres per second (L/s) for hydraulic design.**

$$Q_{design}\ (\text{L/s}) = \dfrac{Q_{max,hr}\ (\text{L/d})}{24 \times 3600} = \dfrac{Q_{max,hr}}{86400}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

> **Problem:** A town has a present population of 50,000 with an average decadal increase of 2,500. The incremental increase in the population for successive decades was observed as 600, 700, and 800. The per-capita water supply is 150 Lpcd. Design the average and maximum hourly sewage flow after 3 decades. Assume 80% of supplied water becomes sewage.

**Step 1 — Compute the design population at the end of 3 decades.**

$\bar{x} = 2500$

$\bar{y} = \dfrac{600 + 700 + 800}{3} = 700$

$$P_3 = 50000 + 3 \times 2500 + \dfrac{3 \times 4}{2} \times 700$$

$$\boxed{P_3 = 50000 + 7500 + 4200 = 61700}$$

**Step 2 — Compute per-capita sewage flow.**

$$q_{sewage} = 0.8 \times 150 = 120\ \text{Lpcd}$$

**Step 3 — Average Daily Sewage Flow.**

$$Q_{avg} = 61700 \times 120 = 7404000\ \text{L/day}$$

Converting to m³/day:

$$Q_{avg} = 7404\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

Converting to L/s (for design use):

$$Q_{avg} = \dfrac{7404000}{86400} \approx 85.69\ \text{L/s}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Daily Flow (1.8 × average).**

$$Q_{max,day} = 1.8 \times 85.69 \approx 154.24\ \text{L/s}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Hourly Flow (2.7 × average).**

$$Q_{max,hr} = 2.7 \times 85.69 \approx 231.36\ \text{L/s}$$

**Step 6 — Final summary in proper SI units.**

| Quantity | Value (m³/day) | Value (L/s) |
|----------|----------------|-------------|
| $Q_{avg}$ | 7404.0 | 85.69 |
| $Q_{max,day}$ | 13327.2 | 154.24 |
| $Q_{max,hr}$ | 19990.8 | 231.36 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conventional Practice:** Always compute $Q_{avg}$ in L/d first, then convert to L/s at the final step. The conversion factor $1\ \text{m}^3 = 1000\ \text{L}$ and $1\ \text{day} = 86400\ \text{s}$ are the standard reference constants.

### 3.3 Derivation: Manning's Equation (Sewer Hydraulics)

For a circular sewer flowing partially full (which is the **normal design condition**), Manning's equation is used:

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

$$Q = A \cdot V = \dfrac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

where:
- $V$ = velocity of flow (m/s)
- $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient (0.013 for concrete, 0.011 for PVC)
- $R$ = hydraulic radius = $A / P$ (m)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of flow (m²)
- $P$ = wetted perimeter (m)
- $S$ = hydraulic gradient (bed slope for uniform flow)

For a circular pipe flowing full:
- $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- $P = \pi D$
- $R = \dfrac{D}{4}$

Substituting:

$$V_{full} = \dfrac{1}{n} \left(\dfrac{D}{4}\right)^{2/3} S^{1/2} = \dfrac{0.397}{n} D^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

$$Q_{full} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot V_{full} = \dfrac{0.3117}{n} D^{8/3} S^{1/2}$$

These two equations are the **workhorse of KTU sewer design problems**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Sewerage System

| Criterion | Separate System | Combined System | Partially Separate |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|--------------------|
| Initial cost | High (two pipe networks) | Low | Moderate |
| Treatment cost | Low (stormwater excluded) | High (sized for max flow) | Moderate |
| Self-cleansing velocity | May fall in dry weather | Always maintained | Mostly maintained |
| Pollution to water body | Low | High during overflows | Low–Moderate |
| Suitable terrain | Flat or rolling | Undulating | Flat or rolling |
| Suitable rainfall | Heavy | Low to moderate | Moderate to heavy |
| Preferred in India | Less common | Older cities | **Most common in new layouts** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Components of a Sewerage System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Household and Building Fixtures] --> B[House Drains Soil and Waste Pipes]
    B --> C[House Connection Laterial]
    C --> D[Branch or Sub Main Sewer]
    D --> E[Main or Trunk Sewer]
    E --> F[Outfall Sewer]
    F --> G[Sewage Treatment Plant STP]
    G --> H[Treated Effluent Discharge to River or Sea]
    G --> I[Sewage Pumping Station if Required]
    I --> E
    D --> J[Manholes Inspection Junctions]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    F --> K[Stormwater Overflow Regulator]
    K --> H
    E --> L[Inverted Siphon Crossing Below Obstruction]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Sewerage Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Sewerage Systems]
    S1 --> S2[Separate System]
    S1 --> S3[Combined System]
    S1 --> S4[Partially Separate or Composite System]
    S2 --> S2A[Sanitary Sewer to STP]
    S2 --> S2B[Storm Sewer Direct to Water Body]
    S3 --> S3A[Single Pipe to STP or Overflow]
    S3 --> S3B[Stormwater Overflow to Water Body]
    S4 --> S4A[Sanitary Plus Roof and Yard Runoff to STP]
    S4 --> S4B[Street Stormwater via Separate Drain to Water Body]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Sewer Hydraulics Design Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Step 1 Estimate Design Population Pn]
    P2[Step 2 Compute Per Capita Sewage Flow 0.8 of Water Supply]
    P3[Step 3 Average Daily Flow Qavg equals Pn times qsewage]
    P4[Step 4 Apply Peak Factor to get Maximum Hourly Flow]
    P5[Step 5 Convert to L per s for Hydraulic Use]
    P6[Step 6 Apply Manning Equation to find V and Q for Trial Diameter]
    P7[Step 7 Check Self Cleansing Velocity greater than 0.6 m per s]
    P8[Step 8 Check Non Scouring Velocity less than 3.0 m per s]
    P9[Step 9 Finalize Diameter D and Slope S]
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above intentionally use uppercase plain text inside the Mermaid node labels and avoid any reserved keywords as node IDs, in accordance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
*Define the terms: (i) Sewage, (ii) Sewerage, (iii) Sullage. Differentiate between a sewer and a sewerage system.*

**Model Answer:**

- **Sewage:** The spent water of a community containing dissolved, suspended, and floating solids, conveyed through sewers. It is the *waste liquid* itself.
- **Sewerage:** The entire network of sewers, manholes, pumping stations, and treatment units that collects, conveys, treats, and disposes of sewage. It is the *infrastructure*.
- **Sullage:** The relatively clean wastewater from kitchens, bathrooms, and laundries that does not contain human excreta. Also called **grey water**.

**Sewer vs. Sewerage:**
- A **sewer** is an individual underground pipe or conduit carrying sewage from houses to the treatment plant.
- A **sewerage system** is the integrated infrastructure of sewers plus all appurtenances (manholes, pumping stations, treatment works) that together transport and treat sewage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (3-mark trap):** Writing "sewerage is sewage" or "sewer is the whole system" — these are the most common errors and cost full marks. Memorize: *Sewage = liquid; Sewerage = system; Sewer = pipe.*

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**
*List any six components of a sewerage system and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Manholes** — Access points for inspection, cleaning, and joining sewers at junctions or bends.
2. **Lampholes** — Vertical pipes used for inspection and flushing of long straight sewer reaches.
3. **Drop Manholes** — Provided where a sewer suddenly drops in elevation, to prevent erosion of the downstream sewer.
4. **Street Inlets (Gullies)** — Surface-level inlets to admit stormwater into the storm sewer.
5. **Catch Basins** — Chambers to trap grit, sand, and debris from stormwater before it enters the sewer.
6. **Inverted Siphons** — Used when a sewer must pass below an obstruction (river, railway) — flow is under pressure.
7. **Stormwater Regulators** — Divert excess flow from a combined sewer to a water body during heavy rain.
8. **Sewage Pumping Stations** — Lift sewage from low-lying areas to higher elevations for gravity flow downstream.

(Any six explained with one line of function = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **separate, combined, and partially separate systems of sewerage**. Compare them on at least five parameters and state which is most commonly adopted in modern Indian cities. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A city has a present population of 80,000, growing at a geometric rate of 2% per annum. The per-capita water supply is 135 Lpcd. Estimate (i) the population after 20 years, (ii) the average daily sewage flow, and (iii) the maximum hourly sewage flow. Assume 80% of supplied water becomes sewage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Sewerage Systems:**

- **Separate System:** Two independent pipe networks — one for sanitary sewage, one for stormwater. Sanitary sewage is treated; stormwater is discharged directly into a water body. *Advantage:* smaller STP, no dilution; *Disadvantage:* higher capital cost, dry-weather silting in storm sewers.

- **Combined System:** A single pipe carries both sanitary and storm sewage. During dry weather, flow is low; during rain, excess flow is discharged via a regulator. *Advantage:* lower initial cost, self-cleansing velocity maintained; *Disadvantage:* large STP required, dilution problem, overflow pollution.

- **Partially Separate (Composite) System:** Sanitary sewer receives domestic sewage plus some stormwater (from roofs, courtyards). Street stormwater flows in a separate drain. This is the **most commonly adopted system in modern Indian cities** because it balances economy and efficiency.

**Comparison Table (5 parameters):**

| Parameter | Separate | Combined | Partially Separate |
|-----------|----------|----------|---------------------|
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Treatment cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Self-cleansing | Poor in dry weather | Good | Good |
| Pollution | Low | High | Moderate |
| Adoption in India | Rare | Old cities | **Most common** |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

*(i) Population after 20 years (Geometric Growth):*

$$P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$$

$$P_{20} = 80000 \times (1 + 0.02)^{20}$$

$$P_{20} = 80000 \times (1.4859)$$

$$\boxed{P_{20} \approx 118872 \text{ persons}}$$

*(ii) Average Daily Sewage Flow:*

$$q_{sewage} = 0.8 \times 135 = 108\ \text{Lpcd}$$

$$Q_{avg} = 118872 \times 108 = 12838176\ \text{L/day}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{avg} \approx 12838.18\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \approx 148.6\ \text{L/s}}$$

*(iii) Maximum Hourly Sewage Flow:*

$$Q_{max,hr} = 2.7 \times Q_{avg}$$

$$Q_{max,hr} = 2.7 \times 148.6$$

$$\boxed{Q_{max,hr} \approx 401.2\ \text{L/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Part B 14-mark trap):**
> 1. In geometric growth, students often forget to use **decade** conversion when the rate is given per annum — they substitute $n = 20$ directly with $r = 2$, which is correct for *per annum* problems, but **wrong** if the rate is per decade. Always read the units of $r$.
> 2. Many students write the final answer in m³/day but forget the L/s conversion that examiners expect for design use. Always state **both units** when possible.
> 3. In the comparison table, "marking is based on rows, not paragraphs" — examiners allot 1 mark per correctly compared parameter. Bullet rows, not essays.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice 2]**

**(a)** Define the term **self-cleansing velocity**. State its value for separate and combined sewerage systems. Explain why maintaining this velocity is critical in sewer design. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A town with a present population of 40,000 has the following population data for the last four decades:

| Decade | Population |
|--------|-----------|
| 1970 | 10,000 |
| 1980 | 16,000 |
| 1990 | 24,000 |
| 2000 | 32,000 |

Estimate the population in **2040 using the Incremental Increase Method**. The per-capita water supply is 150 Lpcd. Compute the **maximum daily sewage flow** in m³/day. Assume 80% of supplied water emerges as sewage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Self-Cleansing Velocity:**

**Definition:** The minimum velocity of flow in a sewer that prevents the deposition of suspended solids and ensures that the sewer remains free from silting and blockage.

- For **separate system:** $V_{min} = 0.6\ \text{m/s}$ (at peak hourly flow)
- For **combined system:** $V_{min} = 0.75\ \text{m/s}$ (at peak hourly flow)

**Why it is critical:**

1. Sewage contains organic and inorganic suspended solids. If velocity falls below $V_{min}$, solids settle at the invert, leading to **silt accumulation**.
2. Silting **reduces the cross-sectional area** available for flow, eventually causing **surcharging and street flooding**.
3. Anaerobic decomposition of organic deposits releases **H₂S**, which corrodes concrete sewers and produces foul odours.
4. Once silt accumulates, cleaning is expensive and disruptive.

Hence, designers must ensure that the **velocity at peak hourly flow ≥ self-cleansing velocity**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (Incremental Increase Method):**

*Step 1 — Calculate decadal increase ($\bar{x}$):*

- 1970 → 1980: $16000 - 10000 = 6000$
- 1980 → 1990: $24000 - 16000 = 8000$
- 1990 → 2000: $32000 - 24000 = 8000$

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{6000 + 8000 + 8000}{3} = \dfrac{22000}{3} \approx 7333$$

*Step 2 — Calculate incremental increase ($\bar{y}$):*

- 1st increment: $8000 - 6000 = 2000$
- 2nd increment: $8000 - 8000 = 0$

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{2000 + 0}{2} = 1000$$

*Step 3 — Apply the formula for population after 4 decades (2000 → 2040):*

$$P_{n} = P_{0} + n \cdot \bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$$

$$P_{2040} = 32000 + 4 \times 7333 + \dfrac{4 \times 5}{2} \times 1000$$

$$P_{2040} = 32000 + 29332 + 10000$$

$$\boxed{P_{2040} = 71332 \text{ persons}}$$

*Step 4 — Compute per-capita sewage flow:*

$$q_{sewage} = 0.8 \times 150 = 120\ \text{Lpcd}$$

*Step 5 — Average Daily Sewage Flow:*

$$Q_{avg} = 71332 \times 120 = 8559840\ \text{L/day} = 8559.84\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

*Step 6 — Maximum Daily Sewage Flow (factor 1.8):*

$$Q_{max,day} = 1.8 \times 8559.84$$

$$\boxed{Q_{max,day} \approx 15407.71\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Alternative 14-mark trap):**
> 1. In the Incremental Increase Method, students often forget that **$n$ = number of decades** — using $n = 40$ (years) is a classic error that yields a 7-fold inflated answer.
> 2. The second term in the formula is $n \cdot \bar{x}$ (linear growth) and the third is $\tfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$ (growth-of-growth). Conflating these two terms loses 3–4 marks.
> 3. In the definition of self-cleansing velocity, examiners specifically look for the phrase **"at peak hourly flow"** — omitting this phrase costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sewage** = the liquid waste; **Sewerage** = the system; **Sewer** = the pipe.
- **Sewerage systems:** Separate, Combined, Partially Separate. The latter is **most common in modern India**.
- **Design population** is forecast by Arithmetic Increase, Geometric Increase, or Incremental Increase methods.
- **Sewage flow ≈ 80% of water supply** (per KTU convention).
- **Peak factors:** $1.8 \times$ (max daily) and $2.7 \times$ (max hourly) — multiply by $Q_{avg}$.
- **Self-cleansing velocity** = 0.6 m/s (separate) / 0.75 m/s (combined) — measured at **peak hourly flow**.
- **Non-scouring velocity** = 2.4 to 3.0 m/s — upper limit to prevent pipe erosion.
- **Minimum sewer diameter:** 150 mm (separate), 200 mm (combined).
- **Manning's equation** is the workhorse for sewer hydraulics; sewers are designed as **open channels**, not pressure pipes.
- **Conversion factors to memorize:** $1\ \text{m}^3 = 1000\ \text{L}$; $1\ \text{day} = 86400\ \text{s}$.
- **Sewer appurtenances** — manholes, drop manholes, lampholes, catch basins, inverted siphons, pumping stations, regulators.
- **Infiltration and inflow** must be accounted for in old, leaky sewers but are usually negligible in new PVC/concrete pipes.
- **KTU examiners love** definitions (3-mark questions) and population forecasting + flow estimation (7-mark sub-parts). Master both for guaranteed marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of a Conventional Activated Sludge Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of a Conventional Activated Sludge Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conventional Activated Sludge Process (ASP):** A **suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment** system in which a **flocculent biomass** (activated sludge) is continuously maintained in suspension in an **aerated tank** under an aerobic environment, metabolizing the dissolved organic matter present in sewage. The treated liquid–solid mixture is separated in a **secondary clarifier**, with a portion of the settled biomass **recycled** to maintain the required **Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS)** concentration, and the **excess sludge** wasted for further handling.

It is classified as a **preceded nitrification / non-nitrification, plug-flow (or completely-mixed) aerobic treatment** unit, designed for **secondary treatment** of municipal sewage. It belongs to the family of **secondary biological treatment units** of the *Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)* hierarchy.

### Design Operating Ranges (KTU Board Standard Values)

| Parameter | Conventional ASP Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| F/M Ratio (Food/Microorganism) | **0.2 – 0.4** | day⁻¹ |
| Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT, θ) | **4 – 8** | hours |
| Sludge Retention Time (SRT, θ_c) | **5 – 15** | days |
| MLSS (X) | **1500 – 3000** | mg/L |
| MLVSS / MLSS ratio | **0.7 – 0.8** | – |
| Recirculation Ratio (R) | **0.25 – 0.75** | – |
| Return Sludge Concentration (X_R) | **6000 – 12000** | mg/L |
| BOD Removal Efficiency | **85 – 95** | % |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aerobic Cafeteria" Model

Imagine a **buffet restaurant** where customers arrive carrying plates of food (the **influent sewage**), and **waiters** (the **microorganisms in activated sludge**) are stationed at tables to consume the food. The restaurant (the **aeration tank**) supplies continuous **fresh air** through HVAC ducts (the **aerators/blowers**) to keep the waiters energetic. Spilled food plates are removed periodically (**wasting of excess sludge**), while the waiters stay on duty to keep serving (**sludge recirculation**). Customers leave through the **exit door** (the **secondary clarifier effluent**), but waiters remain inside.

If the food supply is **too high** (High F/M), the waiters get overwhelmed — poor digestion. If the food is **too low** (Low F/M), the waiters start eating each other (endogenous decay). Hence, a **balanced, steady-state feeding rate** is the engineering design goal of the Conventional ASP.

## 1.3 Components of a Conventional Activated Sludge Plant

> [!NOTE]
> A conventional ASP typically consists of **four major unit operations/processes**:
> 1. **Aeration Tank** — biological reactor where BOD reduction occurs
> 2. **Aeration System** — supply of oxygen (mechanical/diffused aerators)
> 3. **Secondary Clarifier** — solid–liquid separation
> 4. **Sludge Recirculation & Wasting System** — MLSS control

The effluent from primary sedimentation enters the aeration tank, where it is **mixed with return activated sludge (RAS)** and aerated for the required HRT. The mixed liquor then flows to the secondary clarifier, where the clarified supernatant is discharged as treated effluent, and the settled sludge is either returned to the aeration tank or wasted.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis — Substrate Removal Kinetics

The Conventional ASP is designed using the **Lawrence-McCarty model** (1970), which is the most widely accepted framework in KTU-style problems. It is based on two fundamental design parameters:

- **F/M Ratio (Food to Microorganism Ratio)**
- **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT / SRT, θ_c)**

The substrate utilization rate is governed by:

$$r_{su} = -\frac{k \cdot X \cdot S}{K_s + S}$$

For sewage with BOD much higher than K_s, the rate simplifies to **first-order kinetics in biomass**, which is the basis of the **completely-mixed activated sludge** design (used conventionally).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why F/M and SRT control the design:**
> - **F/M too high** → Dispersed growth, poor flocculation, high effluent BOD
> - **F/M too low** → Pin-point floc, bulking risk, endogenous respiration
> - **SRT** controls the **sludge age**, which in turn dictates which microbial species dominate (carbonaceous removers vs. nitrifiers)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Conventional ASP Design

**Step 1 — Quantify Influent & Effluent Characteristics**
- Determine flow rate **Q** (MLD or m³/d)
- Influent BOD (S₀) and required effluent BOD (S or Sₑ)

**Step 2 — Select MLSS (X) and F/M Ratio**
- Based on the desired level of treatment; conventional → X = 1500–3000 mg/L, F/M = 0.2–0.4 d⁻¹

**Step 3 — Compute Aeration Tank Volume (V)**
- Using the F/M relationship: $V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X}$

**Step 4 — Check Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**
- $\theta = V/Q$ should lie between 4 and 8 hours for conventional ASP

**Step 5 — Compute Sludge Production (Px)**
- Using observed yield: $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$

**Step 6 — Compute Recirculation Ratio (R)**
- Mass balance on clarifier: $R = \dfrac{X}{X_R - X}$

**Step 7 — Compute Oxygen Requirement**
- $O_2 = \dfrac{Q(S_0 - S)}{f} - 1.42 \cdot P_x$

**Step 8 — Compute Air Requirement**
- Convert O₂ demand to standard air using transfer efficiency (η) and oxygen content

**Step 9 — Secondary Clarifier Design**
- Surface overflow rate, weir loading, and solid flux

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F/M Ratio | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | d⁻¹ | 0.2 – 0.4 |
| 2 | Aeration Tank Volume | $V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X}$ | m³ | – |
| 3 | HRT | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | h | 4 – 8 |
| 4 | Observed Yield | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | – | 0.2 – 0.4 |
| 5 | Sludge Production (dry) | $P_x = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ | kg/d | – |
| 6 | Recirculation Ratio | $R = \dfrac{X}{X_R - X}$ | – | 0.25 – 0.75 |
| 7 | Oxygen Demand (O₂) | $O_2 = \dfrac{Q(S_0 - S)}{f} - 1.42 \cdot P_x$ | kg/d | – |
| 8 | f-factor (BOD to O₂) | $f = 0.65$ (typ.) | – | 0.6 – 0.8 |
| 9 | Standard Air Required | $V_{air} = \dfrac{O_2}{0.232 \cdot \eta_{OTE} \cdot \rho_{air}}$ | m³/d | – |
| 10 | Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) | $SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A_{clarifier}}$ | m³/m²·d | 25 – 35 |
| 11 | Solid Loading Rate (SLR) | $SLR = \dfrac{(Q+R \cdot Q) \cdot C}{A}$ | kg/m²·d | 100 – 150 |
| 12 | BOD Removal Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100$ | % | 85 – 95 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants used in KTU board problems:**
> - Conversion factor **1.42** = O₂ required to oxidize one kg of cell tissue (theoretical)
> - f = 0.65 (oxygen equivalent of BOD removed = 65% is utilized for synthesis, balance oxidized)
> - Oxygen content in air = **23.2% by weight**
> - Density of air = **1.2 kg/m³** at NTP
> - Standard OTE (oxygen transfer efficiency) for fine bubble diffuser = **8 – 12%**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Conventional ASP is the **backbone of municipal wastewater treatment** in India — adopted in STPs of major cities including **Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, and Kochi**. It serves as a benchmark against which **high-rate ASP variants** (e.g., Extended Aeration, Oxidation Ditch, SBR, MBBR) are compared. The design methodology taught here is a precursor to advanced **biological nutrient removal (BNR)** systems, which extend the conventional design with **anoxic zones** for denitrification and **anaerobic zones** for biological phosphorus removal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Aeration Tank Volume from F/M Definition

The **Food to Microorganism ratio (F/M)** is the daily mass of BOD applied per unit mass of MLSS in the aeration tank:

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{\text{Mass of BOD applied per day}}{\text{Mass of MLSS in tank}}$$

Substituting the standard forms:

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$$

Rearranging for **V**:

$$V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X}$$

This is the **most fundamental design equation** for the aeration tank.

## 3.2 Derivation — Recirculation Ratio from Mass Balance

Performing a **steady-state mass balance on biomass** around the secondary clarifier, with **inflow** = mixed liquor flow (Q + Q·R) at concentration X, and **outflow** split into effluent (Q, concentration X_e ≈ 0) and return sludge (Q·R, concentration X_R):

$$(Q + QR) \cdot X = Q \cdot X_e + QR \cdot X_R$$

Neglecting X_e (negligible solids in clarified effluent), we get:

$$Q \cdot X + Q R \cdot X = Q R \cdot X_R$$

$$X (1 + R) = R \cdot X_R$$

Solving for R:

$$R = \frac{X}{X_R - X}$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Observed Yield Coefficient

The **net sludge production** accounts for both **synthesis** (yield coefficient Y) and **endogenous decay** (coefficient k_d). The observed yield is defined as:

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

The **net waste sludge produced per day** is:

$$P_x = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$$

## 3.4 Derivation — Theoretical Oxygen Demand

The total BOD removed from the wastewater (Q·(S₀ − S)) is divided into two fates:
1. A fraction (1 − f) is **oxidized** to provide energy for synthesis → O₂ demand = Q(S₀−S)(1−f)
2. A fraction f is **assimilated** into new cell mass → no immediate O₂ demand

However, the **endogenous respiration** of the synthesized biomass consumes O₂. The O₂ equivalent of the wasted sludge is **1.42 kg O₂ per kg VSS destroyed**.

Total O₂ demand:

$$O_2 = \frac{Q (S_0 - S)}{f} - 1.42 \cdot P_x$$

The factor **1/f** (with f = 0.65) means that for every kg of BOD removed, 1.54 kg of O₂ is supplied for energy metabolism, while 1.42 kg is recovered as O₂ credit for the sludge that decays endogenously.

## 3.5 Complete Worked-Out KTU Board Problem (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Data (KTU-style Givens):**
> - Average sewage flow, **Q = 10 MLD = 10,000 m³/d**
> - Influent BOD, **S₀ = 250 mg/L**
> - Required effluent BOD, **S = 30 mg/L**
> - MLSS in aeration tank, **X = 2000 mg/L**
> - F/M ratio = **0.3 d⁻¹**
> - Return sludge concentration, **X_R = 10,000 mg/L**
> - Yield coefficient, **Y = 0.5 mg VSS/mg BOD**
> - Endogenous decay coefficient, **k_d = 0.06 d⁻¹**
> - Mean Cell Residence Time, **θ_c = 10 d**
> - f-factor = **0.65**
> - Oxygen transfer efficiency of diffuser, **η_OTE = 8%**

### Step 1 — BOD Removal Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{250 - 30}{250} \times 100 = 88\%$$

### Step 2 — Aeration Tank Volume

$$V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X}$$

$$V = \frac{10{,}000 \times 250}{0.3 \times 2000} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{600}$$

$$V = 4166.67 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{4166.67}{10{,}000} \text{ d} = 0.4167 \text{ d}$$

Converting to hours:

$$\theta = 0.4167 \times 24 = 10 \text{ h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This HRT is on the **higher end** of conventional ASP (typical 4–8 h). For a true conventional plant, F/M = 0.2 d⁻¹ would give HRT ≈ 15 h. The values reflect a moderately loaded system.

### Step 4 — Recirculation Ratio

$$R = \frac{X}{X_R - X} = \frac{2000}{10{,}000 - 2000} = \frac{2000}{8000} = 0.25$$

### Step 5 — Sludge Production (Observed Yield & Mass)

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{0.5}{1 + 0.06 \times 10} = \frac{0.5}{1.6} = 0.3125$$

$$P_x = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$$

$$P_x = 0.3125 \times 10{,}000 \times (250 - 30) \times 10^{-3} = 0.3125 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.22$$

$$P_x = 687.5 \text{ kg/d (dry solids)}$$

Assuming sludge contains ~2.5% solids (typical thickened waste activated sludge):

$$V_{sludge} = \frac{P_x / 0.025}{1000} = \frac{27{,}500}{1000} = 27.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

### Step 6 — Oxygen Requirement

$$O_2 = \frac{Q(S_0 - S)}{f} - 1.42 \cdot P_x$$

$$O_2 = \frac{10{,}000 \times (250 - 30) \times 10^{-3}}{0.65} - 1.42 \times 687.5$$

$$O_2 = \frac{2200}{0.65} - 976.25 = 3384.62 - 976.25$$

$$O_2 = 2408.37 \text{ kg/d}$$

### Step 7 — Air Requirement (Standard Conditions)

Oxygen content in air by mass = 23.2%; density of air = 1.2 kg/m³; OTE = 8%

$$V_{air} = \frac{O_2}{0.232 \times \eta_{OTE} \times \rho_{air}}$$

$$V_{air} = \frac{2408.37}{0.232 \times 0.08 \times 1.2} = \frac{2408.37}{0.02227}$$

$$V_{air} = 108{,}146 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \approx 75.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

### Step 8 — Secondary Clarifier Design

Assuming SOR = 30 m³/m²·d:

$$A = \frac{Q}{SOR} = \frac{10{,}000}{30} = 333.33 \text{ m}^2$$

Diameter (assuming circular clarifier):

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 333.33}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{424.4} = 20.6 \text{ m}$$

### Step 9 — Summary Design Table

| Parameter | Computed Value |
|---|---|
| BOD Removal Efficiency | 88% |
| Aeration Tank Volume | 4166.67 m³ |
| HRT | 10 h |
| Recirculation Ratio | 0.25 |
| Return Sludge Flow (QR) | 2500 m³/d |
| Observed Yield | 0.3125 |
| Sludge Production (dry) | 687.5 kg/d |
| Sludge Volume (thickened) | 27.5 m³/d |
| Oxygen Required | 2408.4 kg/d |
| Air Required | 108,146 m³/d |
| Clarifier Area | 333.3 m² |
| Clarifier Diameter | 20.6 m |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of a Conventional Activated Sludge Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INFLUENT\n(Primary Effluent)"] --> B["AERATION TANK\nMixed Liquor\nMLSS 2000 mg/L\nHRT 4-8 h"]
    B -- "Aeration" --> B1["Blower /\nDiffuser System\nOTE 8%"]
    B --> C["SECONDARY\nCLARIFIER\nSOR 30 m3/m2d"]
    C -- "Clarified\nEffluent" --> D["TREATED\nEFFLUENT\nBOD 30 mg/L"]
    C -- "Settled\nSludge" --> E["SLUDGE\nWELL /\nPUMP STATION"]
    E -- "Return\nActivated Sludge\nR = 0.25" --> B
    E -- "Waste\nActivated Sludge\nWAS" --> F["SLUDGE\nTREATMENT\nUnit"]
    F --> G["DIGESTER /\nDEWATERING"]
    G --> H["DISPOSAL\nLandfill /\nLand Application"]
    B1 -.-> B
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Conventional ASP

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STREAM"]
        In1["Q m3/d Sewage"]
        In2["S0 mg/L Influent BOD"]
    end

    subgraph AERATION["AERATION TANK MODULE"]
        Vbox["Volume V m3\n= Q S0 / F/M X"]
        Xbox["MLSS X mg/L\n1500 to 3000"]
        FMbox["F/M Ratio\n0.2 to 0.4 per d"]
        HRTbox["HRT 4 to 8 h"]
        Vbox --> HRTbox
        Xbox --> HRTbox
        FMbox --> HRTbox
    end

    subgraph CLARIFIER["SECONDARY CLARIFIER MODULE"]
        SORbox["SOR 25 to 35 m3/m2 d"]
        AREAbox["Area A m2\n= Q / SOR"]
        XRbox["Return Sludge XR\n6000 to 12000 mg/L"]
        Rbox["R = X / XR minus X"]
        AREAbox --> Rbox
        XRbox --> Rbox
    end

    subgraph BIOKIN["BIOKINETIC MODULE"]
        Ybox["Y = 0.5 mg VSS/mg BOD"]
        KDbox["kd = 0.06 per d"]
        SRTbox["SRT theta c 5 to 15 d"]
        Yobsbox["Yobs = Y / 1 plus kd theta c"]
        Pxbox["Px kg/d = Yobs Q S0 minus S"]
        Ybox --> Yobsbox
        KDbox --> Yobsbox
        SRTbox --> Yobsbox
        Yobsbox --> Pxbox
    end

    subgraph AERATIONO2["OXYGEN TRANSFER MODULE"]
        O2box["O2 kg/d\n= Q S0 minus S over f\nminus 1.42 Px"]
        AIRbox["Air m3/d\n= O2 / 0.232 eta rho"]
        fbox["f = 0.65"]
        O2box --> AIRbox
        fbox --> O2box
    end

    INPUT --> AERATION
    AERATION --> CLARIFIER
    CLARIFIER --> BIOKIN
    BIOKIN --> AERATIONO2
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Conventional ASP — Annotated Mass Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INFLOW["INFLOW STREAMS"]
        Q1["Q (Sewage)\nBOD S0"]
        QR1["QR (Return Sludge)\nConc XR"]
    end
    subgraph TANK["AERATION TANK"]
        ML["Mixed Liquor X\nVolume V\nHRT theta"]
    end
    subgraph CLAR["SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
        SEP["Solid Liquid\nSeparation"]
    end
    subgraph OUTFLOW["OUTFLOW STREAMS"]
        QE["Q (Effluent)\nBOD S"]
        QR2["QR (Recycle)"]
        QW["QW (Wasted Sludge)\nPx kg/d"]
    end
    Q1 --> ML
    QR1 --> ML
    ML --> SEP
    SEP --> QE
    SEP --> QR2
    QR2 --> QR1
    SEP --> QW
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **ML (Mixed Liquor)** block in the above diagram represents the **steady-state biomass concentration (X)** maintained in the aeration tank. The recycle stream sustains this concentration, while the waste stream controls the **Sludge Retention Time (θ_c)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Conventional Activated Sludge Process? List its major components.**

**Model Answer:**
The Conventional Activated Sludge Process (ASP) is a **suspended-growth aerobic biological wastewater treatment** process in which a **flocculent microbial culture (activated sludge)** is maintained in continuous suspension in an **aeration tank** to oxidize dissolved organic matter under controlled conditions.

**Major Components:**
1. Aeration tank
2. Aeration system (blowers + diffusers / mechanical aerators)
3. Secondary clarifier
4. Sludge recirculation and wasting system

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define F/M ratio and state its significance in the design of an activated sludge plant.**

**Model Answer:**
The **F/M ratio (Food to Microorganism ratio)** is defined as the **daily mass of BOD applied per unit mass of MLSS** in the aeration tank:

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} \quad (\text{day}^{-1})$$

**Significance:**
- It governs the **substrate utilization rate**, **effluent quality**, and **sludge settleability**.
- A **high F/M (>0.4)** causes dispersed growth, poor BOD removal, and high effluent suspended solids.
- A **low F/M (<0.2)** causes endogenous respiration, pinpoint floc, and bulking.
- For **conventional ASP**, the design F/M is kept between **0.2 and 0.4 day⁻¹**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Design Problem

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Design the **aeration tank** of a conventional activated sludge plant for the following data:
- Sewage flow, Q = 8 MLD
- Influent BOD, S₀ = 220 mg/L
- Effluent BOD, S = 20 mg/L
- MLSS, X = 2500 mg/L
- F/M ratio = 0.25 d⁻¹

Compute: (i) Aeration tank volume, (ii) Hydraulic retention time, (iii) Recirculation ratio (assume X_R = 10,000 mg/L), and (iv) BOD removal efficiency.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the same plant, compute: (i) Oxygen requirement, (ii) Standard air required, and (iii) Volume of waste sludge produced per day. Assume Y = 0.5, k_d = 0.06 d⁻¹, θ_c = 10 d, f = 0.65, OTE = 8%, and density of air = 1.2 kg/m³.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Aeration Tank Design

**Given:** Q = 8000 m³/d, S₀ = 220 mg/L, S = 20 mg/L, X = 2500 mg/L, F/M = 0.25 d⁻¹, X_R = 10,000 mg/L

**(i) Aeration Tank Volume:**

$$V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X} = \frac{8000 \times 220}{0.25 \times 2500} = \frac{1{,}760{,}000}{625}$$

$$\boxed{V = 2816 \text{ m}^3}$$

**(ii) Hydraulic Retention Time:**

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{2816}{8000} \text{ d} = 0.352 \text{ d}$$

Converting:

$$\theta = 0.352 \times 24 = \boxed{8.45 \text{ h}}$$

**(iii) Recirculation Ratio:**

$$R = \frac{X}{X_R - X} = \frac{2500}{10{,}000 - 2500} = \frac{2500}{7500}$$

$$\boxed{R = 0.333}$$

**(iv) BOD Removal Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{220 - 20}{220} \times 100 = \boxed{90.9\%}$$

#### Part (b) — Oxygen, Air, and Sludge Design

**(i) Oxygen Requirement:**

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{0.5}{1 + 0.06 \times 10} = \frac{0.5}{1.6} = 0.3125$$

$$P_x = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.3125 \times 8000 \times (220-20) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_x = 0.3125 \times 8000 \times 0.2 = \boxed{500 \text{ kg/d}}$$

$$O_2 = \frac{Q(S_0 - S)}{f} - 1.42 \cdot P_x = \frac{8000 \times 0.2}{0.65} - 1.42 \times 500$$

$$O_2 = \frac{1600}{0.65} - 710 = 2461.54 - 710$$

$$\boxed{O_2 = 1751.54 \text{ kg/d}}$$

**(ii) Standard Air Required:**

$$V_{air} = \frac{O_2}{0.232 \times \eta_{OTE} \times \rho_{air}} = \frac{1751.54}{0.232 \times 0.08 \times 1.2}$$

$$V_{air} = \frac{1751.54}{0.02227} = \boxed{78{,}648 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \approx 54.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(iii) Volume of Waste Sludge (assuming 2.5% solids):**

$$V_{sludge} = \frac{P_x / 0.025}{1000} = \frac{500 / 0.025}{1000} = \frac{20{,}000}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{V_{sludge} = 20 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Design Problem

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] For a conventional activated sludge plant treating **12 MLD** of sewage with **S₀ = 300 mg/L** and **S = 30 mg/L**, compute: (i) the **aeration tank volume** if F/M = 0.3 d⁻¹ and MLSS = 2000 mg/L, (ii) **HRT**, and (iii) the **sludge wasting rate** in kg/d given Y_obs = 0.32.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute: (i) **Recirculation ratio** if X_R = 8000 mg/L, (ii) **Oxygen demand** per day, and (iii) **Standard air requirement** in m³/min using f = 0.65 and OTE = 10%, ρ_air = 1.2 kg/m³.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Aeration Tank & Sludge

**(i) Aeration Tank Volume:**

$$V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X} = \frac{12{,}000 \times 300}{0.3 \times 2000} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^6}{600}$$

$$\boxed{V = 6000 \text{ m}^3}$$

**(ii) HRT:**

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{6000}{12{,}000} = 0.5 \text{ d} = \boxed{12 \text{ h}}$$

**(iii) Sludge Wasting Rate:**

$$P_x = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.32 \times 12{,}000 \times 0.27 = \boxed{1036.8 \text{ kg/d}}$$

#### Part (b) — Recirculation, O₂, Air

**(i) Recirculation Ratio:**

$$R = \frac{X}{X_R - X} = \frac{2000}{8000 - 2000} = \frac{2000}{6000} = \boxed{0.333}$$

**(ii) Oxygen Demand:**

$$O_2 = \frac{Q(S_0 - S)}{f} - 1.42 \cdot P_x = \frac{12{,}000 \times 0.27}{0.65} - 1.42 \times 1036.8$$

$$O_2 = \frac{3240}{0.65} - 1472.26 = 4984.62 - 1472.26$$

$$\boxed{O_2 = 3512.36 \text{ kg/d}}$$

**(iii) Standard Air Required:**

$$V_{air} = \frac{O_2}{0.232 \times \eta_{OTE} \times \rho_{air}} = \frac{3512.36}{0.232 \times 0.10 \times 1.2} = \frac{3512.36}{0.02784}$$

$$V_{air} = 126{,}161 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} = \boxed{87.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in V Calculation:** Students often forget to convert mg/L to kg/m³ (divide by 10⁶) or fail to use **Q in m³/d** — leading to 1000× errors.
> 2. **Sign Error in O₂ Equation:** The credit term **1.42 · P_x** is **subtracted**, not added. A reverse sign leads to grossly inflated air blower sizing.
> 3. **Confusion between F/M and SRT:** KTU questions often test whether the student can relate F/M to θ_c via kinetics. Mixing the two parameters in a single equation loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **HRT must lie in 4–8 h for conventional ASP** — if it does not, students should comment on whether the design represents a *high-rate* or *extended aeration* variant.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert final air answer** from m³/d to m³/min (a frequent 1-mark loss).
> 6. **f-factor confusion:** f = 0.65 is the **BOD-to-O₂ conversion**, not the BOD removal fraction. Many students invert it.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Conventional Activated Sludge Plant Design**

- **Core Equation:** $V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X}$ — memorize in the F/M form.
- **Conventional ASP Design Range:** F/M = 0.2–0.4 d⁻¹, HRT = 4–8 h, MLSS = 1500–3000 mg/L, SRT = 5–15 d.
- **Recirculation Ratio Formula:** $R = \dfrac{X}{X_R - X}$ — derived from a clarifier mass balance assuming X_e ≈ 0.
- **Observed Yield:** $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ — accounts for endogenous decay.
- **Sludge Wasting:** $P_x = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ — in **kg/d** when Q is in m³/d and concentrations in mg/L (× 10⁻³).
- **Oxygen Demand:** $O_2 = \dfrac{Q(S_0 - S)}{f} - 1.42 \cdot P_x$, with **f = 0.65**.
- **Air Requirement:** Divide O₂ by $0.232 \times \eta_{OTE} \times \rho_{air}$ (0.232 = mass fraction of O₂ in air).
- **Conversion Constant 1.42:** kg O₂ required to oxidize 1 kg of cell mass.
- **Secondary Clarifier Design:** SOR = 25–35 m³/m²·d; area $A = Q/SOR$.
- **Check HRT after computing V** — must lie in conventional range, or note the design as high-rate / extended-aeration variant.
- **Effluent Quality Expectation:** 85–95% BOD removal, with effluent BOD 20–30 mg/L.
- **KTU Board Trick:** When given MLVSS instead of MLSS, the F/M formula uses X_v (volatile fraction ≈ 0.7 × MLSS). Read the givens carefully.
- **Process Variants:** Conventional (F/M 0.2–0.4), High-Rate (F/M 0.4–1.5), Extended Aeration (F/M 0.05–0.15).
- **Always convert final answers** to readable units (m³ for V, h for HRT, m³/min for air, kg/d for sludge).

> **End of Topic Notes — Design of a Conventional Activated Sludge Plant | Module 3 | PCCET503**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Up Flow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) Reactor (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Up Flow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) Reactor (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) Reactor** is a high-rate, suspended-growth anaerobic treatment system in which wastewater is introduced at the bottom of the reactor and flows **upward** through a dense blanket of **granular anaerobic sludge**, where biodegradable organic matter is converted into biogas (primarily CH₄ and CO₂) by a consortium of anaerobic microorganisms. The defining engineering feature of the UASB reactor is the integrated **Three-Phase Separator (Gas-Solid-Separator, GSS)** located at the top, which simultaneously separates biogas, retained sludge, and treated effluent — eliminating the need for a separate clarifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This topic is a **"Concept Only"** module in Module 4. Students are expected to know the working principle, components, advantages/disadvantages, and typical design ranges — *not* detailed mathematical design calculations. Questions are typically short (Part A) or descriptive (Part B).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tall, transparent cylindrical fish tank** filled with heavy, sand-like pellets (the granular sludge) at the bottom. When you pour dirty, smelly kitchen wastewater from the *bottom* of the tank (instead of the top), the water has to **weave its way up through the sludge pellets**. As it does, microscopic bacteria living on and between these pellets "eat" the organic waste, releasing tiny gas bubbles (like soda bubbles) that float upward. The bubbles carry treated water with them, but a clever **baffle-and-umbrella arrangement** at the top of the tank catches the bubbles (collecting them as biogas fuel) and knocks the sludge pellets back down so they don't escape. Clean water flows out the top.

This is essentially a UASB reactor — a self-mixing, fuel-producing, wastewater-cleaning device.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Reactor Height:** Typically **4 m to 7 m** (sometimes up to 10 m)
- **Upflow Velocity (V_up):** **0.5 m/h to 1.0 m/h**
- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** **4 to 12 hours**
- **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** **5 to 15 kg COD/m³·d**
- **Sludge Concentration in Blanket:** **30 to 50 g VSS/L**
- **Biogas Composition:** **CH₄: 60–70%**, **CO₂: 30–40%**, trace H₂S

> [!NOTE]
> The UASB reactor was developed in the late 1970s by **Prof. Gatze Lettinga** at Wageningen University, Netherlands. It revolutionized anaerobic treatment by allowing very high biomass concentrations **without any carrier media**, purely through the natural formation of dense microbial granules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The UASB reactor operates on three coupled physical-biogeochemical principles:

1. **Upflow Hydraulic Pattern (Plug-Flow Approximation)**
   - *Why:* Upflow ensures intimate contact between incoming substrate (wastewater) and the sludge blanket.
   - *How:* Wastewater enters through a **distribution system** at the base, distributing flow uniformly across the reactor cross-section to prevent short-circuiting and dead zones.

2. **Sludge Granulation (Self-Immobilization)**
   - *Why:* Dense, fast-settling granules allow extremely high biomass retention (up to 30–50 g VSS/L) without any filter media.
   - *How:* Under favorable conditions (sufficient substrate, appropriate upflow velocity, adequate nutrients), anaerobic bacteria self-aggregate into **0.5–3 mm diameter granules** with excellent settling properties (settling velocity > 20 m/h).

3. **Three-Phase Separation (Internal Clarification)**
   - *Why:* Biogas must be captured, sludge must be retained, and clarified effluent must exit — all simultaneously.
   - *How:* Biogas bubbles rising from the sludge blanket strike the underside of a **deflector hood**, are deflected into a **gas collection dome**, while sludge particles drop back via gravity. Clarified effluent passes over **weir plates** in an external settling zone.

### 2.2 Structural Components (Top to Bottom)

| Component | Location | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas Collection Dome** | Topmost | Captures biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) for use/safe flaring |
| **Deflector Hood / Baffle** | Below gas dome | Deflects rising gas bubbles; allows sludge to settle back |
| **Settling Zone (External)** | Upper section, above hood | Final clarification of effluent before discharge |
| **Effluent Weirs** | Top of settling zone | Uniform collection of treated water |
| **Sludge Blanket Zone** | Middle and lower sections | Site of active anaerobic biodegradation (acidogenesis + methanogenesis) |
| **Inlet Distribution System** | Reactor base | Uniform upward flow distribution via multiple nozzles |
| **Reactor Shell** | Outer wall | Gas-tight construction (typically reinforced concrete or steel) |

### 2.3 Microbial Degradation Pathway (Conceptual)

The anaerobic digestion in the sludge blanket proceeds through four sequential stages:

$$ \text{Complex Organics} \xrightarrow{\text{Hydrolysis}} \text{Monomers} \xrightarrow{\text{Acidogenesis}} \text{VFAs} \xrightarrow{\text{Acetogenesis}} \text{Acetate} + \text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Methanogenesis}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Methanogenesis is the rate-limiting step.** The slow-growing **methanogenic archaea** are highly sensitive to pH, temperature, and toxic shock. That is why UASB reactors require careful operational control.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Range | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Upflow Velocity (V_up)** | $V_{up} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/h | Q = flow rate, A = cross-sectional area |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours | V = reactor volume |
| **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg COD/m³·d | S₀ = influent substrate concentration |
| **COD Removal Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | % | Typically 60–80% for UASB alone |
| **Biogas Yield (approximate)** | $G \approx 0.35 \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot Q$ | m³/d | At 35°C, ~0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed |
| **Sludge Retention Time (SRT)** | $SRT = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q \cdot X_e}$ | days | Very high (≥30 days) due to granule retention |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Relationship:** In UASB, **SRT >> HRT**. This decoupling is the *fundamental* reason anaerobic systems achieve high biomass and high treatment efficiency — biomass is retained independently of hydraulic flow.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar and distillery industries** (high-strength effluents, OLR 15–25 kg COD/m³·d)
- **Pulp and paper mills**
- **Food processing** (dairy, starch, brewery)
- **Slaughterhouse wastewater**
- **Municipal sewage treatment** in tropical climates (e.g., Brazil, India, Colombia)
- **Biogas recovery** for boiler fuel or electricity generation — turns a treatment cost into a revenue stream

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Upflow Velocity and HRT

Starting from the fundamental definitions for a cylindrical UASB reactor:

$$ V = A \cdot H $$

$$ \theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} $$

$$ V_{up} = \dfrac{Q}{A} $$

**Step 1:** Substitute $V = A \cdot H$ into the HRT equation.

$$ \theta = \dfrac{A \cdot H}{Q} $$

**Step 2:** Rearrange by noting that $\dfrac{A}{Q} = \dfrac{1}{V_{up}}$.

$$ \theta = \dfrac{H}{V_{up}} $$

**Step 3:** Solve for the upflow velocity, which is the critical design check.

$$ V_{up} = \dfrac{H}{\theta} $$

**Step 4 (KTU Exam-Style Numerical Example):**
A UASB reactor has a height of $H = 6$ m and a designed HRT of $\theta = 8$ hours. Compute the upflow velocity.

$$ V_{up} = \dfrac{6 \text{ m}}{8 \text{ h}} = 0.75 \text{ m/h} $$

**Interpretation:** This value (0.75 m/h) lies safely within the **0.5–1.0 m/h** design envelope — confirming acceptable hydraulic conditions that will not wash out the granular sludge blanket.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Linking OLR, HRT, and Influent Substrate Concentration

Starting from the definition of OLR:

$$ OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} $$

**Step 1:** Rewrite the numerator and denominator using HRT relations.

$$ V = Q \cdot \theta $$

$$ OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{Q \cdot \theta} $$

**Step 2:** Cancel $Q$ to obtain the cleanest form.

$$ OLR = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta} $$

**Step 3 (KTU Exam-Style Numerical Example):**
An industrial wastewater has $S_0 = 12{,}000$ mg/L (= 12 kg/m³) COD. If the design HRT is $\theta = 24$ hours = 1 day, compute the OLR.

$$ OLR = \dfrac{12 \text{ kg/m}^3}{1 \text{ day}} = 12 \text{ kg COD/m}^3 \cdot \text{d} $$

**Interpretation:** A value of 12 kg COD/m³·d is **comfortably within the typical 5–15 kg COD/m³·d** UASB design range — the design is hydraulically and biologically feasible.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Biogas Production Estimate

The theoretical methane yield per kg of COD removed is approximately **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD** at standard temperature and pressure (STP, 35°C).

**Step 1:** Write the COD balance across the reactor.

$$ \text{COD removed per day} = Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) $$

**Step 2:** Apply the yield factor to compute methane production.

$$ Q_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) $$

**Step 3 (KTU Exam-Style Numerical Example):**
A UASB treats $Q = 500$ m³/d of wastewater. $S_0 = 10$ kg COD/m³ and $S_e = 3$ kg COD/m³ (70% removal). Compute daily methane production.

$$ Q_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 500 \times (10 - 3) $$

$$ Q_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 500 \times 7 = 1225 \text{ m}^3\text{/d} $$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **1225 m³/d of methane** — enough to fire a small industrial boiler or generate ~3000 kWh/d of electricity via a gas engine. This is the *economic heart* of anaerobic treatment.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — UASB vs Other Anaerobic Systems

| Feature | Conventional Anaerobic Digester | Anaerobic Filter | **UASB Reactor** | Anaerobic Fluidized Bed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Biomass Retention | Low (washed out) | High (on media) | **High (granules)** | Very High (on carrier) |
| Media Required | No | Yes | **No** | Yes (sand/charcoal) |
| HRT | 20–30 d | 1–3 d | **4–12 h** | 0.5–2 h |
| OLR (kg COD/m³·d) | 1–3 | 2–6 | **5–15** | 10–40 |
| Effluent SS | High | Low | **Low** | Very Low |
| Startup Time | Long | Medium | **Medium (granulation 2–8 months)** | Long |
| Capital Cost | High | Medium | **Low–Medium** | High |

> [!NOTE]
> The UASB's competitive edge is the **combination of no media + high loading + simple construction** — making it the *workhorse* of high-rate anaerobic treatment worldwide.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — UASB Reactor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Wastewater Inlet - High Strength COD] --> B[Inlet Distribution System - Multiple Nozzles at Base]
    B --> C[Sludge Blanket Zone - Granular Anaerobic Biomass 30-50 g VSS per L]
    C --> D[Biogas Bubble Generation - CH4 plus CO2 from Methanogenesis]
    D --> E[Three Phase Separator - Deflector Hood Assembly]
    E --> F[Gas Collection Dome - Biogas Storage and Outlet]
    E --> G[Settled Sludge Return - Recycles Granules Back to Blanket]
    G --> C
    E --> H[Effluent Settling Zone - Clarification Above Hood]
    H --> I[Weir Plates - Uniform Effluent Withdrawal]
    I --> J[Treated Effluent Discharge - To Post Treatment]
    F --> K[Biogas Utilization - Boiler Fuel or Electricity]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Schematic — Cross-Sectional View of UASB Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        GasDome[Gas Collection Dome]:::gas

        SludgeBlanket[Sludge Blanket - Active Biodegradation Zone]:::sludge
        LowerBlanket[Lower Sludge Bed - Dense Granular Layer]:::sludge

    end

    InletBottom --> Distribution
    Distribution --> LowerBlanket
    LowerBlanket --> SludgeBlanket

    Deflector --> GasDome
    Deflector --> SettleZone
```
