KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between thickening and clarification in wastewater treatment. State the primary objective of each process.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Thickening | Clarification | |---|---|---| | **Primary product** | Concentrated sludge (solids) | Clarified effluent (liquid) | | **Sludge residence** | Long (≈ 24 h HRT) | Short (≈ 2–4 h HRT) | | **Settling regime** | Type III & IV (hindered + compression) | Type II (flocculant settling) | | **Design basis** | Solids flux theory | Overflow rate (Vo) | | **Typical output** | Underflow at 2–6% solids | Effluent with < 30 mg/L SS | | **Located in** | Sludge treatment line | Liquid treatment line | **Primary objective:** Thickening aims to *reduce sludge volume* by concentrating solids (so that downstream digesters/dewatering units handle less volume). Clarification aims to *produce clean effluent* by removing suspended solids from wastewater. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **State the assumptions of Kynch's theory of batch settling. Why is it important for thickener design?** **Model Answer:** **Assumptions:** 1. Settling velocity $V_s$ of a particle is a function *only* of the local solids concentration $C$, independent of time or depth. 2. The sludge is homogeneous and behaves as a single-phase fluid in the hindered settling zone. 3. The flow is one-dimensional (vertical) and the cross-section is uniform. 4. There is no significant resuspension or fluidization. 5. Wall effects are negligible. **Importance for thickener design:** - It allows prediction of the settling behavior of a thickener from a *simple batch column test* (a small-scale, inexpensive experiment). - It forms the *mathematical foundation* of the Solids Flux Theory (Yoshioka, 1957), which is the basis of continuous thickener area calculation via $A = Q C_0 / G_L$. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Thickening Problems:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency** — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you mix mg/L with kg/m³ or m/h with m/day without conversion. **Always convert and show the conversion step.** > 2. **Forgetting the safety factor** — A bare minimum safety factor of **1.25** is expected. Marks lost if you give the "bare" theoretical area. > 3. **Not identifying the settling regime** — A question mentioning "thickener" MUST be answered using Type III/IV settling. If you write Type I/II, you lose coherence marks. > 4. **Mixing up underflow velocity $U$ with settling velocity $V_s$** — These are two different quantities. > 5. **Skipping the underflow mass balance** — Always verify $Q_u = Q_0 C_0 / C_u$ to ensure consistency. > 6. **Not stating the Kynch/Yoshioka assumption** — A 1-mark question is dedicated to this. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **continuous gravity thickener**. Identify the various settling zones and the role of the rake mechanism. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A continuous gravity thickener is to be designed for an activated sludge plant. The sludge flow is $Q_0 = 250 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ at $C_0 = 7500 \text{ mg/L}$. The desired underflow concentration is $C_u = 30{,}000 \text{ mg/L}$. A laboratory batch settling test gives a limiting solids flux of $G_L = 3.5 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)}$ at an underflow velocity of $U = 0.4 \text{ m/h}$. Determine: - (i) The required thickener area. - (ii) The thickener diameter. - (iii) The underflow flow rate. - (iv) The hydraulic retention time (HRT), assuming a side water depth of 4 m. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Diagram and Explanation:** **Working of a Continuous Gravity Thickener (Block Diagram):** ``` SUPERNATANT (CLEAR LIQUID) ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ Zone A: Clear Liquid │ ──→ Effluent Weir (recycled to STP) ├────────────────────────────────┤ │ Zone B: Hindered Settling │ ← Feed Well (inlet) │ (Type III Settling) │ │ ↓ ↓ ↓ Sludge Blanket ↓ ↓ ↓ │ ├────────────────────────────────┤ │ Zone C: Compression Zone │ ← Rake mechanism (slow rotation, │ (Type IV Settling) │ 0.5 to 1.0 RPM, scrapes sludge ├────────────────────────────────┤ toward central hopper) │ Zone D: Thickened Sludge │ ──→ Underflow Pump │ (Conical Hopper) │ └────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Settling Zones (from top to bottom):** 1. **Zone A — Clear Liquid Zone:** Free of suspended solids. Supernatant flows radially outward, over an effluent weir, and is recycled to the head of the plant. 2. **Zone B — Hindered Settling Zone (Type III):** The bulk of the thickener depth. Solids settle as a "blanket" at velocity $V_s(C)$ dependent on concentration. 3. **Zone C — Compression Zone (Type IV):** Solids are in mechanical contact; the weight of overlying solids forces water out. 4. **Zone D — Thickened Sludge Zone:** Concentrated sludge accumulates in the conical hopper, from where it is pumped as underflow. **Role of the Rake Mechanism:** - **Slowly rotates** (0.5 to 1.0 RPM) about the vertical axis of the thickener. - **Gently pushes** the settled sludge toward the central hopper, where it is withdrawn as underflow. - **Breaks up** sludge bridges and gas pockets that may form in the compression zone. - **Promotes water release** by gently agitating the compression zone. - *Caution:* Rotational speed must be low; otherwise, the rake will re-suspend solids and degrade performance. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert influent concentration to kg/m³:** $$C_0 = 7500 \text{ mg/L} = 7.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Mass of solids applied per day:** $$M_{solids} = Q_0 \times C_0 = 250 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 7.5 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1875 \text{ kg/day}$$ **Step 3 — Convert limiting flux to consistent units (kg/m²/day):** $$G_L = 3.5 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)} \times 24 \text{ h/day} = 84 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{day)}$$ **Step 4 — Required thickener area (theoretical):** $$A_{theory} = \frac{M_{solids}}{G_L} = \frac{1875 \text{ kg/day}}{84 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{day)}} = 22.32 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 5 — Apply safety factor 1.25 (KTU design convention):** $$A_{design} = 1.25 \times 22.32 = 27.9 \text{ m}^2 \approx 28 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 6 — Thickener diameter (circular):** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 28}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{35.65} \approx 5.97 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{D \approx 6.0 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 7 — Underflow flow rate from mass balance:** $$Q_u = \frac{Q_0 \cdot C_0}{C_u} = \frac{250 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 7500 \text{ mg/L}}{30{,}000 \text{ mg/L}} = 62.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 8 — Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** Volume of thickener (with side water depth $H = 4$ m): $$V = A \times H = 28 \text{ m}^2 \times 4 \text{ m} = 112 \text{ m}^3$$ HRT (based on influent flow $Q_0$): $$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q_0} = \frac{112 \text{ m}^3}{250 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}} = 0.448 \text{ day} = 10.75 \text{ h}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** The HRT of ~11 h is somewhat less than the typical 24 h, but is acceptable for a *well-designed, continuously operated* thickener with a low underflow velocity. The actual operating HRT is often taken as 24 h, and the depth is correspondingly increased to 8–9 m. Always verify with the standard 24-h design HRT. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** What is **solids flux theory**? Derive the expression for the **total solids flux** in a continuous thickener. State the significance of the **limiting flux** $G_L$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A batch settling test on a primary sludge is conducted. The initial concentration is $C_0 = 12{,}000 \text{ mg/L}$ in a column of initial height $H_0 = 1.0 \text{ m}$. The mud line is observed to settle to $H_c = 0.4 \text{ m}$ at time $t_c = 2.5 \text{ h}$, which marks the transition from hindered to compression settling. Using the Coe–Coe construction, the limiting flux is determined as $G_L = 5.2 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)}$ at $U = 0.6 \text{ m/h}$. The plant must handle $Q_0 = 300 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$. Determine: - (i) The critical concentration $C_c$ at the transition point. - (ii) The required thickener area. - (iii) The underflow flow rate, assuming the underflow concentration is $C_u = 2.5 \times C_0$. - (iv) State two operational problems that may occur if the applied flux exceeds $G_L$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Solids Flux Theory:** **Definition:** The **Solids Flux Theory** (Yoshioka, 1957) is a mathematical framework that models the *steady-state downward transport* of suspended solids through a continuous thickener, expressed as mass per unit area per unit time [kg/(m²·h)]. It is the foundation of modern thickener design. **Derivation of Total Solids Flux:** Consider a horizontal control plane at depth $z$ in a continuous thickener. The total downward mass flux of solids $G_T$ through this plane has two components: 1. **Gravity flux** $G_g$ — due to the settling velocity of the sludge: $$G_g = C \cdot V_s(C)$$ where $V_s(C)$ is a function of concentration (Type III settling behavior). 2. **Underflow-induced flux** $G_u$ — due to the bulk downward movement caused by sludge withdrawal at the bottom: $$G_u = C \cdot U$$ where $U = Q_u / A$ is the underflow velocity (m/s or m/h). 3. **Total flux** — by superposition: $$\boxed{G_T = C \cdot V_s + C \cdot U = C \left( V_s + U \right)}$$ **Significance of Limiting Flux $G_L$:** - $G_L$ is the **maximum value** of the total flux $G_T$ curve, occurring at a critical concentration $C_L$. - It represents the *maximum loading* (kg solids per m² per h) that the thickener can transmit to the underflow without solids accumulation. - **Operating rule:** The applied flux $G_{applied} = Q_0 C_0 / A$ MUST be less than or equal to $G_L$. Otherwise, the thickener fails (solids accumulate and overflow the supernatant launder). - The thickener area is therefore designed as: $$A = \frac{Q_0 \cdot C_0}{G_L}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert $C_0$ to kg/m³:** $$C_0 = 12{,}000 \text{ mg/L} = 12 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Critical concentration $C_c$ at transition point (Kynch mass balance):** $$\boxed{C_c = \frac{C_0 \cdot H_0}{H_c} = \frac{12 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 1.0 \text{ m}}{0.4 \text{ m}} = 30 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of solids applied per day:** $$M_{solids} = Q_0 \times C_0 = 300 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 12 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 3600 \text{ kg/day}$$ **Step 4 — Convert limiting flux to consistent units:** $$G_L = 5.2 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)} \times 24 \text{ h/day} = 124.8 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{day)}$$ **Step 5 — Required thickener area (theoretical):** $$A_{theory} = \frac{3600}{124.8} = 28.85 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 6 — With safety factor 1.25:** $$A_{design} = 1.25 \times 28.85 = 36.06 \text{ m}^2 \approx 36 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 7 — Underflow flow rate:** $C_u = 2.5 \times C_0 = 2.5 \times 12{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ mg/L}$ $$Q_u = \frac{Q_0 \cdot C_0}{C_u} = \frac{300 \times 12{,}000}{30{,}000} = 120 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 8 — Two operational problems if $G_{applied} > G_L$:** 1. **Solids accumulation:** The thickener cannot transmit the incoming mass of solids to the underflow. The sludge blanket rises, the compression zone shifts upward, and the hindered settling zone is compressed. Eventually, solids overflow into the supernatant launder, contaminating the recycled effluent. 2. **Thickener failure / washout:** Continued overloading causes a vicious cycle — the rising sludge level exposes more of the effluent weir to contamination, and the entire thickener eventually loses its ability to clarify. The underflow becomes dilute instead of concentrated (the desired concentration cannot be achieved), and downstream digesters/dewatering units are starved of thickened sludge. > [!IMPORTANT] > **A third potential problem (advanced):** *Septicity and odor:* As the sludge blanket rises and remains in the thickener for a longer effective time, anaerobic conditions develop, generating H₂S, methane, and foul odors. This is a common operational headache in real STPs. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before KTU Exam:** - ✅ Thickening **concentrates** sludge; Clarification **purifies** effluent. - ✅ Design basis of thickener: **Solids Flux Theory (Yoshioka, 1957)**, derived from **Kynch's batch theory (1952)**. - ✅ Settling regimes in a thickener: **Type III (hindered) + Type IV (compression)** — *NOT* Type I or II. - ✅ Master equation: $A = Q_0 C_0 / G_L$. Always include a **safety factor of 1.25**. - ✅ Total flux: $G_T = C V_s + C U = C (V_s + U)$. - ✅ Underflow velocity $U$ ≠ Settling velocity $V_s$. - ✅ Kynch mass balance: $C(t) H(t) = C_0 H_0$ → $C_c = C_0 H_0 / H_c$. - ✅ Typical concentrations: Primary sludge 2–5%; Activated sludge 0.5–1.5%; Combined 3–6%. - ✅ Typical HRT for gravity thickener: ~24 hours. - ✅ Rake speed: 0.5 to 1.0 RPM (low! — to avoid re-suspension). - ✅ Supernatant is recycled to the head of the STP — this is a **mass-recovery** feature, not waste. - ✅ DAF thickening is preferred for *light* biological sludge that does not settle easily by gravity. - ✅ Centrifuge thickeners have *small footprint* but *high power and capital cost*. - ✅ Gravity belt thickeners use *polymer conditioning* and produce a drier cake (4–8% solids). - ✅ KTU design conventions: convert mg/L ↔ kg/m³ (÷1000); convert h ↔ day (×24); state all units; include safety factor; show mass balance check. - ✅ If $G_{applied} > G_L$ → solids accumulate → sludge blanket rises → supernatant contamination → thickener failure. - ✅ Coe–Coe / Talmadge–Fitch construction is the *graphical* method to determine $G_L$ from a batch settling curve. ---

topics.title: Digestion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Digestion ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sludge Digestion** is a controlled biological stabilization process in which the volatile (organic) solids present in the sewage sludge are decomposed by microbial action, converting putrescible organic matter into relatively stable end products (mineralized solids, gases, and water), thereby reducing the sludge volume, mass, and pathogen content, and producing a nuisance-free residue suitable for safe disposal or reuse as a soil conditioner. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCET503 – Environmental Engineering**, Module 4 focuses on **sewage treatment, sludge handling, and disposal**. Digestion is the heart of sludge management — the bridge between raw sludge generation and its ultimate disposal/reuse. ### 1.2 Classification of Digestion Processes There are two principal biological pathways employed in modern wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs): **(a) Aerobic Digestion** - Decomposition occurs in the **presence of dissolved oxygen**. - Aerobic microorganisms oxidize organic matter to **CO₂, H₂O, and cellular biomass (new cells)**. - Operates at ambient or thermophilic temperatures. - Used in smaller installations and activated sludge plants (extended aeration, oxidation ditches). **(b) Anaerobic Digestion (Most Important for KTU)** - Decomposition in the **absence of free oxygen**. - End products: **biogas (≈ 60–70% CH₄ + 30–40% CO₂)**, water, mineralized solids. - Used extensively for primary and combined sludges in conventional STP design. - Produces usable energy in the form of methane — a key sustainability highlight. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stomach" of a Sewage Plant > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box: "The Kitchen Compost Bin" Analogy** > Imagine sealing a kitchen waste bin airtight. Inside, microorganisms slowly break down vegetable peels, food scraps, and paper. After several weeks, the bin emits a faint "biogas" smell, the volume reduces drastically, and what remains is a dark, earthy, stable compost. **An anaerobic sludge digester works exactly like this — but engineered at industrial scale, with temperature control, mixing, gas collection, and continuous feeding.** The "stomach" metaphor is powerful: raw sludge enters, microbes "digest" it for 20–40 days, biogas is released (like flatulence!), and digested sludge exits as a benign, humus-like product. ### 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (Standard Metrics) | Metric | Standard Value | |---|---| | **Volatile Solids Reduction (VSR)** | **40–60%** (standard rate), **50–70%** (high rate) | | **Digester Detention Time (θ)** | **30–60 days** (standard), **10–20 days** (high rate) | | **Mesophilic Temperature** | **30–38 °C** (optimum ≈ **35 °C**) | | **Thermophilic Temperature** | **50–55 °C** | | **pH Range** | **6.8 – 7.4** (critical for methanogens) | | **Biogas Yield** | **0.75 – 1.12 m³ per kg of VSS destroyed** | | **Methane Content of Biogas** | **60 – 70 %** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Biological Stages of Anaerobic Digestion The complex biochemical pathway occurs in four sequential, tightly coupled phases. KTU examiners **frequently** test student understanding of these stages, so memorise them in order. **Stage 1 — Hydrolysis** - Complex polymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) are hydrolyzed by extracellular enzymes (proteases, amylases, lipases) into simpler soluble monomers: amino acids, sugars, fatty acids. - **Rate-limiting step** for lipid-rich and protein-rich sludges. - Catalyzed by facultative bacteria. **Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)** - Monomers are fermented by acidogenic bacteria into volatile fatty acids (VFAs — acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂. - pH may drop sharply if this stage outpaces the next — known as "souring" of the digester. **Stage 3 — Acetogenesis** - VFAs and alcohols are oxidized into acetic acid (CH₃COOH), H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria. - These bacteria are **obligate H₂ producers**; they require very low partial pressure of H₂ (< 10⁻⁴ atm) to remain thermodynamically viable. **Stage 4 — Methanogenesis** - The terminal and most sensitive step. - Two groups of **strict anaerobes** (archaea) act in parallel: - **Acetoclastic methanogens** (≈ 70% of CH₄) — split acetate: CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ - **Hydrogenotrophic methanogens** (≈ 30% of CH₄) — use H₂ as electron donor: 4H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O - Methanogens grow **very slowly** (doubling time 2–6 days), making the digester sensitive to shock loads, toxins, and temperature changes. ### 2.2 Standard-Rate vs High-Rate Digestion (High-Yield KTU Distinction) > [!NOTE] > **Why This Distinction Matters:** KTU questions often ask for a comparative discussion or design selection between these two. Memorise the table below — it is the most exam-relevant content in this module. | Parameter | Standard-Rate Digestion | High-Rate Digestion | |---|---|---| | Mixing | **None** (or minimal) | **Continuous, vigorous mechanical/gas mixing** | | Feeding | Intermittent | **Continuous, once or twice per day** | | Detention Time (θ) | **30 – 60 days** | **10 – 20 days** | | Temperature | Often unheated (ambient) | **Heated to 35 °C (mesophilic)** | | Loading Rate (kg VSS/m³·d) | 0.5 – 1.6 | 1.6 – 4.8 (or higher) | | VS Destruction Efficiency | 35 – 50 % | 50 – 70 % | | Supernatant Quality | Poor (high in suspended solids) | Better, but requires separation | | Capital Cost | Low | Higher (mixers, heaters, gas systems) | | Typical Application | Small communities, tropical regions | Medium-to-large municipal STPs | ### 2.3 Two-Stage Digestion A logical engineering improvement: **separate acidogenesis (Stage 1 reactor) from methanogenesis (Stage 2 reactor)**. - **First (primary) tank** is heated and mixed, used for active digestion and gas collection. - **Secondary tank** is unmixed, used for sludge thickening, supernatant separation, and storage of digested sludge. - This allows the secondary tank to be open-top (gas not collected), reducing capital cost. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — every equation here is fair game in ESE (End Semester Examination).** | # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Volume of Raw Sludge (per day)** | $V_1 = \dfrac{Q \times \text{SS} \times 10^{-3}}{\rho_s \times (1 - p_1) \times 10^{-2}}$ | m³/day | Q = sewage flow, SS = suspended solids, p₁ = moisture %, ρₛ = sludge density ≈ 1.02 × 10³ kg/m³ | | 2 | **Volume of Digester** (Standard-Rate) | $V = V_1 \times \theta$ | m³ | θ = detention time in days | | 3 | **Volume of Digester** (with seed sludge) | $V = (V_1 + V_2) \times \theta$ | m³ | V₂ = volume of seed/ripened sludge | | 4 | **Volatile Solids Loading Rate (VSLR)** | $\text{VSLR} = \dfrac{M_{VS}}{V}$ | kg VS/m³·day | Typical: 1.6 – 4.8 for high-rate | | 5 | **Volatile Solids Reduction (VSR)** | $\text{VSR} = \dfrac{M_{VS,\text{in}} - M_{VS,\text{out}}}{M_{VS,\text{in}}} \times 100$ | % | 40–60% in standard, 50–70% in high-rate | | 6 | **Mass of Volatile Solids Destroyed** | $M_d = M_{VS,\text{in}} - M_{VS,\text{out}}$ | kg/day | Drives biogas production | | 7 | **Theoretical Biogas Yield** | $G = M_d \times 0.75$ to $M_d \times 1.12$ | m³/day | Average ≈ 0.94 m³/kg VSS destroyed | | 8 | **Specific Gas Yield** | $g = \dfrac{G}{M_{VS,\text{loaded}}}$ | m³/kg VS | Typical: 0.4 – 0.5 m³/kg VS loaded | | 9 | **Digester Surface Area** (cylindrical) | $A = \dfrac{V}{H}$ | m² | H = liquid depth (commonly 6–9 m) | | 10 | **Digester Diameter** (cylindrical) | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4V}{\pi H}}$ | m | D:H ratio typically 1:1 to 2:1 | | 11 | **Moisture Content of Digested Sludge** | $p_2 = p_1 - \Delta p$ (typical 92–95%) | % | Higher than raw (p₁ ≈ 95–98%) due to liquid release | | 12 | **Volume of Digested Sludge** | $V_3 = \dfrac{M_s \times (1 - VSR/100)}{\rho_s \times (1 - p_2)}$ | m³/day | M_s = mass of dry solids fed | | 13 | **pH Buffering Capacity** (VFA/Alkalinity ratio) | $\dfrac{\text{VFA (mg/L as acetic)}}{\text{Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)}}$ | — | Healthy digester: ratio < 0.4 | ### 2.5 Factors Affecting Anaerobic Digestion (Triggers Toxicity/Process Upset) - **Temperature** — Methanogens are highly temperature-sensitive. A drop of even 2–3 °C from 35 °C can reduce gas production by 30–50%. - **pH** — Must remain in the narrow window 6.8 – 7.4. VFA accumulation (acid crash) causes methanogen inhibition. - **Toxic substances** — Heavy metals (Cu, Cr, Ni, Zn), ammonia (> 1500 mg/L as N), sulfides, chlorinated organics, and detergents. - **Loading rate** — Overloading causes VFA accumulation and foaming. - **Mixing** — Ensures uniform temperature, pH, and substrate distribution; prevents scum formation. - **Nutrient balance** — Optimum C:N:P ratio ≈ **100 : 1.6 : 0.4** (very approximate, raw sludge is usually adequate). ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Energy recovery** — Captured methane can be flared, used in a boiler to heat the digester, or fed to a combined heat and power (CHP) generator. This makes WWTPs **net-energy-positive**, a key concept in sustainable urban design. - **Pathogen reduction** — Thermophilic digestion and the longer SRT achieve significant Class A/B biosolids compliance. - **Sludge volume reduction** — Reduces downstream dewatering, transport, and landfill costs. - **Nutrient recovery** — Digested sludge is rich in N, P, and organic carbon → usable as a soil conditioner / biofertilizer. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Digester Sizing (Standard-Rate) **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]:** A sewage treatment plant serves a population of **50,000** with an average sewage flow of **135 Lpcd**. The suspended solids (SS) concentration in sewage is **220 mg/L**, of which **70% is volatile**. The moisture content of the raw sludge is **95%** and its specific gravity is **1.02**. Design a **standard-rate anaerobic digester** with a detention time of **30 days**. Assume the digester has a **cylindrical** shape with a liquid depth equal to its diameter. **Required:** - (a) Daily volume of raw sludge. - (b) Required volume of the digester. - (c) Diameter and depth (since D = H). --- **Step 1 — Compute the daily sewage flow (Q)** $$ Q = P \times q = 50{,}000 \times 135 \, \text{L/day} = 6{,}750{,}000 \, \text{L/day} $$ $$ Q = 6750 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} \quad \left(\text{since } 1 \, \text{m}^3 = 1000 \, \text{L}\right) $$ *Logic:* Total sewage generated per day = Population × per capita sewage contribution. **Step 2 — Compute the total mass of suspended solids (SS) entering per day** $$ M_{SS} = Q \times \text{SS} = 6750 \times 220 \, \text{mg/L} \times 1000 \, \text{L/m}^3 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{kg/mg} $$ Converting: $Q = 6.75 \times 10^6$ L/day, and $220$ mg/L = $220 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/L. $$ M_{SS} = 6.75 \times 10^6 \times 220 \times 10^{-6} = 1485 \, \text{kg/day} $$ *Logic:* Mass = Volume × Concentration (with consistent units). **Step 3 — Assume ≈ 60% of SS becomes sludge** (typical for primary settling) $$ M_s = 0.60 \times 1485 = 891 \, \text{kg/day} $$ *Logic:* Not all suspended solids settle; the fraction depends on the primary clarifier efficiency. **Step 4 — Compute the daily volume of raw sludge (V₁)** $$ V_1 = \dfrac{M_s}{\rho_s \times (1 - p_1)} = \dfrac{891}{1020 \times (1 - 0.95)} $$ $$ V_1 = \dfrac{891}{1020 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{891}{51} \approx 17.47 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ *Logic:* Moisture content of 95% means 5% is solids by mass; dividing solid mass by (density × solids fraction) gives the total sludge volume. **Step 5 — Compute the digester volume (V)** $$ V = V_1 \times \theta = 17.47 \times 30 = 524.1 \, \text{m}^3 $$ *Logic:* In a standard-rate (unmixed) digester, the sludge must be retained for the full detention time. **Step 6 — Compute digester dimensions** (D = H, cylindrical) $$ V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times H = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^3 \quad \text{(since } D = H\text{)} $$ $$ D^3 = \dfrac{4V}{\pi} = \dfrac{4 \times 524.1}{3.1416} = 667.3 $$ $$ D = \sqrt[3]{667.3} \approx 8.74 \, \text{m} $$ *Logic:* For an economic D:H ratio of 1:1, depth equals diameter, and we solve the cubic. --- **Summary of Results (Numerical 1):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Daily raw sludge volume, V₁ | **17.47 m³/day** | | Digester volume, V | **524.1 m³** | | Diameter, D | **8.74 m** | | Liquid depth, H | **8.74 m** | --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Biogas Yield Estimation **Problem Statement:** For the digester in Numerical 1, the volatile solids reduction is **50%**. Estimate the **daily biogas production** and the **mass of methane** generated. Assume biogas contains **65% CH₄** by volume at **STP** (density of CH₄ ≈ 0.717 kg/m³). **Step 1 — Compute mass of volatile solids fed daily** $$ M_{VS} = 0.70 \times M_s = 0.70 \times 891 = 623.7 \, \text{kg VS/day} $$ *Logic:* 70% of the dry sludge mass is volatile (organic). **Step 2 — Compute mass of volatile solids destroyed** $$ M_d = \text{VSR} \times M_{VS} = 0.50 \times 623.7 = 311.85 \, \text{kg VS destroyed/day} $$ *Logic:* Half of the volatile solids are biologically mineralized in a standard-rate digester. **Step 3 — Compute biogas volume produced** Using a middle-range specific gas yield of **0.94 m³ per kg VS destroyed**: $$ G = 0.94 \times M_d = 0.94 \times 311.85 = 293.1 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ *Logic:* The standard textbook yield range is 0.75 – 1.12 m³/kg VS destroyed; 0.94 is the average. **Step 4 — Compute methane volume** $$ V_{CH_4} = 0.65 \times 293.1 = 190.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ *Logic:* 65% of biogas is methane by volume in a healthy mesophilic digester. **Step 5 — Compute methane mass** $$ M_{CH_4} = V_{CH_4} \times \rho_{CH_4} = 190.5 \times 0.717 = 136.6 \, \text{kg CH}_4/\text{day} $$ *Logic:* Mass = Volume × Density at STP. This methane has an energy content of ≈ 55.5 MJ/kg, equivalent to ≈ **7576 MJ/day** — enough to heat the digester and supply surplus power. --- **Summary of Results (Numerical 2):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | VS fed daily | **623.7 kg VS/day** | | VS destroyed daily | **311.85 kg VS/day** | | Biogas volume | **293.1 m³/day** | | Methane volume | **190.5 m³/day** | | Methane mass | **136.6 kg/day** | --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Design Choice Decision | Decision Criterion | Choose **Standard-Rate** if… | Choose **High-Rate** if… | |---|---|---| | Plant capacity (population) | < 20,000 | > 50,000 | | Capital budget | Severely limited | Adequate for mixers/heaters | | Operator skill level | Low / untrained | Trained, full-time O&M staff | | Climate | Tropical, warm ambient air | Temperate / cold climate | | Sludge volume | Small | Large — footprint matters | | Energy recovery goal | Not required | Strongly desired (CHP) | | Pathogen reduction requirement | Basic (Class B biosolids) | Stringent (Class A) | | Loading rate tolerance | Low (sensitive) | Moderate | > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Note for Examiners:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, design-type numericals expect a **stated assumption block** before solving. Always list: assumed moisture content, assumed SS-to-sludge conversion (60–70%), assumed VSR, and assumed specific gas yield. Students who skip this lose 1–2 marks routinely. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Architecture — Anaerobic Sludge Digestion Process Flow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an anaerobic digestion system, showing inputs, biological sub-processes, intermediate streams, and outputs (energy, stabilized sludge, supernatant). This is the schematic most KTU board examiners expect students to reproduce in the exam. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Sludge Feed] --> B[Sludge Thickener] B --> C[Heated Primary Digester] C --> D[Methane Biogas Collection Dome] D --> E[Gas Holder] E --> F[Boiler for Digester Heating] E --> G[CHP Generator / Power Export] C --> H[Secondary Digester Tank] H --> I[Supernatant Liquor Recycle to Headworks] H --> J[Digested Sludge Withdrawal] J --> K[Sludge Dewatering] K --> L[Biofertilizer Cake for Land Application] H --> M[Scum and Foam Removal] ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Four-Stage Biochemistry Block This block diagram captures the **microbial cascade** inside the digester — a frequently tested concept. ```mermaid flowchart LR raw[Raw Sludge
Proteins, Carbohydrates, Lipids] --> s1[Stage 1 Hydrolysis
Amino Acids, Sugars, Fatty Acids] s1 --> s2[Stage 2 Acidogenesis
VFAs, Alcohols, H2, CO2] s2 --> s3[Stage 3 Acetogenesis
Acetate, H2, CO2] s3 --> s4a[Stage 4a Acetoclastic Methanogens
70 percent of CH4] s3 --> s4b[Stage 4b Hydrogenotrophic Methanogens
30 percent of CH4] s4a --> out[Biogas 65 percent CH4 + 35 percent CO2] s4b --> out out --> energy[Energy Recovery CHP] out --> stabilized[Stabilized Digested Sludge
Class A B Biosolids] ``` ### 4.3 Standard-Rate vs High-Rate Comparison — Decision Logic Block ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Start Plant Design Decision] --> q1{Population Served?} q1 -->|< 20,000| q2{Budget Tight?} q1 -->|> 50,000| q3{Energy Recovery Needed?} q2 -->|Yes| std[Standard-Rate Digester
30 to 60 days
No mixing no heating] q2 -->|No| q4{Climate?} q3 -->|Yes| hr[High-Rate Digester
10 to 20 days
Heated to 35 C
Mechanically mixed] q3 -->|No| q4 q4 -->|Tropical| std q4 -->|Cold| hr std --> end[Final Design] hr --> end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** In the KTU board exam, when asked to "draw a neat sketch" of a sludge digester, the examiner expects at minimum: (1) the cylindrical tank with conical bottom, (2) the floating gas holder/cover, (3) the inlet pipe, (4) the outlet/draw-off pipe at the bottom, (5) the supernatant draw-off pipe at mid-depth, and (6) a heating coil or external heat exchanger. A labelled sketch carries **5 marks minimum** in ESE questions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define sludge digestion. List the two principal types of sludge digestion and state any two advantages of anaerobic digestion over aerobic digestion.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Sludge digestion is a biological process in which the volatile (organic) solids of sewage sludge are decomposed by microorganisms under controlled conditions into stable end products such as mineralized solids, water, and biogas, thereby reducing sludge volume, mass, and pathogenicity. The two principal types are: 1. **Aerobic digestion** — decomposition in the presence of free oxygen. 2. **Anaerobic digestion** — decomposition in the absence of free oxygen. Two advantages of anaerobic digestion: 1. It produces usable **biogas (methane)** as an energy by-product, which can be harnessed for digester heating or power generation. 2. It produces a much **lower biomass yield** (less new cell growth), resulting in lower residual sludge mass compared to aerobic digestion. *(A third acceptable point: lower energy input for aeration, and better volume/mass reduction.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Explain the four stages of anaerobic digestion with the end products of each stage.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Stage | Process | End Products | |---|---|---| | **1. Hydrolysis** | Complex polymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) are split by extracellular enzymes into soluble monomers. | Amino acids, sugars, long-chain fatty acids | | **2. Acidogenesis** | Monomers are fermented by acidogenic bacteria. | Volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, CO₂ | | **3. Acetogenesis** | VFAs and alcohols are oxidized by acetogens. | Acetic acid (acetate), H₂, CO₂ | | **4. Methanogenesis** | Strict anaerobic archaea convert acetate and H₂/CO₂ into methane. | **CH₄ (≈ 60–70%) + CO₂ (≈ 30–40%)** | --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **ESE Module Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 ESE questions in this module offer Module Internal Choice. Below, **Question A and Question B are independent alternatives**. A student must answer EITHER A OR B (not both). Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a standard-rate anaerobic sludge digester**. Compare it with a **high-rate digester** in terms of mixing, detention time, temperature, VS destruction, and loading rate. **(b) [7 Marks]** A city with a population of **1,00,000** produces sewage at **150 Lpcd** with an SS concentration of **250 mg/L**. The primary clarifier removes **65%** of the SS, and the resulting sludge has **95% moisture content** (specific gravity 1.02). Design a **standard-rate digester** with a **40-day detention time** to handle this sludge. Assume the digester is cylindrical with a **diameter to depth ratio of 1:1.5**. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working [7 Marks]** A standard-rate anaerobic digester is a **cylindrical reinforced-concrete or steel tank**, typically **6–10 m in diameter** and **6–12 m deep**, with a **conical or hopper bottom** for sludge collection and draw-off. **Key Construction Features (sketch labels expected by examiner):** 1. **Tank shell** — cylindrical RCC, often with an outer insulation layer. 2. **Fixed or floating gas dome** — collects biogas from the sludge surface; dome rises and falls with gas pressure. 3. **Inlet pipe** — raw sludge enters through the top side of the digester. 4. **Outlet / draw-off pipe** — at the conical bottom; allows removal of digested sludge. 5. **Supernatant draw-off** — at mid-depth, returns clarified liquor back to the head of the plant. 6. **Sampling and inspection ports** — for monitoring temperature, pH, and gas composition. 7. **Gas collection pipe** — leads biogas to a gas holder, boiler, or CHP unit. **Working Principle:** Raw sludge is fed intermittently (typically once per day). Without mixing, three zones form naturally — a **scum layer on top**, a **supernatant (clear liquor) zone in the middle**, and a **digesting sludge zone at the bottom**. Anaerobic bacteria decompose organic matter slowly, producing biogas. The detention time is long (30–60 days) because the unmixed conditions make mass transfer slow. **Comparison Table [Mandatory in your answer]:** | Parameter | Standard-Rate | High-Rate | |---|---|---| | Mixing | None / minimal | Continuous, mechanical or gas | | Detention Time | 30–60 days | 10–20 days | | Temperature | Ambient (often unheated) | Heated to 35 °C (mesophilic) | | Loading Rate (kg VS/m³·day) | 0.5 – 1.6 | 1.6 – 4.8 | | VS Destruction | 35 – 50% | 50 – 70% | | Capital Cost | Low | Higher (mixers, heaters, gas system) | --- **(b) Numerical Design [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Daily sewage flow** $$ Q = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 150 = 1.5 \times 10^7 \, \text{L/day} = 15{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 2 — Mass of SS in sewage** $$ M_{SS} = Q \times \text{SS} = 15{,}000 \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 3750 \, \text{kg/day} $$ (Equivalent: $M_{SS} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ L/day} \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/L} = 3750$ kg/day) **Step 3 — Mass of sludge solids (after 65% SS removal)** $$ M_s = 0.65 \times 3750 = 2437.5 \, \text{kg/day} $$ **Step 4 — Daily volume of raw sludge** $$ V_1 = \dfrac{M_s}{\rho_s \times (1 - p_1)} = \dfrac{2437.5}{1020 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{2437.5}{51} \approx 47.79 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 5 — Digester volume (Standard-Rate, θ = 40 days)** $$ V = V_1 \times \theta = 47.79 \times 40 = 1911.6 \, \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 6 — Digester dimensions** (D : H = 1 : 1.5, so H = 1.5D) $$ V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times H = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times 1.5D = \dfrac{1.5\pi}{4} D^3 $$ $$ D^3 = \dfrac{4V}{1.5\pi} = \dfrac{4 \times 1911.6}{1.5 \times 3.1416} = \dfrac{7646.4}{4.7124} = 1622.5 $$ $$ D = \sqrt[3]{1622.5} \approx 11.74 \, \text{m} $$ $$ H = 1.5D \approx 17.61 \, \text{m} $$ **Results Summary Table:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Daily raw sludge volume, V₁ | **47.79 m³/day** | | Digester volume, V | **1911.6 m³** | | Digester diameter, D | **11.74 m** | | Liquid depth, H | **17.61 m** | --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **factors affecting anaerobic digestion** of sewage sludge. How does a digester **"sour"**, and what remedial measures are taken to recover it? **(b) [7 Marks]** A digester receives **400 kg VS/day** and achieves a **55% VSR**. If the specific gas yield is **0.90 m³/kg VS destroyed** and the methane fraction is **65%**, calculate the **(i)** daily biogas volume, **(ii)** daily methane volume, and **(iii)** equivalent electrical energy if the methane is burned in a CHP unit with **35% electrical efficiency** (calorific value of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg, density = 0.717 kg/m³). --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Factors Affecting Anaerobic Digestion [7 Marks]** | Factor | Effect | Optimum/Critical Value | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | Methanogens are highly sensitive; gas production drops sharply outside 30–38 °C | 35 °C (mesophilic) or 55 °C (thermophilic) | | **pH** | Methanogens inhibited outside neutral range | 6.8 – 7.4 (critical) | | **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFA)** | High VFA → "souring" (acid crash) | VFA < 2000 mg/L as acetic | | **Alkalinity** | Buffers pH; low alkalinity = acid-sensitive | > 2000 mg/L as CaCO₃ | | **Toxic Substances** | Heavy metals (Cu, Cr, Ni, Zn), ammonia > 1500 mg/L, sulfides inhibit methanogens | Minimize inputs from industry | | **Loading Rate** | Overloading → VFA accumulation, foaming | 1.6 – 4.8 kg VS/m³·day (high-rate) | | **Mixing** | Ensures uniform contact; prevents scum and dead zones | Continuous in high-rate | | **C:N:P Ratio** | Nutrient balance for microbes | ≈ 100 : 1.6 : 0.4 | | **Retention Time (SRT)** | Methanogens need SRT > their doubling time | ≥ 10–15 days for high-rate | **"Souring" of a Digester:** - Occurs when the rate of acidogenesis exceeds methanogenesis. - VFA accumulates → pH drops below 6.5 → methanogens inhibited → gas production collapses. - Common triggers: organic overload, toxic shock, sudden temperature drop, hydraulic washout. **Remedial Measures:** 1. **Stop or drastically reduce feeding** to allow the digester to recover. 2. **Re-seed** the digester with a healthy inoculum from another operational unit. 3. **Restore pH** by adding lime (Ca(OH)₂) cautiously or sodium bicarbonate. 4. **Re-establish temperature** to 35 °C using auxiliary heating. 5. **Gradually reintroduce feed** at reduced loading rates over 1–2 weeks. 6. **Increase alkalinity** through controlled addition of bicarbonate-rich streams. --- **(b) Numerical — Energy Recovery [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Mass of VS destroyed daily** $$ M_d = \text{VSR} \times M_{VS} = 0.55 \times 400 = 220 \, \text{kg VS destroyed/day} $$ **Step 2 — Daily biogas volume** $$ G = M_d \times g = 220 \times 0.90 = 198 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 3 — Daily methane volume** $$ V_{CH_4} = 0.65 \times 198 = 128.7 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 4 — Mass of methane** $$ M_{CH_4} = V_{CH_4} \times \rho_{CH_4} = 128.7 \times 0.717 = 92.28 \, \text{kg CH}_4/\text{day} $$ **Step 5 — Thermal energy in methane** $$ E_{\text{thermal}} = M_{CH_4} \times \text{CV} = 92.28 \times 55.5 = 5121.5 \, \text{MJ/day} $$ **Step 6 — Electrical energy output (35% efficiency)** $$ E_{\text{elec}} = 0.35 \times 5121.5 = 1792.5 \, \text{MJ/day} $$ Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ): $$ E_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{1792.5}{3.6} \approx 497.9 \, \text{kWh/day} $$ **Results Summary Table:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | VS destroyed | **220 kg/day** | | Biogas produced | **198 m³/day** | | Methane volume | **128.7 m³/day** | | Methane mass | **92.28 kg/day** | | Electrical energy | **≈ 497.9 kWh/day** | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch in sludge volume formula** — Most common mistake. Students forget to convert mg/L to kg/m³ or L to m³. Always show unit conversion explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the 60–70% SS-to-sludge conversion** — The exam problem does not always state this. If it says "SS removed by primary clarifier is X%", use that. If it says "raw sludge has SS concentration Y mg/L", then the entire SS becomes sludge solids (no settling loss). > 3. **Using total SS instead of volatile SS for biogas yield** — Biogas comes from **volatile** solids destruction, not total solids. This single error gives an answer 30–40% off. > 4. **D:H ratio mistakes** — If D = H, set H = D and solve cubic. If D : H = 1 : 1.5, set H = 1.5D, substitute, solve cubic. Do not mix the two. > 5. **Methane density confusion** — At STP, ρ(CH₄) ≈ 0.717 kg/m³. At 35 °C and 1 atm, it is ≈ 0.626 kg/m³. KTU problems almost always assume STP unless stated. > 6. **Mark loss on sketches** — Examiners allot 2–3 marks for a labelled sketch. If you skip it, you lose those marks automatically even if the text is excellent. > 7. **Skipping the "State Assumptions" preamble** — A 1-line assumption block at the start of a numerical (e.g., "Assume 65% SS removal, raw sludge moisture = 95%, VSR = 50%") earns 0.5–1 free mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam** - ✅ **Sludge digestion** = biological stabilization of organic solids in sludge. Two types: **aerobic** and **anaerobic**. - ✅ Anaerobic digestion is preferred for **larger plants** because of biogas recovery and lower biomass yield. - ✅ The four stages are **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**. The slowest step is hydrolysis for particulate-rich sludge; methanogenesis for soluble-rich sludge. - ✅ **Methanogens are strict anaerobes** — sensitive to O₂, pH shifts, temperature changes, and toxic shock. - ✅ **Standard-Rate** = no mixing, no heating, 30–60 day θ, low cost, low VS destruction. - ✅ **High-Rate** = mixed, heated to 35 °C, 10–20 day θ, higher VS destruction, energy recovery. - ✅ **Two-Stage** = primary (mixed, heated) + secondary (settling, storage) — most efficient large-scale design. - ✅ Optimum conditions: **T = 35 °C**, **pH = 6.8–7.4**, **C:N:P = 100:1.6:0.4**, **VFA/Alkalinity < 0.4**. - ✅ VSR = 40–60% (standard) and 50–70% (high-rate). - ✅ Biogas yield = 0.75 to 1.12 m³/kg VS destroyed (average ≈ 0.94 m³/kg). - ✅ Methane fraction = 60–70% of biogas by volume. - ✅ Methane calorific value = **55.5 MJ/kg**; density at STP = **0.717 kg/m³**. - ✅ "**Souring**" = VFA accumulation causing pH crash and methanogen inhibition. Remedy = stop feeding, restore pH, re-seed, restart gradually. - ✅ **Sizing formula** (cylindrical, D:H known): Substitute H = kD into $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 H$, solve the cubic for D. - ✅ **Always state assumptions** before solving numericals — this earns free marks. - ✅ **Always include a labelled sketch** in design questions — minimum 2–3 marks reserved. - ✅ Pathogen reduction → digester qualifies sludge for **Class A or Class B biosolids** under USEPA 40 CFR Part 503. - ✅ Biogas can be **flared, used in a boiler for digester heating, or used in a CHP unit for electricity + heat** — making WWTPs net-energy-positive.

topics.title: Dewatering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET503 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dewatering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dewatering** is the unit operation/process employed in environmental engineering for the **physical removal of water from sludge** (residual semi-solid material produced during wastewater treatment) to reduce its moisture content, thereby achieving a significant reduction in volume and converting the sludge into a handleable, transportable, and disposable form.

In the context of KTU Module 4, dewatering is treated as the **penultimate step in sludge management** — it follows thickening and conditioning, and precedes ultimate sludge disposal (landfilling, incineration, or land application).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Dewatering is distinct from **Thickening** (which only reduces water content partially using gravity/ flotation) and from **Drying** (which removes the last traces of moisture thermally). Dewatering is the **intermediate mechanical water-removal stage** producing a sludge cake typically containing **15%–35% dry solids (DS)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine wringing out a soaked sponge by placing it between two heavy plates and pressing them together. The mechanical pressure forces water out, leaving a damp (but no longer dripping) solid. This is precisely what **mechanical dewatering** does to sludge — applies force (vacuum, pressure, centrifugal) to squeeze water out of the sludge matrix.

A second analogy: think of **chenna/paneer making** in a traditional Indian kitchen. Milk solids are coagulated, hung in a muslin cloth, and allowed to drain under gravity — a rudimentary form of sludge dewatering. Industrial sludge dewatering is the engineered, high-volume, accelerated version of the same principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Metric to Remember:**
> - **Raw sewage sludge:** 0.5% – 2% solids (98% – 99.5% water)
> - **Thickened sludge:** 4% – 8% solids
> - **Mechanically dewatered cake:** 15% – 35% solids
> - **Dewatered + Dried sludge:** 90% – 95% solids
> - **Volume reduction from raw to cake:** typically **~85%–95%**

## 1.3 Objectives of Dewatering

- Reduce sludge volume → lower transportation and disposal costs
- Render sludge handleable (non-fluid, can be stacked/transported by truck)
- Enable downstream processes (incineration, composting, landfilling)
- Recover water (filtrate/supernatant) for recycling to head of treatment plant
- Reduce leachate generation in landfills
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Place of Dewatering in the Sludge Management Train

The complete sludge treatment train in a modern sewage treatment plant is:

$$\text{Sludge Generation} \rightarrow \text{Thickening} \rightarrow \text{Stabilization} \rightarrow \text{Conditioning} \rightarrow \textbf{Dewatering} \rightarrow \text{Disposal}$$

Dewatering cannot be efficiently performed **without prior conditioning** (chemical or thermal) because raw sludge colloids hold water tightly and clog filtration media.

## 2.2 Sludge Conditioning (Pre-Dewatering Step)

Conditioning improves the dewaterability of sludge by:
- **Coagulating** the colloidal particles
- **Neutralizing** the surface charges on sludge flocs
- Producing **larger, stronger flocs** that release water easily

**Common Conditioning Methods:**
- **Chemical conditioning** – addition of polyelectrolytes, ferric chloride, lime
- **Thermal conditioning** – heating to 150–200 °C (Porteus process)
- **Freeze-thaw conditioning** – natural (cold climate) or artificial
- **Elutriation** – washing with treated effluent to reduce alkalinity

## 2.3 Methods of Dewatering — Classified

### A. Natural / Passive Methods
- **Sand Drying Beds** (most common in India)

### B. Mechanical Methods
- **Vacuum Filtration** (rotary drum vacuum filter)
- **Pressure Filtration** (plate-and-frame filter press, belt press)
- **Centrifugation** (solid-bowl / decanter centrifuge)
- **Sludge Lagoons** (passive, long retention)

## 2.4 Sand Drying Beds (Detailed — KTU High-Yield Topic)

A sand drying bed consists of:
1. **Top layer:** 150–300 mm of graded sand (effective size 0.3–0.75 mm)
2. **Intermediate layer:** 100–150 mm of gravel
3. **Underdrain system:** open-joint tile drains / perforated pipes
4. **Bottom:** Concrete slab with proper slope (1 in 200) to drain

**Design Parameters (Typical values, memorize for KTU):**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Sludge application depth | 200 – 300 mm per cycle |
| Solids loading rate | 25 – 40 kg DS/m²/year (digested sludge) |
| Drying period | 5 – 15 days (depending on climate) |
| Total cycle time | 2 – 6 weeks (including removal) |
| Area required per capita | 0.06 – 0.10 m²/capita (open beds) |
| Sand effective size | 0.3 – 0.75 mm |
| Sand uniformity coefficient | < 5 |
| Gravel layer thickness | 100 – 300 mm |
| Underdrain diameter | 100 mm minimum |
| Underdrain slope | 1 in 200 to 1 in 400 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Open vs Covered Beds:** Open beds in India are economical but suffer in monsoon (operate only 8–9 months/year). Covered beds allow year-round operation but have higher capital cost.

## 2.5 Rotary Drum Vacuum Filter (RDVF)

A continuously operating filter where a drum rotates partially submerged in a sludge pan while internal vacuum draws water through the filter media, leaving a cake on the outside surface.

**Key Operating Variables:**
- Drum submergence: 15% – 35% of drum area
- Drum speed: 0.1 – 2 RPM
- Vacuum: 300 – 650 mm Hg
- Cake thickness: 3 – 10 mm
- Filter yield: 15 – 40 kg DS/m²/h

## 2.6 Plate-and-Frame Filter Press

A batch process where sludge is pumped at high pressure (700 – 1500 kPa) between alternating plates covered with filter cloth. The cake forms in the frames; filtrate drains through the cloth.

**Key Parameters:**
- Operating pressure: 700 – 1500 kPa
- Cake thickness: 20 – 50 mm
- Cake solids: 35% – 45% DS
- Cycle time: 1.5 – 4 hours
- Filtrate quality: very clear (low suspended solids)

## 2.7 Centrifugation

Uses high gravitational forces (1000 – 3000 × g) to separate solids from liquid. The **solid-bowl (decanter) centrifuge** is the standard type for sludge dewatering.

**Key Parameters:**
- G-force: 1000 – 3000 g
- Cake solids: 20% – 35% DS
- Solids recovery: 90% – 95%
- Power consumption: high (1 – 2 kWh per m³ of sludge)

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Memorize All)

| Formula / Equation | Notation | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\%Moisture = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | W_wet = mass of wet sludge, W_dry = mass of dry solids | % |
| $\%Solids = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100 = 100 - \%Moisture$ | — | % |
| $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot r \cdot C}{2PA^{2}} \cdot V + \dfrac{\mu R_{m}}{PA}$ | Specific resistance equation (t/V vs V linear) | s/m |
| $r = \dfrac{2 \cdot b \cdot P \cdot A^{2}}{\mu \cdot C}$ | Specific resistance to filtration | m/kg |
| $C = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | Mass of solids per unit volume of filtrate | kg/m³ |
| Filter Yield $Y = \dfrac{M}{A \cdot t}$ | M = mass of cake solids, A = area, t = cycle time | kg/m²·h |
| $V_{ratio} = \dfrac{V_{initial}}{V_{final}} = \dfrac{C_{final}}{C_{initial}}$ | Volume reduction ratio | dimensionless |
| Mass of dry solids $M = Q \times X \times 10^{-3}$ | Q = flow (m³/d), X = MLSS (mg/L) | kg/d |
| Solids loading rate $SLR = \dfrac{M_{annual}}{A_{bed}}$ | M_annual = annual dry solids | kg/m²·year |
| $A_{bed} = \dfrac{M_{annual}}{SLR}$ | Bed area required | m² |
| $A_{bed} = 0.06 \text{ to } 0.10 \times P$ | Empirical per-capita area (P = population) | m² |
| Filtrate volume $V = A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2Pt}{\mu r C}}$ | Derived from SRF equation | m³ |
| Filter cake moisture $M_{c} = 1 - \dfrac{DS\%}{100}$ | Cake moisture content | decimal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for the Specific Resistance Equation:**
> - The plot of $\dfrac{t}{V}$ vs $V$ must be a **straight line**.
> - The **slope** $b = \dfrac{\mu r C}{2PA^{2}}$ — used to compute $r$.
> - The **y-intercept** $a = \dfrac{\mu R_{m}}{PA}$ — used to compute the medium resistance $R_m$.
> - For **easy-to-filter** sludge: $r < 10^{13}$ m/kg
> - For **difficult-to-filter** sludge: $r > 10^{14}$ m/kg

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drying beds** are dominant in Indian STPs (low cost, low O&M, suitable for small/medium towns).
- **Filter presses** dominate in large urban STPs producing Class-A biosolids.
- **Centrifuges** are used where footprint is constrained (urban retrofits).
- Dewatering is the **single largest energy-consuming step** in sludge management — design choices impact plant economics significantly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Specific Resistance to Filtration (SRF) Equation

This is a **KTU high-priority derivation**. Mastering it is essential for both Part A and Part B questions.

### Starting Concept — Darcy's Law for Cake Filtration

The basic flow equation for filtration through a compressible cake is **Darcy's Law**:

$$v = \dfrac{dV}{A \cdot dt} = \dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$

where the total resistance $R_{total}$ has two components:
- Resistance offered by the **filter medium**: $R_m$
- Resistance offered by the **filter cake** (which grows with time/volume): $R_c$

So:

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_c$$

### Step 1: Relate Cake Resistance to Filtrate Volume

The cake resistance at any instant is proportional to the mass of cake deposited per unit area. Since solids are deposited continuously as filtrate $V$ flows through:

$$R_c = \dfrac{r \cdot C \cdot V}{A}$$

where:
- $r$ = **specific resistance** of the cake (m/kg)
- $C$ = mass of dry solids per unit volume of filtrate (kg/m³)
- $V$ = cumulative filtrate volume (m³)
- $A$ = filtration area (m²)

### Step 2: Substitute Back into Darcy's Law

$$\dfrac{dV}{A \cdot dt} = \dfrac{P}{\mu \left(R_m + \dfrac{rCV}{A}\right)}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to a Separable Form

Cross-multiplying:

$$\left(R_m + \dfrac{rCV}{A}\right) \cdot dV = \dfrac{PA}{\mu} \cdot dt$$

$$\dfrac{\mu \cdot R_m}{PA} \cdot dV + \dfrac{\mu \cdot rC}{PA^2} \cdot V \cdot dV = dt$$

### Step 4: Integrate from $0 \to V$ and $0 \to t$

At $V = 0$, $t = 0$. Integrating:

$$\dfrac{\mu R_m}{PA} \cdot V + \dfrac{\mu r C}{2 P A^{2}} \cdot V^{2} = t$$

### Step 5: Divide Both Sides by $V$

$$\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{PA} + \dfrac{\mu r C}{2 P A^{2}} \cdot V$$

This is the **final SRF equation** — a straight line in $\dfrac{t}{V}$ vs $V$ coordinates.

**Final Form (KTU Exam-Ready Form):**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{t}{V} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\mu R_{m}}{PA}}_{\text{intercept } a} + \underbrace{\dfrac{\mu r C}{2 P A^{2}}}_{\text{slope } b} \cdot V}$$

### Step 6: Solve for Specific Resistance $r$

From the slope $b$:

$$b = \dfrac{\mu r C}{2 P A^{2}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{r = \dfrac{2 b P A^{2}}{\mu C}}$$

And from intercept $a$:

$$\boxed{R_m = \dfrac{a \cdot P \cdot A}{\mu}}$$

### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example (Solved End-to-End)

**Problem:** A vacuum filter operates with the following data:
- Pressure $P = 35,000$ N/m²
- Viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²
- Filter area $A = 0.05$ m²
- Solids concentration $C = 200$ kg/m³
- From the $t/V$ vs $V$ plot, slope $b = 8 \times 10^{5}$ s/m⁶, intercept $a = 2 \times 10^{4}$ s/m³

**Find:** (a) Specific resistance $r$, (b) Medium resistance $R_m$.

**Solution (a):**

$$r = \dfrac{2 b P A^{2}}{\mu C}$$

Substituting:

$$r = \dfrac{2 \times (8 \times 10^{5}) \times 35{,}000 \times (0.05)^{2}}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 200}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 8 \times 10^{5} \times 35{,}000 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 2 \times 8 \times 10^{5} \times 8.75 \times 10^{4}$$

$$= 1.4 \times 10^{11} \text{ N·s/kg·m² ... let us carefully re-evaluate}$$

Carrying out term-by-term:

$$2 \times 8 \times 10^{5} = 1.6 \times 10^{6}$$

$$1.6 \times 10^{6} \times 35{,}000 = 5.6 \times 10^{10}$$

$$5.6 \times 10^{10} \times (0.05)^{2} = 5.6 \times 10^{10} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.4 \times 10^{8}$$

Denominator:

$$1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 0.2 = 2 \times 10^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$r = \dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{-1}} = 7.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/kg}$$

(Units check: $\dfrac{\text{s/m}^6 \cdot \text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^4}{\text{N·s/m}^2 \cdot \text{kg/m}^3} = \dfrac{\text{s·N·m}^{-2}}{\text{N·s·m}^{-2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \text{m/kg}$ ✓)

**Solution (b):**

$$R_m = \dfrac{a \cdot P \cdot A}{\mu} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{4}) \times 35{,}000 \times 0.05}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= \dfrac{2 \times 10^{4} \times 1750}{10^{-3}} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 10^{7}}{10^{-3}} = 3.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** $r = 7.0 \times 10^{8}$ m/kg is **very low** (since $< 10^{13}$), confirming the sludge is **easy to filter**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sand Drying Bed Area

Given:
- Population $P$ (capita)
- Per capita sewage flow $q$ (L/capita·d)
- Influent SS concentration $X$ (mg/L)
- Dry solids produced per capita per day = $q \times X \times 10^{-6}$ kg/capita·d

**Annual dry solids:**

$$M_{annual} = P \cdot q \cdot X \cdot 365 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/year}$$

**Bed area:**

$$A_{bed} = \dfrac{M_{annual}}{SLR}$$

where SLR is in kg/m²/year. Alternatively, use the empirical relation $A = (0.06 \text{ to } 0.10) \times P$.

## 3.3 Volume Reduction Calculation

If the moisture content of the sludge drops from $M_1$ to $M_2$, the volume reduction is:

$$V_{reduction} = V_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{(1 - M_2)}{(1 - M_1)} \right)$$

For a sludge going from 99% moisture (1% solids) to 80% moisture (20% solids):

$$V_{reduction} = V_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{0.20}{0.01}\right) \rightarrow \text{Error! Use correct form}$$

Using $V_2/V_1 = (C_1/C_2) = (1/20) = 0.05$:

$$\text{Volume reduction} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95 = 95\%$$

This is why **even modest moisture reduction yields massive volume reduction** — and is the single strongest economic argument for dewatering.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

|---|---|---|---|---|---|

| Solids recovery (%) | >95 | 90–95 | >98 | 92–98 | 90–95 |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium | High | High | High |

| Energy use | None | Medium | High | Medium | High |
